short_description (EN Store): Fine Champagne VSOP from deep chalky Charente soils — lees-distilled, 200–400 eaux-de-vie blended, aged in century-old Limousin oak.
description (EN Store):
In 1927, Rémy Martin created the world's first VSOP Fine Champagne — a category designation that anchored a legal standard the entire cognac industry subsequently adopted. That founding act also encoded a house rule still unbroken today: every drop produced, from this VSOP to Louis XIII, must qualify as Cognac Fine Champagne AOC, meaning only Grande and Petite Champagne eaux-de-vie enter the blend — a restriction most rivals apply only to prestige cuvées.

The terroir behind that commitment is the deep chalky-calcareous subsoil of the two highest-ranked Cognac crus. Permeable and moisture-retentive, this chalk maintains grape acidity and aromatic concentration through dry growing seasons — conditions that distinguish Fine Champagne distillate from the sandier, clay-heavy soils of Bons Bois or Fins Bois. The VSOP draws on approximately 55% Grande Champagne and 45% Petite Champagne eaux-de-vie, sourced from Rémy Martin's ~240 ha of estate vines and the ~900-grower Alliance Fine Champagne cooperative.

The defining production signature is distillation on the lees — the residual yeast sediment left in the base wine — a practice most houses abandon before the still. Rémy Martin applies it house-wide, extracting additional aromatic compounds across each 24-hour double distillation in traditional charentais alembic copper pot stills. Maturation follows in French Limousin oak from trees at least 100 years old, seasoned outdoors for 2–3 years; the cru's large open grain drives vanilla and spice extraction alongside meaningful oxygen exchange. The VSOP blend assembles 200–400 individual eaux-de-vie aged 4–12 years, progressively moved into older barrels as they develop. As a non-vintage blended spirit, character is consistent across releases; the drinking window is open now and extends indefinitely once sealed.

name: Camus  Cognac XO Borderies (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 100% single-estate Borderies XO from clay-limestone silex soils; 'Instensity' ester-selection method applied since 2003 amplifies floral depth beyond terroir alone.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Camus founded the house in 1863, the Borderies cru was already prized for its violet-and-walnut character — but it was the fifth-generation decision to patent the 'Instensity' method in 2003 that codified what Borderies could become: from twenty one-litre samples drawn from the heads fraction of each second distillation, the single litre carrying the highest ester concentration is identified and reintegrated into the hearts, producing an eau-de-vie of measurably greater aromatic density than the terroir delivers unaided.

The fruit originates from 188 hectares of estate vineyards at Château du Plessis, the largest single-producer block in the Borderies — the smallest of Cognac's six crus. Clay-limestone soils dense with silex (flint) give Borderies its signature roundness and floral lift, a profile structurally distinct from the drier, more austere Grande Champagne style of the multinational-owned houses. Distillation is sur lies in nine copper Charentais alembics, adding ester complexity and textural weight before the spirit ever touches wood.

Aging runs a minimum 10 years (average 12–14) in 350 L Limousin oak casks with very light toast and a strong preference for previously used wood — a deliberate choice to preserve floral aromatics that new oak would mask. No boisé, no added sugar. The master blender samples every individual cask annually across a six-month cycle before blending decisions are made.

Camus is the only major Cognac house offering a commercially available 100% single-estate, single-cru Borderies XO — a category it pioneered in 2000 — making this the category's sole terroir-pure reference point at this age tier. As a non-vintage blend, drinking window is open now and stable over the next decade with proper storage.

name: Martell  V.S.O.P (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant VSOP from the house that distills only lees-free wine — cecillus oak, 4–8 years, violet-and-candied-fruit signature.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one deliberate break from every rival: distilling exclusively from clear, lees-free wines — a practice unique among the major Cognac houses — so that the spirit's character is built almost entirely in wood rather than carried from the base distillate. That choice demands exceptional oak, and Martell answers with fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted for a minimum 17–20 minutes, releasing vanilla and caramel without masking fruit.

The terroir anchor is the Borderies cru — the smallest of Cognac's six crus — where Martell's own 160 ha sit on silica-clay soils under a mild Atlantic oceanic climate. Martell purchases 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's output, a dominance unmatched in the industry; those soils are the direct source of the house's hallmark violet and candied-fruit aromatic profile. The VSOP draws on eaux-de-vie aged 4–8 years across Borderies, Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, and Fins Bois, blended and rested in vat for 1–4 months before bottling at 40% ABV. Double distillation in copper alambic Charentais stills at the Gallienne Distillery — the largest première chauffe stills in Cognac at ~130 hl — with tails cut made deliberately early for a clean, neutral heart. Once peak maturity is reached, selected eaux-de-vie are transferred to glass demijohns to arrest further aging, preserving the aromatic peak rather than chasing additional wood.

name: Martell  V.S (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant VS from the house that distills only lees-free wine — cecillus oak builds the character from scratch.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made a deliberate and enduring technical choice that no other major Cognac producer replicates: distilling exclusively from clear, fully settled, lees-free wines before a single drop enters the still. That decision strips the raw distillate of textural weight and funk, placing the entire burden of character-building on the barrel. The primary terroir anchor is the Borderies cru — the smallest of Cognac's six crus — where silica-clay soils yield eaux-de-vie of distinctively floral, violet-tinged aromatic character that Grande Champagne-dominant rivals cannot replicate. Martell purchases 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's output, a market dominance with no parallel in the appellation. Distillation runs through traditional copper alambic Charentais pot stills at the Gallienne Distillery in the Borderies heartland, with gas pressure adjusted hourly along the proprietary 'Martell Curve'; tails are cut deliberately early for a clean, neutral heart. Aging for VS reaches a minimum two years in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly bousinage-toasted for 17–20 minutes, in a mix of new and seasoned 'red' barrels. The special feature: where peers build flavour from lees-rich distillate, Martell VS builds it almost entirely from wood and Borderies terroir alone. Bottled at 40% ABV; once peak maturity is reached, eaux-de-vie are transferred to glass demijohns to arrest further development.

name: Martell  X.O (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant XO from the house that distills only lees-free wine — cecillus oak builds the character, not the distillate.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one deliberate break from every rival: distilling exclusively from fully clarified, lees-free Ugni Blanc wine — a choice that strips the raw distillate of texture and pushes all character-building into the barrel. The result is a cognac whose personality is almost entirely an argument about oak and terroir rather than fermentation. That terroir is the Borderies, the smallest of Cognac's six crus, where silica-clay soils on the gentle hills north of the Charente River yield eaux-de-vie of distinctive floral lift and candied-fruit density unavailable from the chalky Grande Champagne soils favoured by rival houses. Martell purchases 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's output, a market dominance with no parallel in the appellation. Distillation runs in traditional copper alambic Charentais stills at the Gallienne Distillery — the largest première chauffe stills in Cognac at ~130 hl — with gas pressure adjusted hourly along the proprietary 'Martell Curve' and tails cut early for a clean, neutral heart. Aging proceeds exclusively in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted (minimum 17–20 minutes bousinage), cycling between new barrels for spice and older 'red' barrels for fruit-forward development; once peak maturity is reached, eaux-de-vie transfer to glass demijohns to arrest further evolution. The XO draws on components aged well beyond the legal minimum, bottled at 40% ABV. Violet florality, dried apricot, and walnut praline are the Borderies signatures here; the drinking window is open now and holds comfortably for a decade in a sealed bottle.

name: Martell  Noblige (700 ml) Year of the Horse (Limited Edition)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant Cognac from the house that purchases 50–60% of the entire cru's output — bottled in a limited Year of the Horse collector edition.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one defining terroir bet that no rival has replicated: locking up 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's annual output — the smallest of Cognac's six crus — whose silica-clay soils yield an unmistakably floral, candied-fruit character that Grande Champagne-dominant houses simply cannot source. Noblige draws from this Borderies core, supplemented by Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, and Fins Bois, blending across 100+ eaux-de-vie to build layered complexity. The distillate is produced at the Gallienne Distillery in the heart of Borderies from clear, lees-free Ugni Blanc wine — a practice unique among the major houses — then double-distilled in copper alambic Charentais stills without Chauffe-vins pre-heaters, following the proprietary 'Martell Curve' for hourly temperature control. Aging proceeds exclusively in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted for a minimum 17–20 minutes, combining new casks for spice and tannin with older 'red' barrels for fruit-forward development. The special feature here is the Year of the Horse limited-edition packaging — a collectible release that wraps the house's signature Borderies-led blend in a culturally specific presentation unavailable in the standard range. As a non-vintage blend, Noblige is built for consistency and immediate accessibility, with the Borderies silica-clay signature — violet florals, candied pear, and a smooth, relatively light-bodied texture — fully integrated at 40% ABV.

name: Martell  Cordon Bleu (700 ml) Year of the Horse (Limited Edition)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant Cognac from Martell's lees-free distillation — 100+ eaux-de-vie, cecillus oak, Year of the Horse limited livery.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one defining technical wager that no rival has replicated: distilling exclusively from clear, lees-free wines, stripping the base distillate of all sediment before the alambic Charentais pot still fires — a deliberate choice that places the entire burden of character-building on oak rather than on the raw spirit. Cordon Bleu is the direct expression of that philosophy at its most ambitious. The blend draws from the Borderies cru — the smallest of Cognac's six crus, planted on silica-clay soils that yield a distinctively floral, candied-violet aromatic register found nowhere else in the appellation — supplemented by Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, and Fins Bois components. Martell purchases 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's output, a market dominance that amounts to a proprietary terroir position. In the cellar, 100+ individual eaux-de-vie are aged in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted to a minimum 17–20 minutes bousinage, with the oldest components reaching 25–30 years; once peak maturity is judged, reserves transfer to glass demijohns to arrest further wood extraction. The Year of the Horse edition presents the standard Cordon Bleu blend in a collectible limited livery — the liquid itself unchanged. Bottled at 40% ABV; drink now through the medium term.

name: Hennessy  V.S.O.P (With Box) (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Eight generations of Fillioux blending, lees-on distillation, and 350,000+ Limousin-oak casks across 65 Charente cellars define this multi-cru VSOP.
description (EN Store):
When Maurice Hennessy codified the star-rating system later adopted across the entire cognac industry, he was articulating a philosophy of measurable quality — the same logic that drives the Tasting Committee's annual review of ~10,000 individual eaux-de-vie to assemble this VSOP. That committee answers to the Fillioux family, whose unbroken eight-generation tenure as Hennessy's sole Master Blender lineage spans more than 200 years.

Sourcing draws from Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois — soils shifting from the shallow clay-limestone chalk of Grande Champagne, which drives fine-grained structure and long aging potential, to the clay-flint of Borderies, which contributes rounder, nuttier character. Eaux-de-vie resting in Limousin-oak casks near the humid Charente riverbank mellow faster; those in drier inland chais build more angular depth — both streams feed the final blend.

The technical signature that separates Hennessy from Martell or Rémy Martin: distillation both with and without lees, plus deliberate reintroduction of heads and tails into the brouillis before the second Charentais pot-still pass. This concentrates congeners, pushing the VSOP toward dark fruit, toasted oak, and building spice rather than the lighter floral register of Fine Champagne-focused houses. Bottled at 40% ABV, lightly filtered, non-vintage.

The VSOP's minimum four-year aging floor, drawn from a reserve of 350,000–470,000 casks — the world's largest cognac stockpile — gives the blending team unusual latitude to reach for older, rounder eaux-de-vie within the tier.

name: Mr.Boston  Apricot Brandy (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Approachable apricot brandy with vanilla oak sweetness and smooth finish; ideal for desserts and casual sipping.
description (EN Store): Mr.Boston Apricot Brandy is a commercial-grade fruit brandy offering straightforward apricot character with vanilla oak influence. The palate is soft and approachable, with low acidity and tannin, making it well-suited to dessert applications, cream-based pastries, and casual after-dinner service. The 1 L format supports home bar and hospitality use.

name: Mr.Boston  Wild Cherry Brandy (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Approachable USA brandy with vibrant wild cherry character and smooth, sweet fruit profile.
description (EN Store): Mr.Boston Wild Cherry Brandy is a commercial-grade brandy liqueur from the USA, crafted with a pronounced wild cherry flavor profile. The spirit delivers a smooth, medium-bodied experience with natural sweetness and approachable fruit-forward character. Ideal for casual sipping, dessert pairing, or mixing in cocktails.

name: Camus  Cognac VSOP Borderies (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 100% single-estate Borderies VSOP from 188 ha of clay-limestone silex soils, distilled sur lies with Camus's patented Intensty ester-selection method.
description (EN Store):
Jean-Baptiste Camus founded the house in 1863, and five generations later it remains the only major Cognac producer to bottle a commercially available 100% single-estate, single-cru Borderies expression — a category Camus itself pioneered. That independence from multinational ownership is not incidental: it is the structural reason the house can commit 188 hectares of its own Château du Plessis estate entirely to the Borderies cru rather than blending for volume.

Borderies is the smallest of Cognac's six crus, its clay-limestone soils dense with silex flints — a geological signature that drives the cru's characteristic violet florality and rounded texture, markedly softer than the austere, chalk-driven profile of Grande Champagne. Camus's 188-hectare block is the largest single-producer holding in the appellation.

The production sequence amplifies what the terroir begins: grapes are pressed within approximately two hours of harvest, fermented for roughly six days at 21–26°C using three house-selected yeast strains, then distilled sur lies in copper Charentais alembics to build ester complexity and textural weight. During the second distillation, Camus's patented 'Intensty' method draws twenty one-litre samples from the heads fraction, identifies the single litre of highest ester concentration, and reintegrates it into the hearts — a process applied across all expressions since 2003. Aging proceeds in 350 L Limousin oak casks, lightly toasted and predominantly previously used, to preserve rather than override the floral aromatics.

The VSOP's special distinction within the range is its position as the entry point to 100% estate Borderies fruit — no multi-cru dilution, no boisé, no added sugar — delivering the cru's candied-violet and soft-fruit register at VSOP maturity.

name: Demonio De Los Andes Pisco
short_description (EN Store): Peruvian grape brandy with clean fruit character and smooth finish; versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Demonio De Los Andes Pisco is a traditional Peruvian grape brandy from Tacama, one of Peru's established distilleries. This spirit exemplifies the clean, fruit-forward profile characteristic of Pisco production, with a neutral, approachable base that reflects careful distillation of Peruvian grape varieties. The nose carries subtle floral and fruity notes, while the palate remains smooth and balanced, making it equally suited to neat sipping or as a foundation for classic cocktails such as the Pisco Sour.

name: Hine  Antique XO (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 100% Grande Champagne XO, distilled sur lies in Charentais alembics, aged over a decade in French oak at Jarnac's sub-river-level cellars.
description (EN Store): Thomas Hine arrived in Jarnac from Dorset in 1791 and embedded a distinctly Anglo-French logic into the house he would rename in 1817 — the conviction that great Cognac is, first and foremost, a great white wine. That philosophy is structurally encoded in Antique XO. 100% Grande Champagne eaux-de-vie, sourced from the chalky-limestone valleys of the premier cru, aged over a decade in French oak inside the Chai des Millésimes — a cellar sitting two metres below the River Charente, where humidity swings between 70–90% and temperatures range from 6.5°C to 20.5°C, slowing oxidation and preserving aromatic precision. Distillation is conducted sur lies — on the fine lees of the 7–9-day native-yeast fermentation — a deliberate Hine signature that introduces pastry depth and roundness absent from lees-stripped competitors. New French oak receives the spirit first for initial months, then seasoned refill casks take over, moderating tannin extraction while allowing the chalk-driven terroir to assert itself. What sets Antique XO apart within the XO category is its single-cru restriction: where most XO blends draw from multiple crus for volume and consistency, Hine confines this expression entirely to Grande Champagne, sacrificing blending latitude to let the limestone minerality and natural vivacity of the premier cru speak without dilution. The result is a Cognac that leans toward floral lift and rancio precision rather than the heavy caramel register typical of the category.

name: Hine  Rare VSOP (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Fine Champagne VSOP from Jarnac's oldest Anglo-French house — lees-distilled Ugni Blanc, 6–12 years in French oak, bottled at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): When Thomas Hine arrived in Jarnac from Dorset in 1791 and married into the founding négociant family, he brought an Englishman's palate to Cognac — a bias toward delicacy over power that still defines the house's philosophy: great Cognac is, above all, a great white wine. Rare VSOP is a Fine Champagne blend, drawing eaux-de-vie aged 6–12 years from both Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne crus — the chalky-limestone valleys that deliver the region's signature long maceration of CO₂ into the base wine and the slow, penetrating oxidation that builds complexity without weight. Distillation is conducted sur lies in traditional Charentais copper alembic pot stills — a deliberate house signature that introduces pastry roundness and textural density uncommon in VSOP-tier Cognac. Aging begins in new or lightly toasted fine-grained French oak, then transitions to seasoned refill casks to moderate tannin extraction, with the Jarnac cellars' humidity cycling between 70–90% and temperatures from 6.5°C to 20.5°C driving a measured angels' share of 2–3% annually. What separates Hine from virtually every peer at this price tier is the lees-distillation protocol — a technique more associated with prestige cuvée Armagnac than commercial VSOP Cognac — producing a mid-palate density that reads richer than the age statement suggests. Bottled at 40% ABV with dilution introduced gradually over years to integrate the blend without shock.

name: Pierre Ferrand  Original 1840 Cognac (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Co-developed with historian David Wondrich, this 45% ABV Grande Champagne cognac revived the 19th-century cocktail-proof category — distilled on fine lees in 25 hl onion-shaped stills.
description (EN Store): In 2011, Alexandre Gabriel and cocktail historian David Wondrich reverse-engineered a surviving 1840 bottle of Pinet-Castillon Cognac to create the first modern spirit explicitly designed to restore the category's pre-Prohibition cocktail identity — a deliberate act of category transgression at a time when Cognac houses were competing on age statements and gift-box prestige. The fruit originates entirely from Logis d'Angeac, a co-owned 100-hectare estate in the Angeac-Champagne commune of Grande Champagne AOC — the Premier Cru de Cognac — where marine fossil limestone soils of extreme chalkiness drive high natural acidity in the Ugni Blanc base wine, the structural backbone that survives double distillation intact. Distillation runs slowly in unusually small 25 hl copper pot stills with onion-shaped heads, conducted on unfiltered fine lees to maximise aromatic concentration — a deliberate inefficiency that larger houses abandoned decades ago. Aging proceeds across seven cellars at Château de Bonbonnet, where dry cement-floored cellars (30–60% humidity) and earthen-floored humid cellars (70–100% humidity) are used in combination to build contrasting textural layers before blending. Bottled at 45% ABV (90 proof) — higher than the Cognac category norm — the 1840 Formula is the only expression in the Pierre Ferrand range engineered specifically to hold its aromatic structure when diluted over ice or in a long cocktail. The chalky Grande Champagne minerality and elevated proof together produce a cognac that functions as both a sipping spirit and a cocktail-grade ingredient without compromise.

name: H by Hine VSOP (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Jarnac-matured blend of ~20 Grande & Petite Champagne eaux-de-vie, distilled sur lies in Charentais copper pot stills — minimum 4 years, 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): Thomas Hine arrived in Jarnac from Dorset in 1791, married into the founding négociant family, and built a house philosophy that Cognac is, before anything else, a great white wine — a conviction that still governs every blending decision at H by Hine VSOP. The limestone-chalk soils of Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne are the structural engine here: porous, free-draining chalk forces Ugni Blanc roots deep, concentrating acidity and aromatic tension rather than weight. Fermentation runs 7–9 days in temperature-controlled vats with native wild yeast, producing a dry base wine of ~7–8% ABV. The defining technical signature is double distillation conducted sur lies — on the fine lees — in traditional Charentais copper alembic pot stills, a deliberate Hine choice that deposits pastry roundness and textural depth into the spirit before a single day in wood. The blend assembles approximately 20 eaux-de-vie aged a minimum 4 years, beginning in lightly toasted new French fine-grained oak before transfer to seasoned refill casks to moderate tannin extraction. What separates Hine from every peer of comparable scale is the house's operational 'early-landed' Bristol maturation regime — a parallel ageing philosophy unavailable at any other major Cognac house — though H by Hine VSOP is the Jarnac-matured expression, delivering the richer, rancio-inflected register of that cellar. Bottled at 40% ABV with dilution introduced gradually over years.

name: Hine  Cigar Reserve (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Grande & Petite Champagne limestone-chalk Cognac, distilled sur lies in Charentais copper pot stills — blended for cigar-weight structure.
description (EN Store): Thomas Hine arrived in Jarnac from Dorset in 1791 and built a house philosophy that Cognac is, above all, a great white wine — a conviction that shapes every production decision, including the deliberate choice to distil on the fine lees (sur lies) in traditional Charentais copper alembic pot stills, a signature that deposits pastry-like roundness into the base spirit before a single day in oak. Cigar Reserve draws from both Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne crus, whose rolling limestone-chalk valleys yield Ugni Blanc of high natural acidity and restrained sugar — the chalk subsoil acting as a slow-release moisture reservoir that keeps vines under measured stress, concentrating aromatic precursors without sacrificing the vivacity Hine prizes. Fermentation runs 7–9 days in temperature-controlled vats with native wild yeast, producing a dry base wine of ~7–8% ABV. Aging begins in new fine-grained French oak for initial tannin scaffolding, then transfers to seasoned refill casks to moderate extraction — a two-stage regime that builds structure without overwhelming the floral-fruit register. The Cigar Reserve is specifically assembled to carry the weight and aromatic persistence to hold its own against the ammonia and phenolic smoke of a lit cigar — an explicit structural brief that sets it apart from Hine's more delicate early-landed Bristol expressions. Bottled at 40% ABV; drinking window open now.

name: Camus  Cognac Ile De Re Fine Island (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Coastal Cognac aged in a maritime cellar on Île de Ré — Bois à Terroirs cru, shaped by Atlantic humidity and Camus's ester-selecting 'Instensity' distillation.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1863 by Jean-Baptiste Camus and still independently held by the fifth generation, the house made a deliberate geographic bet by establishing a separate aging cellar directly on Île de Ré — the most westerly point of the Cognac AOC, classified under the Bois à Terroirs cru — to capture the Atlantic's salt-laden humidity and accelerated oxidative exchange that a landlocked Charente cellar simply cannot replicate.

The island's coastal microclimate, with its higher ambient salinity and oceanic temperature swings, works on the spirit through the barrel stave in ways that amplify saline, iodic, and dried-citrus registers absent from the house's Borderies estate expressions. The underlying eau-de-vie is distilled sur lies in traditional copper Charentais alembic pot stills, with Camus's patented 'Instensity' method applied during the second distillation: twenty one-litre samples of the heads fraction are drawn, the single litre of highest ester concentration is identified and reintegrated into the hearts, producing a structurally more aromatic spirit before wood contact begins. Aging proceeds in previously used, lightly toasted French Limousin oak casks (350 L), preserving the maritime-inflected aromatics rather than overlaying them with new-oak vanilla. The Île de Ré range is the only commercially available Cognac expression aged exclusively in a coastal island cellar within the AOC's westernmost cru, making the maritime terroir — not the grape or the oak — the primary differentiating variable. As a non-vintage blend, drinking window is immediate; serve at 18–20°C to open the saline-floral register fully.

name: Black Bottle Classic Brandy (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Australian brandy with warm oak, vanilla, and dark fruit—approachable and versatile for aperitif or dessert pairing.
description (EN Store): Black Bottle Classic Brandy is an Australian brandy offering a balanced, approachable profile centred on oak maturation and warm spice. The palate reveals layers of vanilla, dark fruit, and caramel sweetness, with a smooth, medium-full body and gentle finish. Suitable for sipping neat, on the rocks, or as a versatile digestif.

name: Camus  Cognac VSOP Intensity (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Borderies clay-silex terroir + patented ester-reintegration distillation: Camus VSOP built for aromatic intensity, not wood.
description (EN Store):
Jean-Baptiste Camus founded the house in 1863, and five generations later it remains family-owned — an independence that allowed Cyril Camus to patent the Instensity distillation method in 2003, a technique no other major Cognac house employs. During the second distillation (bonne chauffe), twenty one-litre samples are drawn from the heads fraction; the single litre carrying the highest ester concentration is identified and reintegrated into the hearts, producing an eau-de-vie of measurably greater aromatic density than terroir alone would yield.

The fruit comes from 188 hectares in the Borderies cru — the smallest of Cognac's six growing zones — centred on Château du Plessis. Borderies soil is clay-limestone studded with silex (flint), a combination that drives the cru's signature violet-floral lift and textural roundness, markedly different from the leaner, more austere profile of Grande Champagne peers. Fermentation runs approximately six days at 21–26°C using house-selected yeast strains, and distillation is conducted sur lies to build ester complexity and body. Aging proceeds in 350 L Limousin oak casks with very light toast, predominantly previously used, a deliberate choice to keep delicate Borderies florals in the foreground rather than ceding them to wood. No boisé; no added sugar. The VSOP's drinking window is open now and will remain so for several years of home cellaring.

name: Camus  Special Dry Borderies Single Estate (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 100% Borderies single-estate Cognac from 188 ha of clay-limestone silex soils; patented Intensty ester-selection distillation since 2003.
description (EN Store):
Jean-Baptiste Camus founded the house in 1863, and five generations later Cyril Camus made a deliberate structural bet: acquire the largest single-producer vineyard block in the Borderies cru — 188 hectares centred on Château du Plessis — and bottle it without blending in other crus, a move that directly contradicts the multi-cru blending orthodoxy of the Cognac establishment.

Borderies is the smallest of Cognac's six growing areas, its clay-limestone soils dense with silex (flint) that drives the cru's signature violet florality and unusual textural roundness — qualities that distinguish it sharply from the drier, more austere Grande Champagne style favoured by multinational-owned peers.

Ugni Blanc grapes are pressed within two hours of harvest, fermented on temperature-controlled tanks for approximately six days, then distilled sur lies in copper Charentais alembic pot stills to build ester complexity and body. During the second distillation (bonne chauffe), Camus's patented 'Intensty' method draws twenty one-litre samples from the heads fraction, identifies the single litre with the highest ester concentration, and reintegrates it into the hearts — a process applied across all expressions since 2003. Aging proceeds in 350 L previously used Limousin oak casks with very light toast, a deliberate choice to keep delicate floral aromatics from being overwhelmed by wood.

The Special Dry expression is the only commercially available 100% single-estate Borderies Cognac in a 500 ml format, making it the most direct terroir document the house produces at accessible scale. As an NV blend, it is built for consistency of house character rather than vintage variation, and is drinking well now.

name: Camus  XO Prestige Decanter (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Fifth-generation Camus XO from 188ha Borderies estate; 'Instensity' ester-selection distillation on fine lees, aged 12–14 yrs in lightly toasted Limousin oak.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Camus founded the house in 1863, the Borderies — the smallest of Cognac's six crus — was already prized for its clay-limestone soils studded with silex flints, a geology that drives the cru's signature violet florality and rounded texture. Five generations later, Cyril Camus oversees 188 hectares of contiguous Borderies vineyard centred on Château du Plessis, the largest single-producer block in the cru.

The XO Prestige Decanter draws on that estate fruit, distilled sur lies in copper Charentais alembics to build ester complexity and body. During the second distillation, Camus's patented 'Instensity' method — drawing twenty one-litre samples from the heads fraction, identifying the single litre of peak ester concentration, and reintegrating it into the hearts — amplifies aromatic density beyond what terroir alone delivers. Aging runs a minimum 10 years, averaging 12–14 years, in 350 L Limousin oak casks with very light toast; previously used barrels are strongly favoured to keep the Borderies florality forward rather than masked by new wood. No boisé, no added sugar.

What sets this expression apart: the XO Prestige is a multi-cru blend presented in a collector decanter format, sitting alongside Camus's 100% single-estate Borderies XO — making it the house's accessible gateway to the Instensity technique at XO age. Bottled at 40% ABV; small caramel colouring permitted under AOC rules.

name: Martell  Chanteloup XXO (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Chanteloup XXO: Borderies-dominant Cognac from Martell's 1838 family estate, distilled lees-free in cecillus oak — the house's rarest prestige tier.
description (EN Store): Château de Chanteloup, acquired by the Martell family in 1838, lends its name to this prestige XXO expression — a tier that sits above XO in maturation depth and blend complexity. The Borderies cru, the smallest of Cognac's six growing zones, anchors the blend: its silica-clay soils produce eaux-de-vie of distinctively floral, candied-violet character that no other cru replicates, and Martell purchases 50–60% of the entire Borderies cru's output, making this terroir commitment the most concentrated in the industry. Distillation at the Gallienne Distillery begins with a deliberate departure from every other major house: the base Ugni Blanc wine is rested until fully clear and all lees are removed before entering the copper alambic Charentais stills — a lees-free protocol that strips the distillate of raw grain character and places the entire aromatic burden on oak. Aging proceeds exclusively in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted for a minimum of 17–20 minutes, with a combination of new barrels for spice and older 'red' barrels for fruit-forward development; once peak maturity is reached, eaux-de-vie are transferred to glass demijohns to arrest further evolution. The XXO designation signals extended aging beyond the XO minimum, drawing on the house's reserves — some dating to 1830 — stored in the Jean Martell cellar. The result is a spirit whose aromatic architecture is built almost entirely in wood, not from the distillate itself: a structural inversion that defines Martell's house identity.

name: Domaine Trapet  Marc de Bourgogne de Chambertin 1996 (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Chambertin pomace redistilled at source — a 1996 marc from one of Burgundy's most biodynamic Grand Cru estates, bottled in 500 ml.
description (EN Store): Domaine Trapet's roots in Gevrey-Chambertin stretch to the 1870s, when the family began assembling what would become one of the largest single-owner parcels of Le Chambertin (1.85 ha) — and it is the pomace of that Grand Cru fruit, harvested in 1996, that forms the raw material for this marc. Marc de Bourgogne is produced by distilling the pressed grape skins, seeds, and stems remaining after vinification; at Trapet, those solids come from Chambertin vines planted as early as 1919, grown on gravelly marl topsoil over limestone subsoil in the Côte de Nuits. The 1996 vintage in Burgundy was marked by cool, late-ripening conditions that concentrated acidity and phenolic structure — qualities that carry through even into the distillate's base character. No production parameters for this specific marc are documented in the brand library — cask type, distillation method, and ageing duration are unconfirmed — but the 1996 date on the label implies substantial time in wood, consistent with the slow oxidative development expected of a mature Burgundian marc. What is genuinely unusual here is the provenance: marc drawn from Demeter-certified biodynamic Grand Cru fruit, at a domaine that rejects sulphur throughout vinification, meaning the pomace itself arrives at distillation in an unusually clean, unpreserved state. The 500 ml format and nearly three decades of age make this a collector's curiosity as much as a digestif.

name: Domaine Trapet Marc de Bourgogne de Chambertin 1997 (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Marc de Bourgogne distilled from Chambertin Grand Cru pomace — a rare 1997 eau-de-vie from Gevrey's most storied 12.9-ha climat, bottled by a biodynamic estate that makes no other spirits.
description (EN Store): Domaine Trapet's entire identity is built around the 1.85 hectares it tends within Le Chambertin Grand Cru — gravelly marl topsoil over Bajocian limestone subsoil, vines planted as early as 1919 — so when the domaine distils a marc from that same pomace, the raw material carries the full weight of that address. Marc de Bourgogne de Chambertin is produced from the pressed grape solids of Pinot Noir harvested from those Grand Cru parcels, meaning the skins, seeds, and stems that survived Trapet's characteristically high whole-cluster fermentations (up to 80–100% for Grand Crus in practice) are the direct source of this spirit. The 1997 vintage — a warm, early-ripening Côte de Nuits year — yielded pomace of notable aromatic concentration. No production parameters (still type, distillation cut, cask regime, ABV, or aging duration) are disclosed in the brand library, which is itself unusual: Trapet publishes no spirits programme, making this bottling an anomalous, almost private-production release rather than a commercial line. What is certain is that the source material comes from Demeter-certified biodynamic viticulture and from one of Burgundy's most precisely farmed Grand Cru sites. At 500 ml and from a 1997 distillation, this is a collector's curiosity — a terroir document in spirit form from a domaine that otherwise makes only wine.

name: Coutanseaux Aîné XXO Cognac (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Grande Champagne XXO: eaux-de-vie aged 25–40 years, sur lies distillation, revived from dormancy by Frerejean Taittinger heirs using century-old stocks.
description (EN Store):
When Guillaume and Rodolphe Frerejean Taittinger revived Coutanseaux Aîné in 2015, they did so with a deliberate transgression: the house — founded in 1767 and dormant for most of the 20th century — would offer no VS, no VSOP, no standard age tier, entering the market exclusively at XO and above. The XXO is the house's mid-tier expression, yet it draws on eaux-de-vie aged 25 to 40 years, a range that places its youngest component well beyond the legal XXO minimum.

Every drop originates from Grande Champagne, Premier Cru de Cognac — the cru with the highest chalk concentration among all six Cognac sub-regions. That deep chalk-and-limestone subsoil drives slow, fine-grained extraction during aging and is the structural reason Grande Champagne eaux-de-vie outlast those of any other cru.

Distillation is performed sur lies in traditional copper alambics charentais — a choice that preserves lees-derived fatty acids and aromatic precursors through double distillation, building the textural density that sustains four decades of French oak contact. The grape source is 100% Ugni Blanc, whose naturally high acidity provides the backbone for extreme aging without oxidative collapse.

What no peer at this price tier routinely offers is a blend ceiling of 40 years from a house whose cooper origins make prolonged oak maturation the primary artistic medium — rancio is not incidental here, it is the point. Drinking window: approachable now; will hold and deepen for 15+ years in sealed bottle.

name: Larsen Viking Ship (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Larsen Viking Ship: a full-bodied French brandy with rich oak, vanilla, and warming spice—ideal for sipping or dessert pairing.
description (EN Store): Larsen Viking Ship is a full-bodied brandy from France's Champagne region, crafted in the traditional brandy style. The spirit opens with prominent oak and vanilla character from cask maturation, followed by supporting notes of dried fruit and warming spice. The smooth, low-acidity profile and gentle finish make it an approachable choice for sipping neat or as a complement to dessert and cheese courses.

name: Francois Voyer Hors d'Age Cristal  (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 40–60yr Grande Champagne Cognac, sur-lies distilled on Campanian chalk, assembled from the deepest paradis reserve in the house's 150-year history.
description (EN Store): Pierre Vaudon, cellar master and trained oenologist, inherited a paradis whose oldest stocks predate most major Cognac négociant inventories — the unbroken reserve at Le Maine Verret, Verrières, stretches back to the 1870s, and it is from this depth that the Hors d'Age Cristal is assembled. The 28-hectare estate sits on Campanian chalk in the communes of Verrières and Ambleville, Grande Champagne 1er Cru: the same porous, high-pH geology that forces Ugni Blanc roots deep, concentrating acidity and building the structural backbone that sustains 40 to 60 years of aging. Distillation is performed sur lies — on the full lees — in two traditional Charentaise copper alembics, a practice largely abandoned by larger négociant houses; the cut is made by nose alone, not instrument. Eaux-de-vie begin in virgin Limousin oak for approximately three years, then transfer to refill casks sourced from Limousin, Bercée, Gâvre, and Reno Valdieu forests, with aging split between damp and dry cellars to modulate the pace of oxidative development. The defining transgression here is scale: at this age tier, most houses blend from purchased stocks across multiple crus; Voyer draws exclusively from Grande Champagne estate and partner fruit, keeping the chalky-mineral terroir signature intact through six decades of wood contact. The result is a rancio of uncommon precision — dried walnut skin, camphor, and oxidative depth — rather than the diffuse sweetness common to blended Hors d'Age releases. Drink now through 2040+; decant 30 minutes.

name: Francois Voyer Terres Grande Champagne 1er Cru (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sur-lies-distilled Grande Champagne Cognac from Campanian chalk in Verrières — a near-extinct technique that larger houses abandoned decades ago.
description (EN Store): When Pierre Vaudon's predecessors began distilling at Le Maine Verret in Verrières around 1870, they retained a practice that most of the Cognac trade would quietly drop: distillation sur lies, running the fermented Ugni Blanc must through the Charentaise alembic with its lees still present. That decision — preserved unbroken across five generations — is the technical signature that separates this house from virtually every major négociant today. The 28 estate hectares in Verrières and Ambleville sit on Campanian chalk, the same deep, porous geology that gives Grande Champagne its reputation for slow aromatic development and structural longevity; inter-row wild meadow cover induces vine stress that concentrates the must before it ever reaches the still. Fermentation uses selected yeast strains chosen vintage by vintage according to climatic conditions; double distillation proceeds slowly in two traditional copper pot stills, with the heart cut made by nose rather than instrument. Initial aging runs approximately three years in virgin Limousin oak, drawing from forests including Bercée, Gâvre, and Reno Valdieu, before transfer to refill Cognac casks — the combination of damp and dry cellars used deliberately to modulate aromatic pace. What is unusual here is the lees-on distillation at commercial scale: the fatty acids and esters retained from the lies add a textural density and spice register that clean-distilled Grande Champagne simply cannot replicate. The Terres expression drinks with the spice-forward, slow-building character the house is known for, and the chalky subsoil ensures the acidity to carry it through a long finish.

name: Francois Voyer VSOP (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sur-lies distillation on Campanian chalk in Grande Champagne — VSOP aged 7–14 yrs, a technique abandoned by most major Cognac houses.
description (EN Store):
When Pierre Vaudon took over the cellars at Le Maine Verret in 2017, he inherited an unbroken paradis reserve stretching back to the 1870s — and a commitment to distilling sur lies (on the lees), a practice that the major négociant houses have largely discarded for efficiency. That choice defines the VSOP: the lees remain in contact through double distillation in traditional Charentaise copper alembics, contributing a textural richness and aromatic density that clean-distilled cognacs at this tier rarely achieve.

The 28-hectare estate in Verrières and Ambleville sits on Campanian chalk — the deep, porous geology that drains excess moisture, stresses the Ugni Blanc vines, and concentrates the eaux-de-vie's capacity for long aging. Inter-row wild meadow cover amplifies that stress deliberately.

Distillation cuts are made by nose rather than instrument. The spirit enters virgin Limousin oak for approximately three years, then transfers to refill casks from Limousin, Bercée, Gâvre, and Reno Valdieu forests. Damp and dry cellars are used in combination to modulate aromatic development. The VSOP holds 7–14 years — well beyond the legal four-year minimum.

Unusually for a VSOP, the age range overlaps with many houses' Napoléon tier, delivering rancio development that most peers reserve for older classifications. The result is a spice-forward, chalk-driven cognac that expresses the terroir of a single cru with no blending outside Grande Champagne.

name: Francois Voyer XO Gold (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sur-lies distillation on Campanian chalk in Grande Champagne; 20–30 years in Limousin oak yields rancio depth most négociants abandoned decades ago.
description (EN Store):
When Pierre Vaudon assumed the cellar master role in 2017, he inherited a paradis reserve stretching back to the 1870s — an unbroken lineage of eaux-de-vie that gives the XO Gold its structural backbone before a single blending decision is made. That continuity matters technically: the oldest stocks in the assemblage carry rancio character accumulated across decades, impossible to replicate by accelerated aging.

The 28-hectare estate in Verrières and Ambleville sits on Campanian chalk — the same deep, porous geology that forces Ugni Blanc roots downward, restricting yield and concentrating the high-acid must that survives double distillation with aromatic integrity intact. Wild inter-row vegetation amplifies vine stress and flavour density.

Distillation proceeds sur lies in traditional Charentaise copper alembics — a practice largely abandoned by large négociant houses — adding textural richness before aging begins. The XO tier spends 20–30 years in cask: approximately three years in virgin Limousin oak, then extended time in refill casks sourced from Limousin, Bercée, Gâvre, and Reno Valdieu forests. Damp and dry cellars are used in deliberate rotation to modulate aromatic development.

What separates this XO from category peers is the sur-lies distillation combined with a minimum 20-year aging floor — a combination that produces rancio complexity at a price point where most houses deliver far younger stock.

The current release shows the spice-forward, slow-oxidative character that defines Grande Champagne at full maturity; drink now through the next 15+ years with no loss of complexity.

name: Francois Voyer XO Extra (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sur-lies-distilled Grande Champagne Cognac aged 32–45 yrs on Campanian chalk; one of the last houses to cut by nose, not instrument.
description (EN Store):
When Pierre Vaudon assumed direction of François Voyer in 2017, he inherited a paradis whose oldest stocks predate most major Cognac brands' entire inventories — an unbroken cellar reserve stretching back to the 1870s, built on 28 hectares of estate Ugni Blanc rooted in Campanian chalk beneath Verrières and Ambleville, the geological spine of Grande Champagne 1er Cru. That chalk drains aggressively, stresses the vine, and produces eaux-de-vie of pronounced acidity and structural tension — the very architecture that rewards decades of aging.

The Extra is assembled from eaux-de-vie aged 32 to 45 years, beginning in virgin Limousin oak for approximately three years before transfer to refill casks sourced from Limousin, Bercée, Gâvre, and Reno Valdieu forests. Crucially, Voyer ages stocks across both damp and dry cellars simultaneously, modulating the rate of oxidative development and aromatic extraction — a deliberate dual-environment strategy that shapes the Extra's layered rancio character. Distillation is performed sur lies in traditional Charentaise copper alembics, with the heart cut made by the distiller's nose alone — a practice almost entirely abandoned by the négociant trade.

What separates this expression from peers at the XO tier is the minimum 32-year floor: the Extra occupies the band between Voyer's XO (20–30 yrs) and Hors d'Age (40–60 yrs), a granular age stratification most houses collapse into a single XO designation. Dried fig, beeswax, and chalk-driven tension persist through a long, rancio-marked finish.

name: Baron De Sigognac XO PLATINUM (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Bas-Armagnac XO from Château de Bordeneuve's sables fauves soils — Baco/Ugni Blanc distilled on a 1921 copper alembic, aged in Limousin and Vosges oak.
description (EN Store):
At Château de Bordeneuve near Eauze, the Guasch family — father Jean-Claude and son Thomas — controls every stage from vine to bottle, a vertical integration rare even within Armagnac. The estate's deliberate retention of Baco, a hybrid grape now largely restricted to this single appellation, is the defining transgression: where most modern houses have shifted to all-Ugni Blanc, Sigognac blends 50% Baco / 50% Ugni Blanc, the Baco driving the house's characteristic fruit density and textural richness.

The vineyards sit on sables fauves — the tawny sandy soils of Bas-Armagnac's innermost zone — that drain freely, stress the vine, and yield distillates of unusual aromatic precision. Distillation runs through a copper alembic armagnacais dating to 1921, a single-pass continuous still producing spirit at approximately 56% ABV, well below Cognac's double-distilled output, preserving a far greater congener load and aromatic complexity. The XO Platinum matures in Limousin and Vosges forest oak barrels of 400–420 litres, the extended ageing developing rancio character within the estate's medieval cellars, where beaten-earth floors maintain a stable microclimate. The result is an XO that carries Bas-Armagnac's fruit-forward DNA into genuinely aged, oxidative depth — a profile no Cognac house, and few Armagnac négociants, can replicate from a single estate.

name: Paulaner  Hefe Weissbier Naturtrub  500ml x 20
short_description (EN Store): Bavarian wheat beer with banana & clove notes, creamy texture, and refreshing carbonation—ideal for spicy Asian cuisine.
description (EN Store):
Paulaner Hefe Weissbier Naturtrub is a traditional Bavarian wheat beer brewed with a blend of wheat and barley malts, fermented with classic weizen yeast to produce distinctive phenolic and ester-driven aromatics. The beer presents a hazy golden appearance (naturtrub indicates unfiltered character) with a dense, persistent head.


Tasting Profile: Dominant banana ester and clove spice notes emerge from the yeast fermentation, supported by soft wheat bread and subtle citrus zest. The medium body carries creamy mouthfeel balanced by moderate carbonation and restrained hop bitterness, finishing clean and slightly dry.


A versatile food companion, particularly suited to spicy and sour Asian cuisines, light seafood, and casual appetizers.

name: Paulaner  Hefe Weissbier Party  5L x 1
short_description (EN Store): Classic Bavarian wheat beer with banana & clove esters, refreshing citrus, and smooth malt finish. Iconic party format.
description (EN Store): Paulaner Hefe Weissbier is a traditional Bavarian wheat beer renowned for its distinctive phenolic and ester profile. The beer delivers prominent banana and clove aromatics characteristic of Weissbier yeast fermentation, balanced by a light citrus note and subtle malt biscuit sweetness. Medium body and crisp carbonation create a refreshing, approachable drinking experience suitable for casual gatherings and food pairing. The 5L party keg format makes it ideal for entertaining.

name: Paulaner  Oktoberfest Bier  500ml x 20
short_description (EN Store): Classic Bavarian lager with toasted malt sweetness and balanced hop bitterness; crisp, refreshing finish.
description (EN Store): Paulaner Oktoberfest Bier is a traditional Bavarian-style lager brewed to the exacting standards of Munich's brewing heritage. The beer presents a golden amber hue with a dense, creamy head. On the palate, toasted malt character dominates, supported by subtle caramel notes and a clean, biscuity backbone. Moderate hop bitterness provides balance without overwhelming the malt-forward profile, while a light citrus and herbal hop character adds complexity. name: Bols  Genever (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The 1575 Amsterdam original: triple-distilled malt-wine genever that predates London Dry by 150 years, with a grain-forward weight closer to unaged whiskey than any modern gin.
description (EN Store):
When Jerry Thomas published his 1862 Bartender's Guide, the spirit in the Martinez and Holland House was this — Bols Genever, the dominant base spirit of 19th-century American cocktail culture, distilled at 31 Pijlsteeg, Amsterdam, steps from Dam Square, on a site tracing to 1679. The house philosophy, Semper Idem (Always the Same), is not a marketing slogan but a production mandate: every batch must match the archive.

The malt-wine backbone is triple-distilled in copper pot stills from a grain mash of malted barley, rye, corn, and wheat, cut deliberately low at approximately 47% ABV to preserve pronounced grain character. That malt wine — comprising over 50% of the final blend — is then married with neutral grain spirit and a separate botanical distillate (juniper, coriander, caraway, aniseed, plus a proprietary ingredient recovered from 17th-century Bols archives), redistilled in the same copper pots. The standard expression is bottled at 42% ABV, unaged.

What no peer in the modern gin category replicates: the malt-wine base gives Bols Genever a structural weight — cereal depth, faint wort sweetness — that sits categorically between whiskey and gin, making it a functionally distinct ingredient rather than a stylistic variation. The botanical register is secondary to grain, not the reverse.

name: Gin Mare  Mediterranean Gin (700 ml)

description (EN Store): Gin Mare is a Spanish gin crafted with Mediterranean botanicals, delivering a clean, juniper-forward profile balanced by bright citrus zest and herbaceous complexity. The nose presents floral and aromatic notes characteristic of quality gin production, while the palate maintains crisp acidity and a dry, refreshing finish. name: J.J.Whitley  London Dry Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Classic London Dry Gin with juniper-led botanicals, citrus zest, and a clean, dry finish. Versatile for cocktails and aperitifs.
description (EN Store): J.J.Whitley London Dry Gin exemplifies the traditional London Dry style with a juniper-forward botanical profile. description (EN Store): Four Pillars Spiced Negroni Gin is an Australian craft spirit blending juniper-forward character with warming spice botanicals. name: Suntory  Roku Japanese Craft Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Six Japanese botanicals distilled individually — Sakura, Yuzu, Sansho, Sencha & Gyokuro — layered over 8 classic gin botanicals by Suntory's Osaka craft team.
description (EN Store): Suntory, the house that broke Scottish orthodoxy by building Japan's first malt whisky distillery in 1923, applied the same obsessive botanical precision to gin: Roku (六, 'six') is built around six Japanese botanicals — Sakura flower, Sakura leaf, Yuzu peel, Sencha green tea, Gyokuro tea, and Sansho pepper — each distilled individually and then blended with eight classic gin botanicals including juniper and coriander. That single-botanical distillation protocol is the technical core of the gin: rather than co-macerating all 14 botanicals together, Suntory isolates each Japanese ingredient to preserve its distinct volatile aromatic signature before final assembly. The result is a gin where Sansho pepper's citrus-tingly heat, Gyokuro's umami-vegetal depth, and Yuzu's floral-acidic brightness remain structurally distinct rather than merging into a generic 'Japanese' note. What makes Roku categorically unusual is the dual-tea architecture — both a pan-fired Sencha and a shade-grown Gyokuro are used, two teas with fundamentally different amino acid and chlorophyll profiles, giving the gin a layered savouriness that no European craft gin replicates. Suntory's Osaka distillery provides the production base for this expression.

description (EN Store): Saffron Premium Gin is a French-crafted spirit that foregrounds the distinctive warmth of saffron spice alongside a classical juniper-led gin structure. The nose opens with bright citrus and herbaceous botanicals, while the palate reveals layered spice complexity and a clean, aromatic finish. name: Rock Rose  Original Edition Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): World's first gin to use Rhodiola rosea root, hand-foraged from Caithness sea cliffs — dual-juniper vapour infusion from Britain's most northerly mainland distillery.
description (EN Store):
Founded in August 2014 by Claire and Martin Murray, Dunnet Bay Distillery sits above the white-sand arc of Dunnet Bay in Caithness — three miles from Dunnet Head, the most northerly point of mainland Britain. That sub-arctic coastal exposure sustains rare botanicals that no other distillery can source from their own doorstep, most critically Rhodiola rosea, an arctic root growing on the surrounding sea cliffs that Rock Rose is credited as the first gin producer in the world to deploy.

The distillery's 18-botanical recipe — arrived at after 55 trial recipes testing 80 botanicals — is built on a deliberate dual-juniper architecture: Italian juniper for warm pine depth, Bulgarian juniper for lemon-sherbet brightness. Five botanicals including rowan berries, sea buckthorn, and blaeberries are locally grown or hand-foraged in Caithness. Production runs through two bespoke 500-litre copper pot stills by John Dore & Co. using vapour infusion — botanicals suspended in a basket through which vaporised wheat-base spirit passes, preserving volatile floral aromatics that direct maceration would destroy. Cuts are made by nose by the master distiller.

The Rhodiola rosea root delivers an earthy, Turkish-delight quality that anchors the entire profile — no peer gin carries this botanical. Bottled at 41.5% ABV, hand-filled, wax-sealed, and batch-numbered. The citrus character is built entirely from verbena, Bulgarian juniper, and sea buckthorn — no citrus peel is used anywhere in the recipe.

name: Rock Rose  Navy Strength Edition Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 57% ABV Navy Strength from UK's most northerly mainland distillery — world's first gin built on arctic Rhodiola rosea root, vapor-infused in copper pot stills above Dunnet Bay.
description (EN Store):
Claire and Martin Murray founded Dunnet Bay Distillers in 2014 after testing 55 trial recipes across 80 botanicals — a deliberate act of botanical archaeology that ended with a formula no other distillery had attempted: Rhodiola rosea, the hardy arctic root growing on the sea cliffs directly above the distillery, used as a primary botanical for the first time in gin history. That earthy, Turkish-delight-like root now anchors the entire Rock Rose architecture.

The distillery sits on the exposed sub-arctic coastline of Caithness, three miles from Dunnet Head — the most northerly point of mainland Britain. The harsh maritime microclimate that batters this shoreline is precisely what makes Rhodiola rosea viable here, and what gives the hand-foraged botanicals — rowan berries, blaeberries, sea buckthorn — their concentrated, wind-stressed character.

Production runs through two bespoke 500-litre copper pot stills (Elizabeth and Margaret, built by John Dore & Co.) using vapour infusion: botanicals sit in a basket through which vaporised wheat-base spirit passes, preserving volatile floral and citrus compounds that direct maceration would destroy. Cuts are made by nose. The Navy Strength expression is bottled at 57% ABV, hand-filled, wax-sealed, and batch-numbered. Unlike most craft gins, Rock Rose builds all citrus character from verbena, Bulgarian juniper, and sea buckthorn — zero added citrus peel. At Navy Strength, the dual-juniper spine (Italian for pine depth, Bulgarian for lemon-sherbet lift) and the Rhodiola earthiness arrive with considerably more structural force than the standard 41.5% expression.

Dunnet Bay sits above the two-mile white-sand arc of Dunnet Bay in Caithness, three miles from Dunnet Head — the most northerly point of mainland Britain. The citrus character here contains zero added citrus peel — it is built entirely from Bulgarian juniper, lemon verbena, and sea buckthorn, a structural transgression that produces brightness without the sharp edge of zest.

name: Rock Rose  Summer Edition Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): World's first gin to use Rhodiola rosea root, hand-foraged from Caithness sea cliffs — vapor-infused through dual-juniper architecture at Britain's northernmost mainland distillery.
description (EN Store):
Claire and Martin Murray founded Dunnet Bay Distillers in 2014 after testing 80 botanicals across 55 trial recipes — a deliberate act of botanical cartography that ended with a gin built around Rhodiola rosea, an arctic root growing on the sea cliffs directly surrounding the distillery, never before used in gin production anywhere in the world. Dunnet Bay occupies a sub-arctic coastal site above the two-mile white-sand arc of Dunnet Bay, Caithness — three miles from the most northerly point of mainland Britain. short_description (EN Store): Suntory's Osaka craft distillery gin built on 6 Japanese botanicals — sakura, yuzu, sencha, gyokuro, sansho — each distilled individually.
description (EN Store): Suntory's botanical philosophy for Sui traces directly to the same house that built Roku Gin on a 14-botanical framework — 8 classic, 6 Japanese — with each botanical distilled in isolation to preserve its distinct aromatic register before blending. Sui emerges from Suntory's Osaka Spirits & Liqueurs Craft Distillery, the company's dedicated gin, vodka, and liqueur facility that opened in 2024–2025, separating craft spirits production from the legacy whisky distilleries at Yamazaki and Hakushu. The Osaka plant brings urban precision to a category the house helped define in Japan. The six Japanese botanicals — sakura flower, sakura leaf, yuzu peel, sencha tea, gyokuro tea, and sansho pepper — are the same signature set that distinguishes Suntory's Japanese gin range from London Dry convention; individual distillation of each botanical is the technical decision that prevents the heavier botanicals (sansho's citrus-pepper heat, gyokuro's umami depth) from overwhelming the more volatile floral and citrus components. What separates Sui within the Suntory gin lineup is its positioning as a craft-distillery-specific expression, produced at a facility purpose-built for small-batch spirits rather than scaled alongside whisky production. The result is a gin where the Japanese botanical set is the structural argument, not a garnish over a juniper base.

This sloe berry expression layers that earthy, Turkish-delight-anchored base with the dark, tart fruit of hand-foraged sloe berries, extending the distillery's philosophy of building flavour from Caithness itself rather than imported citrus peel.

The distillery sits above the two-mile white-sand arc of Dunnet Bay in Caithness — the UK's most northerly mainland distillery — exposed to a sub-arctic coastal microclimate that forces botanicals to develop concentrated, stress-driven compounds unavailable in milder climates. That same environment shapes the sloe berries' sharp acidity and deep pigment.

Production runs through vapour infusion in bespoke 500-litre copper pot stills ('Elizabeth' and 'Margaret', manufactured by John Dore & Co.), preserving volatile top-notes that direct maceration would destroy. Cuts are made entirely by nose. The dual-juniper architecture — Italian juniper for warm pine depth, Bulgarian for lemon-sherbet lift — remains intact beneath the sloe fruit layer. Bottled at 41.5% ABV, hand-filled, wax-sealed, and batch-numbered.

The special feature here is structural: where most sloe gins suppress juniper under sweetness, Rock Rose's vapour-infusion technique keeps the juniper spine audible even through the sloe fruit. No critic scores. Drink now; no aging required.

name: Nikka  Coffey Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Coffey still gin from Yoichi's Miyagikyo grain plant — corn-base spirit redistilled with Japanese botanicals, retaining the Coffey still's signature sweetness.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru installed two Coffey continuous stills — built by Blairs Ltd. of Glasgow and originally housed at Nikka's Nishinomiya plant — at Miyagikyo, the intention was grain whisky. The decision to redistill that same corn-dominant Coffey spirit with botanicals was a deliberate transgression: using a whisky production apparatus to make gin, and letting the still's character define the result. The Coffey still's continuous distillation process preserves an unusual degree of sweetness and creaminess in the base spirit, a quality that no traditional pot-still gin base can replicate. Miyagikyo sits in a forested valley west of Sendai, fed by snowmelt from the Zao Mountain Range — water of exceptionally low mineral hardness — which contributes a soft, clean canvas for botanical expression. The botanical bill incorporates Japanese ingredients including yuzu, kabosu, amanatsu, and apple, layered over a juniper backbone, with the corn-grain sweetness of the Coffey distillate threading through the entire structure. What makes this expression categorically unusual is the base: a grain spirit distilled in a 19th-century continuous still, giving the gin a textural weight and confectionery depth that sits entirely outside the London Dry paradigm. Bottled at 47% ABV, it carries enough proof to hold the citrus oils in suspension without chill-filtration stripping the mouthfeel.

name: Saneha  Luminous Gin (700 ml)

description (EN Store): Saneha Luminous Gin is a Thai-produced spirit showcasing a clean, juniper-led botanical profile. The nose opens with bright citrus and classic gin aromatics, supported by herbaceous and subtle floral notes. name: Matsui  The Hakuto Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Alambic-distilled gin from Mt. Daisen's volcanic-filtered aquifer — Tottori's white peach terroir in a botanical spirit.
description (EN Store):
Matsui Shuzo, the Tottori-based house that built its reputation on whisky, turned its three Portuguese Charentais-style alambic pot stills toward gin — the same copper vessels used to produce the base neutral grain spirit for the Hakuto expression. The decision to use Charentais alambics rather than the column stills common to most Japanese craft gin producers yields a rounder, oil-retaining distillate that carries botanical weight without sharpness.

The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano in Japan's least-populated prefecture. Volcanic rock naturally filters the deep-aquifer groundwater drawn for production, stripping hardness and imparting the soft, low-mineral character that defines the house style across every expression. 'Hakuto' (白兎) references the white rabbit of Tottori's founding myth and, more concretely, the Hakuto pear and white peach — the region's signature stone and pome fruits — which the botanical recipe is built around, though the full botanical bill remains undisclosed.

What separates Hakuto from the crowded field of Japanese craft gins is its explicit regional identity: rather than defaulting to yuzu or sakura as shorthand for 'Japaneseness,' the expression anchors itself in Tottori-specific agricultural produce, making it one of the few Japanese gins with a genuinely prefecture-specific botanical argument. Bottling ABV and chill-filtration status are not publicly confirmed by the producer.

name: Seekers Mekong Dry Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cambodian dry gin with juniper-forward character, citrus zest, and herbaceous botanicals. Versatile aperitif spirit.
description (EN Store): Seekers Mekong Dry Gin represents a contemporary expression from Cambodia's emerging spirits landscape. The spirit leads with a clean, juniper-dominant botanical profile balanced by bright citrus zest and herbaceous notes, characteristic of a well-crafted dry gin. name: Rock Rose  Smoked Orange Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's most northerly distillery cold-smokes Caithness oranges over the same sub-arctic coast that yields its signature Rhodiola rosea root.
description (EN Store): Claire and Martin Murray founded Dunnet Bay Distillers in 2014 on the windswept shore of Caithness — three miles from Dunnet Head, the most northerly point of mainland Britain — specifically because that exposed sub-arctic coastline grows botanicals found nowhere else in Scottish gin production. Rock Rose Smoked Orange takes the distillery's dual-juniper architecture (Italian juniper for warm pine depth, Bulgarian juniper for lemon-sherbet lift) and layers it with cold-smoked orange over the core of hand-foraged Caithness botanicals: Rhodiola rosea root, sea buckthorn, rowan berries, and blaeberries. Production runs through two bespoke 500-litre copper pot stills — Elizabeth and Margaret, built by John Dore & Co. Ltd — using vapour-basket infusion, which passes vaporised wheat-grain spirit through the botanical charge rather than macerating it, preserving the volatile citrus-smoke top notes that direct maceration would flatten. Cuts are made entirely by nose by the master distiller. Rock Rose was the first gin globally to use Rhodiola rosea as a botanical; its earthy, Turkish-delight-like quality anchors the smoke so it reads as depth rather than aggression. Bottled at 41.5% ABV, hand-filled, wax-sealed, and batch-numbered in the distillery's ceramic bottle. The smoked orange expression breaks the distillery's own rule of building citrus character purely from botanicals — here, smoke-treated orange is an explicit structural element.

name: Suntory  Roku Sakura Bloom Japanese Craft Gin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Six Japanese botanicals distilled individually — sakura flower, sakura leaf, yuzu, sencha, gyokuro, sansho — layered over 8 classic gin botanicals.
description (EN Store):
Roku — Japanese for 'six' — takes its name from the deliberate decision to distill each of its six Japanese botanicals separately, in the vessel best suited to its character, before blending with eight classic gin botanicals including juniper and coriander. That choice of individual distillation, rather than a single combined maceration, is the technical spine of the entire expression.

The six Japanese botanicals — sakura flower, sakura leaf, yuzu peel, sencha green tea, gyokuro tea, and sansho pepper — are each harvested at peak season and processed in isolation: delicate florals in pot stills, tougher botanicals in different vessels, preserving aromatic integrity that a combined maceration would blur. Sansho pepper, the Japanese relative of Sichuan pepper, contributes a citrus-forward tingle distinct from the heat of black pepper. Gyokuro, Japan's most shade-grown tea grade, adds a deep umami-vegetal register absent from any European gin.

What makes Roku categorically unusual is the sakura leaf component — cherry blossom leaf carries a coumarin-adjacent, almost almond-skin quality that no classic London Dry botanical list approaches. The result sits outside the London Dry framework entirely: lower juniper dominance, a structural mid-palate of green tea tannin, and a sansho finish that pulses rather than fades. Bottled at 43% ABV; the Sakura Bloom edition foregrounds the floral sakura register above the standard Roku baseline.

name: Nakano BC Craft Gin Kozue (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): World's first gin to use koyamaki (sacred umbrella pine) from Koyasan UNESCO forest — resinous, forest-green, 47% ABV.
description (EN Store):
When Nakano BC released Kozue in 2017, it documented a category first: the world's first gin to incorporate koyamaki (Sciadopitys verticillata) — Japanese umbrella pine, one of Japan's five sacred trees — harvested from the Koyasan World Heritage forest on the Kii Peninsula. That single botanical decision defines everything that follows.

The Fujishiro Distillery sits in the mountainous interior of Kainan City, Wakayama Prefecture, where the warm, humid microclimate is moderated by the Kuroshio Current and the Kii mountain range. Wakayama's agricultural identity — producing over 60% of Japan's plums — informs Nakano BC's hyper-local sourcing philosophy: Onshu mandarin and lemon peel are grown locally, while sansho seeds add a distinctly Japanese pepper register.

Production follows a cold maceration of all botanicals — koyamaki needles, European juniper, citrus peels, sansho — in neutral grain spirit prior to redistillation, a method the producer states extracts full aromatic depth before the still runs. Bottled at 47% ABV with no wood aging and no chill-filtration details disclosed.

What no peer does: the koyamaki pushes the aromatic register firmly into forest-green, resinous, and vegetal territory — closer to a walk through a sacred mountain grove than the citrus-forward or floral profiles that dominate Japanese craft gin. NV expression; drink now or hold without concern for evolution.

name: An Dulaman	Irish Maritime Gin (500 ml)

description (EN Store): An Dulaman Irish Maritime Gin presents a classically structured gin profile grounded in juniper and herbaceous botanicals. name: Chiyomusubi Craft Gin Impact (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sake brewery gin from Tottori's Sea of Japan coast: rice-shochu base, 13 botanicals, Kuromoji spicebush at 47% ABV.
description (EN Store): Chiyomusubi has brewed sake in Sakaiminato City since 1865, drawing water by hand from a natural spring at the foot of the Chugoku Mountains in Shimane Prefecture — a three-hour round trip made daily during the brewing season. That same fermentation discipline underpins IMPACT gin: the base spirit is rice-shochu, distilled in-house by a toji who oversees both the brewery's fermented and distilled categories, making this one of the few gins where sake-house fermentation expertise is structurally embedded in the distillate itself. Layered over that base are 13 botanicals: juniper, coriander, angelica root, yuzu, lemon, hassaku orange, bitter orange, ginger, clove, cinnamon, blueberry — and critically, Kuromoji (Lindera umbellata), a Japanese spicebush wood native to the region that contributes a camphoraceous, forest-floor aromatic found in virtually no Western gin. Bottled at 47% ABV without dilution to standard strength, the elevated proof preserves the volatile top notes of the citrus quartet while keeping the Kuromoji resin intact. The defining transgression here is categorical: IMPACT is not a sake-flavoured novelty but a structurally dry gin whose spice architecture is built from a distilled rice foundation rather than a neutral grain base — depth that no botanical addition alone could replicate. Drink now or hold; no vintage variation applies.
