The warm, mild coastal microclimate of the Kii Peninsula concentrates both sugar and the characteristic bitter-pith aromatic compounds in hassaku fruit, which translate directly into the liqueur's profile: pronounced bitter-citrus rind alongside fresh juice sweetness. Nakano BC's production philosophy — rooted in cold maceration technique developed across sake, shochu, and umeshu categories — prioritises full flavour extraction while minimising oxidative degradation of volatile aromatics. What sets this expression apart within the umeshu/fruit liqueur category is the use of hassaku rather than ume: the fruit's natural astringency and grapefruit-adjacent bitterness produce a structural dryness that most fruit liqueurs from this region do not possess. Drink as produced; no vintage variation applies.

name: Nakano BC  Kishu Hassaku Umeshu (1.8 L)
short_description (EN Store): Nakano BC's patented low-oxidation umeshu built on Kishu hassaku citrus — Wakayama's own hybrid grown where Japan's plum belt meets the Kii Peninsula.
description (EN Store): Nakano BC's roots trace to a soy sauce shop founded in Kainan City in 1932, but it is the brewery's patented reduced-oxidation plum-steeping process — an unusual technical innovation for a regional umeshu producer — that defines its approach to fruit liqueur production. That same culture of precision is applied here to hassaku, the bitter-sweet citrus hybrid native to Kishu (present-day Wakayama Prefecture), a region that produces over 60% of Japan's plums and whose warm, mild climate is moderated by the Kuroshio Current sweeping up from the Pacific and the sheltering mass of the Kii Mountains. Those conditions — long sun hours, maritime warmth, and well-drained hillside soils — concentrate the hassaku's characteristic bitter pith and aromatic zest to an intensity rarely achieved in cooler citrus-growing zones. The 1.8 L format (isshobin) reflects the producer's positioning as a multi-category craft house supplying both household and hospitality markets from the same Fujishiro facility in Kainan City. Specific maceration duration and base spirit parameters are not publicly disclosed, but the patented oxidation-control methodology is the technical signature that separates Nakano BC's fruit liqueurs from conventional umeshu producers. The hassaku's pronounced bitter-citrus register — closer to Campari's grapefruit bitterness than to the stone-fruit sweetness of standard ume — makes this an outlier in the umeshu category. NV; drink now or cellar lightly chilled.

name: Kobe Sparkling Umeshu (200 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sparkling umeshu from a 1751 Nada brewery, built on a nihonshu base drawn from Miyamizu granite-spring water — 5% ABV, 200 ml.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1751 and operated by the Yasufuku family across 13 generations, Kobe Shushinkan brews at Mikage-go in Higashinada Ward — one of the five sub-districts of Nada-Gogo — where Miyamizu, a mineral-rich spring water filtered through Rokko Mountain granite, has defined the region's sake character for centuries. Low in iron and high in phosphorus and potassium, Miyamizu drives vigorous fermentation and a clean, mineral-edged backbone that carries directly into this umeshu's sake base.

The sparkling umeshu is built on that nihonshu (sake) base rather than the shochu or neutral spirit used by most commercial umeshu producers — a deliberate choice that keeps the grain character present beneath the natural ume plum juice. Bottled at 5% ABV in a 200 ml format, it sits far lighter than the category norm, making the carbonation the structural element rather than residual sugar or alcohol weight.

What separates this expression categorically: using a sake base in a sparkling format is genuinely rare — most sparkling umeshu rely on neutral spirit, which erases origin. Here, the Miyamizu mineral signature and the brewery's hand-cultivated koji tradition remain traceable in the finished liquid. The brewery also holds Organic JAS certification and launched the world's first carbon-zero sake in 2022, context that frames this small-format as part of a broader production philosophy rather than a novelty SKU.

name: Hakutsuru  Umeshu Genshu (720 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Hakutsuru's undiluted umeshu from Nada-Gogo — genshu bottling retains full-strength plum intensity drawn from Rokko Mountain miyamizu.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1743 by Jihei Kano, Hakutsuru built its reputation on Nada-Gogo's miyamizu — a mineral-hard spring water drawn from the Rokko Mountain aquifer, rich in potassium, phosphorus, and calcium, that drives vigorous fermentation and structural backbone. That same water underpins this Umeshu Genshu, where the mineral framework prevents the sweetness from becoming cloying. Nada's East Ward sits at the base of Mt. Rokko, where the cold seasonal wind Rokko oroshi descends toward the Seto Inland Sea — a microclimate historically prized for controlled, low-temperature fermentation. The genshu designation is the defining technical fact here: unlike standard umeshu, this expression is undiluted, bottled at full fermentation strength without water addition, concentrating both the ume fruit character and the mineral spine of the miyamizu base. The special feature is precisely this transgression against category convention — most commercial umeshu is diluted to soften and standardize; Hakutsuru refuses that step, delivering a denser, more textured plum liqueur. As a non-vintage blend, it is ready to drink now, equally compelling over ice or as a long pour with soda to let the genshu concentration gradually open.

name: Hakutsuru  Umeshu Genshu (1.8 L)
short_description (EN Store): Hakutsuru's undiluted umeshu from Nada-Gogo: ume fruit macerated to genshu strength, built on miyamizu mineral water from the Rokko Mountain aquifer.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1743 by Jihei Kano, Hakutsuru has operated continuously in East Nada Ward, Kobe — and the same miyamizu hard spring water drawn from the Rokko Mountain aquifer that drives the brewery's famously dry otoko-zake sake profile also underpins this umeshu. Genshu designation means the liquid is bottled undiluted, preserving the full extraction of ume fruit at elevated natural alcohol rather than being cut back with water as most commercial umeshu are. The miyamizu source — high in potassium, phosphorus, and calcium, critically low in iron — provides a mineral backbone that keeps the sweetness from becoming cloying, a structural quality rarely found in mass-market plum liqueurs. At 1.8 L, this is the brewery's large-format offering, designed for high-volume service or household use, yet the genshu concentration means intensity per pour is not compromised. What separates this expression from category peers is precisely that undiluted genshu status: the ume maceration is allowed to speak at full strength, delivering a density of stone-fruit extract that diluted umeshu cannot replicate. As a non-vintage blended product, consistency across batches is a deliberate house priority, and the 1.8 L format reflects Hakutsuru's scale as the world's largest sake producer channelled into accessible, high-quality everyday formats.

name: Hakutsuru  Nigori Yuzushu (720 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuzu-infused nigori from Nada-Gogo's miyamizu aquifer — coarse-mesh filtered, rice sediment intact, 12.5% ABV.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1743 by Jihei Kano in East Nada Ward, Kobe, Hakutsuru built its reputation on miyamizu — the mineral-dense hard spring water drawn from the Rokko Mountain aquifer, high in potassium, phosphorus, and calcium, that drives vigorous fermentation and the region's signature dry, structured otoko-zake profile. The Nigori Yuzushu deliberately inverts that house character: where the brewery's core range pursues crisp dryness, this expression layers yuzu citrus into a coarse-mesh filtered nigori, retaining suspended rice sediment for texture and body that the standard lineup never courts.

Production follows Hakutsuru's in-house proprietary yeast strains and koji cultivation in a humidity- and temperature-controlled koji-muro, with moromi fermentation supported by the cold Rokko oroshi wind descending from Mt. Rokko toward the Seto Inland Sea. The nigori filtration method — a mesh pass rather than full pressing — leaves rice solids in suspension, yielding a 12.5% ABV bottling that is notably lighter than most sake expressions. The yuzu addition is the transgression: no other expression in the Hakutsuru range introduces a botanical element, making this an outlier from Japan's #1-selling sake house. Drink well-chilled and shake gently before pouring; no vintage dating applies.

name: Kunizakari  Chita Umeshu (720 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Farm-to-bottle umeshu: Kunizakari grows its own ume at Kuni Zakari Farm on the Chita Peninsula, steeping fruit from the same 1844 kura that pioneered sensor-driven fermentation.
description (EN Store):
The Nakano family established their kura in Handa City in 1844, on a Chita Peninsula site where warm maritime air (annual average ~15.5°C), natural spring water, and historic transport access made the district one of Edo-period Japan's foremost brewing hubs. What separates this umeshu from category peers is vertical integration: the ume fruit is cultivated in-house at Kuni Zakari Farm, eliminating the commodity sourcing that defines most commercial plum liqueurs.

The base is Kunizakari sake — produced via sandan-jikomi (three-stage moromi addition) with koji-driven simultaneous saccharification and fermentation, monitored by the brewery's proprietary sensor systems that track temperature and humidity alongside toji sensory judgement. Whole ume are steeped in this sake base rather than a neutral spirit, meaning the liqueur carries the umami depth and structural backbone of nihonshu rather than the flat sweetness of shochu-based rivals.

The special feature: sake-based umeshu at this price point is rare — most producers use distilled alcohol to cut cost and extend shelf life. Kunizakari's farm-grown fruit steeped in house-brewed sake produces a liqueur where stone-fruit tartness and fermented-grain richness are genuinely co-present. No vintage applies; drink now or cellar short-term.

name: Kunizakari  Kokusan Nanko-Ume Umeshu (2 L)
short_description (EN Store): Nakano family's own Kuni Zakari Farm ume, steeped in sake from a Handa kura founded 1844 — plum liqueur rooted in provenance.
description (EN Store): The Nakano family, brewing in Handa City since 1844, took the unusual step of cultivating their own Nanko-ume plums at Kuni Zakari Farm rather than sourcing fruit externally — a vertical integration that directly controls the ripeness and variety profile entering the liqueur. Nanko-ume, grown in Wakayama Prefecture, is Japan's most prized plum cultivar, valued for its thick flesh, high juice yield, and pronounced tartaric acidity. Steeping in Kunizakari sake base rather than neutral spirit means the liqueur carries the brewery's characteristic umami depth alongside the fruit. The Chita Peninsula water source — abundant natural spring water drawn from the same aquifer that underpins the sake range — contributes a soft mineral backbone that keeps the sweetness from becoming cloying. At 2 litres, this is a format designed for high-volume service or household use, yet the raw material is farm-specific, not commodity fruit. What separates this from category peers is the closed loop: same family, same water, house-grown ume, house-made sake base — an end-to-end provenance almost unheard of in commercial umeshu. As a non-vintage expression, consistency across batches is the stated goal, and the 1844 institutional knowledge behind fermentation control supports that claim.

name: Kunizakari  Kunizakari Umeshu (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Farm-to-bottle umeshu: Nakano Brewery grows its own ume at Kuni Zakari Farm, steeping fruit from the same Chita Peninsula terroir that has shaped sake here since 1844.
description (EN Store):
The Nakano family established their kura in Handa City in 1844, and the decision to operate Kuni Zakari Farm — growing ume in-house rather than sourcing commercially — is the single fact that separates this umeshu from the category majority. The fruit originates on the Chita Peninsula, a warm coastal strip averaging ~15.5°C annually, where maritime air moderates the growing season and the same abundant natural spring water that feeds the sake fermentation is drawn for liqueur production.


Production follows the traditional shirozake-base or neutral-spirit maceration method standard to umeshu; no wood aging is confirmed. The ume fruit steeps until the stone-fruit sugars and tartaric acids fully integrate, yielding a liqueur that leans on the natural acidity of the plum rather than added citric correction. The house philosophy — engineering umami depth and clean finish over aromatic delicacy — carries directly into this expression: the sweetness is anchored by a firm acidic spine rather than left unchecked.


What is unusual here is vertical integration: from orchard soil to bottled liqueur, no third-party fruit supply chain intervenes — a rarity among industrial-scale Japanese umeshu producers. At 500 ml / ฿300, this is an entry point into a brewery whose flagship Gahoujin uses rice polished to 20% remaining, and that same obsessive sourcing logic applies here.

name: Kunizakari  Obaa-Chan No Yuzushu (300 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuzu liqueur from Kochi's citrus heartland, brewed by a Handa kura founded 1844 — tart, aromatic, food-forward.
description (EN Store): Nakano Sake Brewery, founded in 1844 on the Chita Peninsula, built its reputation on deliberately robust, umami-forward sake engineered to match Aichi's bold culinary culture — a philosophy that carries directly into its fruit liqueur range. Obaa-Chan No Yuzushu sources yuzu from Kochi Prefecture, Japan's most celebrated yuzu-growing region, where the fruit develops its characteristically sharp acidity and intensely aromatic rind oils under the warm, humid Pacific-facing climate. The base is sake-brewed at the Handa facility, drawing on the Chita Peninsula's natural spring water, before yuzu juice and peel are macerated into the liquid — a technique that preserves the volatile citrus aromatics that heat-based extraction would destroy. No wood aging is employed, keeping the profile bright and citrus-dominant rather than rounded; the liqueur retains the brewery's signature clean, sharp finish. What sets this expression apart within the yuzushu category is the frank, grandmother's-kitchen directness implied by its name — this is not a polished cocktail ingredient but a full-flavoured, slightly rustic yuzu liqueur built around the fruit rather than sweetness. At 300 ml and ฿400, it is the most accessible entry point into the Kunizakari range, yet it carries the same food-pairing intentionality as the flagship sake lineup.

name: Kunizakari Obaa-Chan No Yuzushu (720 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuzu liqueur from Kochi's citrus heartland, brewed by a Handa kura founded in 1844 — tart, aromatic, and food-forward by design.
description (EN Store):
The Nakano family has been brewing in Handa City on the Chita Peninsula since 1844, a region historically so central to Japanese sake production that its waterways once served as the primary maritime artery for distributing nihonshu across the country. Obaa-Chan No Yuzushu — loosely, 'Grandma's Yuzu Wine' — steps outside the brewery's core sake identity entirely, drawing on yuzu sourced from Kochi Prefecture, Japan's most celebrated yuzu-growing region, where the fruit develops its characteristically sharp, resinous rind and high citric acid concentration under the warm Pacific-facing climate of Shikoku's mountainous interior.


Production follows the brewery's established liqueur approach: yuzu is integrated into a sake base produced via Kunizakari's standard sandan-jikomi (three-stage addition) moromi fermentation using koji-processed rice, with the resulting liquid coarse-filtered to retain textural weight. No wood aging is applied, preserving the raw citrus volatiles intact. The ABV sits within the brewery's standard 13–15% range, giving this more structural presence than most commercial yuzu liqueurs bottled at 8–12%.


What separates this expression from category peers is the deliberate alignment with Kunizakari's food-forward house philosophy — the yuzu acidity is calibrated to cut through umami-rich dishes rather than function as a standalone aperitif sweetener, an unusual design brief for a fruit liqueur. As an NV product, consistency across batches is prioritised over vintage character.

name: Inchon  Plum Umeshu (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thai plum liqueur with delicate stone-fruit sweetness and floral notes; versatile aperitif or dessert companion.
description (EN Store): Inchon Plum Umeshu is a Thai-produced plum liqueur offering clean stone-fruit character with subtle floral undertones. The light body and moderate sweetness make it equally suited to aperitif service, dessert pairing, or mixing. Primary plum and stone-fruit notes are supported by gentle spice and a crisp finish, positioning the expression as an accessible bridge between traditional umeshu tradition and contemporary cocktail culture.

name: Chalong Bay  Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Agricole-style rum from Phuket's Chalong Bay — first-press Thai sugarcane, Armagnac copper still, stainless-rested 8 months. Cannot legally be called 'rum' in Thailand.
description (EN Store): Founded in 2012 by Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis, Chalong Bay was built around a deliberate philosophical inversion: import a 40-year-old Armagnac copper still from France and use it to make agricole-style rum in Southeast Asia — the ancestral homeland of sugarcane itself — reversing the colonial direction of rum-making knowledge. The distillery sits on Phuket's south-east coast in Chalong sub-district, metres from the Andaman Sea, where tropical monsoon heat and humidity define the growing season. Sugarcane is sourced from small Thai partner farms, hand-harvested without burning, grown without chemical fertilisers, and pressed within approximately six hours of harvest to capture 100% first-press juice from a single indigenous Thai monovarietal — one selection from over 200 varieties cultivated across Thailand. Fermented in stainless steel with a selected yeast strain, the wash is distilled through the Armagnac-type copper column still, exiting at 65–73% ABV, then rested in stainless steel for a minimum of 8 months with slow progressive dilution to 40% ABV — zero wood contact, zero additives, no sugar. The special feature: Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the label; bottles are legally designated 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane.' The result is a clean, tropical-vegetal white spirit whose freshness is entirely a function of cane provenance and distillation craft, not oak.

name: Flor de Caña  7 years Grand Reserve Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged in ex-bourbon oak 8 km from an active volcano — zero additives, zero sugar, pure tropical-climate barrel work.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa founded the Ingenio San Antonio estate in 1890, and five generations later the family still seals every barrel with a local Nicaraguan plantain leaf before sending it into warehouses that have never seen an air-conditioner. That deliberate refusal of climate control is the technical core of the Slow-Aged™ process: barrels rest in the Pacific lowland heat of Chichigalpa, roughly 8 km from San Cristóbal — Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m — where temperatures accelerate wood extraction without artificial intervention. Volcanic ash continuously enriches the 35,000-acre estate's soil, and underground reservoirs carry mineral-laden water filtered through the same volcanic substrate.

The molasses comes exclusively from the estate's own Ingenio San Antonio mill, fermented with a proprietary in-house yeast for approximately 36 hours to ~9% ABV, then distilled five times through a five-column continuous still to ~96% ABV — a deliberately neutral distillate that places the entire flavour burden on the barrel. Spirit enters re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak at 77% ABV and ages for seven years under purely natural tropical conditions across ~320,000 simultaneously aging barrels on-site.

What separates this expression from virtually every Spanish-style rum at its price tier: zero added sugar, caramel, or colouring — the dry, clean texture is structural, not cosmetic. The result is toasted oak, vanilla, and ripe tropical fruit without the sticky residue that defines most sweetened competitors. Fair Trade USA and carbon-neutral certified, powered entirely by sugarcane bagasse.

name: Flor de Caña  12 years Centenario Rum (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged in volcanic heat — zero additives, ex-bourbon oak, 12 years of tropical barrel interaction at Chichigalpa.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa founded the Chichigalpa distillery in 1890 on the Pacific lowlands flanking San Cristóbal, Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m — a deliberate siting decision whose consequences are still felt in every barrel. Volcanic ash continuously enriches the 35,000-acre Ingenio San Antonio cane estate, while mineral-laden underground water, filtered through Ring of Fire geology, feeds fermentation. The non-climate-controlled on-site warehouses — holding roughly 320,000 barrels simultaneously — mean the tropical heat drives accelerated wood extraction with zero artificial intervention, the core of the Slow-Aged™ philosophy.

Production begins with estate molasses pasteurised, diluted, and inoculated with a proprietary in-house yeast, fermenting approximately 36 hours to ~9% ABV before five-column continuous distillation to ~96% ABV. The distillate is cut to 77% ABV for entry into re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak barrels, sealed with local plantain leaves, and left to age for 12 years in open tropical warehouses. What makes this expression categorically unusual: zero added sugar, caramel, or colouring — all colour and sweetness perception derives purely from barrel char and volcanic-climate wood interaction, a strict departure from the sweetened post-distillation norm across Spanish-style rums. The result is a notably dry, clean profile carrying vanilla, toasted oak, and tropical fruit character without the sticky texture that defines most of its peers.

name: Flor de Caña  18 years Centenario Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged in volcanic shadow — additive-free, ex-bourbon barrels, zero added sugar across 18 slow-aged years.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa planted the roots of this operation in 1890 on the Pacific lowlands of Chichigalpa, 8 km from San Cristóbal — Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m — a proximity that defines every barrel in the warehouse. Volcanic ash continuously enriches the 35,000-acre Ingenio San Antonio sugarcane estate, while underground reservoirs carry mineral-laden water filtered through Ring of Fire geology, feeding the molasses that becomes this rum.

Fermentation runs approximately 36 hours in stainless steel using a proprietary in-house yeast strain, producing a wash of roughly 9% ABV. That wash passes through a five-column continuous still, exiting near 96% ABV — a deliberately neutral distillate that places the entire flavour burden on wood. Distillate is diluted to 77% ABV before filling into re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak barrels, sealed with local plantain leaves, then left in non-climate-controlled on-site warehouses where the tropical heat drives accelerated but entirely natural barrel interaction — no humidifiers, no air-conditioning, no artificial accelerants.

The defining transgression: where virtually every Spanish-style rum adds sugar, caramel, or colouring post-distillation, the Centenario 18 carries zero additives — colour, vanilla character, and dried-fruit depth emerge exclusively from 18 years of volcanic-climate oak contact. The result is a notably dry, clean profile unusual for the tier, bottled at 40% ABV with no filtration method disclosed.

name: Chalong Bay  Tropical Note Series Cinnamon (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Agricole-style Thai rum from Phuket's Chalong Bay — vapour-infused with cinnamon via gin-technique alambic basket; cannot legally be called rum in Thailand.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 2012 by Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis, Chalong Bay was built on a deliberate philosophical inversion: applying a 40-year-old Armagnac copper alambic, imported from France, to sugarcane juice in Southeast Asia — the crop's ancestral homeland — reversing the colonial direction of rum knowledge. The Cinnamon expression belongs to the Tropical Notes sub-range, where Thai botanicals are introduced not by maceration but by vapour infusion through a flavour basket mounted on the alambic, a technique borrowed directly from gin production, meaning the cinnamon is carried into the spirit as aromatic vapour rather than steeped into the base liquid.

The base spirit is distilled from 100% first-press juice of a single indigenous Thai sugarcane variety, hand-harvested without burning and pressed within approximately six hours of cutting, from partner farms across Thailand cultivated without chemical fertilisers. The core white spirit is rested in stainless steel for a minimum of eight months with slow progressive dilution to 40% ABV — zero wood contact, preserving the clean tropical-vegetal character of the cane before the botanical layer is applied. Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the home-market label; bottles are designated 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane,' making this one of the few rums in the world legally barred from its own category name in its country of origin.

name: Chalong Bay  Tropical Note Series Sweet Thai Basil (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Agricole-style Thai rum from Phuket's Chalong Bay — vapour-infused with fresh Thai sweet basil via gin-technique flavour basket on a 40-year-old Armagnac alambic.
description (EN Store):
When Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis founded Chalong Bay in 2012, they made a deliberate philosophical inversion: importing a 40-year-old Armagnac copper alambic to Phuket to distil sugarcane spirits in the crop's ancestral homeland — reversing the colonial direction of rum knowledge. The Sweet Thai Basil expression extends that logic into botanicals, applying a vapour-infusion technique borrowed directly from gin production, passing distillate vapour through a flavour basket loaded with fresh Thai sweet basil mounted on the alambic's rectifier column.

The base spirit begins with 100% first-press juice of a single indigenous Thai sugarcane variety, hand-harvested without burning and pressed within approximately six hours of cutting. Fermentation runs in stainless steel with a selected yeast strain before distillation on the Armagnac-type still. The finished spirit is rested in stainless steel — zero wood contact — preserving the raw tropical-vegetal character of the cane before progressive dilution to 40% ABV. Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the label; bottles are legally designated 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane.'

What distinguishes this expression within the Tropical Notes sub-range is the use of a specifically Thai culinary herb — sweet basil rather than the lemongrass or cinnamon variants — anchoring the spirit's Southeast Asian identity in a single, site-specific botanical. Drink now; no aging potential applies to this unaged expression.

name: Chalong Bay  Tropical Note Series Lemongrass (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Agricole-style Thai rum from Phuket's Chalong Bay — lemongrass vapour-infused via gin-technique alambic basket, 40% ABV, zero wood contact.
description (EN Store): Founded in 2012 by Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis, Chalong Bay was built around a deliberate philosophical inversion: importing a 40-year-old Armagnac copper alambic from France to distil sugarcane spirit in Southeast Asia — the crop's ancestral homeland — reversing the colonial direction of rum-making knowledge. The Lemongrass expression belongs to the Tropical Notes sub-range, where Thai botanicals are introduced not by maceration but by vapour infusion through a flavour basket mounted on the alambic, a technique borrowed directly from gin production, making this one of the few sugarcane spirits in the world distilled with botanical vapour contact rather than post-distillation blending. The base is 100% first-press juice of a single indigenous Thai sugarcane variety, hand-harvested without burning and pressed within approximately six hours of cutting — no molasses, no additives. After distillation, the spirit is rested in stainless steel for a minimum of eight months with slow progressive dilution to 40% ABV, preserving the raw tropical-vegetal character of the cane with zero wood influence. What sets this expression apart categorically is the combination of agricole-style raw-juice base with in-still botanical infusion: the lemongrass character is fixed at the moment of distillation, not layered on afterward. Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the home-market label — bottles read 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane.' NV by nature; drink now for maximum aromatic lift.

name: Flor de Caña  Extra Seco Imported Rum 4 Years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum, additive-free, aged 4 years in ex-bourbon oak under the volcanic heat of San Cristóbal — zero sugar, zero colouring.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa planted the Ingenio San Antonio sugarcane estate in Chichigalpa in 1890, and the distillery has never left it — molasses pressed on-site, distilled on-site, aged on-site, across roughly 320,000 barrels resting simultaneously in non-climate-controlled warehouses. That single-estate discipline is the foundation of everything Flor de Caña produces.

The estate sits approximately 8 km from San Cristóbal volcano (1,745 m, Nicaragua's tallest active peak), whose continuous ash fall fertilises the cane fields and whose underground infiltration mineral-enriches the water supply. The Pacific lowland heat — unmediated in open warehouses — drives accelerated wood interaction without artificial accelerants, the producer's so-called Slow-Aged™ process.

Molasses is fermented for approximately 36 hours with a proprietary in-house yeast, then distilled five times through a five-column continuous still to roughly 96% ABV — a deliberately neutral base that places all flavour responsibility on the barrel. Distillate is diluted to 77% ABV before filling into re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak barrels, sealed with local plantain leaves, and aged four years in tropical heat.

The defining transgression: zero added sugar, caramel, or colouring — unusual in Spanish-style rum, where post-distillation sweetening is near-universal. What reads as sweetness here is entirely barrel-derived vanilla and toasted oak. Bottled at 40% ABV with no additives; gluten-free and kosher certified.

name: Plantation  3 Stars White Rum  (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Barbados/Jamaica/Trinidad tri-island blend; unaged to lightly aged molasses distillates from WIRD's coral-limestone seafront site, bottled at 41.2% ABV.
description (EN Store): Alexandre Gabriel built the 3 Stars White as a deliberate argument: that white rum deserves the same multi-terroir thinking applied to aged expressions. The blend draws on three distinct distillation traditions — Barbados column still from Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery (WIRD) at Blackrock, St. Michael, a coastal seafront site on coral-limestone geology where Atlantic winds circulate through the aging warehouse; Jamaican pot still distillate from Long Pond or Clarendon, contributing high-ester funk; and Trinidad column still spirit for structural lightness. WIRD, founded 1893, houses some of the world's oldest operational rum pot stills and produces approximately 85% of Barbados's rum — its coral-limestone terroir imparts a dry, chalky mineral lift that anchors the blend's backbone. The rums are unaged or only lightly aged, then blended and bottled at 41.2% ABV without the double-ageing Cognac finish applied to Plantation's darker expressions. The special feature here is the deliberate omission of the house's signature technique: no French oak, no dosage — just raw terroir contrast between three islands. The result is a white rum that reads as a study in origin rather than a finished, polished product, making it the most transparent expression in the Plantation portfolio.

name: Plantation  XO 20th Anniversary Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Barbados molasses rum, 8–20yr tropical aging at WIRD's seafront warehouse, finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Charentes.
description (EN Store): When Alexandre Gabriel revived the 17th-century practice of shipping Caribbean rum to European cellars for a second maturation, the XO 20th Anniversary became the clearest expression of that thesis. Distilled at Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery (WIRD) in Blackrock, St. Michael — a coastal site on Barbados's Atlantic-facing shoreline, built on coral-limestone geology in 1893 — the molasses-based distillate spends 8 to 20 years in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels inside a seafront warehouse where the tropical angel's share runs 6–7% annually, concentrating the spirit at a rate more than double what France's cooler cellars would allow. The rum then travels to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, where it finishes in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks — the same barrels that shaped Maison Ferrand's own eaux-de-vie — adding dried-fruit depth and baking-spice structure unavailable from American oak alone. A Cognac-style dosage is applied: toasted unrefined brown sugar blended with rum and aged one year in Ferrand Cognac barrels, functioning as a flavour-integrating syrup rather than a sweetener. No other major rum brand systematically applies this dual-climate, dual-oak model across a named-anniversary blend. Bottled at 40% ABV, the XO draws from barrels spanning a 12-year age range, giving the blender latitude to balance the maritime salinity of younger Barbados distillate against the oxidative depth of the oldest stocks.

name: Chalong Bay  Tropical Note Series Kaffir Lime (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Phuket agricole-style rum — vapour-infused kaffir lime via gin-technique alambic basket, bottled at 40% ABV from Thailand's original sugarcane homeland.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 2012 by Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis on Phuket's south-east coast, Chalong Bay was built around a deliberate philosophical inversion: applying a 40-year-old Armagnac copper alambic imported from France to sugarcane grown in Southeast Asia — the crop's ancestral homeland — reversing the colonial direction of rum knowledge entirely. The distillery sits in Chalong sub-district, metres from the Andaman Sea, where year-round tropical monsoon heat and humidity shape the character of the raw material before distillation begins.

The base spirit uses 100% first-press juice of a single indigenous Thai sugarcane variety, hand-harvested without burning and pressed within approximately six hours of cutting. Fermentation runs in stainless steel with a selected yeast strain; the new-make exits at 65–73% ABV before progressive dilution to 40% ABV — with no wood contact at any stage, preserving the raw tropical-vegetal character of the cane. The Kaffir Lime expression belongs to the Tropical Notes sub-range, where kaffir lime is introduced via vapour infusion through a flavour basket on the alambic — a technique borrowed directly from gin production, making this a botanical rum by distillation method rather than post-still blending. Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the label; bottles are legally designated 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane.'

name: Chalong Bay  Spiced Series (White Spiced) (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Phuket agricole-style rum legally barred from calling itself rum — vapour-infused with Thai lemongrass, cinnamon & sweet basil via gin-technique flavour basket on a 40-year-old Armagnac alambic.
description (EN Store):
When Marine Lucchini and Thibault Spithakis founded Chalong Bay in 2012, they imported a 40-year-old copper Armagnac alambic from France to Phuket's south-east coast — deliberately routing European distillation craft back to the ancestral homeland of sugarcane itself. The White Spiced expression extends that inversion further: Thai botanicals are introduced not by maceration but by vapour infusion through a flavour basket mounted on the alambic, a technique lifted directly from gin production, carrying lemongrass, cinnamon, and Thai sweet basil through the distillate as rising vapour rather than steeping them in finished spirit.

The base is 100% first-press juice of a single indigenous Thai sugarcane variety, pressed and fermented within approximately six hours of hand-harvest — no burning, no chemical inputs — on partner farms across Thailand. Fermentation runs in stainless steel with a selected yeast strain. The new-make exits at approximately 65–73% ABV before progressive dilution to bottling strength with no wood contact, preserving the raw tropical-vegetal character of the cane. Thai alcohol licensing law prohibits the word 'rum' on the label; bottles are legally designated 'The Spirit of Phuket distilled from 100% pure sugarcane.' The botanicals read as distinctly regional — the lemongrass is unmistakably Malay Peninsula, not Caribbean — making this a spiced spirit with no credible geographic peer.

name: Asura  (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Potato) (Alc. Volume 40%) (330 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thailand's legally-constrained answer to craft vodka: a potato-based sura khao distilled at 40% ABV, smooth where mass-market lao khao is sharp.
description (EN Store): Thai excise law forces every domestic distilled spirit — regardless of production method — into a single legal category: sura khao (white spirit). Asura operates inside that constraint deliberately, positioning itself as a craft alternative to the abrasive, sharp-edged mass-market lao khao that dominates convenience-store shelves across Thailand. The base here is potato — an unusual raw material in a category dominated by glutinous rice, sugarcane, and molasses — lending a naturally starchy, subtly earthy fermentation character that separates Asura from its grain- and cane-based peers. Bottled unaged at 40% ABV, the spirit bypasses the caramel-and-oak masking common in brown spirits, leaving the distillate's intrinsic character fully exposed. Specific fermentation vessel and yeast strain are not publicly documented, though the clean, low-heat finish cited by trade press suggests tight distillation cuts and likely stainless-steel column or pot-still processing. The 330 ml format — smaller than the category standard — reflects a cocktail-bar and on-premise focus, consistent with Asura's emergence in Bangkok's craft cocktail renaissance. What no peer in the sura khao category does: market a potato base as a premium differentiator in a rice-and-cane world, effectively creating a Thai analogue to Central European potato vodka within a legal framework that won't let it use that word.

name: Phoenix  White Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Phoenix White Rum: smooth Philippine spirit with tropical notes, vanilla, and caramel—ideal for cocktails and casual entertaining.
description (EN Store): Phoenix White Rum is a versatile Philippine spirit offering a clean, approachable profile with tropical fruit, vanilla, and subtle caramel notes. The smooth finish and light body make it well-suited for both sipping and mixing in cocktails. Ideal for casual entertaining, tropical-inspired drinks, and pairing with light appetizers or desserts.

name: Asura  Silver Giant (Thai White Spirits Distilled From SugarCane) (625ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sugarcane-base Thai white spirit distilled under strict Excise Dept. licensing — craft-tier sura khao built for Bangkok's cocktail renaissance.
description (EN Store): Thai law forbids Asura from calling itself a vodka or any Western category — yet that legal constraint is precisely what makes it interesting. Operating within the tightly licensed sura khao framework, Asura is engineered to sit at the opposite end of the spectrum from mass-market lao khao, which is typically sharp, abrasive, and uncompromising. The base here is locally sourced sugarcane, lending a naturally softer fermentation character than glutinous-rice mashes and contributing the faint fresh-fruit lift that distinguishes this expression from grain-based peers. Distillation is carried out under Thai Excise Department licensing through stainless steel apparatus, producing a clean, relatively neutral distillate that retains herbal and light tropical-fruit aromatics without the aggressive fusel heat of traditional village-style spirits. Bottled unaged at 40% ABV in a 625 ml format — a deliberate domestic-market sizing — with no publicly confirmed fining or filtration regime. What sets Asura apart categorically is its positioning as a cocktail-bar-ready Thai spirit at a time when Bangkok's bartenders are actively replacing imported vodka with domestic sura khao expressions, a movement documented as recently as 2025. The result is a white spirit that functions as both a culturally specific ingredient and a technically neutral canvas.

name: Asura  Rose Gold Giant (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Lychee) (625ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thai-licensed sura khao distilled from lychee — clean, mildly sweet, low-heat finish; Bangkok's cocktail scene answer to craft white spirits.
description (EN Store): Thai law forbids any domestic distilled spirit from calling itself a vodka or gin on the label — so Asura, a premium sura khao riding Bangkok's cocktail renaissance, operates under the sura khao classification while deliberately engineering a profile that speaks the language of craft white spirits. That regulatory constraint is itself the story: a producer choosing to push quality upward within a category historically defined by sharp, abrasive mass-market lao khao. The Rose Gold Giant expression is distilled from lychee, a departure from the rice, glutinous rice, and sugarcane bases that dominate the premium Thai white spirit tier — lychee as a fermentation substrate brings a naturally floral, stone-fruit aromatic register that no grain-based peer can replicate. Fermentation employs the traditional look-pang yeast-ball method (ground rice inoculated with yeast and mold, spiced with galangal and long pepper), lending a subtle herbal undercurrent beneath the fruit-forward distillate. Bottled unaged at 40% ABV in a 625 ml format, with no documented chill-filtration, preserving aromatic integrity. What sets this apart categorically: lychee distillate in the sura khao format is vanishingly rare — this is not a rice spirit approximating fruit character, but a spirit whose fermentable base is the fruit. Drink now; no aging upside applies to an unaged white spirit of this style.

name: Plantation  Barbados 5 Year Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-island Barbados molasses rum from WIRD's coral-limestone seafront distillery, double-aged tropically then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks, Charentes.
description (EN Store):
When Alexandre Gabriel revived the 17th-century practice of shipping Caribbean rum to European cellars for a second maturation, he anchored the method to a specific address: Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery (WIRD), Blackrock, St. Michael — a coral-limestone coastal site founded in 1893, whose seafront warehouses sit close enough to the Atlantic that the angel's share carries salt air. That maritime influence is structural here, not incidental.

WIRD's low-elevation, Atlantic-facing microclimate accelerates tropical aging at 6–7% angel's share per year — roughly three times the loss rate in France — concentrating the molasses-derived base before the rum ever boards a ship. The Barbados 5 Year undergoes five years of tropical maturation in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels at WIRD, then travels by sea to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, for a continental finish in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks. That second climate — cool, humid, slow-oxidative — grafts dried-fruit depth and baking-spice tannin structure onto the Caribbean core without erasing it.

Special feature: This is a single-island expression — no blending across origins — making it the clearest unobstructed read of WIRD's coral-limestone Barbados terroir within the Plantation range. The Cognac-style dosage (toasted brown sugar aged in Ferrand Cognac barrels) is openly disclosed, a transparency rare in the category. Drink now through the near term; the Cognac-cask finish integrates fully at room temperature.

name: Asura  (Thai White Spirits Distilled From SugarCane) (Alc. Volume 40%) (330 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sugarcane-base Thai white spirit distilled under strict sura khao licensing — cleaner and softer than mass-market lao khao, built for Bangkok's cocktail bars.
description (EN Store): Thai law permits no label to read 'vodka' or 'gin' — every distilled spirit must be classified as white (sura khao) or brown (lao see), regardless of how it is made. Asura operates inside that constraint deliberately, producing a sugarcane-distilled white spirit whose aromatic profile — herbal lift, fresh-fruit brightness, low-heat finish — reads closer to a craft neutral than to the sharp, abrasive character of mass-market lao khao. The base is locally sourced sugarcane, fermented using a proprietary process before distillation; specific vessel type and yeast strain remain undisclosed, though the clean palate suggests tight distillation cuts and likely stainless-steel column work. Bottled unaged at 40% ABV in a 330 ml format, it carries no oak influence whatsoever — the spirit's character is entirely a function of raw material and distillation precision. What sets Asura apart within its category is its explicit positioning inside Bangkok's cocktail renaissance: where traditional lao khao resists bartender use due to harsh fusel character, Asura is engineered to function as a Thai-origin mixing spirit — a category transgression that Thai excise law forces to remain nameless. Drink now; no aging benefit applies.

name: Asura  (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Pineapple) (Alc. Volume 40%) (330 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Pineapple-based Thai white spirit distilled under sura khao licensing — cleaner and fruit-forward where mass-market lao khao runs sharp.
description (EN Store): Thai law forces every distilled spirit — regardless of production method — into a single legal category: sura khao (white spirit). Asura turns that regulatory constraint into a positioning statement, operating within Bangkok's craft micro-distillery scene where producers are pushing the category toward cleaner, more aromatic distillates rather than the abrasive heat that defines mass-market lao khao. This 330 ml expression is built on pineapple as its declared base ingredient, a departure from the rice and glutinous rice that anchor most Thai white spirits, and one that drives the fresh, tropical-fruit character noted across third-party assessments. Bottled unaged at 40% ABV — consistent with the sura khao category's no-maturation convention — the spirit foregoes any oak influence, keeping the distillate character fully exposed. Specific fermentation vessel, yeast strain, and cut points are not publicly documented, though premium-tier Thai micro-distilleries in this space typically employ stainless steel pot or column stills. The special feature here is categorical: Asura cannot legally label itself a vodka or fruit spirit even if its production approximates one, making it a genuinely Thai expression that Western category language cannot contain. Distribution is effectively confined to the domestic Thai market by excise thresholds, making this a rare find outside the country.

name: Asura  Pearl White Giant (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Potato) (625ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thailand's legally-constrained answer to craft vodka: a potato-base white spirit bottled at 40% ABV, built for Bangkok's cocktail renaissance.
description (EN Store): Thai law permits no label to read 'vodka' or 'gin' — every distilled spirit must be classified as sura khao (white) or lao see (brown), regardless of how it is made. Asura operates squarely inside that constraint while deliberately engineering its way out of the category's reputation: where mass-market lao khao is sharp and abrasive, Asura targets a clean, mildly sweet palate with low finish heat — a profile that Bangkok bartenders have begun treating as a Thai-origin neutral base for cocktails. The Pearl White Giant variant specifies potato as its base ingredient, a deliberate departure from the rice or sugarcane mashes that dominate the Thai white spirit tier, lending a characteristically waxy, subtly earthy texture that rice distillates rarely produce. Bottled at 40% ABV with no aging — consistent with the sura khao category — the spirit retains the raw material's starchy weight rather than masking it with wood. Specific fermentation vessel and yeast strain are not publicly documented, though the premium positioning and cocktail-bar adoption suggest a cleaner distillation cut than traditional look-pang yeast-ball production. Distribution is effectively confined to the Thai domestic market: export is legally restricted for producers below the 300,000-litre/day threshold, making this expression genuinely rare outside Thailand. Drink now; no development benefit from cellaring an unaged white spirit.

name: Plantation Panama 2010 Vintage Limited Edition Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Panama 2010 distillate, double-aged tropically then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks in Charentes — Plantation's single-terroir vintage method applied to Central American column-still rum.
description (EN Store): Alexandre Gabriel's Plantation brand was built on a single conviction: that Caribbean rum, like Cognac, deserves a second life in French oak. The Panama 2010 Vintage Limited Edition is the direct expression of that philosophy — a single-origin, single-vintage distillate that first matured tropically in Panama in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, accumulating concentrated tropical character at the Caribbean angel's share rate of 6–7% per year, before being shipped to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, France, for a second maturation in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks. Panama's lowland cane-growing geography and equatorial heat drive a particularly clean, column-still distillate profile — the tropical stage builds dense raw-sugar and dried-fruit concentration, while the Cognac cask continental phase layers dried-fruit, baking spice, and structured tannin that American oak alone cannot deliver. What separates this expression from Plantation's blended range is its single-vintage, single-origin discipline: no multi-island blending, no averaging across years — the 2010 harvest stands uncut. Select expressions in this range also receive Plantation's Cognac-style dosage: toasted brown sugar blended with rum and aged one year in Ferrand Cognac barrels, used as a flavour enhancer rather than a simple sweetener. The result is a rum that occupies the structural space between an aged agricole and a Cognac-finished blended rum — a category position no Panamanian expression typically holds.

name: Plantation Panama 2008 Vintage Limited Edition Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 2008 Panama distillate, double-aged tropically then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks in Charentes — Plantation's single-terroir vintage method applied to Central American column-still rum.
description (EN Store): Alexandre Gabriel's double-ageing doctrine — reviving the 17th–19th century practice of shipping Caribbean rum to European cellars for further maturation — finds a precise expression in this 2008 Panama vintage. The distillate was first matured tropically in Panama in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, where the equatorial climate drives an angel's share of 6–7% annually, concentrating the spirit far faster than any European cellar could. It then crossed the Atlantic to Château de Bonbonnet in Ars, Charentes, for a second maturation in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks — the same barrels that shape Maison Ferrand's own eau-de-vie, imparting dried stone fruit, baking spice, and a tannin architecture absent from any single-climate ageing regime. Panama's column-still distillation produces a cleaner, lighter-bodied distillate than the pot-still Jamaican marks in Plantation's portfolio, making the Cognac cask's structural contribution unusually legible here — dried apricot, toasted oak, and a waxy, almost Armagnac-like mid-palate emerge without the ester noise of a high-funk base. The Limited Edition vintage format means this is a single-year, single-origin release rather than a blended expression, preserving the character of one specific harvest and distillation campaign. The extended tropical ageing from 2008 onward, combined with the Cognac finish, places this well into extra-aged territory; drink now through the mid-2030s as the French oak integration continues to evolve in bottle.

name: Plantation Venezuela 2010 Vintage Limited Edition Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 2010 Aragua distillate, tropically aged in ex-bourbon then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks in Charentes — Venezuela's cane character through a Cognac-house lens.
description (EN Store): Alexandre Gabriel's double-ageing model was built on a single conviction: that Caribbean rum, like 17th-century trade casks arriving in European port cellars, is transformed by a second maturation in a cooler, drier climate. The Venezuela 2010 Vintage Limited Edition is that argument made tangible — a single-origin distillate from Aragua, Venezuela's rum-producing heartland, aged first in tropical conditions in ex-bourbon American oak, where the angel's share runs at 6–7% per year, concentrating the raw cane character at a rate impossible in Europe. The rum then crossed the Atlantic to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, France, for a second maturation in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks — the tighter grain and residual Cognac congeners pulling the spirit toward dried fruit, baking spice, and a tannin architecture no American oak alone could produce. What makes this expression categorically unusual is its origin: Venezuela contributes a distinctly lighter, drier molasses-based distillate profile compared to Plantation's Barbados or Jamaica marks, making the Cognac-cask influence more legible and the interplay between terroirs more transparent. As a vintage-dated, limited-edition single-origin release, it captures one harvest year's character without blending across origins. Drink now through the mid-2030s; the French oak integration will continue to knit.

name: Plantation Barbados 2013 Vintage Limited Edition Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-vintage Barbados 2013 from WIRD's coral-limestone seafront distillery, double-aged in ex-bourbon then ex-Ferrand Cognac casks.
description (EN Store):
Alexandre Gabriel's double-ageing method — reviving the 17th-century practice of Caribbean rums finishing in European port cellars — finds one of its most transparent expressions in this single-vintage 2013 Barbados. Distilled at Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery (WIRD), Blackrock, St. Michael, a coastal seafront site built on coral-limestone geology with direct Atlantic exposure: the warehouse sits metres from the ocean, and that maritime air permeates the ageing staves.

Stage one sees the molasses-based distillate mature tropically in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels at WIRD, where the tropical angel's share runs 6–7% annually — concentrating the spirit at roughly three times the rate of a Cognac cellar. Stage two transfers the rum to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, where ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks impart dried-fruit structure and baking-spice tannin unavailable from American oak alone. The house's Cognac-style dosage — toasted brown sugar blended with rum and aged one year in Ferrand Cognac barrels — is applied as a flavour enhancer, not a sweetener, and openly disclosed.

What separates this expression from Plantation's blended range is the single-vintage, single-island discipline: every drop traces to one distillation year at one coral-limestone seafront site, making the maritime WIRD character — saline, waxy, subtly oxidative — the unambiguous centre of the glass. Drink now through 2030.

name: Plantation Fiji Islands 2004 Vintage Limited Edition Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Rum Co. of Fiji 2004 distillate, tropically aged then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet — double-climate maturation from the South Pacific to Charentes.
description (EN Store): Alexandre Gabriel's double-ageing model was built on a single conviction: that Caribbean rums finished in European cellars — as they once did in 17th-century port warehouses — develop a structural complexity no single-climate maturation can replicate. The Fiji 2004 Vintage Limited Edition is the proof from the South Pacific. Distilled at the Rum Co. of Fiji from a sugarcane-derived base, the spirit first underwent tropical ageing in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels in Fiji's equatorial climate, where the angel's share runs at 6–7% annually — concentrating the distillate at a rate more than double what France's temperate cellars allow. The aged rum was then shipped to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, for a second maturation in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks, layering dried-fruit depth and baking-spice tannin structure over the tropical core. The brand library notes Fiji expressions receive between 8–15 years of tropical ageing before continental finishing — placing this 2004 vintage among the longer-rested single-terroir releases in the portfolio. What distinguishes this bottling within the Plantation range is its origin: Fiji is the sole Oceanic terroir in a portfolio otherwise anchored in the Caribbean and Latin America, producing a distillate character that sits apart from both the high-ester Jamaican marks and the coral-limestone-influenced Barbados rums. Drink now through the late 2020s; the Cognac-cask tannins are fully integrated and the tropical fruit concentration is at its expressive peak.

name: Plantation Extreme No.5 Barbados 2007 Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-cask 2007 WIRD Barbados, bottled at 58% ABV from Plantation's seafront warehouse — 15 years of coral-limestone maritime ageing, non-chill-filtered.
description (EN Store):
When Alexandre Gabriel's team selected this single cask from the seafront warehouse at Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery — a coral-limestone coastal site at Blackrock, St. Michael, Barbados, where Atlantic salt air permeates the ageing stacks — they were working with a distillate that had spent 15 years accumulating maritime character at tropical angel's-share rates of 6–7% per year. That attrition is not incidental: it concentrates the molasses-derived distillate far beyond what any continental cellar could achieve in equivalent time.

WIRD, founded in 1893 by George Stade, houses both pot and column stills; this Barbados expression is drawn from pot still distillation, fermented with house-cultivated yeast from the on-site nursery, and matured in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels through the tropical phase. The Extrême Collection No.5 release then applies Plantation's defining double-ageing protocol — a second maturation in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes — before bottling at a natural cask strength of 58% ABV, non-chill-filtered.

What separates this expression from the broader Extrême No.5 set is its ABV: at 58%, it is the highest-strength release in the trio, preserving the full texture of a distillate that has lost enormous volume to the Barbados heat but none of its structural intensity. The 2007 vintage delivers the maritime-warehouse signature of WIRD in its most undiluted form.

name: Flor de Caña  18 years Centenario Rum Box Set
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged in volcanic heat, zero additives — ex-bourbon oak and San Cristóbal's shadow do all the work.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa planted the roots of this operation in 1890 on the Pacific lowlands of Chichigalpa, roughly 8 km from San Cristóbal — Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m — a proximity that is not incidental but structural to every barrel in the warehouse. Continuous volcanic ash deposition fertilises the 35,000-acre Ingenio San Antonio sugarcane estate, while underground water reservoirs carry mineral enrichment from volcanic infiltration through the soil profile before reaching the distillery.

Molasses from the estate's own sugar mill is fermented with a proprietary in-house yeast strain for approximately 36 hours, then driven through a five-column continuous still to ~96% ABV — a deliberately neutral distillate that places the entire flavour burden on wood. The spirit is diluted to 77% ABV before filling into re-charred, ex-bourbon American white oak barrels, sealed with local plantain leaves, and left in non-climate-controlled on-site warehouses where the tropical Pacific lowland heat drives accelerated wood interaction with zero artificial intervention — the 'Slow-Aged™' method. Critically, no sugar, caramel, colouring, or additives are introduced at any stage, a direct transgression of standard Spanish-style rum practice where post-distillation sweetening is near-universal.

At the 18-year tier, that additive-free discipline produces a rum of notable dryness: toasted American oak, vanilla, and volcanic-mineral depth without the sticky texture that defines most of its category peers. Flor de Caña is the only spirit simultaneously certified Fair Trade USA and carbon-neutral, distilling entirely on renewable energy from sugarcane bagasse.

name: Flor de Caña  25 years Centenario Rum Box Set
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged 25 years in ex-bourbon oak under San Cristóbal volcano's tropical heat — zero additives, zero sugar.
description (EN Store):
Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa established the Ingenio San Antonio sugarcane estate in Chichigalpa in 1890, and five generations later the family still seals every barrel with a local plantain leaf — a detail that signals how little outside intervention this rum tolerates. The distillery sits roughly 8 km from San Cristóbal, Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m; continuous volcanic ash enriches the 35,000-acre estate's soil while mineral-laden underground water, filtered through volcanic rock, feeds the fermentation. That same volcanic microclimate — unrelenting Pacific lowland heat with no air-conditioning in the warehouses — drives accelerated wood extraction across 25 years of Slow-Aged™ maturation in re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak barrels, without any artificial temperature or humidity control.

The base spirit is fermented for approximately 36 hours using a proprietary in-house yeast strain, then distilled five times through a five-column continuous still to roughly 96% ABV — a deliberately neutral canvas that places the entire flavour burden on barrel and climate. No sugar, caramel, colouring, or additives of any kind are introduced at any stage, which is the transgression here: virtually every Spanish-style rum at this age tier is sweetened post-distillation; this one is not. At 25 years, the result is a bone-dry, deeply oxidative profile shaped purely by tropical oak interaction. Flor de Caña is also the only spirit simultaneously certified Fair Trade USA and carbon-neutral, running entirely on renewable energy from sugarcane bagasse.

name: Flor de Caña  30 years Centenario Rum (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-estate Nicaraguan rum aged in the volcanic shadow of San Cristóbal — zero additives, five-column distillation, 30 years of tropical barrel time.
description (EN Store): Francisco Alfredo Pellas Canessa planted the first canes at Ingenio San Antonio in 1890, and five generations later the Pellas family still seals every barrel with local plantain leaves before locking them into non-climate-controlled warehouses — a deliberate refusal of artificial temperature management that defines the entire Flor de Caña philosophy. The distillery sits roughly 8 km from San Cristóbal, Nicaragua's tallest active volcano at 1,745 m; continuous volcanic ash fall enriches the 35,000-acre estate's soil, while mineral-laden underground water — filtered through Pacific Ring of Fire geology — feeds fermentation. That same volcanic heat drives accelerated wood extraction without any artificial accelerant. Molasses from the estate's own mill is fermented with a proprietary in-house yeast strain for approximately 36 hours, then distilled through a five-column continuous still to ~96% ABV — a deliberately neutral spirit that places the entire flavour burden on oak. The distillate enters re-charred ex-bourbon American white oak barrels at 77% ABV, then ages under purely tropical ambient conditions for 30 years. What makes this expression categorically unusual: unlike virtually every Spanish-style rum at this age tier, it carries zero added sugar, caramel, or colouring — the amber depth and vanilla-toasted oak character are entirely the product of barrel and climate. The result is a notably dry, non-viscous profile rare at three decades of tropical aging.

name: IICHIKO  Standard 25% Vol. (900 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Honkaku mugi shochu from Usa City, Oita — 25+ proprietary single-distillate blends, volcanic spring water, sashimoto house yeast since the 1980s.
description (EN Store):
When Sanwa Shurui launched iichiko in 1979, barley shochu was a regional Kyushu curiosity; within a decade, iichiko had triggered Japan's first national shochu boom by engineering a spirit that was structurally unlike anything else in the category. Rather than a single-recipe distillate, every bottle of iichiko Silhouette is a proprietary blend of more than two dozen individual 100% barley shochu components, each differentiated by koji strain, yeast strain, and fermentation temperature — an architecture closer to blended Scotch than to any peer mugi shochu.

Production draws on two Oita Prefecture sites: the headquarters distillery outside Usa City and the Hita Distillery in southern Oita, both fed by spring water filtered through over 1,000 feet of volcanic rock, yielding exceptionally soft, iron-free water that preserves the cereal delicacy of the barley. The base grain is Nishi no Hoshi ('Western Star'), a two-row Oita cultivar selected for high starch content, inoculated with white barley koji (Aspergillus oryzae) and fermented with a proprietary house yeast maintained via the sashimoto method — starter carried batch-to-batch since the 1980s, building a living yeast lineage unique to the house.

As a honkaku shochu, single distillation is legally mandated — no re-distillation, no sweeteners, no additives. The Silhouette expression is unaged, bottled at 25% ABV, preserving the raw aromatic signature of the mash. What no peer does at this scale: blending 24+ in-house single-distillate recipes into one consistent, umami-forward table spirit without a single drop of outside spirit. Drink now; no cellaring required.

name: IICHIKO  Silhouette Barley Shochu (720 ml)

name: Kamotsuru  Tokusei Gold Daiginjo (1.8 L)
short_description (EN Store): The world's first commercially sold daiginjo (1958), brewed in Saijo with ultra-soft Kamo Mountain water via slow, low-temperature fermentation.
description (EN Store):
In 1958, Kamotsuru released Tokusei Gold Kamotsuru — the world's first commercially sold daiginjo — decades before the category had a name, let alone a market. That single decision, made at Kura No. 8 on Saijo's Sakagura-dori, effectively invented the modern ginjo movement.

The brewery draws ultra-soft subsoil water filtered through the Kamo Mountains of Higashihiroshima — mineral-poor to a degree that once made Hiroshima water a liability in sake brewing. Kamotsuru's answer was a technically demanding slow, low-temperature moromi fermentation that compensates for the water's near-absence of minerals, yielding a silkier, more aromatic profile than anything achievable with Nada's hard water.

Contract-grown sake rice arrives from Hiroshima's northern highlands — elevated terrain with significant diurnal swing and clay-loam soils — and is polished in-house at the brewery's dedicated rice-polishing kura. Koji is cultured deeply into the rice's inner structure to maximise saccharification before fermentation begins; rice is steamed in traditional wooden steamers. The house philosophy is Ama-Kara-Pin-Uma: mildly sweet, dry, refreshing, and tasteful in sequence.

At 1.8 L, this magnum format is itself unusual for a daiginjo tier — a category almost never bottled beyond 720 ml — making it a rare large-format expression of Japan's founding commercial daiginjo.

As a non-vintage blend, the Gold Daiginjo is built for consistency of house style; drink now or cellar short-term in cool, dark conditions.

name: Kamotsuru  Tokusei Gold Daiginjo (720 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The world's first commercially sold daiginjo (1958), brewed in Saijo with ultra-soft Kamo Mountain water via slow, low-temperature fermentation.
description (EN Store):
In 1958, Kamotsuru released Tokusei Gold — the world's first commercially sold daiginjo — decades before the category had a name, let alone a market. That act of category creation was inseparable from a technical gamble: proving that Saijo's ultra-soft subsoil water, filtered through the Kamo Mountains of Higashihiroshima, could sustain premium fermentation at all.

Soft water is mineral-poor, which starves yeast of the ionic support that hard-water breweries in Nada and Fushimi take for granted. Kamotsuru's answer was a slow, low-temperature moromi fermentation that compensates by extending yeast activity over a longer arc — the technical foundation of the modern ginjo movement. Koji is cultured deeply into the rice's inner structure to maximise saccharification before fermentation begins, and rice is steamed in traditional wooden steamers. Kura No. 8, a dedicated brewhouse with its own toji, handles this expression exclusively.

Rice is polished to 38% remaining or lower — a seimaibuai that discards more than three-fifths of each grain — and all Hiroshima-grown rice is polished in-house on the brewery's own machines. The result is the house profile Kamotsuru calls Ama-Kara-Pin-Uma: mildly sweet, dry, refreshing, and deeply tasteful — a silkier, more aromatic signature than any hard-water daiginjo can replicate.

This is the expression that invented its own category; no peer can claim the same lineage.

name: Fukuju  Awasaki Sparkling Sake (300 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sparkling sake from Nada's Miyamizu-fed Fukuju-gura — 270-year Yasufuku family craft, softened with RO-blended water for a wine-like effervescence.
description (EN Store): The Yasufuku family has brewed at Mikage-go in Higashinada Ward — the heart of Nada-Gogo, Japan's most storied sake district — since 1751, now in its 13th generation. Awasaki is the sparkling expression of that lineage, built on Yamadanishiki rice grown in the Rokko Mountain foothills of Ozo-cho, Kita Ward, at 50–200 m elevation, where diurnal swings exceeding 10°C concentrate starch and slow ripening. The brewery's water is Miyamizu, a granite-filtered spring low in iron and high in phosphorus and potassium — historically the engine of Nada's dry, mineral-driven style. For its higher-grade lines, Fukuju deliberately blends Miyamizu with reverse-osmosis-treated water, moderating mineral hardness to slow fermentation and coax a fruitier, softer profile — a deliberate departure from Nada orthodoxy. Koji is cultivated entirely by hand (tezukuri), which the brewery regards as the non-negotiable foundation of quality. The sparkling format translates that fruit-forward house character into a fine-bubbled, low-alcohol format that no conventional Nada brewery has historically prioritised. As a 300 ml NV expression, Awasaki is best enjoyed young and well-chilled, with the effervescence amplifying the rice-derived delicacy and Miyamizu mineral lift.

name: Fukuju  Junmai ginjo (1.8 L)

description (EN Store):
Since 2008, this sake from the Yasufuku family's 270-year-old Mikage-go brewery has been poured at the Nobel Prize banquet in Stockholm — a distinction earned not by prestige alone but by a deliberate departure from Nada orthodoxy. Where most Nada houses lean into the hard, iron-low, mineral-driven character of undiluted Miyamizu, Kobe Shushinkan blends that same Rokko Mountain spring water with reverse-osmosis-treated water for its ginjo lines, slowing fermentation and softening the mineral edge to produce a fruitier, more wine-like texture while preserving the region's characteristic backbone.

The Yamadanishiki rice is contract-farmed in Ozo-cho, Kita Ward, Kobe — foothills and valleys of the Rokko Mountains at 50–200 m elevation, where diurnal swings exceeding 10°C during ripening concentrate starch and build the slow-dissolving koji substrate the brewery demands. Koji is cultivated entirely by hand (tezukuri), which the brewery treats as the non-negotiable foundation of quality. Seimaibuai: 60% (40% of the grain polished away); fermentation conducted at low temperature to develop ginjo aromatic compounds; bottled at 16% ABV. The 1.8 L magnum format preserves thermal stability across Thailand's distribution chain. The Organic JAS certification and world-first carbon-zero production process (Scope 1 & 2, launched 2022) mark this as the most sustainability-credentialed brewery in Nada-Gogo.

name: Fukuju  Junmai ginjo (720 ml)
