name: Don Julio  Reposado (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Los Altos Highland reposado: iron-rich volcanic clay, 8+ months ex-bourbon oak, from the man who invented luxury tequila.
description (EN Store): In 1942, Don Julio González-Frausto Estrada began aging tequila in oak barrels at a time when virtually no other Mexican producer was doing so — a deliberate transgression that effectively created the sipping-tequila category. That founding instinct defines the Reposado today. Distillery La Primavera sits in Atotonilco El Alto, Los Altos (Highlands), Jalisco, at roughly 5,200–6,500 ft elevation on iron-rich volcanic red clay soils. The combination of warm days, cool nights, and mineral-dense earth pushes Blue Weber agave toward a distinctly fruity, floral character that lowland valley tequilas structurally cannot replicate. Agave plants are spaced further apart than industry norm and left to mature 7–15 years before hand-harvest by multi-generational jimador families — yielding denser, sweeter piñas. Piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry hornos for 48–72 hours, milled, then fermented with a proprietary yeast strain developed by the founder himself, before double distillation in stainless steel pot stills with copper presence. The Reposado then rests a minimum 8 months in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels — exceeding CRT minimums — long enough to layer vanilla and dried citrus peel over the cooked-agave core without erasing it. What no peer does at this price point: the house philosophy explicitly calibrates barrel influence to complement agave character rather than dominate it, making this one of the few reposados where the highland terroir remains legible through the oak. Drink now; no further aging required.

name: 1800 Tequila  Blanco (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Crisp blanco tequila with bright citrus and herbaceous agave character; versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): 1800 Tequila Blanco is a straightforward, unaged expression showcasing the core agave profile with clean botanical character. The nose presents bright citrus and herbaceous notes typical of blanco tequila, while the palate delivers peppery spice and subtle mineral undertones. The finish is crisp and dry, making it equally suited to neat sipping, on the rocks, or as a foundation spirit for classic cocktails.

name: Don Julio  Blanco (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Los Altos Blanco from iron-rich volcanic clay at 5,200–6,500 ft — unaged, 30-day steel rest, double-distilled through copper-fitted pot stills.
description (EN Store): When Don Julio González planted his agave fields in Atotonilco El Alto in 1942, he deliberately spaced plants further apart than any neighbor — a deliberate sacrifice of yield to allow each Blue Weber agave to reach full individual maturity over 7–15 years, producing piñas denser in fermentable sugars. That founding decision is still the technical backbone of every bottle. Distillery La Primavera sits in the Los Altos Highlands of Jalisco at roughly 5,200–6,500 ft elevation, on iron-rich volcanic red clay that, combined with warm days and cool nights, drives a distinctly fruity, floral agave character absent from lowland valley tequilas. Hand-harvested piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry hornos for 48–72 hours, converting complex agave starches before milling and fermentation with a proprietary yeast strain developed by González himself. Double distillation runs through stainless steel pot stills with copper presence — first pass removes impurities, second refines. The Blanco is then rested approximately 30 days in stainless steel before bottling at 40% ABV, preserving raw highland agave character without any oak interference. What sets this apart: the Blanco functions as the clearest expression of Los Altos terroir in the portfolio — no barrel influence to mediate the iron-clay minerality and cooked-agave sweetness the elevation produces. Drink now; no cellaring required.

name: Ocho  Plata (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Ocho Plata: crisp, herbaceous silver tequila with bright citrus and mineral backbone. Versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Ocho Plata is a silver tequila presenting a clean, straightforward spirit profile. The nose leads with fresh agave and herbaceous notes, followed by bright citrus aromatics. On the palate, the spirit delivers a smooth, medium-bodied entry with peppery spice and mineral undertones that build toward a crisp, dry finish. The absence of barrel aging preserves the raw agave character and botanical clarity, making this expression suitable for both neat sipping and cocktail applications.

name: Patron  XO Cafe Coffee Liqueur (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Patrón XO Cafe blends Los Altos highland tequila with arabica coffee — a liqueur built on the same tahona-and-copper-pot foundation as the flagship Silver.
description (EN Store):
When John Paul DeJoria and Martin Crowley launched Patrón in 1989, their operational philosophy was to refuse automation as volume scaled — cloning identical small copper pot stills and brick ovens rather than switching to autoclaves or column stills. Patrón XO Cafe is built on that same foundation: the base spirit is Hacienda Patrón's double-distilled, 100% Blue Weber Agave tequila, produced at NOM 1492 in Atotonilco El Alto, where highland elevation and a contaminant-free water source yield agave that is inherently sweeter and fruitier than lowland material.

The production signature carries through: agave piñas are slow-baked for exactly 79 hours in small clay brick ovens, crushed via both volcanic tahona wheel and roller mill, and fermented separately in open pinewood tanks before double distillation in small-batch copper pot stills — the same six-then-five still sequence used for the Silver. The tequila distillate is then married with arabica coffee to produce the liqueur, preserving the tahona fraction's earthy, agave-rich texture beneath the coffee layer.

What separates XO Cafe from category peers is that the tequila base is not a neutral spirit or a column-still commodity — it carries genuine agave character from a craft production chain that passes through approximately 60 pairs of hands per bottle. The result is a coffee liqueur where the agave substrate is identifiable, not erased.

name: Patron  Anejo Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland Jalisco añejo aged ~12 months across American, French & other oak — dual tahona-stone/roller-mill crush preserved in every bottle by hand.
description (EN Store):
When John Paul DeJoria and Martin Crowley launched Patrón in 1989, 100% agave tequila at luxury price points did not exist as a commercial category — the spirit was consumed as a shot with salt and lime. Their founding Master Distiller Francisco Alcaraz chose Atotonilco El Alto in the Los Altos highlands of Jalisco specifically for its contaminant-free water supply and the sweeter, fruitier agave character that highland elevation produces compared to lowland fruit.

At Hacienda Patrón (NOM 1492), piñas are hand-chopped and slow-baked in small clay brick ovens for exactly 79 hours using alternating steam-and-rest cycles, then crushed by two parallel methods: a two-ton volcanic tahona wheel and a modern roller mill. The two juices ferment separately in open pinewood tanks for a minimum of 72 hours before double distillation in small-batch copper pot stills — six tall narrow stills for the first pass, five shorter rounder stills for the second — a geometry Alcaraz designed to strip sulfur and preserve aromatic complexity.

The Añejo rests approximately 12 months in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and French oak barrels inside an 11,000-barrel aging room on site. What no peer at this production scale replicates: rather than automating as demand grew, Patrón cloned identical copper pot stills and brick ovens — the same small-batch geometry now runs at multi-million-case volume. Each bottle passes through approximately 60 hands, hand-numbered, hand-corked, and hand-sealed at 40% ABV.

name: Montezuma  Blue Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Crisp blue agave tequila with bright citrus and herbaceous notes; versatile for cocktails and food pairing.
description (EN Store): Montezuma Blue Tequila is a straightforward, well-balanced expression of Mexican blue agave spirit. The nose opens with clean citrus and herbaceous character, supported by subtle floral and spice undertones. On the palate, medium body and brisk acidity deliver brightness and approachability, making it equally at home in a margarita or sipped neat. The finish is clean and herbaceous, with lingering lime and coriander notes.

name: Jose Cuervo  Tradicional Reposado (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth reposado tequila with vanilla oak and caramel notes; versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Jose Cuervo Tradicional Reposado is a barrel-aged tequila offering approachable smoothness and balanced oak influence. The spirit displays characteristic agave foundation with vanilla and caramel complexity from reposado maturation, finishing with gentle pepper spice. Medium body and clean palate make it suitable for both neat service and cocktail applications.

name: Espolon  Reposado (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth Mexican reposado with cooked agave, citrus, and subtle oak—versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Espolon Reposado is a 100% blue agave tequila aged in oak to deliver a balanced, approachable spirit. The nose opens with cooked agave and bright citrus notes, followed by subtle herbaceous undertones. On the palate, vanilla and caramel emerge from the reposado aging, creating a smooth, warming finish with light oak influence. The spirit maintains clean agave character while gaining roundness and complexity from its time in barrel, making it equally suited to neat sipping or classic cocktails.

name: Espolon  Blanco (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Crisp blanco tequila with bright citrus and agave character; versatile for cocktails and casual dining.
description (EN Store): Espolon Blanco is a straightforward, unaged tequila showcasing the clean botanical and grain-forward character typical of the category. The primary profile emphasizes fresh agave sweetness with citrus brightness and subtle herbal notes. On the palate, a peppery finish and mineral undertone provide structure and balance. Light-bodied with medium-high acidity, this spirit excels in mixed drinks and pairs naturally with Mexican cuisine, tapas, and casual appetizers.

name: Código 1530  Tequila Blanco (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lowlands Blanco from Amatitán's volcanic soils, fermented with heirloom bakery yeast over 4–12 days — additive-free, mineral-driven, no oak intervention.
description (EN Store):
Código 1530 was built on a multi-generational private recipe from Amatitán — the same town that gives the Los Bajos valley its identity — and launched in 2016 by Ron Snyder, Federico Vaughan, and George Strait as a deliberate rejection of additive-dependent ultra-premium tequila. The Blanco is the purest expression of that founding argument.

Amatitán sits in the fertile Lowlands (Los Bajos) of Jalisco, where volcanic soil and rain-fed farmland produce Blue Weber agave of exceptional sugar density — piñas are accepted only at ≥32 Brix, a threshold that concentrates the earthy, herbaceous character specific to this valley. Process water is drawn from freshwater pools filtered through volcanic rock, reinforcing the mineral spine of the distillate.

Production at Varo Destilería (NOM 1616) begins with stainless steel oven cooking, followed by a proprietary shredder/chopper that isolates juice from pulp without tahona or roller mill. Fermentation runs 4–12 days using heirloom bread yeast sourced from a multi-generational Amatitán bakery — an extended timeline that builds mineral complexity and earthy depth where most Lowlands producers push fermentation in hours. Two distillations in custom handmade stainless steel pot stills complete the process.

What sets this Blanco apart: zero additives — no glycerin, no sweeteners, no colorants — in a category where additive use is widespread and largely undisclosed. The result is a Lowlands Blanco that reads as site-specific rather than producer-polished: volcanic minerality, cooked agave earthiness, and bakery-yeast savory lift with nothing layered over them.

name: Clase Azul  Tequila Anejo (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Los Altos red volcanic highland agave, 72-hr brick-oven cook, oak-aged — Clase Azul Añejo built on deliberate sweetness over agave austerity.
description (EN Store):
When Arturo Lomelí launched Clase Azul in 1997 at $100 a bottle — into a market where the average tequila sold for $18 — he was not just pricing differently; he was declaring that tequila could occupy the same cultural space as aged Cognac or single malt Scotch, a conviction that predated the luxury agave boom by over a decade.

The agave source is Los Altos de Jalisco, the Jalisco Highlands near Jesús María and Arandas, where red volcanic, mineral-rich soils at high altitude and a cool diurnal climate push Tequilana Weber Blue Agave to grow larger and accumulate more natural sugars than their Lowland counterparts — the physiological foundation of Clase Azul's characteristically full, sweet profile.

Production begins with 100% organic Blue Weber agave harvested at 6–9+ years maturity, whose piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry brick ovens for a minimum of 72 hours before milling and fermentation with a proprietary yeast strain. The Añejo then undergoes extended oak aging — barrel specifics undisclosed by the brand — before triple filtration at 40% ABV. That triple filtration is the deliberate transgression: where peers such as Fortaleza or G4 preserve raw agave character, Clase Azul systematically strips it in favour of a low-burn, dessert-weight smoothness that critics contest and collectors covet.

The hand-painted ceramic decanter, produced by Mazahua artisans over roughly two weeks each, is itself a collectible art object — the vessel inseparable from the liquid's positioning at this price tier.

name: Clase Azul  Tequila Plata (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Unaged highland agave from Los Altos' red volcanic soils, triple-filtered and bottled at 40% — Clase Azul's deliberate break from artisanal convention.
description (EN Store):
When Arturo Lomelí launched Clase Azul in 1997 at $100 a bottle — a moment when the category average sat near $18 — the Plata expression was a provocation: unaged tequila priced as a luxury object, its vessel hand-painted by Mazahua artisans over roughly two weeks, inseparable from the liquid inside. That positioning has since defined an entire tier of the category.

The agave source is Los Altos de Jalisco, the Jalisco Highlands around Jesús María near Arandas — red volcanic, mineral-rich soils at high altitude with a cool climate that pushes Tequilana Weber Blue Agave to larger, sugar-denser piñas than Lowland equivalents, yielding a rounder, fruit-forward raw material before distillation even begins.

Production centres on 100% organic agave harvested at 6–9+ years maturity, slow-cooked in traditional masonry ovens for a minimum of 72 hours, then fermented with a proprietary yeast strain and distilled twice in stainless-steel pot stills fitted with copper coils. The Plata is then triple-filtered and bottled at 40% ABV with no oak aging.

The special feature — and the ongoing industry debate — is precisely that triple filtration: a deliberate stylistic choice that produces a sweeter, lower-burn profile atypical of artisanal highland tequila, prioritising texture over raw agave expressiveness. No vintage notes apply; drink now or hold as a collectible.

name: Don Julio  1942 (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Triple-distilled at La Primavera, Atotonilco El Alto — 30+ months in recharred ex-bourbon oak from Los Altos' iron-rich volcanic clay highlands.
description (EN Store): When Don Julio González first aged tequila in barrels at La Primavera distillery in 1942, virtually no other Mexican producer was doing so — a deliberate transgression that created the luxury sipping tequila category from scratch. The 1942 expression, named for that founding year, is the house's most technically intensive añejo. Agave plants grown in Los Altos' iron-rich volcanic red clay at roughly 5,200–6,500 ft elevation — where warm days and cool nights slow sugar accumulation over 7–15 years — yield piñas with a distinctly fruity, floral character absent from lowland valley tequilas. Hand-harvested piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry hornos for up to 72 hours, then double-distilled in stainless steel pot stills with copper presence before a third distillation pass in a small-batch still of maximum 3-barrel capacity — a step applied to no other expression in the Don Julio portfolio. The spirit then rests for a minimum of 30 months in recharred American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, nearly double the legal añejo minimum, drawing vanilla, caramel, and dried-fruit depth while preserving the highland agave's cooked-sweetness signature. That third distillation is the category transgression: no other standard Don Julio expression receives it, and few añejos at any price point undergo triple distillation. The NV blend is consistent across releases; drink now through the near term.

name: 1800 Tequila  Cristalino Anejo (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cristalino Anejo tequila: smooth, barrel-aged with vanilla & caramel, crystal-clear finish.
description (EN Store): 1800 Tequila Cristalino Anejo is a premium aged tequila distinguished by its crystal-clear appearance and refined palate. Extended barrel aging imparts vanilla, caramel, and subtle oak character while maintaining the smooth, approachable profile expected of the category. The finish carries warm spice and citrus notes, making it equally suited to sipping neat or as the foundation of sophisticated cocktails.

name: Butterfly Cannon  Silver Cristalino (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Butterfly Cannon Silver Cristalino: smooth, citrus-forward tequila with clean agave character and refined finish.
description (EN Store): Butterfly Cannon Silver Cristalino is a Mexican tequila crafted for approachability and versatility. The spirit opens with bright citrus and fresh agave notes, followed by subtle vanilla and herbaceous undertones that soften the palate. The finish is clean and smooth, making it equally suited to sipping neat or as a foundation for classic cocktails. Ideal for those seeking a balanced, everyday tequila without aggressive heat or complexity.

name: Patron  El Cielo Silver Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Hacienda Patrón's dual-crush Silver: tahona volcanic stone meets roller mill, double-distilled in small copper pots at 1,900m+ Los Altos elevation.
description (EN Store):
When John Paul DeJoria and Martin Crowley launched Patrón in 1989, 100% agave tequila at luxury price points did not exist as a commercial category — the spirit was a shot with salt and lime. Their founding Master Distiller Francisco Alcaraz selected Atotonilco El Alto specifically for its contaminant-free water supply and the highland microclimate's effect on Blue Weber Agave: at Los Altos elevation, the piñas develop measurably higher sugar content and a fruitier aromatic profile than lowland agave.

El Cielo Silver is built on Hacienda Patrón's signature dual-crush architecture: piñas slow-baked for exactly 79 hours in small clay brick ovens, then crushed by both a two-ton volcanic tahona wheel and a modern roller mill. The two resulting juices ferment separately in open pinewood tanks for a minimum of 72 hours using proprietary yeast, then each fraction is double-distilled in small copper pot stills — six tall narrow stills for the first pass, five shorter rounder stills for the second — before blending. No column stills, no additives, no added sugars.

What distinguishes El Cielo within the Patrón Silver family is a four-column activated-charcoal filtration step applied post-distillation, a process no other expression in the range undergoes, which strips residual color and sharpens the agave's citrus and floral register without sacrificing the earthy textural contribution of the tahona fraction. Unaged and bottled at 40% ABV, it drinks with the structural clarity of a blanco and the layered depth of a craft-distilled spirit.

name: Código 1530  Mezcal Artesanal (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lowlands Oaxacan mezcal artesanal — but the brand library confirms this is a Jalisco Lowlands tequila, not a mezcal from Oaxaca.
description (EN Store):
Editorial note: The SKU is classified as "Código 1530 Mezcal Artesanal" from Oaxaca, but every verified fact in the brand library places Código 1530 exclusively in Amatitán, Los Bajos (Lowlands), Jalisco under NOM 1616 — a 100% Blue Weber Agave Tequila DO, not a mezcal, and not from Oaxaca. No mezcal artesanal expression appears anywhere in the validated brand library, which lists only Blanco, Rosa, Reposado, Añejo, Barrel Strength Añejo, and Origen Extra Añejo tequila expressions. Writing a curation-grade description for a "Código 1530 Mezcal Artesanal from Oaxaca" would require inventing facts not present in the brand library — a direct violation of the factual discipline mandate.

Please verify the SKU classification and region against the producer's current portfolio before publishing. If this product is intended to be one of the confirmed Código 1530 tequila expressions, resubmit with the correct expression name and this description will be completed in full.

name: Código 1530  Rosa Reposado (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blanco rested 1 month in uncharred French White Oak ex-Napa Cabernet barrels — the expression that created the rosé tequila category.
description (EN Store):
When Código 1530 launched its Rosa in 2016, no other Jalisco producer had deliberately rested a Blanco in uncharred French White Oak ex-Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon barrels to produce a naturally pink tequila — no colorants, no sweeteners, no glycerin. The pink hue is purely the barrel's residual Cabernet pigment migrating into the spirit over one month of contact. That single decision defined a new sub-category.

The agave originates from the Los Bajos (Lowlands) volcanic soils of Amatitán, Jalisco — rain-fed, mineral-rich terrain sitting at lower elevation than the Highlands, producing piñas harvested only at ≥32 Brix. Process water is filtered through volcanic rock, reinforcing the earthy, mineral character the region is known for. Fermentation is driven by heirloom bread yeast from a multi-generational Amatitán bakery, running 4–12 days — far longer than industrial tequila norms — building textural depth before double distillation in custom handmade stainless steel pot stills.

Where virtually every aged tequila in Jalisco reaches for ex-bourbon American oak, Código 1530 uses French White Oak ex-wine barrels across its entire rested lineup, trading coconut-vanilla for eugenol-driven clove spice and red-fruit lift. The Rosa sits at the lightest end of that philosophy — just enough barrel time to tint and perfume without obscuring the volcanic-soil agave character beneath.

name: Gran Centenario  Plata Tequila (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland tequila with bright citrus, clean agave, and peppery finish—versatile for cocktails and fresh cuisine.
description (EN Store): Gran Centenario Plata is an unaged highland tequila offering clean botanical character and bright citrus aromatics. The palate presents smooth agave sweetness with herbal undertones and a peppery spice finish, delivering the crisp, mineral-driven profile typical of silver tequila. Ideal for margaritas, palomas, and fresh seafood pairings.

name: Gran Centenario  Anejo Tequila (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Aged tequila with vanilla, oak, and caramel notes; smooth, versatile spirit for sipping or cocktails.
description (EN Store): Gran Centenario Anejo is a mature tequila aged in oak, delivering a balanced profile of vanilla, caramel, and subtle citrus. The spirit exhibits a medium-full body with smooth integration of wood influence and underlying agave character. Suitable for neat sipping, on the rocks, or as a foundation for premium cocktails.

name: Don Julio  Ultima Reserva Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Los Altos highland añejo finished in recharred ex-bourbon oak — Don Julio's ultra-premium expression, triple-distilled in small-batch stills of max 3-barrel capacity.
description (EN Store):
When Don Julio González began barrel-aging tequila at La Primavera distillery in Atotonilco El Alto, virtually no other Mexican producer was doing so — a deliberate transgression that effectively invented the luxury sipping tequila category. Ultima Reserva sits at the apex of that legacy, drawing on agave cultivated in the iron-rich volcanic red clay of Los Altos (Highlands), Jalisco, at elevations between 5,200 and 6,500 feet. The dramatic diurnal swing — warm days, cool nights — slows agave maturation to 7–15 years per plant, concentrating the fruity, floral character that distinguishes highland piñas from their lowland counterparts.

Production follows the house's most demanding protocol: hand-harvested piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry hornos for up to 72 hours before milling and fermentation with a proprietary yeast strain developed by the founder himself. The spirit then undergoes triple distillation — a third rectification pass conducted in a stainless steel pot still of maximum three-barrel capacity — a step applied to no other expression in the Don Julio portfolio. Aging proceeds in recharred American white oak ex-bourbon barrels for a minimum of 30 months, well beyond the CRT's añejo threshold, building layered caramel and dried-fruit depth without erasing the agave's highland brightness.

The triple distillation at micro-batch scale is the defining technical departure here: it produces a spirit of exceptional textural clarity that no standard añejo process replicates. Drink now through the near term; the oak integration is already at its intended peak.

name: Don Julio  1942 (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Triple-distilled at La Primavera, Atotonilco El Alto — 30+ months in recharred ex-bourbon oak from iron-rich volcanic highland agave.
description (EN Store): When Don Julio González began barrel-aging tequila in the highlands of Jalisco, virtually no other Mexican producer was doing so — a deliberate transgression that effectively invented the luxury sipping tequila category. The 1942 expression, named for the year he founded Distillery La Primavera in Atotonilco El Alto, is the house's most technically intensive release. Blue Weber agave grown in iron-rich volcanic red clay at roughly 5,200–6,500 ft elevation matures over 7–15 years before hand-harvest by multi-generational jimador families; the highland microclimate of warm days and cool nights concentrates the piña's natural sugars and amplifies the fruity, floral agave character that separates Los Altos tequila from lowland valley production. Harvested piñas are slow-cooked in traditional masonry hornos, then triple-distilled — the third pass conducted in a small-batch stainless steel pot still capped at three-barrel capacity — before a minimum 30 months of aging in recharred American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, well beyond the CRT's 12-month añejo threshold. The triple distillation is the category-breaking signature: no standard añejo in the Don Julio lineup receives this treatment, and few peers at any price point apply a third pass to tequila. Bottled at 40% ABV, the 1942 is non-vintage by nature; the 1.75 L format delivers the same small-batch liquid in a format suited to extended table service.

name: Clase Azul  Tequila Mezcal Guerrero (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Guerrero mezcal from Clase Azul — a rare departure from the brand's Jalisco heartland, distilled from agave native to Mexico's Pacific sierra.
description (EN Store): When Arturo Lomelí founded Clase Azul in 1997 — pricing a bottle at $100 when the category averaged $18 — he was betting that Mexican agave spirits deserved a luxury tier. The Guerrero expression extends that wager beyond tequila's DO entirely: this is mezcal, not tequila, sourced from the mountainous Pacific-coast state of Guerrero rather than Clase Azul's home terroir of Los Altos de Jalisco. The brand library confirms Clase Azul's tequila identity is anchored to red volcanic, mineral-rich highland soils at altitude in Jesús María, Arandas — conditions that yield oversized, sugar-dense piñas. Guerrero's sierra terrain is categorically different: rugged, biodiverse, and home to agave varieties that cannot legally be called Tequilana Weber Blue Agave, placing this expression outside the Tequila DO framework altogether. As a mezcal, production follows traditional small-batch mezcal protocols rather than Clase Azul's confirmed tequila process (72-hour brick-oven roasting, stainless pot stills with copper coils, triple filtration, 40% ABV). The hand-painted ceramic decanter — each requiring roughly two weeks of work by Mazahua artisans in Santa María Canchesda — remains the unifying signature across the portfolio. The special feature here is categorical transgression: Clase Azul, defined by its Jalisco Highlands tequila identity, steps into mezcal territory with a Guerrero-origin spirit that operates under entirely different agave, terroir, and regulatory rules than anything else in the core range.

name: Clase Azul  Tequila Mezcal Ultra (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Los Altos red volcanic soil, multi-year oak aging, triple-filtered Extra Añejo in a hand-painted Mazahua ceramic decanter — Clase Azul Ultra at its most extreme.
description (EN Store): When Arturo Lomelí launched Clase Azul in 1997 at $100 a bottle — into a market where the category average was $18 — he was not selling tequila so much as redefining what the vessel and the liquid inside it could mean together. The Ultra is the apex of that argument. Organic Tequilana Weber Blue Agave, grown in the red volcanic, mineral-rich soils of Los Altos de Jalisco at high altitude near Jesús María, Arandas, produces piñas measurably larger and sweeter than Lowland fruit — a direct consequence of the cool highland climate and extended maturation of 6–9+ years in the field. Those piñas are slow-cooked for a minimum of 72 hours in traditional masonry ovens, fermented with a patented proprietary yeast strain, and distilled twice before entering multi-year oak aging — the specific barrel regime for the Ultra is undisclosed, which is itself a statement of exclusivity. Triple filtration then strips residual heat to near zero, producing a profile that deliberately breaks from the drier, more agave-forward orthodoxy of artisanal peers. What no peer does: the hand-painted ceramic decanter is produced by Mazahua artisans in Santa María Canchesda over roughly two weeks per piece, making the container a certified collectible independent of the liquid. Bottled at 40% ABV, non-chill-filtration status unconfirmed.

name: Fuenteseca 18 Year Tequila Extra Anejo (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 1995 harvest, 18 years across two elevations — re-barreled in 2001 into 47% Canadian white oak / 53% European dark oak, then finished subterranean at El Chapingo, 5,400 ft.
description (EN Store): Enrique Fonseca distilled this lot in September 1995, then made a deliberate mid-life decision in 2001 to re-blend and re-barrel the spirit — a move borrowed from his apprenticeships with Scottish and Cognac producers — transferring it into 47% Canadian white oak ex-rye whiskey casks and 53% European dark oak ex-California red wine barrels before relocating the entire parcel to the cool, subterranean barrel room at El Chapingo, Jalisco (~5,400 ft) for a final 12-year phase. The highland agave originates from hillside plots in Atotonilco El Alto, Los Altos, at 4,600–5,500 ft, where volcanic-influenced soils and diurnal temperature swings push piña sugars to 24–26 °Brix at harvest — a concentration that survives 18 years of maturation as a structural backbone. The distillate is a deliberate blend: 75% copper double-column still / 25% copper alembic pot still, calibrated to marry length and agave intensity. Fermentation runs in open-air stainless steel tanks with a proprietary yeast strain; no diffuser, no additives (Tequila Matchmaker verified). What no peer in the category does: Fonseca physically relocates barrels between two facilities at different elevations mid-maturation to modulate extraction rate — a practice without parallel in tequila. The 18-year drinks with the structural density of a long-aged Armagnac but retains unmistakable cooked-agave identity; drink now through the next decade.

name: Fuenteseca 21 Year Tequila Extra Anejo (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 21 years in Canadian rye & French oak across two Jalisco elevations — the oldest age-stated tequila commercially available, CRT-certified.
description (EN Store): Enrique Fonseca's family has cultivated Blue Weber agave in Jalisco since the 1880s, but it was his apprenticeships with Scottish distillers and French Cognac producers that shaped the most transgressive decision in this bottle: agave piñas harvested in 1993, barreled February 1994, then deliberately relocated mid-maturation — 10 years on the dry earthen floor of the Atotonilco warehouse at 4,600 ft, followed by 11 years in the cool subterranean barrel room at El Chapingo (~5,400 ft) — a two-phase elevation strategy without parallel in the tequila industry. The Highland agave, grown on hillside plots in Atotonilco El Alto at 4,600–5,500 ft where plant sugars average 24–26 °Brix at harvest, delivers a concentrated raw material that can sustain two decades of extraction without collapsing. Distillate is a blend of copper alembic pot-still and copper column-still outputs, proportioned per lot to balance agave intensity against structural length — a Scotch-blending methodology applied to tequila. Cask selection for the 21-year centers on Canadian rye whiskey white oak, chosen for its lighter extraction profile, allowing the agave character to survive the full maturation arc. The Tequileña distillery (NOM 1146) was the first to use French oak for tequila aging and the first to release an extra añejo after the category was codified in 2006; the 21-year carries a CRT-certified age statement, independently verified — not a marketing claim. At bottling, no additives, no fining.

name: Los Cabos Silver Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Crisp Highland silver tequila with bright citrus and herbaceous notes; versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Los Cabos Silver Tequila presents a clean, approachable profile characteristic of unaged Highland tequila. Bright citrus and herbaceous botanicals dominate the nose, with subtle floral and spice undertones. The palate is medium-bodied with balanced acidity, delivering citrus zest, light herbal complexity, and a mineral-driven finish. Ideal for traditional margaritas, fresh cocktails, or neat sipping.

name: Los Cabos Gold (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth Mexican tequila with vanilla oak and citrus notes; versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Los Cabos Gold is a commercial-grade tequila from Jalisco, Mexico, offering approachable sweetness and balanced oak influence. The spirit presents clean agave character with supporting vanilla and caramel notes, finishing with subtle herbaceous warmth. Suitable for casual entertaining, cocktail preparation, or neat sipping.

name: Patron El Alto Tequila (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tahona-and-roller dual-crush from Atotonilco El Alto highlands; extra añejo aged 3+ years in American, French, and Hungarian oak at NOM 1492.
description (EN Store):
When founding Master Distiller Francisco Alcaraz selected Atotonilco El Alto in Jalisco's Los Altos highlands as the site for Hacienda Patrón, the decision was driven by two non-negotiable criteria: a contaminant-free water supply and a highland microclimate that coaxes Blue Weber Agave toward sweeter, fruitier sugar profiles than lowland cultivation ever yields. That agronomic advantage is the foundation on which El Alto is built.

The piñas are hand-chopped and slow-baked in small clay brick ovens for exactly 79 hours using alternating steam and rest cycles, then crushed by two parallel methods: a two-ton volcanic tahona wheel and a modern roller mill — the distillates fermented separately in open pinewood tanks for a minimum of 72 hours before blending. Both streams are double-distilled in small-batch copper pot stills designed by Alcaraz, which strip sulfur and preserve aromatic complexity impossible in column-still production.

El Alto is aged more than three years in a combination of new and used American, French, and Hungarian oak casks — the Hungarian cooperage being the differentiator absent from every other expression in the Patrón range. Each bottle is hand-numbered, hand-corked, and hand-sealed, passing through approximately 60 hands before release. The highland terroir's inherent fruit sweetness, amplified by extended oak contact across three cooperage traditions, produces a textural extra añejo that retains the tahona's earthy agave signature rather than surrendering it to wood.

name: Stolichnaya  Premium Vodka (700 ml) (New Label)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, five-column rectification, birch charcoal filtered 4×, 1.8 g/L sugar — Stoli's narrow-cut philosophy retains grain character most vodkas discard.
description (EN Store): The recipe that became Stolichnaya emerged from Soviet state laboratories in the late 1930s, with a deliberate mandate to preserve cereal identity rather than chase absolute neutrality — a philosophy encoded in a narrow distillation cut that discards all spirit evaporating below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C. Grain — winter wheat and spring wheat with a minority of rye — is sourced from the Black Earth (Chernozem) region of Tambov, Russia's most fertile agricultural belt, where deep humus-rich soils and a continental climate produce starch-dense grain that carries through to the finished spirit. At Talvis Distillery, Tambov, a continuous five-column rectification system — including a vacuum mash column operating at 60°C with a copper cartridge for sulphur removal — rectifies spirit to 96.3% ABV before tanker transport to Latvijas Balzams, Riga, where artesian well water drawn from 200-metre-deep wells is reverse-osmosis treated before blending. Filtration runs four sequential passes: quartz sand, Russian birch wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. A post-filtration addition of 1.8 g/L sugar rounds the palate in the classical Russian tradition without perceptible sweetness. What distinguishes Premium from the broader category is this dual-nation, farm-to-bottle chain — grain grown on SPI-owned Chernozem farmland, distilled in Russia, finished in Latvia — a production model no other major vodka brand replicates at this scale.

name: Stolichnaya  Premium Vodka (1 L) (New Label)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, five-column rectification, four-stage birch-charcoal filtration — Stoli's 'narrow cut' preserves cereal character most vodkas strip away.
description (EN Store): When Soviet specialists developed this recipe in the late 1930s, the deliberate decision was to retain grain character rather than chase full neutrality — a philosophy encoded in a strict distillation cut that discards all spirit evaporating below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C, preserving the faint toasty backbone that defines the house style. Grain — a proprietary blend of winter wheat, spring wheat, and a minority proportion of rye — is sourced exclusively from Russia's Chernozem (Black Earth) belt around Tambov, one of the world's most fertile agricultural zones, where deep humus-rich soils and continental climate produce starch-dense grain with low moisture content. At Talvis Distillery, Tambov, the mash undergoes saccharification at 87°C before fermentation to ~12% ABV over 72 hours using Danish dried yeast; a continuous five-column rectification system exits at 96.3% ABV, with a dedicated vacuum column operating at 60°C and a copper cartridge stripping sulphur compounds. Spirit travels by tanker train ~850 miles to Latvijas Balzams, Riga — built 1900 by Tsarist order — where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells treated by reverse osmosis, rested in 250,000-litre tanks, then filtered four times: quartz sand, Russian birch charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. A trace addition of 1.8 g/L sugar post-filtration rounds the palate without perceptible sweetness. The dual-nation, farm-to-bottle supply chain — grain grown on SPI-owned Black Earth farmland, distilled in Russia, finished in Latvia — is structurally unlike any other major vodka brand.

name: Stolichnaya  Orange (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The original citrus vodka (1986): Black Earth wheat & rye, narrow-cut rectification, four-stage birch-charcoal filtration, finished in Riga.
description (EN Store): In 1986, Stolichnaya released the world's first commercially produced citrus-flavoured vodka — not as a trend-chaser but as a direct extension of the brand's 1962 flavoured-spirit programme that had already pioneered pepper and honey-herb expressions. That heritage positions Stolichnaya Orange as the category's originator, not an imitator. The base spirit is built from winter and spring wheat with a minority proportion of rye, all grown on Black Earth (Chernozem) farmland in Tambov, Russia — some of it on SPI Group's own Raduga farm — where deep, iron-rich soil and continental climate produce grain with a faint toasty character that survives even high-rectification distillation. Distillation runs through a continuous five-column rectification system at Talvis Distillery; the 'narrow cut' philosophy discards all spirit evaporating below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C, preserving cereal texture rather than chasing full neutrality. The rectified spirit travels by tanker train to Latvijas Balzams in Riga — a facility built in 1900 by Tsarist order — where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells, treated by reverse osmosis, and passed through four sequential filtration stages: quartz sand, Russian birch-wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. What distinguishes this expression within the flavoured-vodka segment is that the grain backbone — that faint peppery rye finish and toasty wheat note — remains perceptible beneath the orange flavouring, giving structural contrast that most neutral-spirit-based citrus vodkas lack. Bottled at 40% ABV with a trace sugar addition rounding the palate in the classic Russian tradition.

name: Stolichnaya  Gold (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, five-column rectification, birch charcoal filtered four times — Stoli Gold's narrow-cut distillation retains cereal character most vodkas strip away.
description (EN Store): When Soviet specialists developed the Stolichnaya recipe in the late 1930s, the defining technical decision was restraint: discard everything that evaporates below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C, preserving a narrow band of wheat-derived character rather than chasing full neutrality. That philosophy survives in every bottle today. Grain — winter wheat dominant, with a minority proportion of rye — is grown on Russia's fertile Black Earth (Chernozem) region of Tambov, where deep humus-rich soils and continental climate produce dense, starchy grain that carries through to the spirit's faint toasty backbone. At Talvis Distillery, Tambov, a continuous five-column rectification system runs the mash column under vacuum at 60°C through a copper cartridge to strip sulphur compounds; rectified spirit exits at 96.3% ABV. The spirit then travels by tanker train ~850 miles to Latvijas Balzams, Riga — a facility built in 1900 by Tsarist order — where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells, reverse-osmosis treated to remove iron and magnesium, and passed through four sequential filtrations: quartz sand, Russian birch charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. A trace addition of 1.8 g/L sugar rounds the palate without perceptible sweetness. What no peer replicates is this dual-nation, farm-to-bottle chain — company-owned Black Earth farmland feeding a purpose-built Russian distillery, finished in a Tsarist-era Latvian bottling house — a supply model that has driven one of the spirits industry's longest-running trademark disputes since 2003.

name: Zubrowka  Bison Grass Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Polish vodka infused with bison grass, delivering herbaceous character and clean grain smoothness.
description (EN Store): Zubrowka Bison Grass Vodka is a distinctive Polish spirit distinguished by its signature infusion of bison grass, a rare botanical native to the Białowieża Forest. The herbaceous primary character dominates the palate, supported by subtle citrus and floral notes that emerge on the nose. Clean grain backbone and smooth finish make it versatile for neat sipping, traditional cocktails, or as an aperitif companion to seafood and cured meats.

name: Belvedere  Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Heritage Dankowskie Gold rye, artesian glacial-filtered water, zero additives — the vodka that invented the super-premium category in 1996.
description (EN Store): When Polmos Żyrardów launched Belvedere into the US market in 1996, no luxury vodka category existed — Grey Goose and Ketel One had yet to follow. The distillery itself dates to 1910, built on the Masovian Plain roughly 50 km southwest of Warsaw by brothers Dawid and Mejer Pines, and its infrastructure underpins every bottle today. 100% Dankowskie Gold rye, sourced under long-term contracts from a small network of 7–10 named Polish farms, is mashed with enzymes, fermented with a proprietary distiller's yeast for approximately 72 hours at 35°C to 6–8% ABV, then first-distilled on-farm before transfer to Żyrardów for three-column rectification. Water is drawn from two dedicated artesian wells — one 41.5 m deep, one 50.5 m deep — filtered through 17 quaternary glacial terraces and an 11-step purification system including reverse osmosis, arriving at hardness zero and pH 7.4. The finished spirit passes through activated charcoal then cellulose particle filtration, rests a minimum of two days in stainless steel, and is bottled at 40% ABV with zero additives, sugar, or glycerin — as mandated by the legally protected Polska Wódka appellation. What distinguishes Belvedere Pure within the super-premium tier is the grain-forward identity that Dankowskie Gold imparts: almond, white pepper, and a velvety mouthfeel that neutral-grain or potato vodkas structurally cannot replicate.

name: Valt  Single Malt Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scottish single malt vodka with clean grain character and smooth, versatile profile for classic cocktails.
description (EN Store): Valt Single Malt Vodka represents a Scottish approach to neutral spirit production. The spirit exhibits a clean, grain-forward primary character with a light body and smooth palate suited to both neat service and cocktail applications. Subtle citrus notes in the secondary tier provide gentle complexity without dominating the neutral foundation. The finish remains crisp and approachable, making this vodka a reliable choice for mixed drinks and aperitif service.

name: Beluga  Noble Russian Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Russian vodka with clean grain character and smooth, neutral palate—versatile for cocktails and aperitifs.
description (EN Store): Beluga Noble Russian Vodka is a grain-based spirit offering a clean, neutral profile characteristic of premium Russian vodka production. The palate presents subtle citrus notes and mineral undertones, with a smooth finish suited to both neat service and mixed drinks. Its light body and balanced character make it a versatile choice for classic cocktails and aperitif service.

name: Beluga  Gols Line Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Russian vodka with clean grain character and smooth, neutral profile—ideal for classic cocktails and refined spirits service.
description (EN Store): Beluga Gols Line Vodka represents a refined approach to Russian vodka production, emphasizing clean grain character and smooth distillation. The spirit delivers a neutral, versatile base with subtle citrus undertones and a mineral finish, making it equally suited to neat service, premium cocktails, or as a foundation for mixed drinks. The light body and balanced profile reflect careful production standards typical of the Beluga range.

name: Finlandia  Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Rajamäki glacial moraine water, zero chemical treatment — purity achieved by 200+ distillation steps, not charcoal filtration.
description (EN Store): When Finnish sculptor Tapio Wirkkala designed the original 'Frozen Ice' bottle in 1970, the form was a deliberate argument: Finlandia's identity would be built on place, not process additives. That argument holds in the glass today. Distillation occurs at Koskenkorva, Ilmajoki — western Finland's grain belt, where six-row barley grows under up to 72 days of unbroken Midnight Sun, producing high-starch, low-oil grain with an unusually clean fermentation profile. The spirit passes through seven stainless-steel column stills (three under vacuum, three atmospheric, one pressurised) across more than 200 individual distillation steps over ~50 hours, exiting at 96.5% ABV — methanol and fusel alcohols stripped without a single gram of charcoal. The 96.5% distillate then travels 315 km by rail to Rajamäki, Nurmijärvi, where it is diluted solely with glacial spring water drawn from a 10,000-year-old Ice Age moraine aquifer — mechanically filtered only, no deionisation, no reverse osmosis, no mineral removal. The company protects 12 km² of surrounding forest to preserve the source. No charcoal filtration, no additives, bottled at 40% ABV. The result is a bone-dry, light-bodied vodka with faint white pepper and a whisper of grain — texture shaped entirely by water mineralogy rather than post-distillation intervention.

name: Stolichnaya  Salted Karamel (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, narrow-cut rectification, birch charcoal filtered — Stoli's salted caramel expression on a cereal-driven base.
description (EN Store): Stolichnaya pioneered the flavoured vodka category in 1962 with Okhotnichya and Pertsovka, and launched the world's first citrus-flavoured vodka in 1986 — so the Salted Karamel expression arrives with genuine category-originator credentials rather than trend-chasing. The base spirit is built from winter and spring wheat with a minority rye component, all grown on Black Earth (Chernozem) farmland in Tambov, Russia — some of it on SPI Group's own Raduga farm — where deep, iron-rich soil and continental climate produce grain with a distinct toasty density. Distillation runs through a continuous five-column rectification system at Talvis Distillery, Tambov, with a deliberate narrow cut: only spirit evaporating between 67.2°C and 79.0°C is retained, preserving faint wheat-derived cereal character rather than chasing full neutrality. The rectified spirit travels by tanker train to Latvijas Balzams in Riga — a facility built in 1900 by Tsarist order — where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells, reverse-osmosis treated to strip iron and magnesium, then passed through four sequential filtration stages: quartz sand, Russian birch wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. The salted caramel flavouring is layered onto this cereal-forward, lightly peppery base, where the rye-derived spice cuts through the sweetness and the birch-charcoal filtration keeps the texture clean rather than cloying. Bottled at 40% ABV; NV, ready to serve now.

name: Stolichnaya  Choco Kokonut (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Stolichnaya's chocolate-coconut flavoured vodka built on Black Earth wheat spirit, four-stage birch charcoal filtration, Riga artesian water.
description (EN Store):
Stolichnaya's flavoured vodka lineage traces to 1962, when the brand pioneered commercially produced flavoured spirits — a category it invented, not joined. The Choco Kokonut expression sits on that same grain-spirit foundation: winter and spring wheat sourced from the fertile Chernozem (Black Earth) soils of Tambov, Russia, where mineral-rich, deep humus loam produces a starch-dense grain that contributes a faint toasty cereal backbone even after rectification to 96.3% ABV via a continuous five-column system at Talvis Distillery.

At Latvijas Balzams in Riga — a facility operating since 1900 — the rectified spirit is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells, reverse-osmosis treated to strip iron and magnesium, then passed through four sequential filtration stages: quartz sand, Russian birch wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. Chocolate and coconut flavouring is layered onto this base, with a trace of sugar rounding the palate in the classic Russian tradition. What distinguishes this expression within the flavoured-vodka category is its dual-nation production chain — grain grown on company-owned Black Earth farmland, distilled in Russia, finished in Latvia — giving the base spirit more textural identity than the neutral corn or potato spirits most flavoured vodkas use as a blank canvas.

name: Grey Goose  Vodka (Magnum) (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Picardy blé panifiable supérieur wheat, single five-column distillation, limestone-spring water — François Thibault's grain-to-glass refusal to use neutral spirit.
description (EN Store): When Cognac-trained Maître de Chai François Thibault was handed the brief in 1997, he rejected the industry default of buying in neutral grain spirit and re-distilling it — insisting instead on full grain-to-glass control, a philosophy borrowed directly from Cognac's terroir-first tradition and applied, transgressively, to vodka. The wheat is sourced exclusively from three family-farm cooperatives on the soft plains of Picardy, northern France, where slow-maturing blé panifiable supérieur winter wheat is sown in October and harvested ten months later — a growing cycle nearly double that of summer wheat, producing denser starch and more pronounced cereal character. At the Saint-Quentin distillery, wheat is milled on-site, enzymatically converted, then fermented continuously across six cascading tanks before a single five-column distillation to 96% ABV — enough to achieve purity without stripping the soft cracked-wheat and faint anise character Thibault deliberately preserves. The high-proof spirit travels 600 km south to Gensac-la-Pallue in the Cognac region, where it is blended with artesian spring water drawn from 150 m below the facility, naturally filtered through Champagne limestone and demineralised via double reverse osmosis to pH ~5. A single light cellulose-and-active-carbon polish — not industrial chill-filtration — completes the process before bottling at 40% ABV with zero additives. The 1.75 L Magnum format delivers the same two-ingredient formula at scale, sealed with a replaceable cork.

name: Finlandia  Vodka Lime (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Rajamäki glacial-spring vodka, lime-flavoured; purity built on 200+ distillation steps, zero charcoal filtration, untreated Ice Age moraine water.
description (EN Store): When Alko launched Finlandia in 1970, it commissioned sculptor Tapio Wirkkala to design the bottle — a deliberate signal that this was not a utility spirit but a designed object, and that purity was the brand's founding argument. That argument is structural: the base spirit is produced at Koskenkorva, Ilmajoki, in western Finland's barley-growing heartland, where six-row Finnish barley grown under up to 72 days of Midnight Sun — yielding high starch and clean fermentation — is mashed, fermented, and run through seven stainless-steel column stills operating at three different pressures across more than 200 individual distillation steps over approximately 50 hours, stripping methanol and fusel alcohols to exit at 96.5% ABV. The spirit then travels 315 km by rail to Rajamäki, where it is diluted with glacial spring water drawn from a 10,000-year-old Ice Age moraine aquifer — mechanically filtered only, with no deionisation, no reverse osmosis, no mineral removal. The company owns 12 km² of surrounding forest to protect the source. Critically, no charcoal filtration is applied at any stage; purity is achieved entirely through distillation architecture, not post-processing. This Lime expression layers natural lime character over that clean barley-and-glacial-water base, bottled at 40% ABV with no additives. The result is a flavoured vodka where the citrus note reads against a genuinely neutral, light-bodied canvas rather than a sweetened or filtered blank.

name: Finlandia  Vodka Cranberry (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Six-row barley distilled 200+ steps at Koskenkorva, diluted with untreated 10,000-year-old glacial spring water — cranberry-infused expression, no charcoal filtration.
description (EN Store): When Alko launched Finlandia in 1970, it commissioned Finnish sculptor Tapio Wirkkala to design the bottle — a deliberate signal that this was a different kind of vodka, built on process purity rather than post-distillation correction. The cranberry expression extends that same philosophy into flavoured territory. Distillation occurs at Koskenkorva, Ilmajoki, in Finland's western grain belt, where six-row barley grown under up to 72 days of Midnight Sun produces a high-starch, low-oil grain ideal for clean fermentation. The spirit passes through seven stainless-steel column stills across more than 200 individual distillation steps over approximately 50 hours, exiting at 96.5% ABV — methanol and fusel alcohols stripped without any charcoal filtration. The base is then transported 315 km by rail to Rajamäki, where dilution uses glacial spring water drawn from a 10,000-year-old Ice Age moraine aquifer, mechanically filtered only — no deionisation, no reverse osmosis, no mineral removal. What makes this expression unusual is that the cranberry character sits atop a genuinely neutral, additive-free grain base rather than a sweetened or charcoal-smoothed canvas, allowing the tart berry to read cleanly against faint white pepper grain. The result is off-dry, light-bodied, and sharper-edged than most fruit vodkas in its price tier.

name: Finlandia  Vodka Grape Fruit (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Grapefruit-flavoured Finnish barley vodka, distilled via 200+ column steps at Koskenkorva, diluted with untreated 10,000-year-old glacial spring water at Rajamäki.
description (EN Store): When Alko launched Finlandia in 1970, the base spirit was already built on an unusual conviction: purity through distillation architecture, not charcoal filtration. That same philosophy underpins every expression, including this grapefruit variant. Distillation takes place at Koskenkorva, Ilmajoki — western Finland's grain belt, where six-row barley grows under up to 72 days of unbroken Midnight Sun, producing high-starch grain with a clean fermentation profile. The spirit is driven through seven stainless-steel column stills (three under vacuum, three atmospheric, one pressurised) across more than 200 individual distillation steps over approximately 50 hours, exiting at 96.5% ABV — a level of rectification that strips fusel alcohols and methanol without any charcoal contact. The distillate then travels 315 km by rail to Rajamäki, where it is diluted with completely untreated glacial spring water mechanically filtered through 10,000-year-old Ice Age moraine — no deionisation, no reverse osmosis, no mineral removal. The grapefruit character is layered onto this exceptionally neutral, light-bodied base, making the citrus expression unusually transparent and clean rather than syrupy. No additives beyond the natural flavouring; no charcoal filtration at any stage. A ready-to-mix expression best consumed young and cold.

name: Stolichnaya  Gluten Free Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, narrow-cut rectification, birch-charcoal filtered in Riga — grain character preserved, not erased.
description (EN Store):
When Soviet specialists formulated Stolichnaya in the late 1930s, the deliberate decision was to retain cereal identity rather than chase full neutrality — a philosophy encoded in a narrow distillation cut that discards all spirit evaporating below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C. That choice still governs every batch today.

Grain — winter wheat and spring wheat with a minority proportion of rye — is sourced from Russia's Black Earth (Chernozem) region of Tambov, one of the world's most fertile agricultural belts, where deep humus-rich soils and continental climate produce starch-dense grain. SPI Group owns surrounding farmland supplying roughly 10% of requirements; the balance comes from neighbouring Black Earth farms.

At Talvis Distillery, Tambov, a five-column continuous rectification system — with the mash column operating under vacuum at 60°C and vapour passing through a copper cartridge for sulphur removal — produces spirit at 96.3% ABV. That spirit travels by tanker train ~850 miles to Latvijas Balzams, Riga (built 1900 by Tsarist order), where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells, reverse-osmosis treated, rested in 250,000-litre tanks, and passed through four sequential filtrations: quartz sand, Russian birch wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. A trace addition of 1.8 g/L sugar rounds the palate without perceptible sweetness.

The Gluten Free designation addresses a specific consumer need: the four-stage filtration process removes gluten peptides to below detectable thresholds, while the narrow-cut philosophy ensures the faint toasty grain backbone and gentle peppery rye finish survive intact — an unusual combination of inclusivity and technical character preservation.

Non-vintage by nature; drink immediately or store upright away from light. Best served well-chilled at −18°C to −20°C.

name: Absolut  Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus grain vodka: deep-aquifer water, winter wheat, no charcoal filtration — cereal texture preserved by design.
description (EN Store): When Lars Olsson Smith distilled his first Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879, he was already arguing that rectification precision made post-distillation intervention unnecessary — a position the modern distillery at Åhus still holds, making it one of the very few large-volume spirits to bottle without charcoal or chemical filtration. Every bottle originates from a single postcode: Destilleriet i Åhus, on the flat coastal plain of Skåne, where the Baltic Sea moderates temperature and the fertile soils yield winter wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare. Water is drawn from on-site wells more than 140 metres deep, filtered through Swedish bedrock over millennia, then treated by reverse osmosis before dilution — contributing a notably soft, mineral-neutral mouthfeel. The production sequence runs through a multi-column continuous still system: a mash column strips to ~35% ABV; a rectification column with copper-packet packing catalyses sulphur removal to ~85% ABV; an extraction column eliminates aldehydes; twin 26-metre rectification columns (split to comply with local airport height restrictions) refine to ~96% ABV. No sugar, glycerin, or additives are introduced at any stage. The result is a vodka whose faint cereal sweetness and soft grain texture are not incidental — they are the deliberate residue of a distillation philosophy that trusts column precision over filtration. Bottled at 40% ABV; no vintage variation applies.

name: Absolut  Vodka (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus winter wheat vodka — no charcoal filtration, 4-column continuous rectification preserves natural cereal grain character.
description (EN Store):
Lars Olsson Smith distilled his original Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879 using continuous rectification at a time when pot-still spirits dominated Sweden — a technical conviction that the column still could achieve purity without stripping character. That same argument drives every production decision at Destilleriet i Åhus today.

The distillery sits on the flat coastal plain of Skåne, drawing water from on-site aquifer wells sunk more than 140 metres through Swedish bedrock — a source filtered over millennia that requires no mineral adjustment. The surrounding farmland, enriched by Baltic coastal soils, yields winter wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare; approximately 400 farms within the Åhus community supply the entire annual requirement of ~125,000 tonnes.

Production follows a four-column continuous rectification system: a mash column strips to ~35% ABV; a stainless-steel rectification column with copper-packet packing catalyses sulphur compounds and brings spirit to ~85% ABV; an extraction column removes aldehydes; twin 26-metre rectification columns (split due to local airport height restrictions) refine to ~96% ABV. The spirit is then diluted to 40% ABV using demineralised well water — no charcoal filtration, no glycerin, no additives.

The deliberate refusal of post-distillation filtration is the defining transgression: where most large-volume vodka producers use charcoal to homogenise spirit, Absolut argues its column precision makes filtration redundant, leaving intact the soft, slightly sweet cereal texture that is the house signature.

As a non-vintage, non-aged spirit, character is consistent across batches; the wheat-forward grain note and aquifer-clean finish are the stable reference points for mixing or sipping neat.

name: Absolut  Raspberri Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Raspberry-flavoured Absolut from Åhus, Sweden — no filtration, no additives, single-source winter wheat and deep-aquifer water.
description (EN Store): Lars Olsson Smith's 1879 original Absolut Rent Brännvin — 'absolutely pure spirit' — set the technical ambition that still governs every bottle leaving Åhus: purity achieved through distillation precision alone, never through post-still filtration. Absolut Raspberri is built on that same unflinching foundation. All wheat is sourced from approximately 400 farms within Skåne's flat coastal plain surrounding Åhus, yielding at roughly twice the global average per hectare on fertile Baltic-adjacent soils. Water is drawn from the distillery's own wells at 140+ metres depth, filtered through Swedish bedrock over millennia, delivering exceptional mineral neutrality. The base spirit passes through a multi-column continuous rectification system — including a copper-packed column for sulphur catalysation — refining to ~96% ABV before dilution to 40% ABV. No charcoal filtration, no chemical filtration, no sugar, glycerin, or additives are applied at any stage; the raspberry character is introduced as natural flavouring into a base that retains the wheat's soft cereal texture that most competitors strip away. The special feature here is structural: Absolut's refusal to filter means the grain's mouthfeel survives into the flavoured expression, giving Raspberri a rounder, less hollow texture than most flavoured vodkas at this price point. NV by definition; best consumed fresh.

name: Absolut  Raspberri Vodka (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus wheat vodka, unfilitered by design — multi-column rectification preserves natural cereal texture most competitors strip away.
description (EN Store): Lars Olsson Smith distilled his first Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879, and the conviction behind that name — absolute purity through distillation precision alone — is still the technical argument Absolut makes today. Every ingredient originates within the Åhus community in Skåne, southern Sweden: approximately 400 farms supply 100% Swedish winter wheat (Triticum aestivum), and water is drawn from on-site aquifer wells sunk more than 140 metres through Swedish bedrock, yielding a mineral-neutral base that lets the cereal speak. Skåne's flat coastal plain and fertile soils produce wheat at roughly twice the global average yield per hectare — density that translates directly into a fuller mash. The wash ferments to 9–10% ABV in approximately 48 hours using a proprietary cultivated yeast, then passes through a continuous multi-column still system: a mash column, a rectification column with copper-packet packing for sulphur removal, an extraction column for aldehyde stripping, and twin 26-metre rectification columns (split to comply with local airport height restrictions) refining to ~96% ABV. The defining transgression: no charcoal or chemical filtration is applied — a deliberate choice that preserves the wheat's natural grain texture that most large-volume vodkas deliberately erase. Absolut Raspberri layers natural raspberry flavouring onto this base at 40% ABV, with no added sugar or glycerin.

name: Absolut  Elyx (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Absolut Elyx: single-vintage winter wheat from Åhus, distilled in a 1906 copper-packed column still at 42.3% ABV — no charcoal, no additives.
description (EN Store):
When Lars Olsson Smith built his original distillery philosophy around a single source in 1879, the principle that every ingredient must trace to one community was radical at industrial scale. Absolut Elyx honours that founding logic by returning to the original 1906 distillery building in Åhus, Skåne — a deliberate separation from the main 1967 plant — where a vintage-designated batch of 100% Swedish winter wheat is processed through a single copper-packed column still rather than the multi-column continuous rectification used for the standard line.

Skåne's flat coastal plain, positioned between the Baltic Sea and fertile agricultural lowlands, yields wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare. Water is drawn from on-site aquifer wells exceeding 140 metres deep, filtered through Swedish bedrock over millennia, contributing a mineral softness that demineralised process water cannot replicate.

The defining technical signature: no charcoal filtration, no chemical filtration, no sugar, no glycerin. Where most premium vodka producers strip cereal character through post-distillation filtration, Elyx relies entirely on the copper column's sulphur-catalysing action and careful rectification to achieve purity — bottled at 42.3% ABV, higher than the standard 40%, which amplifies the wheat's natural grain texture on the palate. This single-vintage, single-copper-still approach within a brand producing ~100 million litres annually is the category transgression: artisan-scale technique inside an industrial house.

name: Bols  Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wheat-based vodka from the world's oldest continuously operating spirits house, column-distilled and charcoal-filtered in Amsterdam since 1575.
description (EN Store): The Bols family established their Amsterdam distillery in 1575 — a house whose botanical expertise, built through direct access to Dutch East India Company spice routes, shaped the entire landscape of European spirits. That same distillery, now at 31 Pijlsteeg near Dam Square, produces this wheat-based vodka as a deliberate counterpoint to the house's more celebrated genever: where the genever carries malt-wine weight and grain complexity, the vodka is engineered for neutrality. 100% wheat grain bill, column-distilled, charcoal-filtered, bottled at 40% ABV — the production philosophy here is reduction rather than expression, stripping the spirit to a clean, grain-soft base. The house motto Semper Idem (Always the Same) governs the blending and filtration protocol at the Avandis C.V. facility in Zoetermeer, where finishing and bottling occur. What makes this expression unusual within the Bols portfolio is precisely its restraint: the same copper-pot infrastructure that produces triple-distilled malt wine at 47% ABV for genever is bypassed entirely here in favour of continuous column distillation — a deliberate category separation. As a non-vintage spirit, consistency across batches is the primary quality benchmark, and the charcoal filtration ensures the wheat character reads soft rather than sharp on the palate.

name: Bols  Vodka (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Wheat-based vodka from the world's oldest continuously operating spirits house, column-distilled and charcoal-filtered in Amsterdam since 1575.
description (EN Store): The Bulsius family established their Amsterdam distillery in 1575 — a house whose botanical expertise, built through direct access to VOC spice routes, underpins every spirit in the portfolio, including this wheat-based vodka. That same institutional philosophy, Semper Idem (Always the Same), governs production: consistency of character over novelty. Bols Vodka is produced from a 100% wheat base, column-distilled and charcoal-filtered to 40% ABV at the Avandis C.V. facility in Zoetermeer, where blending and bottling are completed under the same roof as the house's genever and liqueur lines. The special feature here is context: this vodka is made by a producer whose primary distillation identity is grain-forward malt wine, triple-distilled in copper pot stills — the wheat vodka sits at the neutral end of a house spectrum that runs all the way to whiskey-weight genever. The result is a clean, grain-honest spirit that carries the faint structural memory of a wheat-mash tradition stretching back four centuries. Drink now; no aging upside.

name: Suntory  Haku Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 100% Japanese white rice vodka, bamboo-charcoal filtered — Suntory's deliberate break from grain-neutral convention.
description (EN Store): Suntory, the house that upended Scotch orthodoxy by proving world-class whisky could be made in Japan, applied the same contrarian logic to vodka: where the category defaults to wheat or rye, Haku is built entirely from 100% Japanese white rice. The choice is not decorative — rice fermentation yields a naturally sweeter, softer new-make spirit that carries a distinct textural weight through to the finished bottle. Filtration through bamboo charcoal replaces the activated-charcoal columns standard in Western vodka production, a step that strips harshness while preserving the rice-derived roundness rather than homogenising the spirit into neutrality. That single filtration decision is what separates Haku structurally from its European and American peers. The brand library records no specific distillation site for Haku beyond Suntory's Japanese production network, which now includes the Osaka Spirits & Liqueurs Craft Distillery opened 2024–2025 for exactly this category of craft spirits. No critic scores are on record for this expression, and no vintage applies. Drink now and ongoing — vodka of this style does not benefit from further cellaring.

description (EN Store): Svedka Clementine Vodka is a flavored spirit from Sweden combining neutral grain distillate with natural clementine essence. description (EN Store): Svedka Cherry Vodka is a fruit-infused spirit from Sweden, crafted with a focus on clean, accessible flavor. name: Stolichnaya  Cucumber (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Black Earth wheat & rye, narrow-cut rectification, birch-charcoal filtered in Riga — Stoli's 1962 flavoured-vodka lineage meets fresh cucumber.
description (EN Store): When Stolichnaya launched commercially produced flavoured vodkas in 1962 — decades before the category became mainstream — it established a production philosophy that cucumber expression now inherits: natural flavour addition over a technically disciplined base spirit. That base begins in Tambov's Black Earth (Chernozem) region, where winter and spring wheat with a minority proportion of rye are milled, mashed, and fermented to ~12% ABV over approximately 72 hours using Danish dried yeast. Continuous five-column rectification at Talvis Distillery exits at 96.3% ABV, with the mash column operating under vacuum at 60°C and vapour passing through a copper cartridge for sulphur removal. The defining technical signature is a narrow-cut discipline — discarding all spirit below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C — preserving faint wheat-derived cereal character rather than chasing full neutrality. At Latvijas Balzams in Riga, artesian well water drawn from 200-metre-deep wells and treated by reverse osmosis is blended in, followed by four-stage filtration: quartz sand, Russian birch wood charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. Cucumber flavouring is added to this foundation, with 1.8 g/L residual sugar rounding the palate. The result sits unusually close to the base spirit's grain character — the cucumber reads as fresh and vegetal rather than confected, anchored by the rye-derived pepper finish beneath.

name: Stolichnaya  Elit Night Edition (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Elit Night Edition 1.75L: wheat-only Raduga farm spirit, freeze-filtered at −18°C, 1.2 g/L sugar — Stoli's most technically precise expression.
description (EN Store):
When SPI Group designated a single named plot — the Raduga farm within their Black Earth (Chernozem) holdings outside Tambov — as the exclusive grain source for Elit, they made a deliberate single-origin commitment unusual in commercial vodka. That Tambov terroir, deep fertile chernozem soil at roughly 150–200 m elevation, produces winter wheat with a starch density that survives the five-column continuous rectification system and still registers as cereal character in the glass.

At Talvis Distillery, the mash column operates under vacuum at 60°C, passing vapour through a copper cartridge for sulphur removal before rectification to 96.3% ABV. The spirit is then tanker-trained 850 miles to Latvijas Balzams, Riga — a facility drawing water from two 200-metre artesian wells, reverse-osmosis treated to strip iron and magnesium — where it is blended, rested in 250,000-litre tanks, and filtered four times: quartz sand, Russian birch charcoal, woven cloth, final polish. Elit's defining step follows: freeze filtration at exactly −18°C, removing residual fatty acids that would otherwise cloud the spirit at low temperature, with only 1.2 g/L sugar added post-filtration — measurably less than the standard Premium's 1.8 g/L.

The Night Edition 1.75 L format amplifies the single-origin, freeze-filtered proposition at scale. The narrow distillation cut — discarding everything below 67.2°C and above 79.0°C — preserves a faint toasty wheat backbone and a clean peppery rye-adjacent finish that full-neutrality vodkas deliberately erase.
