name: Absolut  Lime Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus vodka: 140m-deep aquifer water, Skåne winter wheat, no charcoal filtration — lime-infused expression of Sweden's most geographically concentrated distillery.
description (EN Store): Lars Olsson Smith distilled his first Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879, and the philosophy of uncompromising grain purity has governed every litre produced at Destilleriet i Åhus since. Absolut Lime is built on that same foundation: 100% Skåne winter wheat from roughly 400 farms within the Åhus community, mashed, fermented with a proprietary Swedish cultivated yeast to 9–10% ABV over approximately 48 hours, then driven through a multi-column continuous rectification system — stripping column, copper-packed rectification column for sulphur catalysation, extraction column for aldehyde removal, and a split 26-metre main rectification column — to reach ~96% ABV before dilution to 40% ABV with water drawn from on-site wells more than 140 metres deep, filtered through Swedish bedrock over millennia. The Skåne plain's fertile soils yield wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare, and that agricultural density translates directly into a soft, slightly sweet cereal base. What distinguishes Absolut categorically is its deliberate refusal of charcoal or chemical post-distillation filtration — a technically bold stance that preserves the wheat's natural grain texture most competitors strip away. The lime character is layered onto this unfilitered grain foundation, delivering sharp citrus zest against a genuinely textured mouthfeel rather than the neutral canvas typical of filtered vodkas.

name: Nikka  Coffey Vodka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Corn-dominant Coffey still spirit from Miyagikyo, distilled on Glasgow-built continuous stills — Japan's most technically unusual vodka.
description (EN Store):
When Masataka Taketsuru installed two Coffey continuous stills — built by Blairs Ltd. of Glasgow and originally operated at Nikka's Nishinomiya plant — at Miyagikyo, he was preserving a 19th-century Scottish distillation technology inside a Japanese whisky house. The Coffey Vodka is the clearest expression of what those stills do to a corn-dominant grain bill when the objective is spirit, not whisky.

Miyagikyo sits in a forested valley west of Sendai, fed by water from the Zao Mountain Range — among the softest, lowest-hardness water sources of any Japanese distillery. That mineral-lean water, combined with the Coffey still's gentle continuous distillation, strips away harsh congeners while retaining the grain's inherent sweetness and a faint cereal creaminess that column-distilled neutral spirits rarely preserve.

Distilled in a Coffey continuous still on a corn-dominant grain bill, the spirit is produced at Miyagikyo rather than a dedicated neutral-spirit facility — an architectural decision that gives it a flavour identity no conventional vodka distillery replicates. Bottling details and filtration specifics are not publicly disclosed by Nikka, but the house's grain distillation philosophy prioritises texture retention over absolute neutrality.

What is categorically unusual: this is a vodka produced inside a whisky distillery on stills designed for grain whisky, making the boundary between categories deliberately porous. Drink now; no aging upside applies.

name: Asura  (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Potato) (Alc. Volume 30%) (330 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Potato-based Thai white spirit at 30% ABV — legally a sura khao, functionally closer to a craft neutral, built for Bangkok's cocktail renaissance.
description (EN Store): Thai law permits no middle ground: every distilled spirit is either sura khao (white) or lao see (brown), regardless of how it is made — so Asura, despite its clean, near-neutral profile, carries the same legal label as rough-edged village lao khao. That regulatory constraint is precisely the transgression worth noting: Asura is engineered to escape the category it is forced to inhabit. Distilled from potato — an uncommon base in a Thai market dominated by rice and sugarcane — it arrives at 30% ABV in a 330 ml format designed for accessibility and cocktail portioning. The lower bottling strength softens the spirit's heat threshold without diluting its aromatic lift, a deliberate departure from the sharp, high-proof character of mass-market lao khao. Herbal and fresh-fruit aromatics are cited by independent reviewers as the defining character, consistent with a clean distillate that carries the faint vegetal sweetness potato fermentation can yield. No aging is applied; the spirit is bottled unaged, preserving raw distillate character. Specific fermentation vessel, yeast strain, and filtration details are not publicly documented. What sets Asura apart categorically is its potato base in a Thai spirits landscape where that raw material is essentially unprecedented at commercial scale — a genuine outlier in Southeast Asian white spirits.

name: Asura  Golden Giant (Thai White Spirits Distilled From Potato) (625ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thailand's licensed sura khao category, reimagined: Asura distils from locally sourced raw materials into a clean, mildly sweet white spirit built for Bangkok's cocktail bars.
description (EN Store): Under Thai Excise Department law, no domestic distilled spirit — however craft its method — may call itself vodka, gin, or any Western category on its label. Asura operates entirely within this constraint, bottled and sold as sura khao (Thai white spirit), yet it has been cited by Bangkok's cocktail renaissance alongside craft micro-distillery peers as a 'Thai vodka'-style expression — a deliberate positioning against the sharp, abrasive character of mass-market lao khao. The base ingredient remains officially unconfirmed, though the brand references high-quality locally sourced raw materials; the aromatic profile — herbal lift, fresh-fruit brightness, mildly sweet palate — points toward a clean, likely stainless-steel-distilled spirit rather than a traditionally fermented rice mash. Traditional lao khao relies on look-pang yeast balls (ground rice inoculated with yeast and mold, spiced with galangal and long pepper) for fermentation; whether Asura employs this method or a more neutral commercial yeast is not publicly documented. Bottled at 40% ABV with no aging — consistent with the unaged sura khao category — its low-heat finish is the defining departure from category convention. Export is effectively prohibited for producers below Thailand's 300,000-litre/day threshold, making Asura a near-exclusively domestic expression. The 625 ml format itself is non-standard, signalling a deliberate break from the category's commodity packaging.

name: Absolut  Vodka (4.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus winter wheat vodka — no charcoal filtration, 140m deep-aquifer water, multi-column continuous rectification preserving natural grain texture.
description (EN Store):
Lars Olsson Smith distilled his first Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879, and the philosophy of purity-through-precision rather than purity-through-filtration has defined the house ever since. Every bottle of Absolut originates from a single postcode: Destilleriet i Åhus, on the flat coastal plain of Skåne, where the Baltic Sea moderates the climate and the fertile southern Swedish soils yield winter wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare. Approximately 400 farms within the surrounding Skåne region supply 100% Triticum aestivum winter wheat — around 125,000 tonnes annually — while process water is drawn from the distillery's own wells sunk more than 140 metres into Swedish bedrock, filtered over millennia before it reaches the still.

Production runs through a continuous multi-column rectification system: a mash column strips the fermented wash (9–10% ABV, ~48-hour fermentation with proprietary cultivated yeast) to ~35% ABV; a stainless-steel rectification column fitted with copper-packet packing for sulphur catalysation brings spirit to ~85% ABV; an extraction column removes aldehydes; twin 26-metre final rectification columns — split low to comply with local airport height restrictions — refine to ~96% ABV. The spirit is then diluted to 40% ABV with no charcoal or chemical filtration applied, and no sugar, glycerin, or additives are added. That deliberate omission of post-distillation filtration is the technical transgression that separates Absolut from most large-volume peers: the wheat's natural cereal texture survives intact. This 4.5-litre format delivers the same single-source, single-distillery liquid — every bottle of Absolut sold globally traceable to one aquifer in Åhus — at hospitality scale.

name: Absolut  Vodka (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-source Åhus winter wheat vodka — no charcoal filtration, 4-column continuous rectification preserves natural grain texture at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): Lars Olsson Smith distilled his original Absolut Rent Brännvin in 1879 using continuous rectification when pot-still producers controlled the Swedish market — the same technical conviction that defines every bottle made today at Destilleriet i Åhus. The distillery sits on Skåne's flat coastal plain between the Baltic Sea and some of northern Europe's most productive farmland, where fertile glacial soils yield winter wheat at roughly twice the global average per hectare. Water is drawn from on-site wells sunk more than 140 metres into Swedish bedrock, filtered over millennia and treated by reverse osmosis before dilution — contributing a notably soft, mineral-neutral mouthfeel. Production follows a four-stage continuous column system: a mash column strips to ~35% ABV; a rectification column with copper-packet packing catalyses sulphur removal to ~85% ABV; an extraction column eliminates aldehydes; twin 26-metre rectification columns (split to comply with local airport height restrictions) refine to ~96% ABV before dilution to 40% ABV. Critically, no charcoal or chemical filtration is applied — a deliberate refusal shared by almost no other large-volume vodka — preserving the cereal's natural grainy sweetness that post-distillation stripping would erase. Every bottle of Absolut sold globally is traceable to one distillery, one aquifer, and approximately 400 farms within the Åhus community — a single-source claim at 100-million-litre annual scale that no comparable global brand can match.

name: Stolichnaya Citrus (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The original citrus vodka — Stoli invented the category in 1986. Black Earth wheat, birch charcoal filtration, Riga artesian water.
description (EN Store): When Stolichnaya launched the world's first citrus-flavoured vodka in 1986, it was not following a trend — it was creating one, building on a flavoured-spirit tradition the brand had pioneered since 1962 with Pertsovka and Okhotnichya. That institutional confidence in flavour is the context for Stolichnaya Citrus. The base spirit is built from winter and spring wheat with a minority rye component, grown on Black Earth (Chernozem) farmland in Tambov, Russia — some of the most fertile agricultural land on earth, yielding grain with a faint toasty cereal character that survives rectification. Distillation at Talvis Distillery, Tambov employs a five-column continuous rectification system, with a deliberate narrow cut — discarding all spirit outside the 67.2°C–79.0°C window — to preserve subtle wheat-derived texture rather than strip to full neutrality. The spirit is then transported by tanker train to Latvijas Balzams, Riga, where it is blended with water drawn from 200-metre artesian wells (reverse-osmosis treated) and passed through four sequential filtrations: quartz sand, Russian birch charcoal, woven cloth, and a final polish. What distinguishes Stolichnaya Citrus within the flavoured vodka category is precisely this provenance: the citrus character sits over a grain base with genuine textural identity — not the blank-canvas neutral spirit most competitors use — giving the flavouring something to interact with rather than simply coat.

name: Asura JawSamut (JawSamut-Coconut Flower Sugar Spirits V3) (625ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thai-licensed sura khao built on coconut flower sugar — a craft distillate navigating strict excise law to deliver clean, mildly sweet aromatics where mass lao khao delivers heat.
description (EN Store): Thai excise law forces every domestic distillate — regardless of technique or ambition — onto a label reading sura khao (white spirit), a regulatory constraint that has paradoxically shielded producers like Asura from category dilution while preventing them from exporting below the 300,000-litre/day threshold. This third version of the JawSamut expression is built on coconut flower sugar as its declared base, a deliberate departure from the glutinous rice or molasses that anchors most Thai white spirits at this price tier. The fermentation and distillation specifics are not publicly documented, but the resulting spirit sits at 40% ABV, bottled unaged — consistent with the sura khao classification — and positioned against mass-market lao khao, which typically presents sharp, abrasive heat from rough cuts and minimal quality control. Where traditional lao khao relies on look-pang yeast balls (rice inoculated with mold cultures and spiced with galangal and long pepper) for fermentation character, Asura's aromatic profile skews toward fresh-fruit and herbal registers rather than the funky, grain-forward notes that define the category's baseline. The coconut flower sugar base is the structural anomaly here: it is neither rice nor cane molasses, placing this expression outside both the folk tradition and the industrial mainstream of Thai distilling. As a V3 iteration, it signals ongoing recipe refinement rather than a fixed house style. Drink now; no aging upside.

name: Finlandia Vodka (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Rajamäki glacial moraine water, zero charcoal filtration — purity built entirely through 200+ distillation steps over 50 hours.
description (EN Store): When Finnish sculptor Tapio Wirkkala designed the original 'Frozen Ice' bottle in 1970, Finlandia became arguably the world's first deliberately positioned designer vodka — a commercial decision that anchored a production philosophy still intact today. Six-row Finnish barley, grown under up to 72 days of unbroken Midnight Sun in Ilmajoki's western grain belt, is mashed, fermented, and run through seven stainless-steel column stills at Koskenkorva — three under vacuum, three atmospheric, one pressurised — accumulating more than 200 individual distillation steps across approximately 50 hours to exit at 96.5% ABV. The spirit is then railed 315 km south to Rajamäki, where it is diluted solely with water drawn from a 10,000-year-old Ice Age moraine aquifer — mechanically filtered only, with no deionisation, no reverse osmosis, and no mineral stripping. The company protects the source by owning 12 km² of surrounding forest. What makes this expression categorically unusual: no charcoal filtration whatsoever — purity is achieved entirely through distillation architecture, not post-still treatment. The result is a light-bodied, bone-dry spirit carrying faint white pepper and a whisper of grain, with the moraine water's untouched mineral profile providing the textural signature that wheat-based Scandinavian peers cannot replicate.

name: Grey Goose Altius (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-distillation Picardy wheat vodka: François Thibault's Cognac-trained grain-to-glass philosophy, limestone spring water, zero additives.
description (EN Store):
When Sidney Frank commissioned Cognac Maître de Chai François Thibault to create Grey Goose in 1997, Thibault refused the industry shortcut of buying in neutral grain spirit — insisting instead on full grain-to-glass control, a discipline borrowed directly from Cognac's appellation culture and unheard of in vodka at the time.

The wheat is blé panifiable supérieur, a soft winter wheat sown each October across three family-farm cooperatives on the flat chalk plains of Picardy, northern France — a 10-month growing cycle (versus six months for summer wheat) that builds a denser starch structure and a distinctly soft, slightly sweet cereal character. Temperatures rarely drop below −5 °C, allowing slow, even maturation.

At the Saint-Quentin distillery, milled wheat undergoes continuous fermentation across six cascading tanks before a single five-column distillation to 96% ABV — a deliberate choice to preserve grain character rather than strip it. The high-proof spirit travels ~600 km south to Gensac-la-Pallue in the Cognac region, where it is blended with artesian spring water drawn from 150 m below the facility, naturally filtered through Champagne limestone and demineralised via double reverse osmosis to pH ~5. A single light cellulose-and-active-carbon polish precedes bottling at 40% ABV with zero additives.

The special feature: where every major competitor pursues maximum neutrality through multiple redistillations, Grey Goose's single-distillation architecture is engineered to leave cracked wheat, faint anise, and mineral freshness intact — a categorical transgression that invented the ultra-premium vodka tier.

name: Grey Goose Altius (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-distilled Picardy blé panifiable supérieur wheat, limestone-spring water from 150 m below Gensac — grain-to-glass control François Thibault refused to compromise.
description (EN Store):
When Cognac-trained Maître de Chai François Thibault co-created Grey Goose in 1997, he rejected the industry's standard practice of purchasing neutral grain spirit and re-distilling it — insisting instead on full grain-to-glass control, a philosophy borrowed directly from the Charente's terroir-driven traditions and applied, provocatively, to vodka.

The wheat comes exclusively from three family-farm cooperatives in Picardy's northern plains, where a continental microclimate with winters no colder than −5 °C enables a 10-month growing cycle for soft winter wheat (blé panifiable supérieur, 100% GMO-free) — nearly double the maturation time of summer wheat, yielding denser starch and more pronounced cereal character. Grain is milled on-site at the Saint-Quentin distillery.

Fermentation runs continuously across six cascading tanks to a ~10% ABV wash; a single five-column distillation to 96% ABV deliberately preserves wheat-derived aromatics rather than stripping them. The high-proof spirit travels ~600 km to Gensac-la-Pallue, where it is blended with artesian spring water drawn from 150 m below the Champagne limestone plateau, demineralised via double reverse osmosis to pH ~5, passed through a light cellulose-and-active-carbon polish, and bottled at 40% ABV with zero additives.

The Altius 1.75 L format delivers the full house signature — cracked wheat, faint anise, mineral limestone freshness — at a scale that underlines Thibault's original transgression: a vodka that refuses to be tasteless.

name: Grey Goose Original (6 L)
short_description (EN Store): Picardy blé panifiable supérieur wheat, single five-column distillation, limestone spring water — François Thibault's grain-to-glass refusal to buy neutral spirit.
description (EN Store):
When Sidney Frank commissioned Cognac Maître de Chai François Thibault to build Grey Goose in 1997, Thibault made one non-negotiable decision: no purchased neutral grain spirit, no redistillation of industrial alcohol — full grain-to-glass control, or nothing. That single refusal defines every litre in this six-litre format.

The wheat is blé panifiable supérieur, 100% GMO-free soft winter wheat sown each October across three family-farm cooperatives in Picardy's flat northern plains, harvested after a ten-month growing cycle — nearly double the maturation time of summer wheat — concentrating the cereal's natural sugars and soft anise character before the grain ever reaches the Saint-Quentin mill.

Fermentation runs continuously across six cascading tanks to a ~10% ABV wash; distillation passes through a single five-column still to 96% ABV, preserving measurable wheat character that repeated redistillation would strip. The high-proof spirit travels ~600 km south to Gensac-la-Pallue in the Cognac region, where it is blended with water drawn from a proprietary artesian well 150 m below the facility, naturally filtered through Champagne limestone and demineralised via double reverse osmosis to pH ~5, then given a light cellulose-and-active-carbon polish — not industrial chill-filtration.

The special feature: this is a 6 L format of a vodka that deliberately broke the category's 'tasteless and odourless' orthodoxy, delivering cracked wheat, faint anise, and limestone minerality where peers offer only absence. Over 550 quality checks govern every batch before release.

name: Game of Thrones Single Malt Scotch Whisky Collection @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description (EN Store): Speyside single malt with approachable malty sweetness, vanilla oak, and subtle spice—ideal for red meat and evening sipping.
description (EN Store): A Speyside single malt Scotch whisky from the Game of Thrones collection, crafted with classic Highland distilling tradition. The spirit opens with warm malty sweetness and vanilla-forward oak influence, followed by gentle spice and dried fruit complexity. Medium-bodied with balanced structure, it suits both neat sipping and food pairing with grilled meats, smoked dishes, and rich charcuterie.

name: Martell Single Cru Level 1 @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description (EN Store): Borderies-dominant Cognac from the house that purchases 50–60% of the entire cru — distilled exclusively from lees-free wine into cecillus oak.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one defining technical commitment that no rival has replicated: distilling exclusively from clear, lees-free wine — a deliberate stripping of the base distillate so that every aromatic dimension must be earned in wood rather than carried from the fermentation vessel. That choice makes the terroir source non-negotiable. The Borderies cru — the smallest of Cognac's six crus, planted on silica-clay soils — is where Martell concentrates its identity, purchasing 50–60% of the entire cru's annual output. Those silica-clay soils yield eaux-de-vie with a distinctively floral, candied-fruit register that chalk-dominant Grande Champagne cannot replicate. Distillation runs through copper alambic Charentais pot stills at the Gallienne Distillery — the largest première chauffe stills in Cognac at ~130 hectolitres — with gas pressure adjusted hourly along the proprietary 'Martell Curve' and tails cut made deliberately early for a clean, neutral heart. Aging proceeds exclusively in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted at minimum 17–20 minutes bousinage, in a rotation of new and older 'red' barrels. The special feature here is structural: Martell's lees-free distillation protocol is unique among the major houses, producing a lighter spirit whose character is almost entirely a function of oak and cru rather than distillate weight. As an entry-level expression, this draws on the Borderies signature at its most accessible — floral and fruit-forward, with cecillus oak lending vanilla and gentle spice without obscuring the cru's candied character.

name: Glenfiddich  12 Years Old (700 ml) (With Box)
short_description (EN Store): Dufftown's Robbie Dhu spring water, swan-neck pot stills, and a 6-month oak-tun marriage define Speyside's most category-defining 12-year-old.
description (EN Store): In 1963, Glenfiddich became the first Scotch distillery to actively export single malt as a premium product in its own right — a commercial transgression that effectively created the modern single malt category. That founding confidence is embedded in the 12 Year Old's production logic. The Robbie Dhu Spring, a mineral-rich highland source in the Conval Hills above Dufftown, supplies every stage from mashing through reduction to bottling strength, and the Grant family purchased the surrounding land specifically to protect this catchment. The valley microclimate and stable earthen-floored dunnage warehouses moderate temperature swings across the minimum 12-year maturation. Cask split runs approximately 85% ex-bourbon American white oak to 15% ex-Oloroso sherry European oak, the bourbon component driving the distillery's signature green pear and candied apple character while the sherry fraction contributes dried fruit depth and colour. Distillation passes through distinctively small, swan-neck copper pot stills — wash stills of roughly 9,000 L and spirit stills of roughly 4,500 L — whose narrow necks enforce high copper contact and maximum reflux, producing an unusually light, clean new make for Speyside. A mandatory 6-month marriage in 2,000-litre oak tuns follows cask selection, integrating the two wood streams before bottling at 40% ABV. What separates this from peers is that integration step: most Speyside distilleries bottle directly from individual casks, whereas Glenfiddich's vatting protocol is non-negotiable across every expression in the range.

name: Glenfiddich  18 Years Old (700 ml) (With Box)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside 18YO from Dufftown's Robbie Dhu spring — 18–20% Oloroso sherry cask component, married in oak tuns before bottling at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): William Grant built Glenfiddich's first still by hand in 1886, and the distillery has remained privately held through five generations of the Grant-Gordon family — a continuity that explains why the 18 Year Old's production logic has never been outsourced or industrialised. Distilled at Dufftown in the Glen of the River Fiddich, the spirit draws exclusively on the Robbie Dhu Spring, a mineral-rich highland source in the Conval Hills whose catchment the family protects across a substantial land holding. The valley's stable microclimate feeds into stone-walled dunnage warehouses with earthen floors, where temperature variance is minimal and maturation proceeds slowly. The 18 Year Old's cask regime is the most sherry-forward in the core range: approximately 18–20% ex-Oloroso sherry European oak is married with the predominant ex-bourbon American white oak component over a minimum of 18 years, then the vatted spirit rests in large oak marrying tuns — a mandatory integration step applied to every Glenfiddich expression before bottling. The result is driven by the distillery's signature small swan-neck copper pot stills (pear-shaped wash stills of ~9,000 L, spirit stills of ~4,500 L), whose geometry forces high copper contact and produces a characteristically light, orchard-fruit new make that the sherry wood then darkens and deepens without overwhelming. What sets this expression apart within the Speyside category is the on-site end-to-end production: distilled, matured, and bottled at Dufftown, with Robbie Dhu water used for both fermentation and reduction to 40% ABV. As a non-vintage age-statement release, consistency across batches is achieved through the marrying step rather than single-cask selection.

name: The Balvenie  DoubleWood 12 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The whisky that invented cask finishing: ~11 yrs ex-bourbon then 9 months first-fill Oloroso sherry, bottled at 40% from Dufftown's Robbie Dhu spring water.
description (EN Store): In 1993, Malt Master David Stewart MBE commercialised a technique he had been quietly developing for a decade — transferring mature whisky into a second cask type — and the DoubleWood 12 became the vehicle that introduced cask finishing to the global Scotch industry. Every major distillery now uses the method; Balvenie did it first. The distillery sits at the foot of the Conval Hills in Dufftown, Speyside, drawing on the Robbie Dhu Springs — soft Highland spring water whose low mineral load preserves the cask-derived sweetness that defines the house character. Barley is partly grown on the distillery's own Balvenie Mains farm and lightly peated to ~4 ppm on one of only seven surviving traditional malting floors in Scotland, adding a subtle malty depth most Speyside peers cannot replicate. The liquid spends approximately 11 years in ex-bourbon American oak before a 9-month transfer into first-fill Oloroso sherry butts — long enough to layer dried fruit and spice without erasing the bourbon-oak vanilla foundation. Fermentation in Douglas fir wooden washbacks (64–68 hours) contributes a creamy lactic complexity that carries through both cask stages. What makes this expression categorically unusual is its origin: it is not an imitation of a technique but the technique's commercial debut, unchanged in structure since 1993. Chill-filtered and bottled at 40% ABV using Robbie Dhu water; a consistent, non-vintage age-statement release.

name: The Balvenie  Portwood 21 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 21 years ex-bourbon maturation, ~4 months Tawny Port pipe finish — David Stewart's own finishing technique turned on its most patient expression.
description (EN Store):
When Malt Master David Stewart MBE transferred a mature whisky into a second cask type at Balvenie in the late 1980s, he invented a technique now practised by virtually every Scotch distillery on earth. The PortWood 21 is the fullest expression of that invention: 21 years in ex-bourbon American oak barrels, followed by a ~4-month transfer into Tawny Port pipes — a finishing window deliberately kept short to layer dried-fruit complexity without overwhelming the decades of bourbon-oak honeyed structure beneath.


The spirit originates in Dufftown, at the foot of the Conval Hills, drawn from the Robbie Dhu Springs — soft Highland water whose low mineral load preserves the distillate's natural sweetness through maturation. Fermentation in Douglas fir wooden washbacks (9 of 14) encourages lactic bacterial activity, building the creamy, rounded house character that survives two decades in oak. Distillation runs through taller-than-average copper pot stills maintained by on-site coppersmiths, favouring lighter, ester-rich new make that accepts long aging without hardening.


What no peer does at this scale: Balvenie floor-malts a portion of its own barley on a 1928 malting floor — one of roughly seven still operating in Scotland — introducing a trace of Speyside-peat malt character (~4 ppm) that threads through even this 21-year expression as a subtle, biscuity depth. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, the PortWood 21 is a non-vintage age-statement release; drinking window is open now and will hold comfortably for a decade in bottle.

name: Chivas Regal  12 Years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-anchored blend from Scotland's oldest Highland distillery; short-necked pot stills and wooden washbacks shape a malt-forward, honey-driven Speyside character.
description (EN Store): When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909 as a 25-year-old blend, no competitor carried an age statement at all — a deliberate act of category invention that established the luxury blended Scotch template still followed today. The heart malt is Strathisla, distilled at Keith, Moray, in continuous operation since 1786, drawing process water exclusively from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite bedrock roughly one mile from the stillhouse. That mineral-clean water, combined with traditional wooden washbacks providing thermal stability during the approximately two-day fermentation, builds a new make of notable weight and texture. Strathisla's short-necked wash stills deliberately allow heavier vapours to ascend, producing a full-bodied, generously fruited spirit; heart cut is collected at 65–70% ABV. Component whiskies rest in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Oloroso sherry European oak, with the 12-year statement marking the youngest whisky present — older parcels from Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Miltonduff, Braeval, and Allt-a-Bhainne contribute without declaration. What separates this expression from grain-heavy peers is its malt-centric architecture and the near-total absence of peat influence — a house philosophy of smoothness-over-smoke held since 1909. Bottled at 40% ABV, the 12-year-old drinks with orchard fruit, heather honey, hazelnut, and vanilla cream; approachable now, with no meaningful cellaring upside on an NV blend.

name: Chivas Regal  18 Years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-anchored blend of 20+ malts, aged minimum 18 years in ex-bourbon & Oloroso sherry oak — Speyside's richest house style without a trace of peat.
description (EN Store): When Chivas Regal launched in 1909 as the world's first luxury blended Scotch carrying an age statement, the founding philosophy was unambiguous: higher malt content, longer maturation, and a deliberate rejection of smoke in favour of orchard richness. The 18-year-old expression holds to that original contract. Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Speyside — founded 1786 and the oldest continuously operating Highland distillery — anchors the blend. Its short-necked pot stills allow heavier vapours to ascend, producing a full-bodied new make drawn from Broomhill Spring water, naturally filtered through granite. Over 20 single malts contribute to Chivas 18, including Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Braeval, and Miltonduff, alongside Strathclyde grain from the Lowlands — all carrying a minimum 18 years in American oak ex-bourbon and European oak ex-Oloroso sherry casks. Master Blender Sandy Hyslop has confirmed that longer maturations in this range favour second- and third-fill casks, deliberately moderating wood extraction to let the malt character lead. What sets Chivas 18 apart within blended Scotch is the sheer breadth of its malt roster at this age tier: where most 18-year blends rely on three to five malts, this expression assembles more than twenty, each contributing a distinct Speyside or Highland register. Bottled at 40% ABV, the current release drinks with the layered density of a long-aged blend rather than the grain-forward lightness typical of the category.

name: Chivas Regal  12 years (4.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-anchored Speyside blend from Scotland's oldest Highland distillery, matured min. 12 years in ex-bourbon & Oloroso sherry oak. 4.5 L format.
description (EN Store): When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909 as a 25-year-old blend, no competitor carried an age statement at all — the category of luxury blended Scotch was, in effect, invented at that moment. The heart malt of every expression remains Strathisla, distilled at Keith, Speyside, in continuous operation since 1786, drawing process water exclusively from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite one mile from the stillhouse. Strathisla's short-necked pot stills deliberately allow heavier vapours to ascend, producing a full-bodied, glyceric new make that anchors the blend's characteristic density. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks — not stainless steel — providing thermal insulation for a steady, approximately two-day fermentation before double distillation with the heart cut collected at 65–70% ABV. Component whiskies are matured in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Oloroso sherry European oak casks, with the 12-year statement marking the youngest whisky present — older components from Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Miltonduff, Braeval, and Allt-a-Bhainne are blended in without declaration. The 4.5 L format delivers the same 40% ABV blend as the standard bottle, making it the rare large-format entry point into a lineage that reaches to a 25-year-old. Drink now through indefinite shelf life sealed.

name: Chivas Regal  25 Years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): The original 1909 luxury blend, rebuilt at 25 years: Strathisla's short-neck pot stills, Broomhill Spring granite-filtered water, 20+ malts, ex-Oloroso and ex-bourbon oak.
description (EN Store): When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909, it carried a 25-year age statement — at a time when no other blended Scotch made such a claim, effectively inventing the premium blended category from scratch. That founding expression is the direct ancestor of what sits in this bottle today. Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Speyside — the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands, founded 1786 — anchors the blend as its heart malt. Process water is drawn exclusively from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite approximately one mile from the distillery, contributing a mineral softness to the base spirit. Strathisla's short-necked pot stills deliberately allow heavier vapours to rise, producing a full-bodied, glycerol-rich new make that carries weight through decades of maturation. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks for thermal stability before double distillation, with heart cut collected at 65–70% ABV. The 25-year blend draws on over 20 single malts — including Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Braeval, Miltonduff, and Allt-a-Bhainne — matured across ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Oloroso sherry European oak casks, with Master Blender Sandy Hyslop favouring second- and third-fill casks at this maturation length for integration over extraction. The age statement marks only the youngest component; older whiskies are present but unlabelled. Dried orchard fruit, beeswax, and dark toffee dominate, with the sherry oak lending walnut and dried fig depth; the drinking window is open now and will hold comfortably for years in bottle.

name: Jack Daniel's  Old No.7 (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cave Spring Hollow limestone water + 10-ft sugar maple charcoal mellowing: the single step that legally separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon.
description (EN Store):
The technique at the core of Old No. 7 was first practiced by Nathan 'Nearest' Green, an enslaved man who served as the distillery's original Master Distiller — a contribution formally erased for over a century and only reinstated in the 2010s. That technique, the Lincoln County Process, remains the defining act: new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days before any barrel contact, stripping harsh congeners in a way no standard bourbon undergoes.

The distillery occupies Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Moore County, anchored by a limestone cave spring locked at a constant 56°F (13°C). Limestone filtration removes iron — a known flavor contaminant — while contributing calcium and magnesium to the iron-free water used throughout production.

The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill ferments in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash process (up to 30% backset) for approximately six days, then distilled in a 100-foot copper column still with doubler. Spirit enters 100% new charred American white oak at 125 proof; multi-storey rickhouses exploit Tennessee's seasonal temperature swings across 7–8 floors. Old No. 7 is blended from 175–200 barrels per batch, mingling upper-floor intensity with lower-floor subtlety, then bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered.

The singular transgression: Old No. 7 meets every U.S. federal definition of straight bourbon yet deliberately refuses that label — the Lincoln County Process is the single legal and stylistic dividing line under Tennessee HB 1084 (2013).

name: Jack Daniel's  Old No.7 (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Cave Spring Hollow limestone water + 10-ft sugar maple charcoal vats: the Lincoln County Process that legally separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon.
description (EN Store):
The technique at the core of Old No. 7 was originally taught to Jack Daniel by Nathan 'Nearest' Green, an enslaved man who served as the distillery's first Master Distiller — a contribution erased from official histories for over a century and only formally recognized in the 2010s. That technique, the Lincoln County Process, remains the defining act: before any barrel contact, new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof via a 100-foot copper column still is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days, stripping harsh congeners and depositing a velvety texture no standard bourbon undergoes.

The distillery sits in Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, anchored by a limestone cave spring at a constant 56°F (13°C) — limestone filtration removes iron (detrimental to whiskey flavor) while contributing calcium and magnesium. Multi-storey rickhouses expose barrels to Tennessee's full seasonal temperature swings, driving aggressive wood interaction on upper floors, slower extraction below. Old No. 7 is blended from 175–200 barrels per batch, deliberately mingling upper- and lower-floor casks to balance intensity with restraint. The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill ferments in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash backset before reduction to 125 proof for barrel entry into 100% new charred American white oak. The special feature: this whiskey meets every federal legal definition of straight bourbon yet deliberately refuses the label — the charcoal mellowing step is the single act that makes it something else entirely.

name: Johnnie Walker  Black Label (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): ~40 malt & grain whiskies, all 12+ years, anchored by Cardhu sweetness, Caol Ila peat, and Clynelish wax — blended at Leven, Fife.
description (EN Store): When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his tea-blending instinct — that combining sources across regions eliminates batch variation — became the founding logic of modern blended Scotch. Black Label is where that logic reaches its most deliberate expression. Approximately 40 malt and grain whiskies, every one aged a minimum of 12 years, are drawn from distilleries spanning Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland Scotland. The architecture is tripartite: Cardhu (Speyside) supplies honeyed, fruit-forward sweetness as the structural heart; Caol Ila (Islay) threads clean maritime peat smoke through the mid-palate; Clynelish (Highland) contributes a distinctive coastal wax that no Speyside-only blend can replicate. Maturation runs in ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, with the precise split undisclosed — the cask selection is made cask-by-cask by the blending team, currently led by Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender role in the brand's 200-year history. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered. What no single-region producer can achieve — simultaneous smoke, coastal wax, and orchard-fruit sweetness in one coherent, batch-stable liquid — is precisely what Black Label is engineered to deliver.

name: Famous Grouse Finest (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Highland blended Scotch with honeyed warmth, vanilla oak, and subtle smoke—approachable daily dram.
description (EN Store): Famous Grouse Finest is a blended Scotch whisky from the Highland region, crafted for accessible everyday enjoyment. The palate opens with honeyed sweetness and vanilla oak character, supported by gentle spice and dried fruit notes. A subtle smoke finish and hazelnut undertone provide complexity without demanding intensity. Medium body and balanced structure make this a versatile pour for both neat sipping and mixing.

name: Canadian Club (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth Canadian whisky with vanilla oak and subtle spice; approachable everyday dram.
description (EN Store): Canadian Club is a blended Canadian whisky offering straightforward character anchored by vanilla and oak influence from maturation. The spirit presents a medium-full body with balanced sweetness and gentle spice notes, delivering a smooth, accessible profile suited to casual enjoyment, mixing, or pairing with light appetizers and comfort-food settings.

name: Glenmorangie  The Lasanta (Sherry Wood)-12 Years  (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tain Highland malt finished in Oloroso/PX sherry butts — Scotland's tallest stills meet Jerez oak for a 12-year extra maturation.
description (EN Store):
When William Matheson converted Morangie Farm's brewery into a distillery in 1843, he sourced second-hand London gin stills — the same towering geometry, now standing at 5.14 m, that defines Glenmorangie's house character to this day. Those extreme heights enforce intense copper reflux, stripping heavier congeners and producing one of Scotland's lightest, most floral new-make spirits — a structural baseline that makes the Lasanta's sherry finishing all the more deliberate in contrast.

Water for mashing rises from the Tarlogie Springs in the Tarlogie Hills above Tain, filtering through limestone and sandstone for up to a century before emerging hard and mineral-rich — atypical for Scotland and a quiet contributor to the spirit's clean, precise character. The coastal microclimate of the Dornoch Firth moderates temperature swings across the on-site dunnage and racked warehouses.

The Lasanta begins in Glenmorangie's own Ozark Mountain ex-bourbon casks (Missouri white oak, air-dried two years, bourbon-seasoned four years) before a secondary extra maturation in Oloroso and PX sherry butts — the finishing philosophy the distillery pioneered commercially in the early 1990s. Bottled at 43% ABV after 12 years total. The tight heart cut (74% down to 60% ABV) excludes feinty notes that would muddy the sherry integration.

What no peer replicates: the combination of Scotland's tallest stills with a proprietary cask supply chain — from Ozark forest to Jerez butt — gives the Lasanta a sherry-influenced profile built on an unusually delicate, low-congener base spirit, inverting the typical logic of heavy-still, sherry-forward Highland malts.

No vintage variation applies to this NV expression; the drinking window is open now, with the PX sweetness and dried-fruit density best appreciated at room temperature or with a single drop of water.

name: Glenmorangie  The Quinta Ruban (Port Wood) - 14 Years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 14-year Highland malt finished in Ruby Port pipes — Scotland's tallest stills (5.14 m) build the delicate base that port wood then deepens.
description (EN Store): When William Matheson converted Morangie Farm into a distillery in 1843, he acquired second-hand London gin stills — the tallest in Scotland at 5.14 metres — and inadvertently engineered a spirit so light and floral that it would one day become the ideal canvas for cask finishing. The Quinta Ruban is the direct expression of that logic. Tain sits on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth, Ross-shire, where a coastal microclimate moderates temperature swings and the Tarlogie Springs — rainwater filtered for up to a century through limestone and sandstone in the Tarlogie Hills — deliver hard, mineral-rich water that is structurally unlike the soft water of most Speyside distilleries. Primary maturation proceeds in Glenmorangie's proprietary American white oak ex-bourbon casks, sourced from the distillery's own Ozark Mountain forests in Missouri: staves air-dried two years, then bourbon-seasoned for four years before a single litre of spirit enters them. After 14 years, the whisky transfers into Ruby Port pipes for its secondary 'Extra Maturation' finish — a technique Glenmorangie pioneered commercially in the early 1990s. The extreme reflux enforced by those 5.14 m spirit stills keeps the base spirit lean enough that the port wood adds dark fruit and cocoa depth without overwhelming the distillery's signature citrus and peach register. No other Highland distillery combines this still height with this level of proprietary cask control. Bottled at 43% ABV; approachable now, with structure to develop further over 3–5 years.

name: Glenmorangie  The Nectar d'Or (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Glenmorangie's Sauternes-finished Highland malt: Scotland's tallest stills meet Bordeaux dessert-wine casks for a rare sweet-acid counterpoint.
description (EN Store): When William Matheson converted Morangie Farm into a distillery in 1843 using second-hand London gin stills, he inadvertently set the structural blueprint for one of Scotland's most delicate spirits — those repurposed stills, standing 5.14 m tall, remain the tallest in Scotland and enforce extreme copper reflux, stripping out heavy congeners before a single drop reaches the cask. The Nectar d'Or takes that inherently light, floral new-make and subjects it to a secondary finish in Sauternes casks sourced from Bordeaux's premier dessert-wine châteaux — a finishing regime unique within the Glenmorangie range and rare across Highland malts. Primary maturation proceeds in ex-bourbon American white oak from Glenmorangie's own Ozark Mountain forests in Missouri: staves air-dried two years, then bourbon-seasoned for four years before receiving spirit, yielding a baseline of citrus, peach, and vanilla. The Sauternes finish layers botrytised stone-fruit richness and honeyed acidity over that foundation — a structural counterpoint that no ex-sherry or port finish replicates. Water drawn from the Tarlogie Springs, filtered through limestone and sandstone for up to a century, contributes the mineral hardness that keeps the spirit from collapsing into sweetness. Bottled at 46% ABV, the Nectar d'Or carries enough weight to integrate the finish without masking the distillery's signature floral register. Drink now or hold 5–8 years.

name: Glenmorangie  18 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tain's tallest stills (5.14 m) meet 18 years in Ozark-grown ex-bourbon oak — Highland single malt at its most architecturally precise.
description (EN Store):
When William Matheson converted Morangie Farm's brewery into a distillery in 1843, he acquired second-hand London gin stills — and their extraordinary height became Glenmorangie's defining structural signature. At 5.14 m, the spirit stills remain Scotland's tallest, enforcing such intense copper reflux that heavy congeners never reach the condenser, producing one of the lightest new-make spirits in the Highlands.

The distillery sits on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth in Tain, Ross-shire, drawing water from Tarlogie Springs — rainwater filtered through limestone and sandstone for up to 100 years, yielding hard, mineral-rich water that stands in sharp contrast to the soft water of most Speyside peers. Glenmorangie owns ~650 acres of the Tarlogie catchment specifically to protect this source.

The 18 Year Old matures in ex-bourbon American white oak casks sourced from Glenmorangie's proprietary Ozark Mountain forests in Missouri: staves air-dried two years, then bourbon-seasoned for four years before a single drop of spirit enters. The heart cut runs from 74% ABV down to 60% ABV — a tight window that excludes feinty notes entirely. Bottled at 43% ABV.

What no peer replicates: Glenmorangie controls wood grain density, air-drying, and bourbon seasoning from forest to cask — a supply-chain depth virtually unmatched in Scotch production, and the reason 18 years in these barrels reads as precision rather than weight.

Extended maturation amplifies the distillery's citrus and stone-fruit character while Ozark oak contributes deepening vanilla and dried-orchard layers; drink now through the next decade.

name: Benchmark Bourbon Whisky (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Approachable Kentucky bourbon with vanilla oak and subtle spice—a versatile everyday sipper.
description (EN Store): Benchmark Bourbon Whisky represents a straightforward expression of Kentucky bourbon craftsmanship. The spirit opens with warm vanilla and caramel sweetness from barrel maturation, followed by supporting notes of oak and hazelnut. Black pepper and subtle spice emerge on the palate, leading to a smooth, medium-full finish. This is a reliable, unpretentious bourbon suited to neat sipping, cocktails, or pairing with grilled meats and casual fare.

name: Fireball  Cinnamon Whisky (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Canadian cinnamon whisky with bold spice, vanilla sweetness, and smooth oak—a versatile spirit for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Fireball Cinnamon Whisky is a Canadian whisky infused with cinnamon spice and natural sweetness. The spirit opens with pronounced cinnamon warmth balanced by vanilla and caramel notes from oak aging, delivering a smooth, approachable character. Designed for both neat sipping and cocktail mixing, it offers a distinctive flavor profile that appeals to those seeking bold spice and accessible drinkability.

name: Arran  10 Year Single Malt Whisky  (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Unpeated island malt from granite-filtered Loch na Davie water, non-chill filtered at 46% ABV — Arran's rule-breaking answer to smoky island convention.
description (EN Store):
When Harold Currie, former Managing Director of Chivas Brothers, opened Lochranza Distillery in 1995, he made a deliberate transgression: on an island where maritime peat had defined whisky identity for generations, he built an entirely unpeated distillery — reviving legal distillation on Arran after a 160-year absence on his own terms.

The distillery sits in Glen Eason Biorach ('Valley of the Sharp Waterfalls') on the granite-dominant northern tip of the island, above the Highland Boundary Fault. Water descends from Loch na Davie through six waterfalls along the Easan Biorach burn, purified by granite and softened by trace peat — delivering a mineral-clean, soft charge water that underpins the distillery's characteristically bright, fruit-forward new make. The Gulf Stream microclimate accelerates maturation in on-site dunnage and racked warehouses.

Fermentation runs 60–75 hours in six Oregon pine washbacks using Kerry M yeast — the extended window deliberately targeted to develop fruity congeners. Double distillation passes through broad-pot wash stills (7,100 L) with tall necks and long lyne arms into bulb-shaped spirit stills (4,300–4,800 L), maximising copper contact and producing a clean, creamy new make collected at ~68% ABV. The 10 Year matures primarily in ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry European oak, then bottles at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, no caramel colouring — a standard Arran holds across virtually its entire core range.

The special feature here is the combination: a fully unpeated Islands malt bottled without filtration or colouring at a strength most peers reserve for premium expressions, at an entry-level age statement price point.

name: Highland Park  12 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Orkney's Hobbister Moor heather-peat — the only such source on earth — meets Oloroso-seasoned oak in a 12-year island single malt bottled at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store):
Highland Park's origin traces to illicit distilling on Holm Road, Kirkwall — a hillside site on Mainland Orkney exposed to both North Sea and Atlantic airstreams, where cool, temperate consistency slows maturation and concentrates character over time. The distillery draws from a hard-mineral spring and sits within reach of Hobbister Moor, roughly seven miles southwest, where hand-cut peat contains no tree roots — only heather, fern, and shallow organic matter — kilned to ~20 ppm phenol, producing smoke that is floral and aromatic rather than medicinal or tarry.

Approximately 20% of the annual malt requirement is floor-malted on-site using traditional hand-turning, then kilned with that Hobbister heather-peat; the remaining 80% is unpeated mainland malt. Fermentation runs 50–80 hours in 12 Oregon pine washbacks, and double distillation through low-reflux, pear-shaped stills yields a medium-heavy new make. Maturation proceeds in Oloroso-seasoned European and American oak — first-fill and refill — across 19 earthen-floored dunnage warehouses on-site, where Orkney's stable maritime cold works against excessive angel's share.

What separates this 12 Year Old from any peer is geographic exclusivity: no other distillery on earth has access to Orkney heather-peat, and trials with mainland peated malt produced a measurably different spirit — confirming the moor itself as an irreplaceable ingredient. Bottled at 40% ABV, the 12 YO represents the entry point to a house style that Michael Jackson once called the greatest all-rounder in malt whisky.

name: The Balvenie  Caribbean Cask 14 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 14 years ex-bourbon at Dufftown, finished 6–9 months in in-house rum-seasoned casks — David Stewart's finishing technique applied to Caribbean oak.
description (EN Store): When Malt Master David Stewart MBE invented cask finishing at Balvenie in the 1980s–90s, he established a principle the industry has since universally adopted — and the Caribbean Cask 14 is among its most direct expressions. Fourteen years of maturation in ex-bourbon American oak barrels builds the distillery's signature honeyed, rounded character before the spirit transfers into Caribbean rum casks seasoned in-house for a further 6–9 months, a finishing window long enough to impart tropical sweetness without erasing the bourbon-oak foundation. The whisky is drawn from Dufftown's Robbie Dhu Springs — soft Highland water that William Grant & Sons purchased surrounding land to protect — and distilled on copper pot stills taller than those at neighbouring Glenfiddich, a design choice that drives lighter, more aromatic new make. Barley is partly grown on the distillery's own Balvenie Mains farm and partly floor-malted on the 1928 malting floor, one of only ~7 still operating in Scotland, contributing a subtle malty depth at approximately 4 ppm. What sets this expression apart within the finishing category is the in-house rum cask seasoning programme: rather than sourcing pre-used rum barrels, Balvenie controls the seasoning itself, determining exactly how much rum character each cask imparts. Bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered, with Robbie Dhu spring water used for dilution to proof.

name: Jack Daniel's  Gentleman Jack Rare Tennessee Whiskey (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Double charcoal-mellowed at Cave Spring Hollow: Gentleman Jack passes through sugar maple charcoal twice — before and after aging — a step no standard Tennessee Whiskey takes.
description (EN Store):
The contribution of Nathan 'Nearest' Green — the enslaved man who taught Jack Daniel the Lincoln County Process and served as the distillery's first Master Distiller — underpins every drop produced at Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Moore County. Gentleman Jack takes that founding technique further than any other expression in the core range: where Old No. 7 is charcoal-mellowed once before barrel entry, Gentleman Jack undergoes a second full pass through 10-foot sugar maple charcoal vats after aging, stripping the post-barrel spirit of residual wood tannin sharpness before bottling at 40% ABV.

The distillery's limestone cave spring — a constant 56°F (13°C), iron-free — supplies water that carries calcium and magnesium without the metallic interference that iron introduces to fermentation. New-make is distilled to 140 proof on a 100-foot copper column still with doubler, reducing sulfur compounds via copper contact, then entered into 100% new charred American white oak barrels at 125 proof across multi-storey rickhouses where Tennessee's seasonal temperature swings drive active wood extraction. The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill and sour mash backset fermentation establish the sweet-forward caramel and banana-bread baseline. The double mellowing is the rule-break: no other Tennessee Whiskey expression in the standard market applies post-aging charcoal filtration at this scale, producing a texture measurably softer than the single-mellowed flagship.

name: Jack Daniel's  Single Barrel Tennessee Whiskey (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single barrel from the 8th-floor of Lynchburg's Cave Spring Hollow rickhouse — hand-selected, bottled at 47% ABV, Lincoln County Process mellowed.
description (EN Store):
The contribution of Nathan 'Nearest' Green — the enslaved man who taught Jack Daniel the Lincoln County Process and served as the distillery's first Master Distiller — is the technical and moral foundation of every drop produced at Cave Spring Hollow. That process, pre-barrel gravity filtration through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days, is what legally and stylistically separates this from bourbon under Tennessee's 2013 HB 1084.

The distillery sits anchored to a limestone cave spring maintaining a constant 56°F (13°C), delivering iron-free water — iron being actively detrimental to whiskey flavor — while contributing calcium and magnesium. Tennessee's pronounced seasonal temperature swings drive aggressive wood interaction across the multi-storey rickhouses.

Single Barrel Select breaks from the flagship's blending philosophy entirely: each bottle is drawn from a single, individually hand-selected barrel on the 8th (top) floor of the rickhouse — the most thermally active position, producing the most wood-forward, concentrated character in the house. Bottled at 47% ABV (94 proof), unchill-filtered relative to Old No. 7's 40% standard, and drawn from an 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill fermented in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash backset for pH control.

The top-floor single-barrel selection is the transgression here: where Old No. 7 deliberately mingles 175–200 barrels across floors to neutralize extremes, Single Barrel Select amplifies them. Barrel-to-barrel variation is real and intentional — caramel and toasted oak deepen noticeably, and the higher proof preserves aromatic intensity the standard expression sacrifices for approachability.

name: Jack Daniel's  Honey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tennessee Whiskey liqueur from Cave Spring Hollow: Lincoln County charcoal-mellowed spirit blended with natural honey, bottled at 35% ABV.
description (EN Store):
Jack Daniel's Honey begins where Old No. 7 ends — with new-make spirit gravity-fed through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days at Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Tennessee. That Lincoln County Process mellowing, originally taught to Jack Daniel by Nathan 'Nearest' Green, strips harsh congeners before a single day of barrel contact, producing the velvety base that makes this expression work as a liqueur rather than a cloying novelty.

The distillery's water source — a limestone cave spring locked at 56°F year-round — filters iron from the water while contributing calcium and magnesium, giving the spirit a clean, mineral-neutral canvas. The underlying Tennessee Whiskey is built on an 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill, fermented in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash backset, then distilled through a 100-foot copper column still to approximately 140 proof before charcoal mellowing and barrel entry at 125 proof in new charred American white oak.

What separates this from standard honey liqueurs is the structural integrity of the base spirit: the Lincoln County Process delivers a smoothness that allows the honey to integrate rather than dominate, preserving the distillery's signature caramel and toasted-oak backbone. Bottled at 35% ABV, it sits lower than Old No. 7's 40%, making it a deliberate departure from the flagship's proof point without sacrificing the Tennessee Whiskey character beneath.

name: Woodford Reserve (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Kentucky bourbon with rich vanilla, oak, and spice—a balanced, full-bodied classic for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Woodford Reserve is a premium Kentucky straight bourbon crafted with a distinctive high-rye mash bill and aged in new charred oak barrels. The spirit opens with warm vanilla and caramel notes, followed by layers of oak, spice, and dark fruit. Mid-palate reveals tobacco and leather complexity, while the finish lingers with cocoa and subtle smoke. Full-bodied and smooth, it exemplifies the refined character of American bourbon tradition.

name: Loch Lomond  Reserve Blended Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Self-sufficient Highland blend from Alexandria's multi-still campus — malt and grain distilled under one roof on the Highland Boundary Fault.
description (EN Store): When Duncan Thomas installed straight-neck pot stills fitted with fixed copper rectification plates at Loch Lomond in the 1960s — a design he had pioneered at Littlemill and that no other distillery has ever replicated — he gave the Alexandria campus the ability to produce radically different spirit streams from a single address. That technical self-sufficiency is the foundation of the Reserve Blended Scotch: both the malt and grain components are distilled on-site, an arrangement unique in Scotland. The distillery sits on the Highland Boundary Fault in Dunbartonshire, drawing water from Loch Lomond, and its former Victorian foundry buildings create varied maturation microclimates across the warehousing estate. Fermentation runs a minimum of 92 hours — extending to 160 hours in some washback cycles, roughly double the Speyside norm, building long-chain esters that carry through to the blend's fruit character. Primary maturation is in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels, the cooperage re-charring up to 20,000 casks annually on-site. The special feature here is structural: no other blended Scotch can claim every drop — malt and grain alike — was produced at one address. Approachable and fruit-forward, the Reserve is best consumed now through the near term.

name: Auchentoshan  Single Malt Scotch Three Wood (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lowland single malt finished in three wood types for layered vanilla, dried fruit, and smooth spice complexity.
description (EN Store): Auchentoshan Three Wood is a Lowland single malt Scotch whisky matured across three distinct cask types, creating a harmonious blend of vanilla oak, dried fruit, and subtle spice. The triple-wood finishing process imparts layers of complexity: honeyed sweetness and toasted wood character from the primary maturation, followed by creamy oak influence and gentle smoke in the mid-palate. A smooth, approachable dram with balanced warmth and refined character, ideal for both casual enjoyment and contemplative sipping.

name: Auchentoshan  Single Malt Scotch Whisky 12 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lowland single malt with bright citrus, vanilla, and orchard fruit—elegant and approachable.
description (EN Store): Auchentoshan 12 Year Old is a Lowland single malt Scotch whisky known for its light, elegant character and triple-distillation heritage. The spirit opens with bright citrus zest and orchard fruit, followed by soft vanilla and honey notes from maturation in ex-bourbon casks. A gentle spice and creamy mouthfeel round out the profile, making this whisky an accessible yet refined choice for both newcomers and experienced drinkers. Best enjoyed neat, with a splash of water, or as a sophisticated aperitif.

name: Johnnie Walker  Green Label (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Grain-free blended malt from Talisker, Caol Ila, Cragganmore & Linkwood — all minimum 15 years in oak, bottled at 43% ABV.
description (EN Store): Green Label occupies a structurally distinct category within the Johnnie Walker range: it contains no grain whisky whatsoever, making it a blended malt — a format that demands each component carry its own weight without the softening scaffold of column-still grain spirit. The four malt contributors are Talisker (Isle of Skye, volcanic basalt coastline, intense maritime smoke and black pepper spice), Caol Ila (Islay, clean medicinal peat and brine), Cragganmore (Speyside, complex malt fragrance and dried fruit), and Linkwood (Speyside, floral grassiness and orchard fruit) — each aged a minimum of 15 years in predominantly ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks before blending. At 43% ABV — higher than both Red and Black Label — the spirit retains more of the distillate's native character, particularly Talisker's volcanic intensity and Caol Ila's coastal iodine. The blend is chill-filtered and reduced to bottling strength at Diageo's Leven, Fife facility. What no peer in the standard blended Scotch category replicates is this grain-free architecture at commercial scale: the absence of grain whisky forces the four malts into direct dialogue, producing a smoke-and-wax tension that grain-inclusive blends structurally cannot achieve. Non-vintage by design; consistent batch-to-batch.

name: Suntory  Hakushu Distillers Reserve Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 708 m Alpine distillery; granite-filtered water, wooden washbacks, lightly peated malt — Hakushu's cool-forest character in an NAS expression.
description (EN Store):
When Suntory established Hakushu in 1973 — deliberately siting it 708 m up in the Southern Japanese Alps rather than near any population centre — the decision was architectural: cool mountain air (averaging ~5°C below Yamazaki) slows ester development and preserves the delicate, green-herbal register that no lowland distillery can replicate. The site sits within an 825,000 m² protected nature reserve on Mt. Kaikomagatake, and water is drawn from aquifers filtered through thousand-year-old granite, arriving soft and mineral-balanced at the mash tun.

Fermentation runs exclusively in wooden washbacks — a deliberate retention of heat in the cold mountain environment — where naturally occurring lactobacillus and resident microflora contribute the lactic, herbal complexity that defines Hakushu's house character. The Distillers Reserve draws from multiple malt types (unpeated to moderately peated) and a varied cask regime including American white oak ex-bourbon and Spanish oak ex-Oloroso sherry, assembled under Suntory's in-house Tsukuriwake philosophy — producing 100+ distinct malt styles without trading a single cask externally.

What separates this expression from age-stated Hakushu is its blender's freedom: no vintage or age constraint means the master blender can select barrels at peak aromatic expression rather than calendar compliance, pulling forward the smoke-and-citrus tension that defines the distillery's identity at accessible proof.

name: Douglas Laing's  Rock Island (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Islands blended malt from Islay, Arran, Jura & Orkney — no colouring, no chill-filtration, bottled at 46% by a third-generation Glasgow independent.
description (EN Store): When Fred Douglas Laing established his Glasgow bottling house in 1948, he built filling contracts with distilleries across every Scottish region — agreements still active today and the foundation upon which Rock Island is assembled. Rock Island is a blended malt drawn exclusively from distilleries on Scotland's Atlantic and North Sea islands: Islay, Arran, Jura, and Orkney — four archipelagos whose coastal peat bogs, maritime air, and salt-laden warehouses leave a collective imprint no mainland malt can replicate. The sea-facing maturation environments accelerate interaction between spirit and wood while depositing a saline mineral character that threads through the entire blend. Bottled at 46% ABV without added colouring or chill-filtration, the natural oils and fatty acid esters that chill-filtration would strip remain intact, giving the spirit a texture and mouthfeel that lower-ABV, filtered blends cannot match. This is the defining commitment of Douglas Laing's 'As Natural As It Gets' philosophy — adopted before it became industry fashion. What distinguishes Rock Island within the Remarkable Regional Malts range is its geographic premise: rather than a single flavour region (Speyside, Highland), it maps an entire maritime arc, yoking Islay's medicinal peat smoke to Orkney's honeyed, lightly peated character and Arran's fruit-forward coastal style into a single coherent expression. Drink now or hold; the non-chill-filtered bottling will continue to develop in bottle.

name: Douglas Laing's  Timorous Beastie (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland blended malt from Glen Garioch, Glengoyne & Blair Athol — non-chill filtered, no colouring, 46.8% ABV from Douglas Laing's Remarkable Regional Malts range.
description (EN Store): Named after Robert Burns's 1785 poem addressing a field mouse, Timorous Beastie is Douglas Laing's structured argument that the Highlands speak in one coherent regional voice — a deliberate counterpoint to single-distillery thinking. The blend draws exclusively from three Highland single malts: Glen Garioch (Aberdeenshire), Glengoyne (Stirlingshire boundary), and Blair Athol (Perthshire), each contributing a distinct topographic register — coastal grain influence from the east, unpeated fruit-driven spirit from the south, and heathered, waxy weight from the central Highlands. This geographic triangulation is the house's method of building terroir into a blended malt. Bottled at 46.8% ABV with no added colouring and no chill-filtration, the natural oils and fatty acid esters remain intact — a non-negotiable standard across the entire Douglas Laing portfolio, adopted long before the industry normalised it. Maturation relies on refill cask stocks selected personally by Fred Laing Jr. and Cara Laing from filling contracts that trace back to the company's 1948 founding. What sets Timorous Beastie apart within the blended malt category is its strict regional exclusivity — every component distillery must sit within the Highland designation, making it one of the few blended malts with a genuinely enforced appellation logic. The NV expression delivers dried heather, beeswax, and orchard fruit with a waxy, full-textured palate that benefits from 10–15 minutes of air.

name: Kavalan  ex-Bourbon Oak Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuan Shan Distillery's subtropical heat (42°C warehouses) compresses maturation — ex-bourbon American oak delivering tropical cream in 4–6 years.
description (EN Store):
When T.T. Lee founded Kavalan in 2005 inside King Car Group's portfolio, the deliberate choice to forgo warehouse air-conditioning was a technical wager: upper-floor casks at Yuan Shan Distillery, Yuanshan Township, Yilan County, routinely reach 42°C in summer, driving wood extraction at a rate that renders four to six years of Taiwanese maturation broadly equivalent to 15–25 years in Scotland. Snow Mountain spring water — naturally filtered through gravel and sand from the Xueshan Range (peak 3,886 m) — forms the distillation base, contributing a characteristic creaminess that runs through every Kavalan expression.

The new-make spirit is built for fruit: a three-stage fermentation cycle in closed stainless-steel washbacks runs approximately 60–72 hours total, with a dedicated 'fruit producer' yeast strain generating ester development before a final lactic round adds textural depth. Double distillation through Forsyths copper pot stills (12,000-litre wash / 7,000-litre spirit) uses a narrowed spirit cut of 65–55% ABV — tightened from the original 78–72% range specifically to increase complexity. The ex-Bourbon Oak expression then rests in American oak ex-bourbon casks, which in Yilan's subtropical microclimate yield pronounced vanilla, coconut, and ripe tropical fruit without the extended timeline demanded by cooler climates.

What separates this from any Scotch bourbon-cask expression is the climate itself: the same wood regime produces a fundamentally different flavour trajectory when ambient warehouse temperatures swing between 24°C and 42°C within a single building.

name: Kavalan Solist Oloroso Sherry Single Cask Strength Single Malt Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-cask Oloroso maturation at Yuanshan's 42°C subtropical warehouses — non-chill-filtered, cask-strength tropical sherry intensity from Taiwan.
description (EN Store):
When Kavalan founder T.T. Lee commissioned the Yuan Shan Distillery in Yuanshan Township, Yilan County in 2005, the operating logic was deliberate transgression: prove that subtropical heat could compress decades of Scottish maturation into years. The Solist Oloroso Sherry is the sharpest expression of that thesis.

The distillery sits at the confluence of cold air descending from the Xueshan (Snow Mountain) Range — peak elevation 3,886 m — and humid Pacific Ocean breezes, with Snow Mountain spring water, filtered through gravel and sand, forming the production base. Upper-floor warehouse temperatures reach 42°C in summer, driving wood extraction at a rate that renders four to six years of Taiwanese maturation broadly equivalent to 15–25 years in Scotland.

The spirit is double-distilled in Forsyths copper pot stills (12,000-litre wash / 7,000-litre spirit) from unpeated malted barley, with the spirit cut narrowed to 65–55% ABV specifically to increase complexity. A multi-stage fermentation — including a dedicated 'fruit producer' yeast phase and a final lactic round across 60–72 hours total — builds the ester architecture that the Oloroso cask then amplifies.

What separates this expression from the Solist range peers is the single-barrel, non-chill-filtered, natural cask-strength bottling (typically 50–59.9% ABV) from a solitary ex-Oloroso sherry cask — no blending, no dilution, no filtration — meaning every textural and aromatic variable traces to one vessel and one subtropical maturation trajectory.

In January 2010, Kavalan beat three Scotch whiskies in a blind tasting on Burns Night in Leith — the credibility event that made expressions like this one internationally legible. In 2026, Kavalan became the first Taiwanese distillery named overall Distiller of the Year at the World Whiskies Awards.

name: Kavalan  Solist Vinho Barrique Single Cask Strength Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-cask, cask-strength Taiwanese malt finished in ex-Vinho Barrique oak — subtropical heat at Yuan Shan compresses decades of maturation into years.
description (EN Store):
When a Kavalan expression defeated three Scotch whiskies in a blind tasting on Burns Night 2010 — held in Leith, Scotland — it forced a reckoning with everything assumed about climate and maturation speed. The Solist Vinho Barrique is the expression that most directly challenges the European wine-cask canon on its own terms: a single barrel, drawn at natural cask strength, finished in ex-Portuguese wine barriques rather than the more familiar sherry or port pipes.

Yuan Shan Distillery sits in Yuanshan Township, Yilan County, where cold air descending from the Xueshan (Snow Mountain) Range at 3,886 m collides with humid Pacific Ocean breezes. Summer warehouse temperatures reach 42°C on upper floors with no air-conditioning — an intentional choice that drives wood extraction at a rate broadly equivalent to 10–15+ years in Scotland within a single Taiwanese maturation cycle. Water is drawn from Snow Mountain springs, filtered through gravel and sand, and contributes a signature creaminess to the new-make.

The spirit is double-distilled in Forsyths copper pot stills (12,000-litre wash stills; 7,000-litre spirit stills), with the cut narrowed to 65–55% ABV to maximise ester complexity. Fermentation runs a three-stage cycle — approximately 60–72 hours total — using a dedicated 'fruit producer' yeast strain for ester development followed by a lactic fermentation round.
