The Solist range is single-cask, non-chill-filtered, bottled at natural cask strength (typically 50–59.9% ABV), meaning every variable of that individual barrel is preserved unmediated.

What distinguishes this expression within the Solist lineup is the ex-Vinho Barrique cask: a Portuguese wine vessel that layers red-fruit tartness and dried grape skin grip over Kavalan's tropical mango-and-papaya base — a combination no Scottish or Irish distillery replicates under equivalent subtropical pressure. Individual cask fill and dump dates appear on each bottle label.

name: Jack Daniel's  Fire (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tennessee Whiskey filtered through 10-ft sugar maple charcoal before barrel entry — then ignited with cinnamon heat. Cave Spring Hollow water, Lincoln County Process.
description (EN Store): Jack Daniel's Tennessee Fire begins where Old No. 7 ends: the base spirit passes through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days — the Lincoln County Process, a pre-barrel filtration step that legally and stylistically separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon under Tennessee's 2013 HB 1084. That mellowing strips harsh congeners before a drop of oak is involved. The distillery sits at Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Moore County, anchored by a limestone cave spring at a constant 56°F (13°C); iron-free water adds calcium and magnesium while protecting the spirit's flavor integrity. The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill ferments in open-top cypress vats via sour mash (up to 30% backset for pH control), then distills through a 100-foot copper column still to approximately 140 proof — copper contact eliminating sulfur compounds. Barrels are new charred American white oak, aged across multi-storey rickhouses where Tennessee's seasonal temperature swings drive active wood interaction. Tennessee Fire's special feature is the layering of a cinnamon liqueur element over a fully Lincoln County Process-mellowed Tennessee Whiskey base — a flavored expression built on a technically rigorous foundation, not a shortcut. Bottled at 35% ABV, it delivers the house caramel-vanilla core amplified by sharp cinnamon heat, with the charcoal mellowing keeping the finish from turning harsh.

name: Jack Daniel's  Old No.7 (3 L)
short_description (EN Store): Cave Spring Hollow limestone water + 10-ft sugar maple charcoal mellowing before barrel — the step that legally separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon.
description (EN Store):
The technique at the core of this whiskey was first practiced by Nathan 'Nearest' Green, an enslaved man who served as the distillery's original Master Distiller — a contribution formally erased from official histories until the 2010s. That method, the Lincoln County Process, remains the defining act: new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof in a 100-foot copper column still is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days before any barrel contact, stripping harsh congeners in a way no bourbon undergoes.

The distillery occupies Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Moore County, where a limestone cave spring delivers iron-free water at a constant 56°F (13°C) year-round — iron being actively detrimental to whiskey flavor. Multi-storey rickhouses expose barrels to Tennessee's full seasonal temperature swings, driving wood interaction across a typical 4–8 year aging cycle in 100% new charred American white oak.

Old No. 7 is a mingle of 175–200 barrels per batch, drawing from multiple rickhouse floors to balance upper-floor wood intensity against slower lower-floor development. The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill is fermented in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash process with up to 30% backset, then bottled at 40% ABV after chill filtration. This 3-litre format delivers the identical liquid at scale — no formula change, no age-statement compromise. Moore County, where this whiskey is made, remains legally dry: it cannot be purchased locally.

name: Southern Comfort  (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth, vanilla-forward American whisky with caramel sweetness and oak influence—approachable and versatile.
description (EN Store): Southern Comfort is a recognizable American whisky brand positioned as an accessible, smooth spirit with pronounced vanilla and caramel character derived from oak maturation. The profile emphasizes approachability and versatility, making it suitable for casual sipping, mixing, or entertaining contexts. Moderate body and low acidity support its role as a straightforward, unpretentious whisky.

name: Suntory  Whisky Kakubin (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Suntory's 1937 square-bottle blended whisky — the original Japanese highball canvas, built on Yamazaki malt and Chita grain.
description (EN Store): When Shinjirō Torii launched Kakubin in 1937, the square (kaku) bottle was a deliberate commercial signal: this was whisky for everyday Japan, not a collector's cabinet. That populist intent shaped a technical brief that has remained consistent — a blended whisky drawing malt distilled at Yamazaki (Shimamoto-cho, Osaka, ~25 m elevation, soft aquifer water historically used by tea master Sen no Rikyū) and grain spirit from Chita (column-distilled at ~94% ABV from a corn-dominant bill on the Aichi Peninsula). Yamazaki's warm, humid microclimate at the confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers accelerates maturation and builds the soft, orchard-fruit character that anchors the blend. The grain component, produced on continuous column stills, contributes a light, clean cereal backbone that keeps the palate accessible. Cask maturation draws from American white oak (ex-bourbon) as the primary vessel, lending vanilla and coconut without the weight of sherry or Mizunara influence reserved for premium tiers. What sets Kakubin apart within the Suntory range is its role as the original proof-of-concept for the Japanese highball culture Suntory itself engineered — a whisky technically calibrated for dilution, where carbonation lifts the cereal grain and the soft-water malt character rather than suppressing it.

name: Chivas Regal  Mizunara (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside blend partly matured in Japanese Mizunara oak — Strathisla's short-necked stills meet an Eastern cask tradition rarely seen outside single malts.
description (EN Store):
When Master Blender Sandy Hyslop chose to introduce Mizunara oak into a blended Scotch, he was breaking a rule the category had held for over a century: that Japanese oak belonged to Japanese whisky. Chivas Regal Mizunara is partly matured in Mizunara casks — a wood so porous and slow-growing that it imparts incense-like sandalwood and coconut lactones at a fraction of the rate of American or European oak, demanding patience from any producer who uses it.

The heart malt, Strathisla, is drawn from the Broomhill Spring, granite-filtered process water in Keith, Speyside. Strathisla's short-necked pot stills suppress copper reflux, allowing heavier, ester-rich vapours to carry through — producing a new make with the density needed to absorb Mizunara's slow-release compounds without being overwhelmed. Wooden washbacks and a roughly two-day fermentation preserve that full-bodied character before distillation.

The blend draws on Strathisla as its core malt alongside Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Braeval, and Strathclyde grain, bottled at 40% ABV. What distinguishes this expression within the Chivas range is the Mizunara finish itself — a cask type almost exclusively associated with Japanese single malts, applied here to a Speyside-anchored blend, producing a profile that sits between the house's characteristic orchard-fruit richness and the resinous, temple-incense register of Kyoto cooperage.

name: Royal Salute  21 years (700 ml) (Blue Wade Decanter)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-vaulted blend where every component — malt and grain — carries a minimum 21-year age statement, bottled in hand-crafted Wade Ceramics porcelain.
description (EN Store):
Royal Salute was born on 2 June 1953, created by Chivas Brothers to mark the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II — a deadline that forced a deliberate decision: no whisky younger than 21 years would enter the blend, a floor that has never been lowered. That standard, applied to both malt and grain components, is the defining transgression of this portfolio; most luxury blends guarantee only the youngest component, not every cask in the vatting.

The malt anchor is Strathisla, the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands (founded 1786), situated on the east bank of the River Isla in Keith, Moray. Process water is drawn from the Fons Bulliens Well, a peat-free natural spring prized for its softness, which carries through to the distillate's notably clean, creamy texture. The Speyside valley microclimate — mild, humid, low-elevation — promotes slow, even extraction from wood over the mandatory two decades-plus of aging.

Maturation draws on a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry casks, all held in the dedicated Royal Salute Vault within Strathisla's bonded warehouses. The Signature Blend is bottled at 40% ABV. The hand-crafted blue porcelain flagon — authenticated by a Wade Ceramics hallmark — is produced from Cornish clay with double-layer glazing over up to six days, making the vessel itself a documented production artifact.

As a non-vintage blend, this expression delivers consistent house character: ripe pear, dried citrus peel, vanilla, and a long, gently spiced finish — ready to pour now, with no meaningful cellaring upside.

name: Jack Daniel's  Old No.7 (3 L) with Candle
short_description (EN Store): Cave Spring Hollow limestone water + 10-ft sugar maple charcoal vats define Tennessee Whiskey's founding logic — 3L format, same 175–200-barrel mingle.
description (EN Store):
The craft Nathan 'Nearest' Green — the distillery's first Master Distiller, an enslaved man whose technique was largely erased from official histories until the 2010s — passed to Jack Daniel is still executed at Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, where a limestone cave spring delivers iron-free water at a constant 56°F year-round. Iron removal is not cosmetic: iron compounds actively degrade whiskey flavor, and the calcium-magnesium profile of this specific spring is inseparable from the house character.

Before any barrel contact, new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof on a 100-foot copper column still is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days — the Lincoln County Process, legally codified under Tennessee HB 1084 (2013), that separates this from bourbon by a single deliberate step. The 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill ferments in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash backset for pH control, then enters 100% new charred American white oak at 125 proof. Old No. 7 is mingled from 175–200 barrels drawn across multiple rickhouse floors — upper floors for wood intensity, lower floors for slower integration — before bottling at 40% ABV, chill-filtered.

The special feature: Jack Daniel's meets every federal legal definition of straight bourbon yet deliberately refuses the label — the only major American whiskey to legally qualify as bourbon and categorically reject the designation. This 3-litre format delivers the identical blend in a format scaled for extended service or display.

name: Jameson  Irish Whiskey (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-distillery blend from Midleton, Cork — triple-distilled over unmalted barley, a tax dodge turned Irish signature.
description (EN Store): When 18th-century Irish distillers began mixing unmalted barley into their pot still mash to sidestep British malt taxes, they inadvertently created a style found nowhere else on earth — and Jameson, founded at Bow Street, Dublin in 1780, built its entire identity around it. Today, production runs entirely through New Midleton Distillery in County Cork, fed by the Dungourney River and barley sourced from over 200 farms within 50 miles of the site. Unlike blended Scotch, which typically draws from multiple distilleries, every component here — pot still and grain alike — originates from a single site, giving the blending team grain-to-glass control that most blended categories cannot claim. The house method mandates triple distillation through copper pot and column stills, stripping harsh congeners to a degree double-distilled peers cannot match. Barley is kiln-dried over natural gas, not peat, preserving a clean, unsmoked malt character. Maturation relies on a combination of ex-bourbon American oak casks and ex-sherry European oak butts, with the standard expression aged a minimum of four years, predominantly in ex-bourbon wood. The defining transgression: unmalted barley in a pot still mash — a 250-year-old workaround that became the legal definition of Single Pot Still Irish Whiskey. Approachable now, though the ex-sherry fraction rewards an extra year in glass.

name: Southern Comfort  Lime  (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lime-infused American whisky liqueur with smooth, approachable character and citrus-forward profile.
description (EN Store): Southern Comfort Lime is a flavored whisky liqueur blending American whisky with natural lime flavoring. The spirit delivers a smooth, accessible drinking experience centered on bright citrus notes balanced against the warm, slightly sweet oak undertones of the whisky base. Designed for casual enjoyment, it works equally well neat, on ice, or as a mixer in contemporary cocktails.

name: Suntory  Hibiki Japanese Harmony (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Three-distillery blend — Yamazaki malt, Hakushu malt, Chita grain — unified across five cask types including rare Mizunara oak. 43% ABV, chill-filtered.
description (EN Store):
Hibiki Harmony was engineered as a direct answer to the discontinuation of the age-stated Hibiki 17 — a deliberate, no-age-statement construction designed to sustain the house's blending philosophy, Tsukuriwake, without the constraint of declared maturation minimums. That philosophy holds that a master blender should control every flavour variable in-house, drawing from Suntory's own three distilleries rather than trading casks with outside producers — a structural break from Scotch blending orthodoxy that remains unique among major whisky houses.

The liquid draws malt from Yamazaki (Shimamoto-cho, Osaka; ~25 m elevation; soft aquifer water fed by the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers; warm, humid microclimate) and Hakushu (Southern Japanese Alps; ~708 m; granite-filtered water; cool mountain air that slows ester development), then integrates corn-dominant grain spirit from Chita's column stills on the Aichi coast. The grain component, distilled to ~94% ABV, constitutes the structural majority of the blend by volume.

What distinguishes Harmony within its category is the simultaneous use of five cask types: American white oak (ex-bourbon), Spanish oak (Oloroso sherry), new American oak, wine casks, and Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica) — a wood so porous it is cooperated almost nowhere else in world whisky, yet delivers irreplaceable sandalwood, incense, and kyara aromatics. Bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered, the Mizunara thread is subtle here but structurally present — a signature absent from any comparable blended expression at this price tier.

name: Suntory  Hibiki 21 Years Old Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Six-time World's Best Blended Whisky: Yamazaki, Hakushu & Chita malts aged 21+ years across rare Mizunara, sherry & bourbon casks.
description (EN Store):
When Suntory's master blenders construct Hibiki 21, they draw on a self-contained flavour library that no other whisky house on earth possesses — malt from Yamazaki's warm, river-misted lowlands and Hakushu's granite-filtered Alpine air, unified by grain from Chita's column stills, every component aged a minimum of 21 years. This is the direct expression of Tsukuriwake, Suntory's founding philosophy of producing 100+ distinct malt styles in-house rather than trading casks, as Scottish blenders must.

Yamazaki's component matures in a warm, humid microclimate at ~25 m elevation fed by aquifers from the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers — the same water source revered by tea master Sen no Rikyū — accelerating extraction and deepening fruit. Hakushu's contribution, drawn from ~708 m in the Southern Japanese Alps, develops over a cooler annual average, preserving aromatic precision against Yamazaki's richness.

The defining technical signature is the simultaneous deployment of five cask types: American white oak ex-bourbon, Spanish oak Oloroso sherry, new American oak, ex-wine casks, and — critically — Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica), a wood so porous it is cooperated almost nowhere else in world whisky, yet irreplaceable for its sandalwood, incense, and kyara aromatics. At 21 years, Mizunara's contribution is amplified far beyond what appears in younger Hibiki expressions.

Hibiki 21 has been awarded World's Best Blended Whisky at the World Whiskies Awards six times, including 2013, 2016, and 2019 — a competitive record unmatched in its category. Bottled at 43% ABV; drinking window is open now and will hold for decades in proper storage.

name: Suntory  Hakushu 12 Years Old Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single malt from 708 m in the Southern Japanese Alps — granite-filtered water, wooden washbacks, lightly peated; bottled 43% chill-filtered.
description (EN Store):
When Suntory established Hakushu in 1973 on the forested slopes of Mt. Kaikomagatake, the deliberate choice of altitude was a technical one: at ~708 m, the cool mountain air — running approximately 5°C colder annually than Yamazaki — slows ester development and preserves the green, camphor-edged freshness that defines the distillery's house character. The site sits within an 825,000 m² protected nature reserve, 83% of which remains forested, and the water source is soft, mineral-balanced, filtered through thousand-year-old granite — a fundamentally different mineral profile from Yamazaki's river-aquifer water.

Production at Hakushu is defined by two deliberate constraints: fermentation exclusively in wooden washbacks, which retain heat against the cold mountain air and allow lactobacillus and naturally occurring microflora to build the distillery's characteristic lactic complexity; and multiple pot still shapes and sizes paired with four malt types ranging from unpeated to moderately peated, executing Suntory's in-house 'Tsukuriwake' philosophy without trading a single cask externally. The 12 Year Old is bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered, drawing on varied cask maturation across the distillery's 450,000+ barrel inventory.

What separates Hakushu 12 within the age-stated Japanese single malt category is its peat register: where Scotch Highland malts use peat for smoke, Hakushu's lightly peated malt at high altitude produces a botanical, mossy character — closer to forest floor than bonfire — that no lowland or coastal distillery replicates. Drinking well now with no pressing urgency to cellar.

name: Suntory  Hakushu 18 Years Old Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single malt from 708 m granite-filtered Alps air, aged 18 years in varied casks at Hakushu's cool mountain forest distillery — bottled at 43% ABV.
description (EN Store):
When Suntory established Hakushu in 1973 in the Southern Japanese Alps, the deliberate choice of a site ~5°C cooler than Yamazaki was a production decision, not an aesthetic one: slower maturation at 708 m elevation extracts oak compounds at a measured pace, preserving the distillate's signature green, camphor-edged character across 18 years without the accelerated tannin pickup that warmer lowland warehouses would impose. The distillery sits within an 825,000 m² protected nature reserve on Mt. Kaikomagatake, and its process water — soft, mineral-balanced, filtered through thousand-year-old granite — is the backbone of Hakushu's lighter, more delicate malt profile.

Production at Hakushu is governed by Tsukuriwake — Suntory's in-house diversity philosophy — deploying multiple pot still shapes and sizes, fermentation exclusively in wooden washbacks (which retain heat in the cool mountain air and encourage lactobacillus activity), and four malt types ranging from unpeated to moderately peated. The 18-year age statement draws from Hakushu's estimated 450,000+ barrel inventory, with maturation across American white oak ex-bourbon, Spanish oak ex-Oloroso sherry, and additional cask types. Bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered per the standard expression protocol.

What separates the 18 from younger Hakushu expressions is the extended high-altitude maturation's effect on peat integration — smoke that began sharp becomes mossy and forest-floor in character — a transformation no lowland distillery replicates at equivalent age.

name: Nikka From the barrel (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yoichi coal-fire + Miyagikyo grain married in cask, bottled non-chill-filtered at 51.4% ABV — maximum texture, zero compromise.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru built two distilleries with deliberately opposing flavour profiles, he did so because Japanese regulations forbid distillate exchange between competing groups — forcing him to construct an entire blending palette in-house. From the Barrel is the fullest expression of that architecture: Yoichi malt (heavy, coastal, produced via rare direct coal-fired pot stills with downward lyne arms) married with Miyagikyo malt (lighter, floral, steam-heated at 130°C through bulging-neck stills with upward lyne arms) and Miyagikyo Coffey grain whisky (corn-dominant, distilled in continuous stills originally built by Blairs Ltd., Glasgow). The three components are returned to cask for a marriage period at Nikka's Tochigi barrel storage facility before bottling. The critical technical decision: bottled at 51.4% ABV, non-chill-filtered — above the 46% threshold at which oils and esters remain in suspension, preserving every congener the coal-fire distillation contributed. No peer in the Japanese blended category bottles at this proof without chill-filtration at this price point. The result is a whisky of uncommon density and textural weight, where the maritime character of Hokkaido's Shakotan Peninsula coast and the soft-water floral register of Miyagikyo's Zao Mountain Range snowmelt coexist in the same glass.

name: Nikka Super Nikka (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blended whisky from Nikka's dual-distillery system — coal-fired Yoichi malt meets Coffey grain, bottled as a NV blend from Hokkaido's maritime pioneer.
description (EN Store):
When Masataka Taketsuru founded Dai Nippon Kaju Co., Ltd. in 1934 at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, he chose Yoichi specifically because its cool, fog-laden Sea of Japan microclimate and peat-rich Ishikari Plain mirrored the Scottish Highlands he had trained in — a deliberate act of terroir transplantation that no other Japanese producer attempted at the same scale.

Yoichi's snowmelt water from the Yoichi River feeds direct coal-fired pot stills with downward lyne arms — one of the last distilleries on earth to retain this labour-intensive method — which suppresses reflux and retains heavy congeners, producing a dense, maritime malt. This is married with lighter spirit from Miyagikyo's steam-heated stills and Coffey continuous still grain whisky (corn-dominant, distilled on Glasgow-built Blairs stills transferred from the Nishinomiya plant), adding a creamy, sweet counterweight.

Super Nikka is a blended whisky drawing on Nikka's proprietary full-palette system — the only major Japanese producer legally compelled to build opposing flavour profiles in-house, since Japanese regulations prohibit inter-group distillate exchange. The result is a blend with structural range that most Japanese houses cannot replicate without external sourcing.

As a NV expression, Super Nikka is built for consistency across releases; no vintage character data is applicable, and the drinking window is open now.

name: Nikka  Coffey Grain Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Corn-dominant grain whisky from Miyagikyo's Glasgow-built Coffey stills — continuous distillation preserving native sweetness rarely found in Japanese grain.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru transferred a pair of Coffey continuous stills — originally built by Blairs Ltd. of Glasgow and installed at Nikka's Nishinomiya plant — to the Miyagikyo distillery in Sendai's forested Hirose River valley, he gave Japanese grain whisky a production anchor unlike anything else in the category. Miyagikyo's water source, drawn from the Zao Mountain Range, is among the softest and lowest-hardness of any Japanese distillery, a condition that keeps the corn-dominant spirit clean and unencumbered through distillation. The Coffey still's gentle, continuous process preserves the grain's inherent sweetness and creamy texture — congeners that a pot still would strip away — while the humid, cool forest microclimate of the valley moderates maturation. Aging proceeds in ex-bourbon American oak casks (re-filled, remade, and re-charred), which layer vanilla and coconut without overwhelming the grain's delicate character. What makes this expression categorically unusual is the Coffey still itself: these are antique continuous stills of Scottish manufacture operating inside a Japanese distillery, producing a grain whisky with a textural richness and aromatic fidelity to raw corn that modern column stills rarely achieve. Bottled as a standalone grain expression, it exposes a component that most blenders keep hidden — the sweet, waxy backbone that anchors Nikka's From the Barrel blend.

name: Nikka  Coffey Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Malted barley through Miyagikyo's Coffey continuous still — a category-defying grain-still malt bottled at 45% ABV with uncommon tropical richness.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru transferred a pair of Coffey continuous stills — originally built by Blairs Ltd. of Glasgow — to Miyagikyo Distillery, he made a deliberate choice that no Scottish or Irish producer would sanction: running 100% malted barley through a column still designed for grain whisky. The result is the Coffey Malt, an expression that sits in a category of one. Miyagikyo Distillery occupies a forested valley west of Sendai, fed by snowmelt from the Zao Mountain Range via the Hirose and Nikkawa rivers — water of exceptionally low mineral hardness that strips nothing from the distillate's natural sweetness. The humid, cool forest microclimate slows maturation gently. Distillation through the Coffey continuous still at lower cut points than a pot still preserves a higher proportion of fruity esters and creamy congeners while shedding the heavier sulfurous compounds that pot-still malt retains — producing a spirit of unusual aromatic density for a column-distilled product. Maturation in ex-bourbon American oak amplifies the vanilla and coconut register already inherent in the distillate. The special feature: no other major Japanese producer distils malted barley exclusively in a Coffey still, making this expression structurally impossible to replicate elsewhere. Drinking beautifully now, with the sweetness and ester complexity holding well over the next five-plus years.

name: Chivas Regal  12 Years (1 L)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-anchored Speyside blend; short-neck pot stills + wooden washbacks build the house's signature malt-forward creaminess. 12-year minimum.
description (EN Store): When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909 as a 25-year-old blend, no competitor carried an age statement at all — the move effectively invented the premium blended Scotch category and locked in a philosophy of malt generosity over grain economy that still governs every expression today. The heart of the blend is Strathisla single malt, distilled at Keith, Speyside, in stills with notably short necks that allow heavier vapours to ascend, producing a full-bodied, oily new make; process water is drawn exclusively from the Broomhill Spring, filtered through granite approximately one mile from the distillery. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks — not stainless steel — providing thermal insulation for a steady, flavour-building two-day ferment before double pot-still distillation with the heart cut collected at 65–70% ABV. Component whiskies rest in American and European oak, including ex-bourbon and ex-Oloroso sherry casks, with the 12-year statement marking the youngest whisky present — older parcels from Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Miltonduff, Braeval, and Allt-a-Bhainne contribute without declaration. The grain component arrives from Strathclyde Distillery in the Lowlands, keeping the Speyside malt character dominant. Bottled at 40% ABV, the 1-litre format delivers the same blend as the standard 700 ml, with no cask-strength variant in the core range.

name: The Glenlivet  12 years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Minmore Farm, Livet Valley: lantern-still architecture and a narrow 18–20% heart cut deliver Speyside's defining fruit-and-vanilla blueprint at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): When George Smith secured the first legal Highland distillery licence under the Excise Act of 1823, his deliberate break from the heavy illicit drams of the era was encoded in the copper itself: the lantern-shaped pot stills — pinched waist, long narrow neck — were engineered to maximise reflux and strip impurities, producing a lighter, cleaner spirit that competitors across Speyside spent the next century trying to replicate. The Minmore Farm site sits high above the River Livet near Ballindalloch, fed by Josie's Well, a natural mountain spring draining the slopes toward Ben Rinnes; the soft, mineral-low water integrates cleanly into the mash without competing with the estery distillate character. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in wooden washbacks — retained over stainless steel at higher maintenance cost specifically for the floral, estery uplift they contribute — followed by slow double distillation across 14 copper pot stills, with a narrow heart cut of roughly 18–20% of total distillate recycling heads and tails. The 12 Year Old rests in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and European oak, bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, with standard caramel colouring. What sets this expression apart within the core range is its role as the direct heir to the house style George Smith codified: unpeated barley from Crisp Maltings, Portgordon, and a still geometry unchanged in principle since 1859. The result is a non-vintage malt whose approachability is an architectural achievement, not a concession.

name: The Glenlivet  15 years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Minmore Farm, Livet Valley: 15-year Speyside malt finished in French oak, the house's deliberate counter to sherry-dominant peers.
description (EN Store): When George Smith secured the first legal Highland distillery licence under the 1823 Excise Act, he made a calculated stylistic break — building lantern-shaped copper pot stills with pinched waists and long narrow necks specifically to maximise reflux and strip the heavy, illicit character of the era. That architectural decision still governs every drop at Minmore Farm, high above the River Livet, where over 65,000 casks rest in dunnage warehouses fed by Josie's Well, a natural spring descending from the surrounding Speyside hills. The cool, elevated microclimate of the Livet Valley slows maturation, preserving the distillery's signature estery lift through extended wood contact. The 15 Year Old is built on French oak casks — a deliberate departure from the ex-bourbon-anchored core range — imparting tighter grain structure and dried-orchard character rather than the vanilla-and-coconut register of American oak. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in wooden washbacks, retained at higher maintenance cost precisely for the floral, estery quality wood vessels contribute; double distillation through 14 copper pot stills yields a narrow heart cut of roughly 18–20% of total distillate. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered — the standard core-range protocol. What separates this expression from the 12 and 18 is the exclusive French oak regime: no Oloroso sherry influence, no ex-bourbon vanilla dominance — just the Livet fruit character shaped by Gallic wood over a decade and a half.

name: The Glenlivet  18 years (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Minmore Farm, Livet Valley: 18 years across ex-bourbon & Oloroso sherry casks, from the distillery that legally defined Speyside in 1824.
description (EN Store):
When George Smith secured the first legal Highland distilling licence under the Excise Act of 1823, he made a deliberate technical choice that shaped an entire region: long-necked, lantern-shaped copper pot stills with a pinched waist to maximise reflux, producing a lighter, cleaner spirit that broke sharply from the heavy illicit drams of the era. That same still geometry — unchanged in principle — governs every drop of the 18 Year Old today.

The Minmore Farm distillery sits high above the River Livet near Ballindalloch, Moray, drawing water from Josie's Well, a natural spring descending from the surrounding hills. The elevated, cool site slows maturation gently, preserving the distillery's signature estery fruit character through extended wood contact. Unpeated malted barley from Crisp Maltings, Portgordon is fermented in wooden washbacks for approximately 48 hours — a deliberate retention of timber over stainless steel for the floral, estery quality the wood imparts — before slow double distillation through 14 copper pot stills.

The 18 Year Old rests in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and Oloroso sherry casks, held in on-site dunnage warehouses among over 65,000 maturing casks. The sherry component introduces dried fruit and spice depth without displacing the house's fruit-and-vanilla axis — a balance point that separates this expression from the sherry-dominant architecture of Macallan or Glenfarclas at the same age tier. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, with caramel colouring standard to the core range.

Eighteen years in this cool Highland valley draws the ex-bourbon vanilla into a richer register while the Oloroso influence builds without overwhelming — a drinking window that rewards opening now through the next several years.

name: The Glenlivet  Founder's Reserve (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): NAS Speyside single malt from Minmore Farm's lantern-still distillery — first-fill American oak plus virgin oak finish, 40% ABV, chill-filtered.
description (EN Store): When George Smith secured the first legal Highland distillery licence under the 1823 Excise Act, he made a deliberate technical choice that defined an entire region: abandon the heavy, dense illicit drams of the era and build a lighter, fruit-forward spirit around maximum copper contact. That philosophy is encoded in the lantern-shaped pot stills — pinched waist, long narrow neck — still in use across two stillhouses at the Minmore Farm site above the River Livet, fed by Josie's Well spring water descending from the surrounding hills. The Founder's Reserve is a no-age-statement expression built on first-fill American oak casks (ex-bourbon sourced from Jack Daniel's, Jim Beam, and Heaven Hill) with a virgin oak finish, bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in wooden washbacks — retained over stainless steel specifically for the floral, estery character they generate — before slow double distillation with a narrow heart cut of roughly 18–20% of total distillate. What sets Founder's Reserve apart within the core range is the virgin oak component: where the 12 Year Old relies on refill and first-fill ex-bourbon alone, the added virgin wood here pushes coconut and vanilla extraction forward without age-statement constraints. Drink now through the near term; no cellaring upside given the NAS, chill-filtered format.

name: Glengoyne  10 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Distilled at Dumgoyne Hill's volcanic foot at Scotland's slowest spirit-still rate (~4–5 L/min), zero peat, sherry-cask matured in the Lowlands.
description (EN Store): When Ian Macleod Distillers acquired Glengoyne in 2003, they inherited a distillery already defined by a deliberate act of restraint: barley dried by warm air alone, yielding 0 ppm phenols — a house rule in place since the surrounding Campsie Fells soil contained no peat to burn in the first place. The site sits at the foot of Dumgoyne Hill, a volcanic landmark of the Stirlingshire Campsie Fells, where process water filters through volcanic rock for up to 40 years before emerging soft and mineral-clear from the Glengoyne Burn. That softness amplifies ester expression in the new make. The signature technical commitment is the spirit still run at ~4–5 litres per minute — the slowest distillation rate in Scotland — paired with boil-bulb stills that maximise copper reflux and drive the orchard-fruited, toffee-forward character. The 10 Year Old matures in ex-bourbon American oak, bottled at 43% ABV, non chill-filtered, no caramel added. What breaks category convention: this is legally a Highland single malt distilled in the Highlands, yet every cask crosses the A81 road into the Lowlands for maturation — the Highland Line bisects the property. The 10 Year Old delivers the distillery's clean, copper-driven fruit profile at its most accessible, with the bourbon oak providing vanilla scaffolding before the sherry-led expressions take over at higher age statements.

name: Glengoyne  12 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Distilled in the Highlands, matured in the Lowlands — Glengoyne's split-site 12YO runs Scotland's slowest spirit still at ~4–5 L/min, zero peat, sherry-seasoned European oak.
description (EN Store): When Ian Macleod Distillers acquired Glengoyne in 2003, they inherited a distillery whose identity is literally divided by a road: the A81 cuts through the site at Dumgoyne, placing the stillhouse in the Highlands and the eight dunnage warehouses across the tarmac in the Lowlands — a geographic anomaly with no parallel in Scotch whisky. Process water filters through Dumgoyne Hill's volcanic basalt for up to 40 years before emerging soft and mineral from the Glengoyne Burn, contributing to a new make of unusual textural clarity. The surrounding soil contains no peat, a geological fact that permanently fixed the house style: 0 ppm phenols, barley dried solely by warm air. In the stillhouse, two boil-bulb spirit stills run at ~4–5 litres per minute — the slowest distillation rate in Scotland — maximising copper contact and ester formation. For the 12 Year Old, maturation leans on ex-Oloroso-seasoned European oak casks, part of a bespoke six-year cask programme in which Glengoyne selects Spanish oak, air-dries it for two years, and seasons it with Oloroso sherry for a further two before a single drop of spirit enters. No caramel colouring, non chill-filtered, bottled at 43% ABV. What makes this expression unusual is the complete absence of smoke in a Highland malt — complexity here is built entirely from copper contact, slow distillation, and sherry wood. Approachable now, the 12 Year Old will continue to integrate over 3–5 years in bottle.

name: Glengoyne  21 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Distilled in the Highlands, matured in the Lowlands across the A81 road — 21 years in bespoke Oloroso-seasoned European oak, zero peat, Scotland's slowest spirit run.
description (EN Store):
The Highland Line runs directly beneath the A81 road in front of Glengoyne's stillhouse at Dumgoyne — a geographic split that means every drop of this 21-year-old is legally Highland-distilled yet Lowland-matured, a jurisdictional anomaly unique in Scotch whisky. Process water filters through the volcanic basalt of Dumgoyne Hill for up to four decades before reaching the distillery, arriving soft and mineral-free, a direct contributor to the clean, orchard-forward new make.

The spirit itself is shaped by two deliberate extremes: 0 ppm phenols (barley dried by warm air alone, no peat) and Scotland's slowest spirit still run at ~4–5 litres per minute, maximised further by boil-bulb stills installed in 1990 to drive copper contact and ester formation. Maturation spans 21 years in Oloroso-seasoned European oak butts and hogsheads, casks Glengoyne commissions through a full six-year programme — selecting Spanish oak, air-drying for two years, seasoning with Oloroso sherry for two more before a single fill in Scotland. No caramel colouring; non chill-filtered; bottled at 43% ABV.

What is unusual here: a Highland malt whose complexity is built entirely on copper contact and sherry-wood time, with no smoke, no grain shortcuts, and a cask provenance most distilleries outsource entirely. At 21 years the sherry-oak integration is at full expression — dried fruit density balanced by the soft, volcanic-filtered water character that defines the Dumgoyne house style.

name: Nikka  Miyagikyo Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Miyagikyo's forested valley, Zao snowmelt, and steam-heated bulging-neck stills produce Japan's most floral, fruit-driven single malt.
description (EN Store):
When Masataka Taketsuru founded Miyagikyo Distillery in 1969 — his second site, built 35 years after Yoichi — he chose a forested valley west of Sendai specifically because its humid, cool microclimate and the ultra-soft water drawn from the Hirose and Nikkawa rivers (fed by the Zao Mountain Range snowmelt) mirrored the gentler Highland conditions he needed to create a stylistic counterpoint to Yoichi's heavy, coal-fired character. That deliberate opposition was not aesthetic preference: Japanese regulations prohibit distillate exchange between competing groups, so Taketsuru had to engineer his own full blending palette in-house across two sites.

The distillery's pot stills feature bulging necks and upward lyne arms, maximising copper contact and reflux during steam-heated distillation at 130°C — a configuration that strips heavier congeners and drives the spirit toward lighter, more volatile esters. Fermentation runs through 22 stainless-steel washbacks. The water source, noted as among the lowest-hardness of any Japanese distillery, further suppresses extraction of heavy phenolics during mashing. Cask maturation draws on ex-bourbon American oak, with sherry cask influence available in select expressions.

What no peer replicates: Miyagikyo is the only Japanese single malt distillery purpose-built as the soft counterweight to a coal-fired sibling, its entire still geometry engineered around that single blending philosophy. As a non-vintage expression, character is consistent across batches — drink now through the next decade.

name: Jameson  Black Barrel Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single-distillery blend from Midleton, double-cask finished in ex-bourbon and charred new American oak barrels — Jameson's richest standard expression.
description (EN Store): When Jameson's blending team sought to amplify the vanilla and toasted-oak register already present in the house style, they turned to a second pass in heavily charred new American oak barrels — a finishing step that sets Black Barrel apart from every other expression in the range. All component whiskeys originate from a single site: New Midleton Distillery, County Cork, where the Dungourney River supplies process water and locally contracted barley — sourced from over 200 farms within 50 miles — feeds the mash. This single-distillery architecture is the structural transgression of the Jameson range: most blended whiskeys draw from multiple distilleries, but Midleton's blending team controls every grain-to-glass variable in-house. The spirit itself is a marriage of Single Pot Still whiskey (malted and unmalted barley, triple-distilled in copper pot stills — the unmalted barley a legacy of 18th-century Irish tax law that became the defining mark of the style) and maize-based grain whiskey (triple-distilled in column stills), both streams matured first in ex-bourbon American oak casks before the Black Barrel finishing in charred new American oak. Triple distillation strips harsh congeners, and the new-oak finish layers dense caramel and baking spice over the lighter grain base. The result is the heaviest oak imprint in the core Jameson lineup without crossing into single malt territory — a deliberate widening of the house's sweet, unpeated character.

name: Michter's  US*1 American Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Kentucky whiskey with vanilla oak and warming spice—smooth, approachable American spirit.
description (EN Store): Michter's US*1 American Whiskey is a Kentucky-distilled spirit showcasing the classic profile of American whiskey craftsmanship. The palate opens with vanilla and oak character from barrel maturation, followed by warming spice notes and a smooth, rounded finish. Full-bodied and approachable, it reflects conventional distillation and aging practices typical of the category.

name: Suntory  Hibiki 21 Years Old Art Lable Fuji Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Six-time World's Best Blended Whisky; Yamazaki, Hakushu & Chita aged 21+ years across Mizunara, sherry & bourbon casks. Fuji Art Label limited edition.
description (EN Store):
Six times named World's Best Blended Whisky at the World Whiskies Awards — including 2013, 2016, and 2019 — Hibiki 21 Year Old is the expression that forced the global spirits industry to reckon with Japan as a serious whisky nation. This Fuji Art Label release is a limited-edition presentation of that same liquid, dressed in a design celebrating Mount Fuji.

The blend draws malt from two radically different sites: Yamazaki, sitting at ~25 m elevation in the warm, humid confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers outside Kyoto — conditions that accelerate maturation and build dense, fruit-forward complexity — and Hakushu, perched at ~708 m in the Southern Japanese Alps, where granite-filtered water and an annual average temperature ~5°C cooler than Yamazaki slow extraction and preserve green, camphor-edged freshness. Chita grain whisky, corn-dominant and column-distilled to ~94% ABV on the Aichi Peninsula, provides the structural backbone.

All components carry a minimum 21 years of maturation across up to five cask types simultaneously: ex-bourbon American white oak, Oloroso sherry Spanish oak, new American oak, wine casks, and — most critically — Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica), a wood so porous it is cooperated almost nowhere else on earth, yet irreplaceable for the sandalwood, incense, and kyara aromatics it deposits over decades.

The Mizunara influence is markedly amplified at 21 years versus younger Hibiki expressions, making this the tier where Suntory's 'Tsukuriwake' philosophy — producing 100+ distinct malt styles entirely in-house without inter-distillery cask trading — becomes fully audible in the glass.

name: Chivas Regal  XV (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside blend finished in Grande Champagne Cognac casks — a rare Scotch-French crossover anchored by Strathisla's short-necked pot stills.
description (EN Store): When Master Blender Sandy Hyslop specified Grande Champagne Cognac casks for the XV finish, he created one of the few blended Scotches that formally bridges two of Europe's most codified spirits traditions — a deliberate transgression that no other expression in the Chivas core range replicates. Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Speyside — founded in 1786 and the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands — supplies the heart malt, drawing process water from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite approximately one mile away. The distillery's short-necked pot stills allow heavier vapours to rise, producing a full-bodied, generously textured new make that resists the lighter, more floral character typical of taller Speyside stills. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks for approximately two days, providing thermal insulation that sustains a steady, flavour-building ferment. Component whiskies — including Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Braeval, Miltonduff, and Allt-a-Bhainne alongside Strathclyde grain — are matured in ex-bourbon and ex-Oloroso sherry American and European oak to a minimum of 15 years before the Grande Champagne Cognac cask finish layers dried apricot, beeswax, and brandy-soaked oak onto the house profile. Bottled at 40% ABV. Drink now through the medium term; the Cognac finish is most expressive within two to three years of purchase.

name: Bushmills  Black Bush (Matured in Sherry cask) (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Irish single malt matured in sherry cask; honey-forward with vanilla oak and warming spice.
description (EN Store): Bushmills Black Bush is a blended Irish whisky finished in sherry cask, delivering a warm, approachable profile centered on honey and vanilla notes. The sherry maturation imparts dried fruit character and subtle spice complexity, balanced by smooth oak integration. Medium-full bodied with a gentle finish, this expression suits casual entertaining, dessert pairings, and contemplative sipping.

name: Old Pulteney  12 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wick's urban distillery, 300 m from the North Sea — worm-tub condensers and a truncated boil-ball still deliver oily maritime weight at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store): In 1930, Wick voted itself dry under the Temperance (Scotland) Act, forcing Pulteney Distillery to shutter for 21 years — the only major Scottish distillery ever closed by local prohibition rather than commercial failure. That same coastal town, embedded in Caithness lowland with warehouses sitting approximately 300 metres from the North Sea, is the defining physical fact of every cask maturing here: persistent sea air, high humidity, and cold year-round temperatures work into the dunnage warehouses' clay floors and the racked stores alike. Water arrives from Loch Hempriggs via a lade engineered by Thomas Telford in 1807 — soft and mineral-lean, it contributes to the distillery's characteristically clean, fruit-forward new make. The production signature is architectural: the wash still's oversized boil ball and flat-topped truncated swan neck — cut short to fit the still house ceiling — restrict copper contact and produce a heavier, oilier spirit than most Highland peers. Worm-tub condensers compound this, preserving sulphury weight that would otherwise be scrubbed away by shell-and-tube systems. The 12 YO rests in ~90–95% ex-Bourbon American oak refill casks with a minority of ex-Oloroso Sherry Spanish oak, bottled at 40% ABV. What is unusual: the boil-ball's silhouette is literally replicated in the bottle's shape — form following function in both glass and copper.

name: Old Pulteney  Huddart (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wick's urban distillery, worm-tub condensers, ex-Bourbon/Huddart wood finish — coastal brine meets orchard sweetness on Caithness's North Sea shore.
description (EN Store): When Wick voted itself dry under the Temperance (Scotland) Act, Pulteney Distillery shuttered from 1930 to 1951 — the only major Scottish distillery closed by local prohibition rather than commercial failure. That defiant history is baked into the Huddart expression, named for the street the distillery still occupies in the heart of town. Warehouses sit approximately 300 metres from the North Sea, where clay-floored dunnage stores and persistent coastal humidity work salt and sea air directly into maturing casks — a maturation environment with no meaningful elevation buffer, just cold Caithness air and brine. The Huddart's character begins with unpeated Concerto-variety malted barley distilled through one of Scotland's most architecturally eccentric wash stills: a flat-topped, truncated swan neck (cut short to fit the still house ceiling) mounted on an oversized boil ball that forces heavy, oily spirit vapour back into the copper before condensing through traditional external worm-tub condensers — a combination found in only a handful of Scottish distilleries and the primary reason Old Pulteney carries unusual weight and texture for a Highland malt. The Huddart expression layers a secondary wood finish over the ex-Bourbon American oak base, adding dried fruit depth and spice to the distillery's signature saline-malty core. What sets Huddart apart within the range is that additional cask influence applied without an age statement, allowing the wood finish — not calendar time — to define the final profile. Drinking well now; the coastal salinity keeps the finish clean and long.

name: Old Pulteney  15 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wick's coastal dunnage warehouses, worm-tub condensers, and 15 years in ex-Bourbon oak deliver an oily, saline Highland malt unlike any peer.
description (EN Store): In 1930, Wick voted itself dry under the Temperance (Scotland) Act — the only Scottish town to shutter its distillery by local prohibition rather than commercial failure. Pulteney reopened in 1951, and that enforced silence makes every age-stated expression a document of deliberate patience. The distillery sits on Huddart Street in the heart of Wick, Caithness, its five warehouses — three dunnage with clay floors, two racked — positioned roughly 300 metres from the North Sea, where persistent cold humidity and salt-laden air permeate maturing casks year-round. Water drawn from Loch Hempriggs via a lade engineered by Thomas Telford in 1807 contributes a soft, mineral base to the spirit. The 15 Year is built on approximately 90–95% ex-Bourbon American oak refill casks with a 5–10% ex-Oloroso Sherry (Spanish oak) component, bottled at 46% ABV — a strength at which the expression is generally considered non-chill-filtered by industry convention, preserving the oily, waxy texture generated by the distillery's worm-tub condensers. That texture is the direct product of an architecturally eccentric wash still: its swan neck was cut short to fit the still-house ceiling, and its oversized boil ball — immortalised in the shape of the bottle itself — restricts copper contact, producing a heavier new make than any standard Highland peer. Fifteen years of coastal maturation amplifies rather than tames that weight, layering dried orchard fruit and toasted oak over a persistent briny, saline backbone.

name: Old Pulteney  18 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wick's urban distillery, 300 m from the North Sea: 18-year second-fill Bourbon then first-fill Oloroso Sherry finish, bottled at 46% ABV.
description (EN Store):
In 1930, Wick voted itself dry under the Temperance (Scotland) Act — the only Scottish town to shutter its distillery by local prohibition rather than commercial failure. Pulteney sat silent for 21 years, reopening in 1951 with the same eccentric copper geometry intact. That geometry matters: the wash still's swan neck was cut short to fit the still-house ceiling, and its oversized boil ball forces heavy, oily spirit back into the copper before condensing through traditional external worm-tub condensers — a configuration shared by fewer than a dozen Scottish distilleries — producing a new make of unusual weight and texture for the Highland region.

The distillery sits on coastal lowland in Wick, Caithness, with five warehouses — three dunnage with clay floors — lying approximately 300 metres from the North Sea. High humidity and persistent sea air penetrate the casks year-round, drawing brine and iodine-edged salinity into the spirit during maturation. Water comes from Loch Hempriggs via a lade engineered by Thomas Telford in 1807. The 18 Year follows a two-stage cask regime: second-fill ex-Bourbon American oak for primary maturation, then a finish in first-fill Spanish oak ex-Oloroso Sherry butts, bottled at 46% ABV. That Oloroso layer — arriving late over an already oily, saline base — deposits dried fruit and dark spice without erasing the coastal edge that defines the house character. The dual-wood finish on a worm-tub distillate is the structural anomaly here: most worm-tub whiskies are left in single-cask regimes to preserve their sulphur-touched weight; Old Pulteney uses the Sherry finish to redirect rather than mask it.

name: AnCnoc  12 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland single malt with 12 years maturation; balanced vanilla oak, dark fruit, and subtle smoke.
description (EN Store): AnCnoc 12 Year is a Highland single malt whisky matured for 12 years in oak cask. The spirit presents a balanced profile anchored by vanilla and oak from wood contact, supported by dark fruit and hazelnut notes. Subtle smoke and spice add complexity, with cocoa undertones rounding the finish. Medium body and approachable alcohol warmth make this a versatile dram suitable for both casual enjoyment and food pairing.

name: AnCnoc  18 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland single malt aged 18 years with rich vanilla oak, warming spice, and layered dried fruit complexity.
description (EN Store): AnCnoc 18 Year Old is a Highland single malt whisky representing mature craftsmanship and extended wood contact. The spirit displays a full body with warming spice notes, vanilla-forward oak influence, and supporting hazelnut character. Dried fruit and subtle smoke emerge in the mid-palate, while deeper tertiary notes of tobacco and leather develop with time in glass, reflecting the depth gained through 18 years of maturation.

name: Lambertus  Single Cask Whisky #4 (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Belgian single cask whisky with rich oak, vanilla, and spice character. Full-bodied with layered complexity.
description (EN Store): Lambertus Single Cask Whisky #4 is a full-bodied Belgian spirit showcasing the depth and character of single cask maturation. The whisky presents a rich profile dominated by oak and vanilla notes from extended cask contact, complemented by warming spice and dark fruit undertones. Leather and subtle smoke emerge in the finish, revealing the complexity that develops through patient aging. This expression is best enjoyed neat or with a single ice cube to unlock its layered character.

name: Wolfburn  Northland Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's most northerly mainland distillery: Northland matured in ex-Islay refill quarter casks, non-chill filtered, 46% ABV, zero automation grain-to-glass.
description (EN Store): When Aurora Brewing Ltd. lit the stills at Henderson Park, Thurso on Burns Night 2013, they chose to revive a distillery silent for over 150 years — and to do so without a single automated step from milling through on-site bottling, a discipline almost no Scottish distillery of any scale maintains today. Northland is the expression that most directly channels Caithness itself: the cask selection — ex-Islay refill American oak quarter casks — is the defining technical decision, threading a ghost of coastal peat smoke through an otherwise unpeated spirit without Wolfburn ever peating its own barley for this expression. Process water is drawn from the Wolf Burn, a spring-fed stream filtered through Caithness flagstone, cold and mineral-laden, feeding a spirit still with a reflux bowl that deliberately drives copper contact and a lighter, fruitier house character. Fermentation is intentionally long across all Wolfburn production to build biscuity, orchard-fruit esters before distillation. Maturation occurs in traditional dunnage warehouses on-site — clay floors, low ceilings, maximum three casks stacked — where the proximity to the north coast imparts a mild maritime salinity. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, natural colour. What sets Northland apart within the core range is borrowed smoke: the ex-Islay refill casks carry residual phenolic memory into a distillery that otherwise produces zero-peat spirit, creating a layered coastal character no other Wolfburn expression replicates.

name: Wolfburn  Aurora Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Revived 2013 at Scotland's most northerly mainland distillery, Aurora blends ex-Bourbon and Oloroso Sherry casks — non-chill filtered, 46% ABV, bottled on-site.
description (EN Store): When Aurora Brewing Ltd. lit the stills at Henderson Park on Burns Night 2013, they were restarting a distillery silent for over 150 years — and they chose to do it entirely by hand. No automation governs the mash tun, the washbacks, or the stills; the only computer on-site regulates mash temperatures. The distillery sits in Caithness, the most northerly whisky-producing county on the Scottish mainland, where process water is drawn from the Wolf Burn, a spring-fed stream filtered through ancient Caithness flagstone, arriving crystal-clear and cold. Proximity to the north coast imparts a mild maritime edge during maturation in traditional dunnage warehouses — clay floors, low ceilings, casks stacked no higher than three. The Aurora expression draws from ex-Bourbon American oak hogsheads and ex-Oloroso Sherry casks, the Bourbon wood driving the house's characteristic light, fruity, biscuity character — itself a product of the spirit still's reflux bowl, which maximises copper contact — while the Sherry component adds dried-fruit depth. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, with no artificial colouring, and filled at Wolfburn's own on-site bottling facility — one of very few Scottish distilleries to distil, mature, and bottle entirely under one roof. The dual-cask architecture is what separates Aurora from the single-wood Langskip and the ex-Islay-inflected Northland, making it the most accessible expression of the house's full flavour range.

name: Wolfburn  Morven Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's most northerly mainland distillery — lightly peated Morven (10 ppm) from Caithness flagstone-filtered spring water, non-chill filtered at 46% ABV.
description (EN Store):
When Aurora Brewing Ltd. revived Wolfburn in 2013 after more than 150 years of silence, they made a deliberate choice to run the entire operation — milling, distillation, maturation, and bottling — without automation, a grain-to-glass integrity almost unheard of at any scale in Scotland. Morven is the lightly peated expression in that lineup, built on malted barley kilned to approximately 10 ppm phenol, a restrained smoke level that sits far below Islay convention and is designed to complement rather than dominate the house's naturally fruity, biscuity spirit character.

The distillery sits at Henderson Park, Thurso, Caithness — the most northerly whisky-producing site on the Scottish mainland. Process water is drawn from the Wolf Burn itself, a spring-fed stream filtered through Caithness flagstone, arriving cold and mineral-clean. Proximity to the north coast introduces a mild maritime influence during maturation in traditional dunnage warehouses (clay floors, maximum three casks stacked), slowing the interaction between spirit and wood.

Distillation runs through two Forsyths copper pot stills — a 5,500-litre wash still and a 3,600–3,800-litre spirit still fitted with a reflux bowl for extended copper contact, the engineering decision that locks in Wolfburn's lighter, fruitier house profile. Long fermentation in stainless steel washbacks (sourced from the closed Caperdonich distillery) further builds fruity, biscuity congeners before the spirit ever sees wood. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, natural colour — on-site, in Wolfburn's own purpose-built bottling facility, one of very few Scottish distilleries to do so. What sets Morven apart is the precise calibration of peat against a light-bodied, copper-driven spirit: the smoke is structural, not dominant, shaped by a coastal maturation environment that no Speyside or Islay peer can replicate.

name: Wolfburn  Langskip Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cask-strength Langskip from Scotland's most northerly mainland distillery — first-fill ex-Bourbon only, zero automation, grain-to-glass at Henderson Park, Thurso.
description (EN Store):
When Aurora Brewing Ltd. revived Wolfburn in 2013 after more than 150 years of silence, they made a deliberate choice to build a purpose-built on-site bottling hall — one of the only Scottish distilleries of any scale to distil, mature, and bottle entirely under one roof with zero automation, from milling through to labelling. Langskip is the expression that most nakedly exposes that grain-to-glass philosophy.

Henderson Park sits at Thurso, Caithness — the most northerly whisky distillery on the Scottish mainland — where process water is drawn from the Wolf Burn, a spring-fed stream filtered through ancient Caithness flagstone, arriving crystal-clear and cold. The coastal proximity imparts a mild saline maritime edge during maturation in traditional dunnage warehouses with clay floors, casks stacked no higher than three.

Langskip is matured exclusively in first-fill ex-Bourbon American oak casks — the only expression in the core range to use first-fill wood — and is released at natural cask strength, non-chill filtered, with no artificial colouring. The spirit still's reflux bowl delivers additional copper contact, producing the lighter, fruitier house character that first-fill oak then amplifies rather than overwhelms. Fermentation is deliberately long to build biscuity, orchard-fruit complexity before distillation.

Where most cask-strength Highland malts lean on sherry or port for weight, Langskip strips back to first-fill Bourbon alone — a structural transgression that lets the distillery's unusually delicate, high-copper spirit speak at full proof.

As a NAS expression, Langskip draws on maturing stock from 2013 onward; drink now or hold to watch the first-fill oak integrate further over three to five years.

name: Balblair  12 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Northern Highlands single malt from Edderton's dunnage warehouses — 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, ex-bourbon maturation over 12 years on beaten-earth floors.
description (EN Store): In 2019, Balblair abandoned the vintage-release format it had championed since 2007 — one of very few Scottish distilleries ever to label by distillation year — and returned to age statements, making this 12-year-old the current entry point into a house style built on structural patience. The distillery sits on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth near Edderton, Ross-shire, on an ancient Pictish site marked by the standing stone Clach Biorach; water is drawn from the Allt Dearg burn, piped roughly five miles from the Edderton Hills — the same source used since 1790. Fermentation runs approximately 62 hours in Oregon pine washbacks, generating a clean, ester-forward wash; distillation proceeds through squat, onion-shaped copper pot stills at a deliberately slow pace, producing a new make filled to cask at roughly 68.5% ABV — well above the Scottish industry norm of ~63.5% — which slows extraction and extends the effective maturation curve. The 12-year-old rests exclusively in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels inside traditional dunnage warehouses dating to 1894, their beaten-earth floors moderating temperature and humidity to minimise angel's share. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, natural colour — no caramel addition across the entire core range. That unusually high fill strength means the spirit at 12 years retains more of its original orchard-fruit and citrus character than most Highland peers of the same age.

name: Balblair  15 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Northern Highlands single malt from Edderton's dunnage warehouses — ex-bourbon primary, first-fill Oloroso finish, 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered.
description (EN Store): When Balblair abandoned its vintage-release model in 2019 — one of Scotland's most committed single-year labelling programmes since 2007 — the 15 Year became the clearest statement of what the distillery's unhurried craft actually produces. Situated on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth in Edderton, Ross-shire, the distillery draws water from the Allt Dearg burn, piped five miles from the Edderton Hills — the same source retained even when the distillery relocated in 1872. Slow distillation through squat, onion-shaped copper pot stills with wide necks and no reflux bowls builds a dense, ester-forward new make, filled into cask at approximately 68.5% ABV — well above the Scottish industry norm of ~63.5% — which structurally slows maturation and preserves original spirit character. The 15 Year undergoes primary maturation in American oak ex-bourbon hogsheads, then a finishing period in first-fill Oloroso sherry casks, all aged in traditional dunnage warehouses with beaten-earth floors dating to 1894. What sets this expression apart: the high fill strength combined with on-site dunnage ageing means the sherry finish encounters a spirit of unusual density, producing dried-fruit and baking-spice integration rather than surface sweetness. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, natural colour — no caramel addition across the entire core range.

name: Balblair  18 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Northern Highlands single malt from Edderton's dunnage warehouses: 18 years ex-bourbon then first-fill Spanish oak butts, 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered.
description (EN Store):
When Balblair relocated half a mile north in 1872–1894 to meet the Inverness–Ardgay railway, the distillery's founders insisted on piping the Allt Dearg burn five miles from the Edderton Hills to the new site — the same water source drawn since 1790, a deliberate act of continuity that defines the house's commitment to unhurried craft. That same patience governs the 18 Year: ex-bourbon hogsheads provide primary maturation, followed by a finishing period in first-fill Spanish oak Oloroso butts, all resting on beaten-earth floors in dunnage warehouses dating to 1894, where low evaporation rates preserve spirit integrity across nearly two decades.

The distillery's squat, onion-shaped pot stills with wide necks and no reflux bowls — run deliberately slowly over approximately 4.5 hours per spirit-still charge — build a dense, ester-forward new make that demands time to resolve. Fermentation in Oregon pine washbacks for ~62 hours and mashing for clear worts lay the fruity foundation. The result is bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, natural colour. The special feature here is structural: Balblair fills casks at ~68.5% ABV versus the Scottish industry norm of ~63.5%, meaning the spirit matures more slowly and retains more of its original character — the sherry-butt finish amplifies rather than overwrites. The 18 Year sits in the current age-statement core range introduced in 2019, representing the distillery's mature-era expression at its most textured.

name: Balblair  25 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 25-year Northern Highland malt from Edderton's dunnage warehouses: ex-bourbon primary, reshaped in Oloroso Spanish oak, 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered.
description (EN Store):
When Balblair relocated half a mile north in 1872 to meet Charles Doig's new distillery design, the one thing the owners refused to leave behind was the water — the Allt Dearg burn, piped five miles from the Edderton Hills, still feeds every mash today, and its mineral clarity is the foundation on which a 25-year maturation is built. The distillery sits on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth in Ross-shire, where traditional dunnage warehouses with beaten-earth floors — most dating to 1894 — maintain the slow, low-evaporation conditions that make patience a structural feature rather than a marketing claim.

Production begins with unpeated barley from Glen Ord Maltings, mashed for clear wort and fermented in Oregon pine washbacks for approximately 62 hours, generating an ester-forward wash. Distillation through squat, onion-shaped copper pot stills — deliberately slow, approximately 4.5 hours per spirit still run — builds the fruity, meaty new make that defines the house. Spirit enters cask at a notably high ~68.5% ABV (versus the Scottish industry norm of ~63.5%), slowing extraction and preserving original character across the full 25 years: primary maturation in American oak ex-bourbon hogsheads, followed by reshaping in Oloroso Spanish oak butts. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, natural colour.

The special feature here is structural: that elevated fill strength means the spirit has spent a quarter-century giving up its character on its own terms, not the cask's. The Oloroso finishing layer arrives late, adding dried fruit and warm spice without erasing the citrus and orchard-fruit core that the slow distillation built. Drink now through the next decade.

name: Kavalan  Concertmaster Port Cask Finish Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Port-cask-finished single malt from Yilan's subtropical heat — Taiwan's 42°C warehouses compress decades of maturation into years.
description (EN Store): On Burns Night 2010, a Kavalan expression defeated three Scotch whiskies in a blind tasting held in Leith — the moment that forced the whisky world to reckon with what subtropical heat can do to oak. The Concertmaster Port Cask Finish is the core-range expression built around that tropical-meets-Iberian proposition. Yuan Shan Distillery sits in Yuanshan Township, Yilan County, where cold air descending from the Xueshan (Snow Mountain) Range collides with humid Pacific Ocean breezes; warehouse upper floors reach 42°C in summer, driving wood extraction at a rate that renders four Taiwanese years broadly equivalent to 10–15 Scottish ones. The new-make spirit — double-distilled in Forsyths copper pot stills (12,000-litre wash, 7,000-litre spirit) from unpeated Scottish and Finnish malted barley, with a spirit cut narrowed to 65–55% ABV for added complexity — is first matured in ex-bourbon American oak before a Portuguese port barrique finish layers dried fig, dark plum, and cocoa over the house's signature mango-and-cream tropical base. What no peer in Scotland replicates: the same subtropical microclimate that accelerates maturation also preserves vivid fruit intensity, so the port influence reads as amplification rather than correction. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, and drawn from blended casks rather than a single barrel — the accessible entry point into Kavalan's port-cask architecture.

name: Kavalan  Distillery Select No.1 Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuan Shan Distillery's subtropical heat (42°C warehouses, no A/C) compresses maturation: 4–6 years equals ~15–25 Scottish. Multi-cask NAS bottled at 40% ABV.
description (EN Store):
On Burns Night 2010, a Kavalan expression defeated three Scotch whiskies in a blind tasting held in Leith, Scotland — the moment that forced the industry to reconsider whether cool, slow maturation was a prerequisite for great single malt, or merely a habit of geography. Yuan Shan Distillery sits in Yuanshan Township, Yilan County, at the collision point of cold air descending from the Xueshan (Snow Mountain) Range and humid Pacific Ocean breezes, drawing process water from Snow Mountain springs naturally filtered through gravel and sand. Summer warehouse temperatures reach 42°C on upper floors with no air-conditioning — a deliberate non-intervention that drives wood extraction at a pace unmatched in any Scottish or Irish context, compressing the equivalent of 15–25 Scottish maturation years into 4–6 Taiwanese ones.

Distillery Select No.1 is built from 100% unpeated malted barley (imported from Scotland and Finland), double-distilled in Forsyths copper pot stills with a spirit cut narrowed to 65–55% ABV to maximise ester complexity. Fermentation runs a three-stage cycle — a core alcohol round, a 20-hour ester-development pass with a dedicated 'fruit producer' yeast strain, and a final ~30-hour lactic round — totalling 60–72 hours. The resulting new-make is matured across multiple cask types including ex-bourbon American oak, ex-Oloroso, and ex-port before vatting, then bottled at 40% ABV. What distinguishes this expression within the core range is its role as an accessible entry point to the house's tropical-fruit signature — mango, pineapple, papaya, and vanilla cream — without the single-cask intensity of the Solist tier, making the distillery's subtropical terroir legible in its most transparent form.

name: Southern Comfort  Black (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smooth American whisky liqueur with vanilla, oak, and warming spice—versatile for sipping or mixing.
description (EN Store): Southern Comfort Black is a full-bodied American whisky liqueur characterized by prominent vanilla and oak notes from barrel aging, complemented by cinnamon and subtle spice. The smooth, low-acidity profile and warm finish make it approachable for both neat sipping and cocktail applications. A recognizable commercial spirit suited to casual entertaining and dessert pairings.

name: Suntory  Toki Whisky (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yamazaki, Hakushu & Chita malt-grain blend; chill-filtered at 43% ABV — Suntory's entry point into Tsukuriwake blending philosophy.
description (EN Store):
Toki — Japanese for 'time' — is the expression Suntory built specifically to demonstrate the Tsukuriwake philosophy in its most accessible form: the deliberate production of stylistically diverse spirits within a single house, then blended without trading a single cask externally. The name anchors a technical reality: every component is drawn from Suntory's own three distilleries, each operating under radically different conditions.

Yamazaki (25 m, warm-humid confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers) contributes pot-still malt matured in American white oak and ex-bourbon casks; Hakushu (708 m, granite-filtered alpine water, ~5°C cooler annual average) adds lightly peated malt fermented exclusively in wooden washbacks where lactobacillus microflora shape a distinctively herbal, green character; Chita (coastal Aichi, column-distilled corn-dominant grain at ~94% ABV) provides the structural spine. The cool Hakushu elevation slows maturation, while Yamazaki's humidity accelerates it — the blend deliberately exploits both tempos simultaneously.

Bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered, Toki is the only Suntory blended whisky positioned as an everyday expression rather than a prestige release, yet it draws from the same distillate pools as Hibiki Harmony. What no peer blend at this price tier replicates is the Hakushu wooden-washback fermentation character — lactobacillus-influenced, subtly smoky green apple — sitting alongside Yamazaki's warmer stone-fruit register.

name: Highland Park  Valfather (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Orkney's heather-peat smoke meets Oloroso sherry oak in a Viking-mythology expression from Hobbister Moor's uniquely tree-root-free peat beds.
description (EN Store): Named for Odin in his aspect as father of the slain, Valfather draws directly on Orkney's Norse heritage — a deliberate choice by Highland Park to anchor its mythology range in the distillery's actual Viking-settled landscape rather than borrowed iconography.
