The distillery sits on Mainland Orkney at Kirkwall, where cool North Sea and Atlantic air currents maintain near-constant low temperatures across the 23 on-site warehouses, slowing maturation and concentrating character over time. Peat is hand-cut from Hobbister Moor, roughly seven miles southwest — a shallow, heather-and-fern-rich deposit entirely free of tree roots, kilned to approximately 20 ppm phenol, producing aromatic floral smoke categorically different from the tarry, medicinal phenols of Islay peat. Roughly 20% of the malt is floor-malted on-site using traditional hand-turning, then blended with unpeated mainland malt before double distillation through low-reflux, pear-shaped stills that yield a medium-heavy new make. Maturation proceeds exclusively in Oloroso-seasoned European and American oak casks — both first-fill and refill — warehoused on earthen-floored dunnage stores, with a cask marrying period employed before bottling. What sets Valfather apart within the range is its amplified sherry-oak weight and smoke intensity, pushing the house's characteristic heather-honey and dried-fruit register toward darker, more resinous territory without abandoning the floral peat signature that no other distillery on earth can replicate.

name: Sexton  Single Irish Malt Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Irish single malt with vanilla oak, dark fruit spice, and subtle smoke—approachable yet layered.
description (EN Store): The Sexton Single Irish Malt Whiskey presents a medium-full bodied profile anchored by vanilla and oak character from cask maturation. Dark fruit and warming spice form the primary impression, supported by notes of smoke and cocoa that emerge on the palate. Hazelnut and subtle oak complexity round the finish, offering balance between sweetness and dryness typical of Irish whiskey tradition.

name: Douglas Laing's  The Gauldrons (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Campbeltown blended malt from Douglas Laing's Remarkable Regional Malts range — no colouring, no chill-filtration, 46% ABV minimum.
description (EN Store): Founded in Glasgow in 1948 by Fred Douglas Laing, the house built its reputation on long-standing distillery filling contracts that gave it access to casks most blenders could never source — a network now stewarded by the third generation, Fred Hamilton Laing Jr. and Cara Laing. The Gauldrons sits within the Remarkable Regional Malts range, Douglas Laing's structured, terroir-driven tier in which every component malt originates exclusively from a single Scottish region — in this case, Campbeltown, the compact peninsula on the Kintyre coast whose maritime microclimate, salt-laden Atlantic air, and proximity to the sea floor the spirit with a distinctly briny, coastal character found nowhere else in Scotland. Campbeltown's distilleries draw from local water sources filtered through ancient rock, and the region's damp, cool warehousing conditions promote slow, even maturation. Bottled at 46% ABV, without added colouring and without chill-filtration, The Gauldrons preserves the natural oils and textural weight that chill-filtration would strip — a house-wide commitment Douglas Laing adopted before it became industry convention. What sets this expression apart within its category is the sheer rarity of a regionally-pure Campbeltown blended malt: with only three active distilleries remaining in the region, assembling a multi-distillery Campbeltown vatting demands both access and patience that few independent bottlers can claim.

name: Tenjaku  Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Japanese whisky with vanilla oak and subtle spice; approachable and versatile for casual occasions.
description (EN Store): Tenjaku is a Japanese whisky offering a balanced profile centered on vanilla and oak character with supporting spice notes. The medium body and smooth finish make it accessible for both whisky newcomers and seasoned drinkers. Suitable for neat sipping, on the rocks, or in classic whisky cocktails.

name: Arran  Amarone Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Unpeated Lochranza malt finished in ex-Amarone casks — no colouring, non-chill filtered, 46% ABV. Granite-water clarity meets Valpolicella's dried-fruit density.
description (EN Store):
When Harold Currie revived legal distillation on Arran in 1995 — ending a 160-year island silence — he built the house on a deliberate transgression: an unpeated, fruit-forward malt in a category dominated by maritime smoke. That clean new make, collected at ~68% ABV from tall-necked copper pot stills with long lyne arms and shell-and-tube condensers, is the canvas onto which the Amarone Cask Finish is painted.

The spirit originates at Lochranza Distillery, Glen Eason Biorach ('Valley of the Sharp Waterfalls'), where water descends through six falls of the Easan Biorach burn, filtered through granite-dominant terrain on the northern side of the Highland Boundary Fault. That granite geology strips the water of mineral hardness, producing a soft, clean charge that amplifies the fruity congeners developed during 60–75-hour fermentations in Oregon pine washbacks using Kerry M yeast.

Primary maturation in refill ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks is followed by a finish in ex-Amarone di Valpolicella casks — vessels that previously held wine made from appassimento-dried Corvina, Rondinella, and Molinara grapes, leaving behind concentrated dried-cherry, dark chocolate, and bitter-herb residues that graft onto Arran's citrus-and-orchard-fruit core. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, with no caramel colouring added — a non-negotiable house standard across virtually the entire range.

The Amarone finish is among the more structurally assertive in Arran's wine-cask portfolio, pushing the malt toward a darker, more tannic register than the distillery's signature brightness alone would suggest.

name: Old Pulteney  25 Year (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 25 years in coastal Wick warehouses 300 m from the North Sea — worm-tub distilled, 46% ABV, shaped by salt air and ex-Bourbon oak.
description (EN Store): In 1930, Wick voted itself dry under the Temperance (Scotland) Act, forcing Pulteney Distillery to shutter for 21 years — the only major Scottish distillery ever closed by local prohibition rather than commercial failure. That defiant history frames a whisky that has always operated on its own terms. Pulteney Distillery sits on Huddart Street in the coastal town of Wick, Caithness, its dunnage warehouses with clay floors positioned approximately 300 metres from the North Sea. Persistent cold humidity and salt-laden air penetrate the casks across a quarter-century of maturation, driving a saline, briny edge that no inland Highland distillery can replicate. The wash still's truncated flat-top swan neck — cut short to fit the still-house ceiling — and its oversized boil ball force heavier, oilier spirit into the cut; worm-tub condensers (used by only a handful of Scottish distilleries) preserve sulphur-adjacent weight and texture that column or shell-and-tube condensing would strip away. The 25 YO rests in approximately 90–95% ex-Bourbon American oak refill casks alongside 5–10% ex-Oloroso Sherry Spanish oak, bottled at 46% ABV without reduction to 40%. What distinguishes this expression within the range is sheer time: two and a half decades of coastal oxidation in Caithness dunnage warehouses produce a depth of waxy, dried-fruit integration that the 18 and 21 YO expressions cannot achieve. Drinking window is open now and will hold comfortably through the mid-2030s.

name: Woodford Reserve  Double Oak (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Kentucky bourbon with dual-oak maturation: rich vanilla, caramel, and spice with layered complexity.
description (EN Store): Woodford Reserve Double Oak is a Kentucky straight bourbon finished in heavily charred new American oak barrels after initial maturation in standard barrels. The dual-oak regimen intensifies vanilla, caramel, and toasted oak character while introducing deeper spice and cocoa notes. Medium-to-full body with smooth integration of wood influence and a warming finish marked by hazelnut and subtle smoke.

name: Jack Daniel's  Apple (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tennessee Whiskey mellowed drop-by-drop through 10-ft sugar maple charcoal at Cave Spring Hollow — now infused with natural apple flavour.
description (EN Store): The technique that defines Jack Daniel's was taught to founder Jasper Newton Daniel by Nathan 'Nearest' Green, an enslaved man who served as the distillery's first Master Distiller — a contribution formally recognised only in the 2010s. That technique, the Lincoln County Process, remains the legal and stylistic dividing line between Tennessee Whiskey and bourbon: new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof in a 100-foot copper column still is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days before any barrel contact, stripping harsh congeners and producing the house's characteristic velvety texture. The base spirit is drawn from Cave Spring Hollow's iron-free limestone spring water, held at a constant 56°F year-round — iron removal being critical to clean fermentation — and built on an 80% corn / 12% malted barley / 8% rye mash bill, fermented in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash backset. Maturation follows in new charred American white oak barrels across multi-storey rickhouses where Tennessee's seasonal temperature swings drive active wood extraction. Jack Daniel's Tennessee Apple breaks the house's straight-whiskey discipline by layering natural apple flavour over the charcoal-mellowed base — a deliberate departure from the unflavoured flagship range. The result is a sweet-forward, low-friction expression best served cold; no age statement applies.

name: Loch Lomond  Single Grain Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's only single-site malt-and-grain producer runs Coffey-style column stills on 100% malted barley — a classification transgression bottled at 46% ABV.
description (EN Store): When Duncan Thomas installed straight-neck pot stills with fixed copper rectification plates at Loch Lomond in the 1960s — a design he had pioneered at Littlemill and that no other distillery has ever replicated — he also commissioned continuous column stills running an all-malted-barley mash. Under SWA rules, that output is classified as Single Grain, yet the raw material is identical to single malt production. The result is a spirit produced on the Highland Boundary Fault at Alexandria, Dunbartonshire, drawing water from Loch Lomond and maturing in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels — the same cask regime that defines the house's honey-and-vanilla signature. Fermentation in large stainless steel washbacks runs to a minimum of 92 hours, extending toward 160 hours, roughly double the Speyside industry norm, building long-chain esters that survive the column's higher rectification. Bottled at 46% ABV, this expression sits above the 40% ABV core malt range, preserving texture that chill-filtration at lower strength would strip. The special feature here is categorical: this is the only peated single grain whisky produced anywhere on earth, and the only grain whisky in Scotland made entirely from malted barley on the same campus as its malt counterpart. The no-age-statement release drinks with youthful grain sweetness framed by light oak integration, best approached now through the next three to four years.

name: Kurayoshi  SAN-IN Blended Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blended whisky from Mt. Daisen's volcanic aquifer — malt and grain sourced from undisclosed domestic and international partners, proofed in Tottori's extreme Sea of Japan climate.
description (EN Store): Matsui Shuzo, a Tottori company founded in 1910, acquired its whisky distillation license only in 2015 — meaning the San-In Blended sits in a tier built on sourced malt and grain whiskies from undisclosed domestic and international partners, not in-house distillate. That provenance gap is the buyer's first fact to hold. What the distillery does control is the water: deep-aquifer groundwater filtered through the volcanic basalt of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose mineral profile strips hardness and imparts the house's signature soft, low-astringency mouthfeel before a single cask is filled. Tottori's Sea of Japan coastal climate swings from near-freezing winters to ~38 °C summers, compressing maturation cycles in a manner the producer explicitly compares to Islay. Primary maturation is in ex-bourbon American white oak; the exact cask split, age composition, and grain-to-malt ratio are undisclosed. At the entry tier, the San-In is bottled in the 43–46% ABV range and is not confirmed as un-chill-filtered. The special feature here is geographic rather than technical: San-In is one of the few Japanese blended whiskies anchored to Japan's least populous, most geographically isolated prefecture — a deliberate regional identity play in a category dominated by Suntory and Nikka's urban-facing brands. Best approached as a highball or on-the-rocks format per the producer's own NAS guidance.

name: Kurayoshi  Pure Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blended malt from Kurayoshi's Mt. Daisen foothills — sourced Scotch malt matured in Japan, softened by volcanic-aquifer water. Transparent provenance matters here.
description (EN Store): Matsui Shuzo, founded in 1910 in Kurayoshi City, did not acquire a whisky distillation licence until 2015 — meaning the age-stated Pure Malt expressions predate any in-house distillation and are built on sourced Scotch single malt whisky matured in Japan, a provenance distinction the producer has historically understated and the Japanese whisky trade has actively debated. This is the NAS entry of that tier. The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose porous volcanic substrate filters deep-aquifer groundwater to exceptional softness — that water is the defining textural signature across every Kurayoshi expression, producing a mellow, low-mineral mouthfeel that distinguishes it from the harder-water profiles of Nikka or Suntory. Maturation relies primarily on ex-bourbon American white oak, delivering vanilla and green apple character; the extreme seasonal temperature range of Tottori's Sea of Japan coast — sub-zero winters against summers reaching 38 °C — drives accelerated cask interaction. Bottled at 43% ABV; chill-filtration status is not publicly disclosed for this expression. The critical differentiator within the Kurayoshi portfolio: this NAS Pure Malt is the most transparent entry point for understanding the house's sourced-malt style before committing to the in-house-distilled Matsui single malt tier launched in 2018.

name: Kurayoshi  8 Year Old Pure Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sourced Scotch malt matured in Japan, 46% ABV — Kurayoshi's controversial 8yr is transparent about its blended-malt provenance where rivals are not.
description (EN Store):
When Matsui Shuzo acquired its whisky distillation licence in 2015 — over a century after the company's 1910 founding — it had already been releasing age-stated Pure Malt expressions built on sourced Scotch single malt matured on Japanese soil. The 8 Year Old sits squarely in that tier: a blended malt combining imported Scotch whisky with Japanese malt, aged a minimum of eight years in ex-bourbon American white oak, then proofed and bottled at 46% ABV in Kurayoshi City, Tottori Prefecture.

The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose porous volcanic substrate filters the deep-aquifer groundwater used throughout production — a geological softening agent that pulls the spirit toward the house's characteristic mellow, fruit-forward register. Tottori's Sea of Japan coastal climate compounds this: near-freezing winters and summers reaching 38 °C create extreme seasonal temperature swings that accelerate cask interaction at a rate the producer explicitly compares to Islay maturation.

The ex-bourbon white oak regime drives the 8yr's profile toward green apple, light malt grain, and vanilla, with the volcanic-filtered water keeping the mouthfeel notably soft for the ABV. What separates this expression categorically from peers is its frank two-tier positioning: unlike the newer Matsui Single Malt sub-range (100% in-house distilled from 2017), the Kurayoshi Pure Malt tier does not claim Japanese distillation — a transparency that remains rare in a category still navigating post-2021 JSLMA labelling standards. Drink now; no meaningful cellaring upside.

name: Kurayoshi  12 Year Old Pure Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sourced-Scotch-plus-Japanese-malt blend aged 12+ yrs at Kurayoshi's Mt. Daisen foothills — almond, raisin, faint smoke at 43% ABV.
description (EN Store): Matsui Shuzo, a Tottori company founded in 1910, acquired its whisky distillation licence only in 2015 — meaning every bottle of the age-stated Kurayoshi Pure Malt range predates in-house distillation entirely. The 12 Year Old Pure Malt is a blended malt combining sourced Scotch single malt with Japanese malt, matured and proofed in Japan; the exact ratio is undisclosed, and this provenance distinction is the single most important fact a buyer should hold. Maturation takes place at the Kurayoshi Distillery in Tottori Prefecture, sited at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose porous volcanic substrate filters deep-aquifer groundwater to exceptional softness — that water character threads through the spirit as a round, low-mineral sweetness. The Sea of Japan coastal climate delivers extreme seasonal swings (near-freezing winters to 38 °C summers), accelerating cask interaction in a way the producer likens to Islay conditions. Primary maturation is in ex-bourbon American white oak; at 12+ years this draws out almond paste, dried raisin, and a faint background smoke, with the tart-sweet tension that separates this tier from the lighter 8yr. Bottled at 43% ABV; chill-filtration status is not publicly confirmed for this expression. What makes it genuinely unusual is the deliberate use of Mizunara and Sakura (cherry wood) casks elsewhere in the house toolkit — sandalwood and cherry-blossom registers absent from any Scottish-influenced peer — though their specific role in this 12yr blend is undisclosed.

name: The Matsui  Pure Malt Whisky Mizunara Cask (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Mizunara-cask Pure Malt from Mt. Daisen's volcanic aquifer — sandalwood and coconut where most Japanese malts reach for bourbon sweetness alone.
description (EN Store): Matsui Shuzo, a Tottori sake and spirits house operating since 1910, acquired its whisky distillation licence only in 2015 — meaning the Pure Malt tier, which blends sourced Scotch malt with Japanese-matured malt, predates the distillery's own copper pots and represents a deliberately different provenance from the in-house Matsui Single Malt sub-range. That distinction matters here: this is a blended malt, not a single malt, and buyers should understand the tier before the cask. What the cask does, however, is the point. Mizunara (Japanese oak) is among the rarest maturation vessels in world whisky — slow-growing, notoriously porous, difficult to cooperage — and its signature compounds (β-methyl-γ-octalactone, eugenol) drive the sandalwood, incense, and coconut register that no ex-bourbon or ex-sherry cask replicates. The spirit is proofed and matured at the Kurayoshi Distillery, set at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose deep aquifer water is naturally softened through volcanic rock filtration — the house's primary argument for its characteristically low-astringency mouthfeel. Extreme Sea of Japan seasonality (sub-zero winters to 38 °C summers) accelerates cask interaction in a way the producer explicitly compares to Islay. Bottled at 43% ABV; chill-filtration status not disclosed. The Mizunara cask finish is the special feature: an indigenous wood type so scarce that even Suntory and Nikka deploy it sparingly, here used as the defining maturation vessel rather than a finishing layer.

name: The Matsui  Pure Malt Whisky Sakura Cask (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sakura-cask finished Pure Malt from Mt. Daisen's volcanic foothills — cherry wood imparts sandalwood and blossom notes absent in any Scotch-influenced peer.
description (EN Store): Matsui Shuzo's decision to finish whisky in Sakura (cherry wood) casks — heads-swapped bourbon barrels re-coopered with cherry-wood staves — is a deliberate departure from the ex-bourbon/sherry orthodoxy that defines most Japanese and Scottish malts; no major Japanese house deploys Sakura at this scale. The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano in Tottori Prefecture whose volcanic substrate filters the deep-aquifer groundwater used in production, yielding a characteristically soft, low-mineral base spirit that amplifies rather than competes with delicate wood character. The Sea of Japan coastal climate drives extreme seasonal swings — sub-zero winters to 38 °C summers — accelerating cask interaction in a way the producer explicitly compares to Islay maturation conditions. This expression belongs to the Kurayoshi Pure Malt tier: a blended malt combining sourced Scotch single malt with Japanese malt matured in Japan, bottled at 43–46% ABV; it is distinct from the fully in-house-distilled Matsui Single Malt sub-range launched in 2018. The Sakura cask finish is the defining technical feature — cherry wood contributes sandalwood, coconut, and a faint cherry-blossom lactone profile that no ex-bourbon or Mizunara regime replicates.

name: The Matsui  Pure Malt Whisky The Peated (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Kurayoshi Distillery's peated Pure Malt, un-chill-filtered, built on Mt. Daisen volcanic-aquifer water — smoke meeting sandalwood in Japan's least-populated prefecture.
description (EN Store):
Matsui Shuzo acquired its whisky distillation licence only in 2015, making Kurayoshi Distillery one of Japan's newest operational sites — yet the Matsui Pure Malt The Peated draws on infrastructure that predates most Scottish craft distilleries: two Japanese-built copper pot stills (wash still 5,000 L; spirit still 3,000 L) fitted with adjustable lyne arms, allowing the distiller to dial reflux from heavy-oil to feather-light by rotating the arm angle — a mechanical flexibility rare even among established Japanese producers.

The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano in Tottori Prefecture whose volcanic substrate naturally filters deep-aquifer groundwater to exceptional softness. That water character underpins the house style even in a peated expression, cushioning smoke rather than amplifying it. Tottori's Sea of Japan coastal climate — sub-zero winters against ~38 °C summers — drives accelerated cask interaction the producer explicitly compares to Islay conditions.

The Peated is bottled at 43% ABV, un-chill-filtered, natural colour — the only expression in the range with confirmed non-chill-filtration, preserving the full texture of ex-bourbon American white oak maturation. Unlike the age-stated Kurayoshi Pure Malt tier (which blends sourced Scotch malt with Japanese malt), this expression sits within the Matsui sub-brand, representing genuinely in-house distilled spirit from the 2017-onwards production era.

The special feature: peat smoke filtered through Mt. Daisen volcanic-softened water and finished without chill-filtration produces a textural weight — faint waxy mouthfeel, unstripped phenolics — that most entry-tier Japanese peated expressions sacrifice for commercial clarity.

name: Kurayoshi  Umeshu Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Kurayoshi Umeshu Whisky: Mt. Daisen volcanic-filtered water meets Japanese ume plum liqueur — fruit maceration over blended malt base, 700 ml.
description (EN Store):
Matsui Shuzo, operating from the foothills of Mt. Daisen — Tottori Prefecture's 1,729 m extinct volcano — built its house style around one geological asset: deep-aquifer groundwater filtered through volcanic rock, which strips hardness and amplifies the soft, fruit-forward sweetness that defines every expression in the range. The Umeshu Whisky applies that same water philosophy to a liqueur format, macerating Japanese ume plum into a blended malt whisky base rather than the neutral spirit or shochu used by most domestic umeshu producers — a deliberate category transgression that anchors fruit character to malt complexity.

The base spirit belongs to Kurayoshi's Pure Malt tier: a blend of sourced Scotch single malt and Japanese malt whisky, matured in ex-bourbon American white oak in Tottori's extreme Sea of Japan climate, where seasonal swings from near-freezing winters to 38 °C summers accelerate cask extraction. That thermal cycling drives vanilla and dried-fruit extraction from the oak, which the ume maceration then amplifies rather than masks. Specific maceration duration, ume variety, and final ABV are not publicly disclosed by the producer.

What separates this from standard umeshu is the malt backbone: where rice-spirit or neutral-base umeshu delivers one-dimensional stone fruit, the whisky base introduces grain depth, light oak spice, and the faint almond character typical of the Kurayoshi 12yr profile — making this a crossover format with genuine whisky structure beneath the plum sweetness.

name: Aber Falls  Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Welsh single malt whisky with balanced malt sweetness, oak influence, and approachable character.
description (EN Store): Aber Falls Single Malt Whisky represents a contemporary Welsh distillery expression. The spirit opens with characteristic malty sweetness and grain-forward character, supported by vanilla and subtle oak influence from maturation. Mid-palate reveals layers of dried fruit and gentle spice, leading to a smooth, warming finish. This medium-bodied whisky balances approachability with complexity, suitable for both casual enjoyment and food pairing.

name: Kilchoman  Sanaig (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Islay single malt with bold peat smoke, coastal brine, and dark fruit complexity—a robust dram for serious whisky enthusiasts.
description (EN Store): Kilchoman Sanaig is a non-vintage Islay single malt whisky from Scotland's Islands region, characterized by a dominant peat-smoke profile balanced with underlying dark fruit and spice notes. The spirit exhibits medium-full body with medium acidity and tannin structure typical of peated malts. Secondary cask influence introduces vanilla and oak sweetness, while tertiary aging complexity develops leather, dried fruit, and subtle tobacco undertones. The whisky's robust character and smoky intensity make it well-suited to pairing with grilled and smoked meats, aged charcuterie, and hard cheeses.

name: Kavalan  Concertmaster Sherry Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yuan Shan Distillery's subtropical heat compresses maturation to a fraction of Scottish timelines — sherry-finished, tropical-dense single malt from Yilan's Snow Mountain spring water.
description (EN Store): In January 2010, a Kavalan expression defeated three Scotch whiskies and one English whisky in a blind tasting held in Leith on Burns Night — a result that forced the industry to reckon with what subtropical heat does to oak maturation. The Concertmaster Sherry Finish is the core-range expression of that argument. Yuan Shan Distillery sits at the intersection of cold air descending from the Xueshan (Snow Mountain) Range and humid Pacific Ocean breezes in Yuanshan Township, Yilan County; warehouse upper floors reach 42°C in summer, driving wood extraction at a rate that renders four Taiwanese years broadly equivalent to 10–15 Scottish ones. Spirit is distilled from unpeated malted barley (imported from Scotland and Finland) through Forsyths copper pot stills, with a spirit cut narrowed to 65–55% ABV to maximise ester complexity. Fermentation runs a three-stage cycle — a core fermenter for alcohol yield, a 'fruit producer' yeast strain for ester development, and a final lactic round — totalling 60–72 hours. The new-make is matured in multiple cask types before a deliberate sherry-cask finish layers dried fruit and spice over the distillery's signature mango-and-papaya tropical base. What distinguishes the Concertmaster within Kavalan's lineup is precisely this layering strategy: sherry complexity grafted onto a tropical-fruit chassis that no Scottish or Irish distillery can replicate at equivalent age. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, and built for accessibility without sacrificing the house's defining creaminess — sourced from Snow Mountain spring water filtered through gravel and sand.

name: Royal Salute  30 Year Old Key To The Kingdom (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 30-year-old blended Scotch from Strathisla's Royal Salute Vault — every component, malt and grain, aged a minimum three decades in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry oak.
description (EN Store): Royal Salute was born on 2 June 1953 to mark the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II — a founding purpose that locked in an absolute minimum of 21 years' maturation across every expression in the portfolio, a standard the 30 Year Old Key To The Kingdom extends by a full decade. Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Moray — the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands, established 1786 — anchors the blend, with process water drawn from the Fons Bulliens Well, a peat-free spring prized for its softness and mineral clarity. The Speyside valley's mild, humid microclimate slows evaporation and encourages the gradual extraction of wood sugars over the extended maturation period. All component whiskies — malt and grain alike — spend a minimum of 30 years in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry casks, held in the dedicated Royal Salute Vault within Strathisla. Strathisla's double distillation through short, squat pot stills (wash stills at 12,500 litres, spirit stills at 8,500 litres) produces a characteristically heavy, waxy new-make that absorbs three decades of oak without losing its fruit core. The defining transgression here: Royal Salute is the only Scotch whisky portfolio where even the grain whisky component carries a 21-year floor — at 30 years, that guarantee extends to every single cask in the vatting. Bottled at 40% ABV and presented in a hand-crafted ceramic flagon authenticated by a Wade Ceramics hallmark, requiring up to six days of production from Cornish clay with double-layer glazing.

name: Nikka  Taketsuru Pure Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blended malt from Yoichi's coal-fired stills and Miyagikyo's steam-heated pot stills — Taketsuru's deliberate two-distillery palette in a single glass.
description (EN Store):
When Masataka Taketsuru founded Yoichi in 1934 and later Miyagikyo in 1969, he did so with a precise structural logic: Japanese regulations forbid distillate exchange between competing groups, so Taketsuru built two distilleries with diametrically opposed flavour profiles to give his blenders a complete palette in-house. Taketsuru Pure Malt is the direct expression of that architecture — a blended malt combining casks from both sites, with zero grain whisky.

Yoichi sits at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, facing the Sea of Japan, its water drawn from Yoichi River snowmelt through a cool, fog-laden maritime microclimate. Its straight-head pot stills with downward lyne arms, fired directly by coal — one of the last distilleries globally to retain this method — retain heavier congeners and pronounced maritime character. Miyagikyo, set in a forested valley fed by the Hirose and Nikkawa rivers from the Zao Mountain Range, uses steam-heated pot stills with bulging necks and upward lyne arms, promoting reflux and a lighter, fruit-forward spirit from water among the softest of any Japanese distillery.

The special feature here is structural: no other major Japanese producer commands this breadth of in-house malt contrast — peated coastal weight against delicate floral softness — without sourcing outside the group. The NV blend is consistent in character, designed for depth over vintage variation, and drinks well now with room to open further over 1–2 hours in glass.

name: Kyoto Whisky  Kuro-Obi (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Japanese whisky with balanced oak, vanilla, and dark fruit—approachable yet layered.
description (EN Store): Kyoto Whisky Kuro-Obi represents a contemporary Japanese whisky expression, crafted with attention to balance and refinement. The nose opens with toasted oak and vanilla, followed by dark fruit and warming spice notes. On the palate, medium-full body carries caramel sweetness and hazelnut, with a subtle smoke character adding depth. The finish reveals dried fruit and gentle oak tannins, creating a rounded, contemplative dram suitable for both casual enjoyment and food pairing.

name: Highland Park  15 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Orkney's Hobbister Moor heather-peat — the only such source on earth — meets 15 years in ex-Oloroso sherry casks on a windswept North Sea hillside.
description (EN Store): Highland Park's origin story begins with illicit distilling on Orkney's Holm Road hillside — a site so geographically isolated that its defining raw material, heather-peat hand-cut from Hobbister Moor seven miles southwest, cannot be replicated anywhere else on the planet. Trials with mainland peated malt confirmed it: the whisky simply changes. Orkney's peat is shallow, tree-root-free, and dense with heather and fern, kilned to ~20 ppm phenol — producing aromatic floral smoke entirely unlike the medicinal, tarry character of Islay. The distillery draws from a hard-mineral spring source, unusual among Scotch producers, and matures in 19 traditional earthen-floor dunnage warehouses under Orkney's cool, consistent North Sea maritime climate — conditions that slow oxidation and preserve aromatic complexity across the full 15-year span. Roughly 20% of the malt is floor-malted on-site using traditional hand-turning — one of only a handful of Scottish distilleries still doing so — then vatted with ~80% unpeated mainland malt before double distillation through low-reflux, pear-shaped stills that yield a medium-heavy new make. Since approximately 2004, all casks used are ex-Oloroso sherry-seasoned European and American oak, both first-fill and refill, sourced directly from Spanish producers. What distinguishes the 15-year expression within the core range is the additional maturation time beyond the 12 YO, allowing the sherry-oak dried-fruit register to fully absorb and soften the heather-smoke without either element dominating the other.

name: Famous Grouse  Smoky Black Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Smoky Scottish blended whisky with peat, dark fruit, and warming spice—a robust dram for grilled meats and evening relaxation.
description (EN Store):
Famous Grouse Smoky Black is a blended Scotch whisky from Scotland, crafted to deliver pronounced smoke and peat character. The nose opens with heather and peat smoke, followed by dark fruit and subtle vanilla oak. On the palate, smoke dominates the primary impression, supported by warming spice, leather, and a hint of cocoa. The finish is long and warming, with lingering smoke and oak tannins.

This medium-full-bodied spirit pairs excellently with grilled red meats, smoked brisket, and BBQ preparations. It also complements cured charcuterie and works as a contemplative aperitif neat or with a splash of water.

name: Highland Park  10 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Orkney's only distillery floor-malts with heather-peat from Hobbister Moor — producing floral smoke no mainland peat can replicate. Sherry-oak sweetness, maritime salt.
description (EN Store):
Highland Park traces its origins to 1798 on a hillside above Kirkwall, Orkney — a site so geographically isolated that its defining raw material, heather-peat hand-cut from Hobbister Moor (~7 miles southwest), cannot be substituted without fundamentally altering the whisky. Trials with mainland peated malt confirmed exactly that. Orkney's peat is shallow, tree-root-free, and dense with heather and fern, kilned to approximately 20 ppm phenol — producing aromatic floral smoke that is neither medicinal nor tarry, a character impossible to source elsewhere on earth.

Roughly 20% of annual malt is floor-malted on-site using traditional hand-turning, blended with ~80% unpeated mainland malt. Fermentation runs 50–80 hours in 12 Oregon pine washbacks (29,200 L each), and double distillation through low-reflux, pear-shaped stills yields a medium-heavy new make. The 10 Year Old matures exclusively in Oloroso-seasoned European and American oak casks — first-fill and refill — in 19 dunnage warehouses with earthen floors, where Orkney's cool, temperate maritime climate slows extraction and preserves freshness. Water is drawn from a hard-mineral spring on Mainland Orkney, unusual among Scotch distilleries and a quiet contributor to the spirit's texture. The result sits at 40% ABV, vatted and married before bottling to integrate the heather-smoke and sherry-fruit axes that define the house.

name: Tomintoul  Merlot Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside single malt from 345 m Ballindalloch, primary ex-Bourbon maturation finished in Merlot casks — fruit-forward transgression from Tomintoul's 'gentle dram' house.
description (EN Store): Founded as blending stock for Whyte & Mackay and largely invisible as a single malt for 35 years, Tomintoul only began expressing its full range of cask creativity after Angus Dundee acquired the distillery in 2000 — the Merlot Cask Finish is a direct product of that liberated ambition. The distillery sits at approximately 345 m altitude near Ballindalloch in the Cairngorms National Park, drawing process water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a structural reason the new make spirit runs light and clean at ~69% ABV collection strength. That delicate cereal-and-orchard-fruit base is built on long fermentation (54–60 hours) in stainless-steel washbacks and high-reflux distillation through steam-heated stills, then laid down in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks for primary maturation before transfer into Merlot wine casks for finishing. The Merlot finish is the transgression: where Tomintoul's core range stays resolutely pale and malty, the red-wine wood drives dark berry character and a soft tannic grip onto the distillery's otherwise feather-light Speyside frame. As a non-vintage expression, it draws from Tomintoul's four on-site warehouses holding up to 75,000 casks, allowing the blending team to select parcels where the Merlot influence integrates rather than overwhelms the house's signature gentleness.

name: Tomintoul  Pinot Noir Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside single malt finished in Pinot Noir casks — Ballantruan Spring water, 345 m altitude, light spirit architecture meeting red-fruit wood influence.
description (EN Store):
Tomintoul spent its first 35 years as near-invisible blending stock for Whyte & Mackay — a paradox for a distillery running 3.3 million litres annually from the Cairngorms National Park. When Angus Dundee took ownership in 2000, the house pivoted toward a cask-finishing programme that now spans Oloroso, port, Madeira, white port, Tempranillo, and Pinot Noir — the latter being the expression here.

The distillery sits at 345 m altitude near Ballindalloch, Banffshire, drawing process water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct contributor to the signature light, high-reflux spirit. Long fermentation of 54–60 hours in stainless-steel washbacks and new make collected at ~69% ABV build a clean, cereal-forward base before any wood influence begins.

Primary maturation proceeds in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, establishing the vanilla and orchard-fruit framework. The Pinot Noir cask finish then layers red-berry and subtle tannic grip onto that gentle Speyside architecture — a finishing wood choice that remains uncommon in the category, where sherry and port dominate. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered for the core range.

The Pinot Noir finish is the rarest wood deployment in Tomintoul's portfolio, introducing a Burgundian red-fruit register that no standard Speyside house replicates at this scale. Drink now; no declared vintage.

name: Tomintoul  Cognac Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside single malt from 345m Cairngorms altitude, finished in Cognac casks — the 'gentle dram' taken into brandy territory.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1964 by Glasgow whisky traders Hay & Macleod and W. & S. Strong, Tomintoul spent its first 35 years almost entirely as anonymous blending stock — a paradox for a distillery running 3.3 million litres annually at Ballindalloch, Banffshire. When Angus Dundee acquired it in 2000, the house began systematically exploring cask finishing as its primary identity statement. The Cognac Cask Finish is among the most laterally ambitious in that programme.

Situated at approximately 345 metres in the Cairngorms National Park, the distillery draws water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct contributor to the light, high-reflux spirit character that makes cask influence so legible here. New make is collected at ~69% ABV from steam-heated pot stills, then matured primarily in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks before transfer into Cognac casks for finishing — a fruit-eau-de-vie wood source Tomintoul deploys alongside Oloroso, Madeira, Pinot Noir, and Tempranillo across its range.

What distinguishes this expression within Speyside is the Cognac cask itself: Limousin or Tronçais oak previously seasoned with French brandy introduces dried apricot, beeswax, and faint rancio layers onto a malt base that, by design, offers minimal resistance. The result is a finishing experiment where the wood does unusually visible work on an intentionally delicate canvas.

name: Tomintoul  Pedro Ximenez Sherry Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside's 'gentle dram' finished in Pedro Ximénez sherry casks — soft Ballantruan Spring water meets raisin-dense Andalusian oak at 345 m altitude.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1964 by Glasgow whisky traders Hay & Macleod and W. & S. Strong, Tomintoul spent its first 35 years almost entirely as anonymous blending stock — a paradox for a distillery running 3.3 million litres annually. The Pedro Ximénez finish is among the most assertive moves in its cask programme, layering the distillery's deliberately light new make with one of sherry's most viscous, sugar-saturated wood types.

Situated at 345 m altitude in the Livet Glen within Cairngorms National Park, the distillery draws water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct architect of the featherweight spirit that makes PX finishing so legible: there is little structural weight to compete with the cask's contribution.

The base spirit is produced from unpeated malted barley, collected at approximately 69% ABV off steam-heated pot stills, with fermentation running 54–60 hours in stainless-steel washbacks. Primary maturation proceeds in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, after which the spirit enters Pedro Ximénez sherry casks for its finish — PX being the sweetest, most concentrated wood in Tomintoul's finishing arsenal, which also spans Oloroso, Madeira, port, Pinot Noir, and Tempranillo.

What distinguishes this expression is the tension between the distillery's signature low-weight, high-reflux spirit and PX's near-syrup density — a deliberate mismatch that produces saturation without the structural tannin grip of Oloroso. Drink now through the medium term; no age statement, bottled at 40% ABV with chill filtration.

name: Tomintoul  Tawny Port Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside's 'gentle dram' from 345 m altitude, finished in Tawny Port casks — soft Ballantruan Spring water meets oxidative wood.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1964 by Glasgow whisky traders Hay & Macleod and W. & S. Strong, Tomintoul spent its first 35 years almost entirely as anonymous blending stock — a distillery of 3.3 million litres annual capacity that the broader whisky world barely knew existed. The cask-finishing programme that emerged after Angus Dundee's 2000 acquisition is, in many ways, the first time the distillery's character has been allowed to speak for itself.

Situated at approximately 345 m altitude in the Livet Glen within Cairngorms National Park, the distillery draws water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct architectural reason for the light, low-phenol new make spirit. That delicacy is engineered further by high-reflux copper stills, steam heating, and fermentation of 54–60 hours in stainless-steel washbacks, producing spirit collected at approximately 69% ABV.

Primary maturation proceeds in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, building vanilla and cereal structure before the Tawny Port cask finish layers dried red fruit, walnut, and oxidative spice — a finishing regime that sits within Tomintoul's unusually broad cask portfolio spanning Oloroso, Madeira, Pinot Noir, and Tempranillo wood. The core range is bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered. What distinguishes this expression is the Tawny Port finish applied to one of Speyside's most deliberately lightweight base spirits — the oxidative richness of the wood meets almost no resistance, producing a textural contrast few Speyside peers attempt.

name: Tomintoul  White Port Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside's 'gentle dram' finished in white port casks — soft Ballantruan Spring water meets oxidative sweetness at 345 m altitude.
description (EN Store): Founded as blending stock for Whyte & Mackay and largely invisible as a single malt for 35 years, Tomintoul only began expressing its full range of cask creativity after Angus Dundee acquired the distillery in 2000 — the white port finish is a direct product of that creative freedom. Situated at approximately 345 m in the Livet Glen near the River Avon within Cairngorms National Park, the distillery draws process water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a geological condition that directly enables the high-reflux distillation style and the famously light, clean new make spirit. Primary maturation proceeds in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, building the house's characteristic vanilla and orchard-cereal base before the white port cask finish layers in dried stone fruit, almond paste, and a gentle oxidative sweetness without the tannic weight of red port wood. The core range is bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, with E150a colouring — a transparency point worth noting against the single-cask releases bottled at natural cask strength without filtration. What distinguishes this expression within the Tomintoul portfolio is the white port vessel itself: among a finishing programme that spans Oloroso, Madeira, Pinot Noir, and Tempranillo, white port is the lightest oxidative intervention, preserving the distillery's 'gentle dram' identity rather than overwriting it. Drink now through the near term; no declared vintage.

name: Tomintoul  Caribbean Rum Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside's 'gentle dram' — ex-Bourbon new make from Ballantruan Spring water, finished in Caribbean rum casks at 345 m altitude in the Cairngorms.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1964 by Glasgow whisky traders Hay & Macleod and W. & S. Strong, Tomintoul spent its first 35 years almost entirely as anonymous blending stock — a paradox for a distillery running 3.3 million litres annually from one of Speyside's highest sites. The Caribbean Rum Cask Finish is a direct product of the creative finishing programme Angus Dundee Distillers unlocked after acquiring the distillery in 2000, deploying cask types — rum, Oloroso, Madeira, Pinot Noir — that the previous Whyte & Mackay ownership never pursued.

Situated at approximately 345 m elevation near Ballindalloch, Banffshire, within the Cairngorms National Park, the distillery draws water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct contributor to the light, low-congener new make spirit collected at ~69% ABV via high-reflux pot stills. Primary maturation proceeds in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, building the vanilla and orchard-fruit base before the rum cask finish layers tropical and brown-sugar register onto the cereal-light Speyside frame. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered with E150a colouring for consistency. The special feature here is structural contrast: rum-cask sweetness imposed on one of Speyside's most deliberately delicate distillates — a house that built its identity on restraint, then finished it in the Caribbean.

name: Chivas Regal  13 Years Sherry Cask Selection Blended Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside blend finished on a higher proportion of Oloroso sherry casks — Strathisla's short-necked stills at the core, 13 years minimum.
description (EN Store): When Master Blender Sandy Hyslop specified a higher proportion of Oloroso sherry casks for the Extra 13, he was deliberately pulling against Chivas Regal's own house signature — a blend built since 1909 on creamy orchard fruit and honey rather than dried-fruit weight. The result is the most sherry-saturated expression in the standard Chivas lineup. Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Speyside — founded 1786, the oldest continuously operating Highland distillery — anchors the malt component. Its short-necked pot stills allow heavier vapours to rise, producing a full-bodied new make from water drawn exclusively from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite one mile away. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks, providing thermal insulation for a steady, flavour-building two-day ferment. The 13-year minimum age statement denotes only the youngest component; older Longmorn, Miltonduff, and Braeval malts are present but unlabelled. Grain whisky from Strathclyde Distillery, Glasgow provides structural backbone. What sets this expression apart within the range is its sherry-cask emphasis at a younger age statement than the 15 or 18 — a deliberate inversion of the house logic that typically reserves first-fill cask intensity for shorter maturations. Drink now through the next five years; the dried-fruit density is at its most expressive without further oxidative softening.

name: Chivas Regal  13 Years Tequila Cask Selection Blended Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Speyside blend finished in tequila casks — Strathisla's short-neck pot stills meet agave-seasoned oak for a rare cross-spirit bridge.
description (EN Store):
Master Blender Sandy Hyslop's deliberate rule-break: finishing a Speyside-anchored blend in tequila casks, a vessel with no precedent in the Chivas Regal lineage. The brand home, Strathisla Distillery in Keith, Moray — the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands, drawing process water from the granite-filtered Broomhill Spring — contributes the heart malt. Its notably short-necked pot stills allow heavier vapours to rise, producing a full-bodied, generous new make that can absorb assertive cask influence without losing structure.


The blend draws on Strathisla, Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Allt-a-Bhainne, Miltonduff, and Braeval malts alongside Strathclyde grain, all matured a minimum of 13 years — the age statement denoting the youngest component, with older whiskies present unlabelled. The tequila cask finish introduces agave-derived compounds — earthy, waxy, subtly vegetal — layered over the house signature of orchard fruit, heather honey, and hazelnut from ex-bourbon and ex-Oloroso sherry oak. Bottled at 40% ABV; chill-filtration and colouring practices are not publicly disclosed by the producer.


What no peer in the blended Scotch category does: using tequila cask seasoning to bridge Scottish grain sweetness with Mexican agave character — a cross-spirit transgression that repositions the 13-year tier well outside the standard sherry-or-bourbon binary. Drink now through the near term; the agave influence is most vivid in the first few years after release.

name: Glendalough  Pot Still Irish Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Triple-distilled Wicklow pot still Irish whiskey, finished up to 1 year in virgin indigenous Irish oak (Dar Ghalda), non-chill filtered at 43% ABV.
description (EN Store): Founded in 2011 by five partners determined to revive craft distilling in Ireland, Glendalough made a deliberate break from convention by finishing its Pot Still expression in virgin indigenous Irish oak (Dar Ghalda) — a wood type with a larger cell structure than American or European oak, accelerating extraction of vanilla, chocolate, and spice in ways no standard cask regime replicates. The distillery draws process water from Wicklow Mountain springs, approximately 40 km south of Dublin, where a mild maritime climate on Ireland's east coast moderates maturation and preserves aromatic lift through the aging cycle. Production follows a 2:1 ratio of unmalted to malted Irish barley, triple-distilled through copper pot stills — a wash still of 250L with constriction, and a spirit still fitted with a reflux ball and ascending lyne arm — maximising copper contact and driving a clean, fruit-forward new make. The spirit enters cask at 62.5% ABV, matures for 3 years in ex-Bourbon barrels, then receives up to 1 year of finishing in virgin Dar Ghalda casks before bottling non-chill filtered at 43% ABV, with each bottle individually numbered by tree, cask, and bottle. The use of native Irish oak for finishing is a category first — no other major Irish whiskey producer has deployed this wood commercially at scale. Ready to drink now; the Dar Ghalda finish rewards decanting.

name: Glendalough  Double Barrel Irish Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wicklow Mountain spring water cuts this Coffey-still single grain: ~3.5yr ex-Bourbon then 6-month first-fill Oloroso Sherry finish, 42% ABV.
description (EN Store): Founded in 2011 by five partners who named their venture after Glendalough — Gleann Dá Loch, the 'Valley of the Two Lakes' — the distillery built its identity around the Wicklow Mountains as a literal ingredient, drawing process and cutting water from mountain springs roughly 40 km south of Dublin. That mild east-coast maritime climate slows temperature swings in the warehouse, moderating extraction and preserving the lighter grain character the house deliberately pursues. The Double Barrel is a single grain whiskey: a mash of Irish malted barley and corn, continuously distilled in a Coffey column still, which produces a lighter, higher-rectified spirit than the pot-still dominant style of Ireland's larger distilleries — a deliberate transgression of the country's heavier heritage. Maturation runs approximately 3.5 years in first-fill American oak ex-Bourbon barrels, followed by roughly 6 months in first-fill Spanish Oloroso Sherry casks, the Sherry finish layering dried fruit and spice over the grain's natural sweetness. Bottled at 42% ABV, cut with purified Wicklow Mountain mineral water; NAS. What sets this expression apart within Irish whiskey is the dual first-fill regime — both primary and finishing casks are first-fill, maximising wood-sugar and oxidative contribution at each stage without the diluting effect of re-used staves. The result is a fruit-forward, approachable grain whiskey where the Oloroso influence is immediate rather than background.

name: Glendalough  Grand Cru Burgundy Cask Finish Single Cask Irish Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single cask Irish whiskey from Wicklow's craft pioneer, finished in Grand Cru Burgundy barrels — a category-defying move from Ireland's first modern craft distillery.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 2011 by five partners who drew their water from Wicklow Mountain springs and named their distillery after a 6th-century monastic valley — Gleann Dá Loch, 'Valley of the Two Lakes' — Glendalough has consistently broken Irish whiskey convention. This single cask expression pushes further still: a Grand Cru Burgundy cask finish applied to Irish whiskey is virtually uncharted territory in the category, where ex-Bourbon and Oloroso Sherry dominate by default.

Distilled in a Coffey column still from a mash bill of Irish malted barley and corn, the spirit enters oak at 62.5% ABV, building its primary character in American oak ex-Bourbon barrels before transfer into a single Grand Cru Burgundy cask for finishing. The Pinot Noir-seasoned wood introduces red fruit extraction — dried cherry, raspberry reduction — layered over the grain's inherent cereal sweetness, while the porous Burgundian oak integrates without overwhelming.

Special feature: the single-cask bottling format means no vatting, no blending to house style — this is one cask's unmediated conversation between Wicklow grain spirit and Grand Cru Pinot Noir wood. Glendalough's maritime Wicklow climate, mild and east-coast moderated, slows oxidative exchange through the wood, preserving aromatic lift that a warmer warehouse would sacrifice. Drink now through the near term; the Burgundy influence is at its most expressive before further integration softens the fruit-forward edge.

name: Glendalough  Canteiro Aged Madeira Cask Finish Single Cask Irish Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Wicklow Mountains single cask Irish whiskey finished in Canteiro-aged Madeira casks — a rare oxidative wood layer atop ex-Bourbon grain spirit.
description (EN Store): Founded in 2011 in the shadow of the Wicklow Mountains, Glendalough built its reputation on challenging Irish whiskey's cask orthodoxy — first with indigenous Irish oak, then Mizunara, and now with Canteiro-aged Madeira casks, a finishing wood virtually unseen in the Irish category. Canteiro maturation is Madeira's slow, heat-driven oxidative process, where fortified wine ages in casks exposed to natural warmth rather than the estufagem heat-tank method — meaning the wood arriving at Glendalough carries concentrated rancio, dried fruit, and saline oxidative character baked in over years. The base spirit is single grain Irish whiskey, Coffey-still distilled from a mash of Irish malted barley and corn, entering oak at 62.5% ABV for primary maturation in first-fill American oak ex-Bourbon barrels before transfer to this single Madeira cask for finishing. Bottled as a single cask expression, output is by definition extremely limited, and cask variation means this release stands entirely apart from the Double Barrel core range. The Canteiro finish layers sun-dried grape, walnut oil, and briny oxidative depth onto the grain spirit's naturally lighter, cereal-forward frame — a structural contrast no standard Irish grain whiskey achieves. Drink now through the near term; the Madeira influence will be most vivid in the first years after bottling.

name: Glendalough  Calvados XO Cask Finish Single Cask Irish Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Single cask Irish whiskey finished in Calvados XO casks — Wicklow Mountain spring water, craft-distilled at Ireland's first modern craft distillery.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 2011 by five partners in the shadow of the Wicklow Mountains, Glendalough was built on a deliberate transgression: where the major Irish houses leaned on ex-Bourbon and ex-Sherry alone, this distillery began experimenting with finishing casks that had no precedent in the Irish whiskey canon. The Calvados XO Cask Finish Single Cask expression is one such departure — a single grain whiskey finished in casks that previously held aged Calvados XO, layering Norman apple-brandy character over the base spirit's grain-sweet core.

The distillery draws process water from Wicklow Mountain springs, approximately 40 km south of Dublin on Ireland's east coast. The mild maritime climate of this eastern valley moderates maturation rates, preserving delicate grain character through the aging cycle. Base spirit is Coffey (column) still distilled from a mash bill of Irish malted barley and corn — continuous distillation yielding a lighter, fruit-forward new make that enters cask at 62.5% ABV. The Calvados XO cask finish imparts dried apple, pear eau-de-vie, and oxidative orchard-fruit complexity that the house's standard Oloroso finish does not. As a single cask bottling, this is unrepeatable — no blending across casks, no averaging of character.

The drinking window is open now; single grain Irish whiskey at this profile is approachable on release and does not require further cellaring.

name: Jack Daniel's  Apple (350 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Tennessee Whiskey flavored with apple; Lincoln County charcoal-mellowed at Cave Spring Hollow, bottled at 35% ABV on an 80% corn mash base.
description (EN Store): The story of Jack Daniel's Apple begins at Cave Spring Hollow in Lynchburg, Moore County, Tennessee — a limestone cave spring locked at a constant 56°F that supplies iron-free water, the same source used for every expression in the distillery's lineup. That iron-free, calcium-rich water underpins the house's characteristically soft, sweet-forward profile before a single grain is mashed. The 80% corn, 12% malted barley, 8% rye mash bill ferments in open-top cypress vats using a sour mash process (up to 30% backset for pH control), then is distilled in a 100-foot copper column still to approximately 140 proof, with copper contact stripping sulfur compounds throughout. Before any barrel contact, the new-make is gravity-fed through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days — the Lincoln County Process that legally and stylistically separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon. The resulting mellowed spirit is aged in new, charred American white oak barrels across multi-storey rickhouses, then blended with natural apple flavoring to produce this expression at 35% ABV. What sets this apart within the flavored whiskey category is its foundation: a fully Lincoln County Process-mellowed Tennessee Whiskey base, not a neutral spirit, meaning the charcoal-derived velvety texture persists beneath the apple character. Best served chilled or over ice; no aging window applies.

name: Jack Daniel's  Old No.7 (375 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cave Spring Hollow limestone water + 10-foot sugar maple charcoal vats: the Lincoln County Process that legally separates Tennessee Whiskey from bourbon.
description (EN Store):
The craft behind Old No. 7 was originally taught to Jack Daniel by Nathan 'Nearest' Green — an enslaved man who served as the distillery's first Master Distiller, a contribution erased from official histories for over a century and only formally recognized in the 2010s. That foundational technique, the Lincoln County Process, remains the defining act: new-make spirit distilled to 140 proof in a 100-foot copper column still is gravity-fed drop-by-drop through 10-foot vats of handmade sugar maple charcoal over 3–5 days before any barrel contact, stripping harsh congeners in a step that no standard bourbon undergoes.

The distillery sits in Cave Spring Hollow, Lynchburg, Moore County, anchored by a limestone spring locked at a constant 56°F (13°C). Limestone filtration removes iron — detrimental to whiskey flavor — while contributing calcium and magnesium to the water profile. Barrels age across multi-storey rickhouses, where Tennessee's seasonal temperature swings drive active wood interaction; upper floors accelerate extraction while lower floors develop slower, subtler character. Old No. 7 is blended from 175–200 barrels per batch to balance these extremes, then bottled at 40% ABV after chill filtration. The mash bill — 80% corn, 12% malted barley, 8% rye — runs through a sour mash process with up to 30% backset for pH and yeast consistency. The result is a sweet-forward, velvety profile that is technically bourbon-compliant under federal law yet deliberately refuses that label.

name: Nikka  Days Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Nikka Days: a light, approachable blend drawing from coal-fired Yoichi and steam-pot Miyagikyo — Taketsuru's dual-distillery palette in its most accessible form.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru founded Yoichi in 1934 on Hokkaido's northwest coast, he deliberately built a second distillery in 1969 at Miyagikyo — not for scale, but because Japanese regulations forbid distillate exchange between competing groups, forcing him to engineer his own full blending spectrum in-house. Nikka Days is the expression that draws most directly on that architectural decision. Yoichi sits at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, cooled by Sea of Japan maritime air and river fog from snowmelt off the Yoichi River; its direct coal-fired pot stills with downward lyne arms — one of the last such operations on earth — retain heavier congeners and a faint coastal smokiness. Miyagikyo, set in a forested valley fed by the Hirose and Nikkawa rivers from the Zao Mountain Range, uses steam-heated pot stills with bulging necks and upward lyne arms that promote reflux, yielding a lighter, fruit-forward spirit from water ranked among the softest of any Japanese distillery. Days blends these two malt profiles with Coffey grain distilled on Glasgow-built continuous stills, bottled at 40% ABV with standard chill-filtration for clarity and consistency. What sets Days apart within the Nikka range is its explicit orientation toward drinkability over complexity — a deliberate inversion of the From the Barrel philosophy — making it the only Nikka blend engineered specifically around low-dilution, high-frequency consumption. Drink now through the near term; no cellaring required.

name: Nikka  Yoichi Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Yoichi's rare direct coal-fired pot stills — last of their kind in Japan — forge a heavy, maritime single malt from Hokkaido's Sea of Japan coastline.
description (EN Store):
When Masataka Taketsuru founded the Yoichi Distillery in 1934 at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, he chose this site precisely because its cool, fog-laden maritime microclimate and peat-rich surroundings mirrored the Scottish Highlands he had trained in — a deliberate act of terroir transplantation that shaped every technical decision that followed.

Yoichi sits where mountains on three sides funnel sea-breeze and river fog off the Sea of Japan, with distillery water drawn from snowmelt via the Yoichi River. That cold, mineral-laden water, combined with the coastal saline air during maturation, drives the malt's characteristic weight and coastal character.

The defining technical signature is direct coal-fired distillation through straight-head pot stills with downward lyne arms — the same configuration once used at Longmorn in Scotland. Coal firing generates intense, uneven heat that retains heavier congeners and complex sulphurous compounds that steam-heated stills would drive off. The result is a spirit of unusual density and textural grip before a single day in cask.

Yoichi is now one of the last distilleries anywhere in the world to use direct coal-fire — a method abandoned globally for being labour-intensive and technically demanding — making this expression categorically unreplicable by any peer.

As a non-vintage expression, the house style prioritises consistency of that heavy, peated maritime profile; the drinking window is open now and will reward cellaring of 5–10 years for further integration.

name: Nikka  Tailored Blended Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blended whisky from Nikka's dual-distillery system — coal-fired Yoichi malt meets Coffey grain from Miyagikyo's continuous stills.
description (EN Store): When Masataka Taketsuru founded Nikka in 1934, Japanese regulations barring distillate exchange between competing groups forced a singular solution: build two distilleries with opposing flavour profiles and blend entirely in-house. Nikka Tailored is the direct expression of that architecture. Yoichi, on Hokkaido's northwest coast at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, draws snowmelt from the Yoichi River through a cool, sea-misted maritime microclimate historically rich in local peat and coal — conditions Taketsuru chose to replicate Scottish Highland terroir. Its malt is distilled in straight-head pot stills with downward lyne arms over direct coal fire, a method retained from Longmorn, Scotland, that preserves heavier congeners and amplifies coastal, smoky complexity. Miyagikyo's contribution arrives via Coffey continuous stills — originally built by Blairs Ltd. in Glasgow — using a corn-dominant grain bill that delivers sweetness and a creamy mid-palate. The two components are married at Nikka's Tochigi facility before bottling. What sets this apart within the blended whisky category is the structural impossibility of replication: no outside distillate enters the blend, and Yoichi remains one of the last distilleries globally still operating direct coal-fired pot stills — labour-intensive, difficult to control, and categorically irreplaceable.

name: Suntory  Hibiki Japanese Harmony 100th Anniversary Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Hibiki Harmony in a 24-faceted crystal-cut decanter marking Suntory's centenary — Mizunara, sakura, and five-cask architecture in a single commemorative pour.
description (EN Store):
When Shinjirō Torii broke ground at Yamazaki in 1923 — at the mist-laden confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers outside Kyoto — he was building Japan's first malt whisky distillery on water historically drawn by tea master Sen no Rikyū for its exceptional softness. The 100th Anniversary Limited Edition of Hibiki Harmony commemorates that founding act in a release that distils the entire Suntory estate into a single decanter.

Hibiki is constructed from malt whiskies drawn from two geographically opposed sites: Yamazaki (25 m elevation, warm humid microclimate, accelerated maturation in soft river-fed aquifer water) and Hakushu (708 m in the Southern Japanese Alps, granite-filtered water, cool air averaging ~5°C below Yamazaki, slower extraction), blended with Chita grain whisky (corn-dominant, column-distilled to ~94% ABV on the Aichi Peninsula coast). Chita grain constitutes approximately three-quarters of the blend by volume, providing the structural canvas onto which the malt components are layered.

The blend draws simultaneously from five cask types: American white oak ex-bourbon, Spanish oak ex-Oloroso sherry, new American oak, ex-wine casks, and rare Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica) — a wood so porous it is cooperaged almost nowhere else in world whisky, yet irreplaceable for its sandalwood, incense, and kyara aromatics. The sakura (cherry) cask component, also unique to Suntory's palette, adds a delicate floral register found in no Scotch or Irish equivalent.

The special feature here is the vessel itself: the commemorative decanter replicates the 24-faceted design of the original Hibiki bottle — each facet representing one of the 24 Japanese seasonal micro-divisions (nijūshi sekki) — pressed in heavier crystal-weight glass exclusive to this centenary edition, making it a collectible object as much as a whisky. Bottled at 43% ABV, chill-filtered, per Hibiki Harmony's standard specification; no vintage age statement.

name: Jameson  Irish Whiskey (4.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): Single-distillery blend from Midleton, Cork — triple-distilled over unmalted barley, a tax dodge turned Irish signature. 4.5 L format.
description (EN Store): When 18th-century Irish distillers began mixing unmalted barley into their pot still mash to sidestep British malt taxes, they inadvertently invented a style that exists nowhere else on earth — Single Pot Still Irish Whiskey, the backbone of every Jameson blend. New Midleton Distillery in County Cork, fed by the Dungourney River and supplied by over 200 local barley farmers within 50 miles, is the sole production site for every component in the bottle. Triple distillation through copper pot stills (for the pot still whiskey) and column stills (for the maize-based grain whiskey) strips harsh congeners to a degree double-distilled Scotch peers cannot match, yielding a structurally lighter spirit from the outset. Maturation draws on ex-bourbon American oak casks as the dominant vessel, supplemented by ex-sherry Spanish butts, with a minimum of four years on the island of Ireland — one year beyond the legal floor. Barley is kiln-dried over natural gas rather than peat, a deliberate choice that keeps the malt character clean and unsmoked. The defining transgression: unlike virtually every other blended whiskey of scale, Jameson sources all component whiskeys from a single distillery, giving the blending team grain-to-glass control over house character that multi-source blends cannot replicate. As a non-vintage blend, this 4.5 L expression delivers the same consistent profile as the standard bottling — vanilla, orchard fruit, and toasted oak — with the practical advantage of volume for extended service.

name: The Glenturret  Tripple Wood Single Malt Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's oldest documented distillery (1763), Triple Wood layers ex-bourbon with American & European oak sherry casks from the Turret Burn glen.
description (EN Store): When Lalique Group acquired Glenturret in 2019, they made one decisive cut: severing the distillery's decades-long role as backbone malt for The Famous Grouse to produce exclusively under its own name — a commercial gamble that forced every cask in the six dunnage warehouses to justify itself as single malt. The distillery sits in the sheltered 'Hosh' glen on the Turret Burn, roughly 2 miles northwest of Crieff, Perthshire, where the secluded high-sided valley historically drew illicit distillers precisely because it hid smoke. Process water arrives via a dedicated pipeline from Loch Turret in the Grampian Mountains, fed by Ben Chonzie runoff through granite-and-schist geology that strips mineral hardness — the resulting soft water is a direct contributor to the distillery's characteristically light, high-ester new make. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in traditional pine washbacks, and distillation passes through a 12,500-litre wash still and a 9,000-litre spirit still fitted with a reflux bowl for extended copper contact. The Triple Wood expression draws from three distinct cask types: American oak ex-bourbon barrels, American oak sherry-seasoned casks, and European oak sherry-seasoned casks — the European oak adding dried-fruit and spice registers that the American oak alone cannot deliver. What sets this apart from most Highland NAS releases is the deliberate restraint: the house style pursues tropical fruit and honey over the heavy sherry saturation common in the category.

name: The Glenturret  10 Years old Peat Smoked Single Malt Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scotland's oldest documented distillery (1763), now smoke-forward: peated malt from Loch Turret soft water, single copper pot stills, 10 years in cask.
description (EN Store):
When Lalique Group acquired Glenturret in 2019, the distillery severed its decades-long role as the backbone of The Famous Grouse blend and rebuilt its identity around single malt exclusivity — a categorical reinvention for a site documented to 1763 on the banks of the Turret Burn in Crieff, Perthshire. The Peat Smoked expression is the deliberate counterpoint to that post-2019 house direction: where the core range pursues unpeated delicacy, this bottling reaches back to the kiln-fired tradition the distillery is abandoning from 2025 onward, making it a transitional artifact of Glenturret's own history.

Process water is drawn exclusively from Loch Turret via a dedicated pipeline from the Grampian Mountains above Ben Chonzie — the catchment geology yields exceptionally soft water that amplifies ester formation during fermentation. Barley is kiln-dried over peat smoke before mashing at ~70°C. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in traditional pine washbacks, building the distillery's characteristically fruity, acidic new make. Distillation passes through a 12,500-litre wash still and a 9,000-litre spirit still fitted with a reflux bowl, maximising copper contact and lifting the spirit above the smoke rather than letting peat dominate. Ten years of maturation in American oak sherry-seasoned casks lays honey and dried fruit beneath the phenolic register. What no Highland peer replicates: this level of smoke from one of Scotland's smallest still pairs, where the reflux bowl structurally resists heaviness even in a peated expression.

name: The Matsui  Single Malt Whisky Collection Set
short_description (EN Store): Kurayoshi's 100% in-house distilled Matsui Single Malts — Mt. Daisen volcanic-filtered water, Mizunara & Sakura casks, 48% ABV, genuinely distillery-born.
description (EN Store):
When Matsui Shuzo acquired its whisky distillation licence in 2015 and fired its copper pot stills in 2017, it drew a deliberate line between its controversial sourced-malt past and a new, provenance-certain future — the Matsui Single Malt sub-range is the direct product of that decision, 100% distilled in-house at Kurayoshi Distillery.

The distillery sits at the foothills of Mt. Daisen (1,729 m), an extinct volcano whose fractured basalt filters deep-aquifer groundwater to exceptional softness — that mineral-light water is the structural backbone of the house's characteristically mellow sweetness. The Sea of Japan coastal climate delivers near-freezing winters against 38 °C summers, compressing maturation cycles in a way the producer explicitly compares to Islay.

Two Japanese-made copper pot stills — a 5,000 L wash still and 3,000 L spirit still — feature adjustable lyne arms: angled upward for lighter, floral spirit; horizontal for fuller body. The collection spans cask regimes from ex-bourbon American white oak to Mizunara (Japanese oak) and Sakura (cherry wood) heads-swapped barrels, the latter imparting sandalwood and cherry-blossom registers absent from any Scottish-influenced peer. All Matsui Single Malts are bottled at 48% ABV; the Peated expression is explicitly un-chill-filtered, natural colour.

The special feature: Sakura cask finishing — cherry wood maturation at commercial scale — remains vanishingly rare in global whisky, making these expressions categorically distinct from any Scottish, Irish, or mainstream Japanese single malt.

name: Kurayoshi  Pure Malt Sherry Cask (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sourced-malt blend matured at Mt. Daisen's volcanic foothills, finished in ex-sherry casks — fruit-forward and controversially borderless.
