short_description (EN Store): Highland malt from Scotland's most technically complex distillery — 18 years in ex-bourbon oak, shaped by 92–160hr fermentations on the Highland Boundary Fault.
description (EN Store):
Loch Lomond's Alexandria distillery occupies a former Victorian iron foundry on the southern shore of Loch Lomond, straddling the Highland Boundary Fault — a geological divide that places this site firmly in Highland classification while sitting just 25 km from Glasgow. The distillery's founder developed straight-neck pot stills fitted with 17 fixed copper rectification plates, a design never replicated elsewhere, capable of producing spirit at up to ~90% ABV versus the ~68–70% of conventional swan-neck stills — yielding a lighter, more floral malt character that distinguishes Loch Lomond from every other Highland producer.

The 18 Year Old is built on minimum 92-hour fermentations — extended deliberately to 160 hours in some batches, roughly double the Speyside industry norm — to develop long-chain esters and deep fruit complexity before distillation. Primary maturation runs 18 years in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels, the house's preferred vessel, which drives the characteristic honey and vanilla register. Caramel colouring and chill-filtration status are not universally confirmed across the core range.

What no peer does: the same campus that produced this malt also runs continuous column stills on 100% malted barley mash — making Loch Lomond the only distillery globally to produce peated single grain whisky alongside its single malts. The 18 Year Old delivers the extended-fermentation fruit architecture at full maturity, with the bourbon-oak sweetness fully integrated after nearly two decades.

name: Loch Lomond  14 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Highland malt from Alexandria's Highland Boundary Fault site — 14 years in ex-bourbon oak, shaped by 92–160hr fermentations unique to Loch Lomond's three-still campus.
description (EN Store): Duncan Thomas built Loch Lomond's distillery in 1964 on the site of the Alexandria Works, a Victorian iron foundry that once supplied components for Clyde shipbuilding — the same brick warehouse bones now create varied maturation microclimates across the campus. The distillery sits precisely on the Highland Boundary Fault in Dunbartonshire, a geological seam that places it within the Highland classification while drawing water from Loch Lomond itself, a cold, soft-water source that keeps fermentation clean and ester-driven. Fermentation runs a minimum of 92 hours and extends to 160 hours — roughly double the Speyside industry norm — deliberately building long-chain esters and fruit complexity before the wash ever reaches a still. The 14 Year Old matures in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels, the house's primary cask choice, which the distillery credits for its honey and vanilla character; the on-site cooperage, operational since 1994, re-chars up to 20,000 casks annually, maintaining tight control over wood contribution. What separates this expression from any peer is the still architecture behind it: Loch Lomond operates straight-neck pot stills fitted with 17 fixed copper rectification plates — a design developed by Thomas at Littlemill in the 1950s and used nowhere else on earth — capable of producing spirit at ~90% ABV versus the ~68–70% of conventional swan-neck stills, yielding a lighter, more floral malt character at the same Highland address. At 14 years, the extended ex-bourbon maturation has fully integrated that high-rectification distillate into a fruit-forward, honey-sweet profile with soft approachability.

name: Loch Lomond  21 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 21 years on ex-bourbon oak at Alexandria's Highland Boundary Fault site — from Scotland's only distillery running three still types under one roof.
description (EN Store): Duncan Thomas built Loch Lomond's distillery in 1964 on a former Victorian iron foundry in Alexandria, Dunbartonshire — a site whose old brick warehouses and modern racked structures create genuinely varied maturation microclimates across the same campus. That industrial heterodoxy extends to the stills: three distinct still types operate simultaneously, including straight-neck pot stills fitted with 17 fixed copper rectification plates — a design Thomas developed at Littlemill in the 1950s and never replicated elsewhere — capable of producing spirit at ~90% ABV versus the ~68–70% of conventional swan-neck stills. The 21 Year Old draws on primary maturation in ex-bourbon American oak hogsheads and barrels, a regime the distillery credits with driving honey and vanilla character, underpinned by fermentations running a minimum 92 hours and up to 160 hours — roughly double the Speyside norm — which builds the long-chain esters that sustain complexity across two decades of oak contact. What no peer can replicate: this whisky was distilled at the only address in Scotland producing both malt and grain spirit on a single site, sitting precisely on the Highland Boundary Fault. After 21 years, the extended fermentation fruit has integrated fully into the oak framework; drink now through the next decade.

name: Dandy Classic Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Classic Scottish whisky with warm vanilla oak, spice, and subtle smoke—approachable and versatile.
description (EN Store): Dandy Classic Whisky is a Scottish whisky offering a balanced profile anchored by vanilla and oak character from maturation. The palate develops spice and dark fruit notes with underlying smoke, creating a medium-full body suitable for both neat sipping and mixing. The whisky's accessible structure and warm finish make it a reliable choice for everyday enjoyment and food pairing.

name: Dandy Rebel Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Scottish whisky with vanilla oak, dark fruit, and subtle smoke—versatile for neat sipping or classic cocktails.
description (EN Store): Dandy Rebel Whisky is a Scottish whisky offering a balanced profile anchored by vanilla and oak character from cask maturation. Dark fruit and spice notes provide supporting complexity, with smoke adding depth on the finish. Medium-full body and moderate tannin structure make it approachable yet substantial, suitable for neat enjoyment or mixing in traditional whisky cocktails.

name: Boutique-y Ledaig Batch 4 - 19 Years Old (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 19-year Island malt from Ledaig (Tobermory's peated side), bottled at cask strength — no colour, no chill filtration — by cult independent TBWC.
description (EN Store):
That Boutique-y Whisky Company launched in September 2012 with a deliberate provocation: its first four releases included two long-closed distilleries alongside living legends, signalling that closed-distillery rarities and active icons would be treated with identical rigour. Ledaig — the heavily peated expression produced at Tobermory Distillery on the Isle of Mull — sits squarely in that spirit of serious intent wrapped in irreverent packaging.

Tobermory Distillery occupies the harbour front of Mull's main town, drawing process water from the Mishnish Lochs above the town. The island's Atlantic-facing position and maritime air permeate long maturation, driving saline, coastal character into the spirit over time. Ledaig's phenolic malt (typically peated to around 30–35 ppm) provides the smoky backbone that distinguishes it from the unpeated Tobermory house style.

This Batch 4 expression has spent 19 years in cask — the specific cask type is not disclosed in TBWC's library for this batch, but the house policy is unambiguous: bottled at natural cask strength, without artificial colouring, and without chill filtration, preserving every fatty acid and ester the long Island maturation has built. Batch size runs to approximately 1,000 bottles.

What sets this release apart is the age itself: 19-year-old Ledaig is genuinely rare — Tobermory's relatively modest annual output means aged peated stock seldom surfaces through independent channels at this maturity. The 500 ml format further signals a collector-grade, limited-allocation release rather than a standard serve.

name: Boutique-y Port Charlotte Sherry Cask - Bangkok Skyline Series Vol 2 (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Port Charlotte sherry cask, bottled at natural cask strength — no colour, no chill filtration — by the indie bottler that launched with Port Ellen on day one.
description (EN Store): That Boutique-y Whisky Company launched in September 2012 with four releases — two from living distilleries, two from long-closed legends — a founding statement that liquid seriousness and irreverence are not mutually exclusive. This expression draws on Port Charlotte, Bruichladdich's heavily peated spirit, matured in ex-sherry casks and selected by Head of Whisky Sam Simmons for the Bangkok Skyline Series Vol 2, a Thailand-specific limited edition. Port Charlotte is distilled on Islay's Atlantic-facing west coast, where maritime air, high humidity, and the island's peat bogs — cut from decomposed sphagnum and heather — drive both the phenolic character of the spirit and the oxidative pace of maturation. The sherry cask regime layers Oloroso-derived dried fruit and dark spice over that coastal peat signature, a combination that demands careful cask selection to avoid either element dominating. True to TBWC's non-negotiable house policy, this release is bottled at natural cask strength, without artificial colouring and without chill filtration, preserving the full texture and volatile aromatic compounds that cold filtration would strip. What sets this apart within the TBWC portfolio is the series format itself: a geographically dedicated, small-batch release built for a single market, making it a collector's piece that will not reappear in identical form. Drink now or hold — the sherry wood integration will continue to develop over 3–7 years in bottle.

name: Boutique-y Blended Malt #1 24 Year Old - Batch 8 (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): 24-year blended malt from TBWC's zero-compromise independent cellar — no chill filtration, no colouring, Batch 8 of a small-run series capped near 1,000 bottles.
description (EN Store):
When Ben Ellefsen launched That Boutique-y Whisky Company in September 2012, the debut four releases included both living legends and long-closed distilleries — a deliberate signal that age and rarity would be treated with equal rigour, not reverence. That founding philosophy underpins every cask selection made by Head of Whisky Sam Simmons, and it is precisely what defines this 24 Year Old Blended Malt, Batch 8.

As a pure independent bottler with no owned warehouses, TBWC's entire value proposition rests on cask selection: malts from multiple Scottish distilleries, matured in casks at source — most likely ex-bourbon American oak given the age profile and blended malt classification, though the specific cask regime is determined entirely by the contributing distilleries. Twenty-four years of slow oxidation in oak at Scottish ambient temperatures typically drives deep integration of wood sugars, dried fruit concentration, and a reduction of sharper spirit esters.

Bottled without artificial colouring and without chill filtration — a non-negotiable house standard applied across every TBWC release — the whisky retains its full texture and natural haze-forming congeners. Batch 8 sits within a production run of approximately 1,000 bottles, making each batch a genuinely finite proposition. The special feature here is structural: a 24-year blended malt at this price point, from an independent bottler that applies single-malt-grade bottling discipline to every expression in its range, is a category anomaly.

name: Boutique-y Ledaig 19 Year Old - Batch 19 (500 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Islay's Ledaig at 19 years, bottled at cask strength — no colour, no chill filtration — by the indie house that launched with Port Ellen on day one.
description (EN Store): That Boutique-y Whisky Company launched in September 2012 with four releases, two of which were from long-closed distilleries — a deliberate signal that this Kent-based independent would treat closed legends and living producers with identical rigour. Ledaig, the peated expression from Tobermory Distillery on the Isle of Mull, sits in that same spirit: a coastal-island malt that occupies an often-overlooked corner between Islay's heavy hitters and the lighter Highland style. Nineteen years of maturation in cask — the specific cask type for Batch 19 is not disclosed in available documentation, though TBWC's range spans ex-bourbon American oak, ex-Oloroso sherry butts, and ex-port pipes — has had time to work against Ledaig's characteristically medicinal, briny peat. Bottled at natural cask strength, without artificial colouring and without chill filtration, the spirit retains the fatty, waxy texture that cold-filtration would strip. What sets this release apart within the independent bottling category is TBWC's founding commitment — confirmed across every batch — to zero-compromise bottling standards applied to a 500 ml format that lowers the barrier to entry for a nearly two-decade-old cask-strength Islay-adjacent malt. Batch 19 represents a mature, time-softened iteration of Ledaig's signature coastal peat character, with the extended aging expected to have drawn out deeper wax and dried-fruit integration alongside the smoke.

name: Ezra Brooks 99 Rye (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Spicy rye whiskey with vanilla oak and caramel notes; approachable American spirit for neat sipping or cocktails.
description (EN Store): Ezra Brooks 99 Rye is a straightforward American rye whiskey offering core rye grain character with warm spice and black pepper on the palate. Oak aging contributes vanilla sweetness and caramel notes, creating a medium-full body with smooth, lingering finish. Designed for versatility—equally suited to neat sipping, on the rocks, or as a foundation for classic rye cocktails.

name: Kilchoman Sauternes Cask (Limited) (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Islay single malt finished in Sauternes cask—honeyed sweetness, dried fruit, and smooth oak complexity.
description (EN Store): Kilchoman's Sauternes Cask expression marries Islay's characteristic peat-influenced spirit with the luxurious influence of French dessert wine cask maturation. The finish in Sauternes wood imparts honeyed sweetness, dried stone fruit, and vanilla oak notes, creating a sophisticated balance between the distillery's maritime character and rich, rounded sweetness. A limited release offering depth and elegance for contemplative sipping or pairing with fine desserts and aged cheeses.

name: Johnnie Walker Black Label (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): ~40 whiskies aged ≥12 yrs — Cardhu sweetness, Caol Ila peat, Clynelish wax — blended at Leven, Fife. 1.75 L format.
description (EN Store): When John Walker began blending spirits in his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, he borrowed a logic from the tea trade: source across regions and seasons to eliminate batch variation — a direct transgression against the single-malt orthodoxy of his era. That same architecture drives Black Label today. Approximately 40 malt and grain whiskies, each aged a minimum of 12 years in predominantly ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, are drawn from distilleries spanning Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland Scotland. No single terroir defines this whisky — that is the deliberate point. Cardhu (Speyside) anchors the blend with honeyed, fruit-forward sweetness; Caol Ila (Islay) threads in clean maritime peat smoke; Clynelish (Highland) contributes a distinctive waxy coastal depth that no Speyside-only blend can replicate. The blending team — currently led by Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender role in the brand's 200-year history — selects from a reserve of approximately 10 million casks, with each individual cask analyzed and approved before entry into the blend. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, the 1.75 L format delivers the same batch-consistent house character at scale. The special feature here is structural: smoke, sweetness, and spice are held in deliberate equilibrium across a pan-Scotland sourcing network that no single-distillery producer can architecturally replicate.

name: Johnnie Walker  Red Label (1.75 L)
short_description (EN Store): 30+ Scotch whiskies blended across Speyside, Islay, Highland & Lowland — John Walker's tea-trade logic applied to whisky since 1820.
description (EN Store):
When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, he applied the same cross-origin blending logic he used for tea — eliminating batch variation by drawing from multiple regions simultaneously. That founding insight is still the structural principle of Red Label, which assembles over 30 malt and grain whiskies sourced from all four major Scottish regions: Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland. No single-region producer can replicate this architecture by definition.

The blend's backbone follows the house's pan-Scotland formula: Cardhu (Speyside) contributes honeyed, fruit-forward sweetness as the 'heart'; Caol Ila (Islay) delivers clean maritime peat smoke; east-coast grain whiskies from column stills add textural lightness and accessibility. The result is a deliberate threading of smoke, sweetness, and cereal spice — not a grain-heavy light blend, not a malt-forward richness, but a calibrated middle path. Matured in ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks to a minimum of 3 years, then chill-filtered and bottled at 40% ABV. The 1.75 L format delivers the full house character at volume — the same recipe, no reformulation.

Red Label's special distinction within the Johnnie Walker range is its role as the widest-sourced expression: the 30+ component count draws from more distilleries than any other label in the core lineup, making it the broadest geographic cross-section of Scotch in a single bottle.

name: Chivas Regal Crystalgold (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Strathisla-anchored blended Scotch from Scotland's oldest Highland distillery, short-necked stills and wooden washbacks shaping a malt-forward, grain-generous house style.
description (EN Store): When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909 as a 25-year-old blend — at a time when no competitor carried an age statement at all — he effectively invented the premium blended Scotch category. That founding logic, malt volume over grain economy, still governs every expression. The heart malt is Strathisla, drawn from a distillery in Keith, Speyside, operating continuously since 1786 — the oldest in the Scottish Highlands — whose process water filters through granite from the Broomhill Spring roughly one mile away. Strathisla's notably short-necked pot stills allow heavier vapours to rise into the spirit, producing a full-bodied, generously textured new make that anchors the blend's characteristic creaminess. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks rather than stainless steel, providing thermal insulation for a steady, flavour-building two-day ferment. Component whiskies are matured in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Oloroso sherry European oak casks, with grain whisky supplied by Strathclyde Distillery in the Lowlands and additional malts from Longmorn, The Glenlivet, Miltonduff, Braeval, and Allt-a-Bhainne. What sets Crystalgold apart within the range is its positioning as a no-age-statement expression designed to foreground the Speyside malt character without the structural weight of the 12-year minimum. Bottled at 40% ABV, it delivers the house's orchard-fruit and heather-honey signature in a lighter, more immediate register.

name: Prakaan  Single Malt Whisky Tribura Series Double Cask (700 ml) (Special Pack)
short_description (EN Store): Thai single malt with double cask maturation, delivering vanilla oak and dark fruit complexity.
description (EN Store): Prakaan Single Malt Whisky Tribura Series Double Cask represents a distinctive expression from Thailand's whisky production landscape. The double cask maturation strategy imparts layered character: vanilla and oak from wood contact, supported by dark fruit and warming spice notes. Suitable for neat sipping or with water to open secondary smoke and complexity.

name: Johnnie Walker Vault The Couture Blend (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Johnnie Walker's apex luxury blend — hand-selected casks from Cardhu, Caol Ila & Clynelish, drawn from a reserve of ~10 million casks by master blender Dr. Emma Walker.
description (EN Store): John Walker's founding insight in 1820 Kilmarnock was borrowed directly from the tea trade: blending across regions and harvests eliminates the tyranny of batch variation — a principle that, two centuries later, reaches its most extreme expression in the Vault Couture Blend. The Couture Blend draws from Diageo's reserve of approximately 10 million casks, with each individual cask individually analyzed and approved before selection — a selection discipline that makes even Blue Label's 1-in-10,000 cask threshold look permissive by comparison. The blend's structural architecture follows the house's pan-Scotland logic: Cardhu (Speyside) anchors the honeyed, fruit-forward core; Caol Ila (Islay) threads clean maritime peat through the mid-palate; and Clynelish (Highland) contributes its signature waxy coastal depth — a combination no single-region distillery can replicate. Maturation draws on both ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, the precise split undisclosed but calibrated by Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender role in the brand's 200-year history. What separates the Couture Blend within the Vault tier is its positioning above the standard luxury ceiling: this is not a volume expression but a bespoke, batch-specific assembly — closer in philosophy to a couture atelier than a production line. Non-vintage by design; intended for immediate appreciation or long-term cellaring at cask-appropriate conditions.

name: Johnnie Walker Blue Label Festive VAP 2025 – Decanter Edition (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blue Label's 1-in-10,000 cask threshold — Cardhu sweetness, Caol Ila maritime peat, Clynelish wax — in a collectible 2025 Decanter Edition.
description (EN Store): When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his tea-blending instinct — that combining sources eliminates batch variation — became the founding logic of what is now the world's most recognised blended Scotch house. Blue Label is where that logic reaches its most selective expression: only 1 in every 10,000 casks in Diageo's reserve of approximately 10 million oak vessels qualifies for inclusion, a threshold enforced by individual cask analysis before selection. The blend draws from malt distilleries spanning all four Scottish regions — Cardhu (Speyside) for honeyed fruit, Caol Ila (Islay) for clean maritime peat, and Clynelish (Highland) for waxy coastal depth — a pan-Scotland architecture that no single-region producer can structurally replicate. Maturation is split across ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, with each parcel assessed independently; the final blend is bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered. What sets Blue Label apart within its own tier is the periodic inclusion of whisky from permanently closed 'ghost' distilleries — Brora and Port Ellen have appeared in the Ghost & Rare series — making select batches a rare commercial vehicle for historically extinct spirit. The 2025 Festive Decanter Edition carries no vintage declaration by design; house consistency across batches is the explicit goal, overseen by Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender role in the brand's 200-year history.

name: Johnnie Walker Blue Label Chinese New Year Of Horse (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Blue Label's 1-in-10,000 cask threshold meets Year of the Horse collectible packaging — rare ghost-distillery reserves, 40% ABV, chill-filtered.
description (EN Store): John Walker's original insight — borrowed from his Kilmarnock tea trade — was that blending across Scotland's regions could eliminate the batch variation that plagued single-cask whisky. Blue Label is where that philosophy reaches its most selective expression: only 1 in every 10,000 casks in Diageo's reserve of approximately 10 million barrels qualifies, hand-assessed by the blending team before selection. The blend draws from distilleries spanning all four major Scottish regions: Cardhu (Speyside) anchors the honeyed fruit core, Caol Ila (Islay) threads clean maritime peat through the mid-palate, and Clynelish (Highland) contributes its signature waxy coastal depth — a triptych no single-region producer can replicate. Occasional contributions from permanently silent 'ghost distilleries' such as Brora and Port Ellen (as seen in the Ghost & Rare series) mean Blue Label is one of the only commercially available vehicles for tasting whisky from closed sites. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, non-vintage, the recipe is a trade secret that shifts by batch. The Year of the Horse limited edition packages this rare-cask blend in a collectible format, making it simultaneously a drinking whisky and a culturally significant shelf piece for Lunar New Year.

name: Johnnie Walker Gold Label XR 21 Chinese New Year Of horse (750 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Gold Label XR 21 — a pan-Scotland blend aged a minimum 21 years, dressed in Year of the Horse livery for collectors and connoisseurs alike.
description (EN Store): John Walker's founding insight in 1820 Kilmarnock was borrowed directly from tea merchants: blend across regions and seasons to eliminate batch variation. The Gold Label XR 21 is that philosophy taken to its logical extreme — a minimum 21-year age statement drawn from Diageo's reserve of approximately 10 million casks, selecting only those that have reached a depth no younger expression in the core range can offer. The blend draws on the same pan-Scotland architecture that defines the house: Cardhu (Speyside) honeyed fruit as the structural heart, Caol Ila (Islay) for clean maritime smoke, and Clynelish (Highland) for its characteristic waxy, coastal density — components matured predominantly in ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, with the extended 21-year contact amplifying coconut and dried-fruit extraction far beyond what the NAS Gold Label Reserve achieves. What separates XR 21 from every peer in the Johnnie Walker tier is precisely that age guarantee: unlike Blue Label (NAS, hand-selected rarity) or Gold Label Reserve (NAS premium), this expression commits to a declared minimum that anchors its complexity in verifiable time. This Chinese New Year Year of the Horse edition adds collectible ceramic-style packaging without altering the liquid — the whisky inside is identical to the standard XR 21 release, bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered to Diageo's standard house specification.

name: Arran Port Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Unpeated Lochranza malt finished in Port casks — no colouring, no chill filtration, 46% ABV; fruit-forward island whisky that breaks every smoky-island rule.
description (EN Store):
When Harold Currie, former Managing Director of Chivas Brothers, revived legal distillation on Arran in 1995 after a 160-year island silence, he made a deliberate transgression: no peat, no maritime brine, no concession to island-whisky convention. The Port Cask Finish is that philosophy taken to its logical extreme — a clean, orchard-driven spirit pushed into Port wood to amplify sweetness rather than mask it.

Lochranza Distillery sits in Glen Eason Biorach ('Valley of the Sharp Waterfalls') on granite-dominant terrain north of the Highland Boundary Fault. Water descends from Loch na Davie through six waterfalls, purified by granite and softened by trace peat, arriving at the mash tun exceptionally soft — a direct contributor to the distillery's characteristically delicate, cereal-clean new make.

Concerto-variety Scottish barley is fermented for 60–75 hours in Oregon pine washbacks using Kerry M yeast — the extended fermentation window deliberately targeted to build fruity ester congeners before double distillation through tall-necked, long-lyne-arm copper pot stills (wash stills 7,100 L; spirit stills 4,300–4,800 L), all steam-heated with shell-and-tube condensers. New make is collected at ~68% ABV. Primary maturation in ex-bourbon and/or ex-sherry refill casks is followed by a Port cask finish, layering dark berry and dried plum character over the house's signature citrus-and-apple core. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, no caramel colouring — parameters Arran holds across virtually its entire range.

The special feature: among Islands malts, Arran's pioneering use of exotic wine-cask finishes in the early 2000s helped normalise the practice industry-wide — this expression is a direct descendant of that original rule-breaking impulse, delivered without the smoky camouflage most island producers rely on.

name: Arran Sauternes Cask Finish (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lochranza granite-water new make, finished in Sauternes casks — unpeated island malt, non-chill filtered at 46% ABV.
description (EN Store): When Harold Currie, former Managing Director of Chivas Brothers, revived legal distillation on Arran in 1995 after a 160-year island silence, he made a deliberate transgression: no peat, no maritime brine — a clean, fruit-forward Highland style on an island where convention demanded otherwise. That founding philosophy is what makes the Sauternes Cask Finish possible. Lochranza Distillery sits in Glen Eason Biorach ('Valley of the Sharp Waterfalls') in Arran's granite-dominant north, drawing water from Loch na Davie through six cascades of the Easan Biorach burn — granite-filtered, peat-softened, exceptionally soft — a water profile that drives the distillery's characteristically clean, citrus-bright new make. Double distillation through tall-necked wash stills (7,100 L) and bulb-shaped spirit stills (4,300–4,800 L) with long lyne arms and shell-and-tube condensers, combined with 60–75-hour fermentations using Kerry M yeast in Oregon pine washbacks, builds the ester-rich, orchard-fruit congener base that Sauternes finishing amplifies rather than masks. Primary maturation in refill ex-bourbon American oak is followed by a Sauternes cask finish — the botrytised sweetness and waxy lanolin texture of the French dessert-wine wood layering apricot preserve and honeyed citrus over the distillery's native green apple and lime. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, no caramel colouring — a bottling standard Arran applies across virtually its entire range, unusual for a producer at this price point. The Gulf Stream microclimate at Lochranza accelerates maturation, meaning the wood integration here is denser than the age might suggest on a mainland distillery.

name: Talisker 14 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
short_description (EN Store): Islay-style island whisky: peppery smoke, maritime brine, dark fruit, and charred oak in a full-bodied 14-year expression.
description (EN Store): Talisker 14 Year Old represents the distillery's signature house style: a full-bodied single malt with pronounced peppery smoke and coastal minerality. The spirit opens with dominant black pepper and seaweed brine, followed by dark fruit and charred oak from maturation. Secondary notes of spiced warmth and leather emerge mid-palate, while tertiary complexity reveals dried fruit and tobacco undertones. The finish is long and warming, with lingering smoke and maritime character. A classic island whisky suited to contemplative sipping or pairing with robust cuisine.

name: Ardnamurchan AD Sauternes Cask Western Highland (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Sauternes-cask Highland whisky with honeyed sweetness, stone fruit, and vanilla oak complexity.
description (EN Store): Ardnamurchan's AD Sauternes Cask expression showcases the influence of ex-Sauternes wood on Highland spirit character. The cask maturation imparts pronounced vanilla and caramel notes alongside dark fruit depth, creating a medium-full bodied dram with soft, approachable palate structure. Stone-fruit and dried-fruit layers emerge alongside subtle spice, while oak integration provides backbone without harshness. Suited to contemplative sipping or pairing with dessert courses.

name: Ardnamurchan Single Malt Cask Strength Western Highland (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Cask-strength Highland single malt with rich vanilla oak, smoke, and dark fruit complexity.
description (EN Store): Ardnamurchan Single Malt Cask Strength represents the Highland distillery's commitment to full-proof spirit character. Bottled at cask strength without chill filtration, this expression delivers robust vanilla and oak from maturation, layered with smoke and dark fruit notes. The palate develops spice and cocoa undertones, finishing with leather and subtle tobacco complexity. A versatile dram suited to contemplative sipping or pairing with rich cuisine.

name: King's Inch Glasgow Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Lowland single malt with vanilla oak, subtle smoke, and warming spice—approachable Scottish whisky for evening sipping.
description (EN Store): King's Inch Glasgow Single Malt represents the lighter, more delicate tradition of Lowland Scotch whisky. This 700 ml expression balances malty sweetness with vanilla-forward oak influence and a whisper of smoke, finishing with gentle spice and dried fruit complexity. Ideal for those seeking an accessible yet characterful single malt with Scottish heritage and smooth drinkability.

name: Masahiro Pure Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Japanese pure malt whisky with balanced sweetness, oak influence, and approachable character.
description (EN Store): Masahiro Pure Malt Whisky represents a refined expression of Japanese whisky craftsmanship. The spirit opens with a malt-driven core, delivering subtle sweetness and grain character. Oak maturation contributes vanilla and light spice notes, while extended aging introduces layers of dried fruit and gentle complexity. The palate is smooth and medium-bodied, with a balanced finish that lingers pleasantly. This whisky suits both neat sipping and cocktail applications, offering versatility for the discerning drinker.

name: KAIJIN Blended Whisky (700 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Japanese blended whisky with vanilla oak and warm spice—approachable everyday dram.
description (EN Store): Masahiro's blended whisky presents a balanced, accessible profile suited to both casual sipping and food pairing. Vanilla and oak dominate the palate, supported by warm spice and dried fruit notes that develop with air. The medium body and moderate intensity make this an ideal aperitif or companion to light appetizers and comfort fare.

name: Onson Premium (330 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Thailand's only coconut flower sap lao khao — wood-fire distilled in Sakon Nakhon under a 5-horsepower community license. Gin-adjacent, legally uncategorizable.
description (EN Store):
When Tammawit 'Tiger' Limlertcharoenwanich founded Onson in 2020, he rejected every conventional Thai white spirit base — rice, molasses, sugarcane — and revived a pre-modern ingredient: caramelized coconut flower sap (nam wan dok maphrao), sourced additive-free from traditional nectar farms in Samut Songkhram on the central plains. That single sourcing decision makes Onson categorically unlike any other lao khao in commercial production.

The sap is caramelized before fermentation — a deliberate step that builds aromatic depth and natural sweetness into the wash before the still is even lit. Fermentation occurs in food-grade containers under ambient Isan conditions at the concrete distillery on the outskirts of Sakon Nakhon city, a rural northeastern province of rice paddies and rolling hills near the Mekong basin. The still is heated by wood fire, mandated by a Thai government community license that caps electricity at five horsepower — the equivalent of two hair dryers — and limits the operation to six employees.

The result is an unaged, clear spirit with no wood contact, bottled directly from distillation. name: Fever-Tree  Premium Indian Tonic Water (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Quinine from a single DRC-Rwanda border plantation, Staffordshire spring water, zero artificial sweeteners — the mixer that redrew the category in 2005.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow traced quinine sourcing back to 1620 via archival research at the British Library, they located one of the last surviving plantations of original Cinchona Ledgeriana trees on the Rwanda–Democratic Republic of Congo border — a species prized for its alkaloid concentration and the foundation of every bottle of Premium Indian Tonic Water. That single-origin quinine decision is the technical spine of the product: no synthetic quinine substitutes, no sodium benzoate preservatives, no artificial sweeteners — only cane sugar, Staffordshire Elmhurst spring water, and cold-pressed citrus oils. The spring water's soft mineral profile is deliberate; hard water would compete with the botanical aromatics rather than carry them. Carbonation is dialled to a fine, high-pressure bubble structure — not the coarse effervescence of mass-market tonics — specifically to lift delicate gin botanicals rather than flatten them. The special feature here is categorical: Fever-Tree launched in 2005 into a segment monopolised by a single global brand and no premium alternative, and by 2018 had overtaken Schweppes by value in the UK market on the strength of this formulation alone. Each 200 ml glass bottle is a single-serve format engineered to open fresh at the moment of mixing, preserving carbonation integrity through the final pour.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Natural Soda Water (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Elmhurst spring water, bicarbonate, Scottish West Coast sea salt — zero artificial preservatives, fine-bubble carbonation built to carry, not mask, premium spirits.
description (EN Store):
When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their founding provocation was archival: if the world's best spirits deserved a worthy mixer, why did none exist? The answer they built starts with water — Elmhurst spring, Staffordshire, chosen for its soft mineral profile — blended with bicarbonate of soda and Scottish West Coast sea salt, then carbonated to a fine, small-bubble specification that sustains lift without the aggressive fizz of mass-market alternatives.

The formula is deliberately spare: no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate or synthetic preservatives of any kind. The salt addition is the technical signature — a trace mineral layer that rounds the palate and amplifies the aromatic compounds of whatever spirit it accompanies, functioning less as a flavour note and more as a textural and sensory amplifier.

What separates this from commodity soda water is the carbonation philosophy: fine-bubble, high-volume CO₂ that carries delicate botanical oils upward rather than overwhelming them — the same principle Fever-Tree applies across its tonic range, here stripped to its purest expression. For a category where most competitors treat water as a neutral afterthought, the sourcing of a named Staffordshire spring and the deliberate inclusion of sea salt represent a genuine formulation decision, not marketing.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Ginger Ale (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Three-origin ginger (Ivory Coast, Nigeria, Cochin) blended with Staffordshire spring water — no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their founding provocation was archival: if mass-market mixers were built on synthetic aromatics and sodium benzoate preservatives, the quality gap between premium spirits and commodity mixers was a commercial opportunity, not an inevitability. The Premium Ginger Ale is the direct expression of that logic applied to ginger. Three distinct ginger varieties are sourced by origin — Ivory Coast, Nigeria, and Cochin (India) — each contributing a different aromatic register: the West African roots deliver heat and earthiness, while the Cochin ginger adds a brighter, more citric lift. The base is Staffordshire spring water from the Elmhurst source, chosen for its soft mineral profile, which allows the ginger oils to read cleanly rather than compete with dissolved solids. No artificial sweeteners, no high-fructose corn syrup, and no sodium benzoate are used — cane sugar is the sole sweetener. Carbonation is deliberately fine-bubbled, a textural decision that carries volatile ginger aromatics to the nose rather than dispersing them. What separates this from category peers is the multi-origin ginger architecture: no single-source ginger achieves the same aromatic complexity, and the formulation is built around that layering as a deliberate technical choice rather than a cost variable.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Mediterranean Tonic Water (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Lemon thyme from Provence, quinine from a DRC plantation traced via British Library archives — Mediterranean Tonic built on single-origin botanicals, no artificial sweeteners.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their founding argument was blunt: if three-quarters of a G&T is the mixer, a £40 gin deserves better than sodium benzoate and synthetic quinine. The Mediterranean Tonic is where that argument becomes most aromatic — its botanical signature is built around essential oils from lemon thyme sourced in Provence and other coastal Mediterranean herbs, blended with quinine extracted from Cinchona Ledgeriana bark harvested from one of the last original plantations on the Rwanda–Democratic Republic of Congo border, a source the founders identified through archival research at the British Library tracing quinine provenance back to 1620. The water base is drawn from the Elmhurst spring in rural Staffordshire — soft, low-mineral spring water chosen specifically to carry delicate botanical oils without interference. Sweetness comes from cane sugar only; no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate. Carbonation is deliberately fine-bubbled and high-pressure, a textural decision designed to lift herbal aromatics rather than overwhelm them. What separates this expression from the flagship Indian Tonic is the shift from citrus-forward bitterness to a drier, herb-driven profile — the Provençal lemon thyme introduces a floral, almost savory register that opens up botanical gins, dry vermouths, and low-intervention spirits in ways a standard tonic cannot.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Elderflower (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): English elderflower, fine-bubble carbonation, zero artificial sweeteners — built to match the spirit, not mask it.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their founding provocation was blunt: if three-quarters of a drink is the mixer, a commodity product sweetened with artificial additives is a sabotage of the spirit it accompanies. The Premium Elderflower Tonic is a direct expression of that argument. Elderflowers are sourced from England, their delicate aromatic oils preserved through careful extraction and blended with soft spring water from the Elmhurst source in rural Staffordshire — chosen for its low mineral hardness, which allows botanical character to read cleanly without interference. Quinine is drawn from Cinchona Ledgeriana trees on the Rwanda–DRC border, one of the last surviving plantations of the original species, identified through archival research at the British Library tracing quinine provenance back to 1620 — delivering a bitterness that is precise rather than blunt. The formulation contains no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate, and no synthetic aromatics; cane sugar is the sole sweetener. What separates this expression from the standard Indian Tonic is the substitution of citrus-forward quinine aromatics for the softer, honeyed floral register of English elderflower — a profile that opens the door to spirits where citrus would compete rather than complement. Fine, small-bubble carbonation carries the elderflower oils upward rather than dispersing them, making the aromatic delivery unusually persistent for a mixer.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Ginger Beer (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Three-origin ginger blend — Ivory Coast, Nigeria, Cochin — built on Staffordshire spring water. No artificial sweeteners, no preservatives. 200ml × 24.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their founding argument was blunt: if three-quarters of a drink is the mixer, a premium spirit deserves a premium mixer — and no such product existed. Rather than a single commodity source, the formula draws on three distinct ginger varieties: fresh root from the Ivory Coast, dried ginger from Nigeria, and the pungent, high-oleoresin crop from Cochin, India — each contributing a different aromatic register, from green and citric to earthy and fiercely spiced. The base is Staffordshire spring water sourced from the Elmhurst spring, chosen for its soft mineral profile, blended with cane sugar as the sole sweetener — no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate, no synthetic preservatives of any kind. What separates this from every mass-market ginger beer is the layered botanical architecture: the three-origin sourcing strategy produces a ginger character that shifts from bright and zesty on entry to a slow, building heat on the finish — a complexity no single-origin formulation achieves.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Refreshingly Light Indian Tonic Water (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Reduced-sugar tonic built on Cinchona Ledgeriana quinine from the Rwanda-DRC border & Staffordshire spring water — no artificial sweeteners, no sodium benzoate.
description (EN Store):
When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow traced quinine supply chains through British Library archives back to 1620, they located one of the last surviving plantations of original Cinchona Ledgeriana trees on the Rwanda–Democratic Republic of Congo border — the same species that defined Victorian-era tonic water before commodity producers switched to cheaper bark varieties. That single sourcing decision is the structural backbone of every Fever-Tree tonic.

The base water is drawn from the Elmhurst spring in rural Staffordshire, chosen for its softness, which allows the quinine's natural bitterness and the citrus oils to register cleanly rather than being muted by mineral load. The Refreshingly Light expression reduces cane sugar relative to the flagship Premium Indian Tonic while preserving the full botanical architecture: Cinchona Ledgeriana quinine extract blended with citrus oils and Staffordshire spring water, carbonated to a fine, high-pressure bubble structure designed to carry aromatic lift rather than overwhelm it.

The special feature here is the caloric reduction achieved without any sweetener substitution — a formulation constraint most competitors solve with aspartame or acesulfame-K, which Fever-Tree refuses categorically. For gin-and-tonic service, the lower residual sweetness keeps the spirit's botanical profile in the foreground across the full length of the drink.

name: Fever-Tree  Premium Pink Grapefruit Soda Water (200 ml) (Pack 24 Bottles)
short_description (EN Store): Staffordshire spring water, Scottish West Coast sea salt, and fine-bubble carbonation — Fever-Tree's grapefruit soda built without a single artificial sweetener or preservative.
description (EN Store): When Charles Rolls and Tim Warrillow formally founded Fever-Tree in 2004, their core argument was structural: if three-quarters of a drink is the mixer, the mixer must match the spirit. What distinguishes this SKU within the soda category is the deliberate absence of sweetness: unlike most flavoured sodas, there is no added cane sugar, making it a genuinely dry, high-carbonation platform for spirits or standalone drinking.
