short_description (EN Store): California Cabernet Sauvignon with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—elegant and food-friendly.
description (EN Store): Fly By Cabernet Sauvignon is a full-bodied California red showcasing the varietal's signature blackcurrant and dark plum character, complemented by cedar and oak complexity. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide structure and aging potential, while subtle tobacco and leather notes add tertiary depth. A versatile expression suited to grilled meats, lamb, and aged cheeses.

name: District Series Alexander Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Alexander Valley Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins.
description (EN Store): Precision's District Series Alexander Valley Cabernet Sauvignon delivers the hallmark richness and structure expected from Sonoma's premier red wine region. Ripe blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the palate, supported by cedary oak and black pepper spice. The wine's full body and high tannin structure suggest careful extraction and oak aging, with emerging tertiary notes of tobacco and leather adding complexity. Ideal for cellaring or pairing with grilled red meats and braised beef dishes.

name: District Series Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Napa Valley Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins—classic California elegance.
description (EN Store): Precision's District Series Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon delivers the hallmark richness and structure expected from California's premier red wine region. Blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the palate, supported by cedary oak and black pepper spice. Firm, well-integrated tannins provide aging potential and food-friendly grip. A wine of balance and depth, suited to premium red meat dishes and cellar investment.

name: Corryton Burge  South Australian Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Eden Valley Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins—a classic Australian red.
description (EN Store): Corryton Burge's South Australian Cabernet Sauvignon from Eden Valley delivers the hallmark structure and depth expected of the region. Dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit form the primary character, supported by pronounced cedar and oak from careful winemaking. The wine's high tannin backbone and medium-high acidity provide excellent aging potential and food compatibility, while subtle tobacco and leather notes suggest complexity beyond the initial fruit impression. A serious, well-crafted expression suited to premium red-meat cuisine and cellar investment.

name: Corryton Burge  South Australian Shiraz
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Eden Valley Shiraz with dark fruit, spice, and smoky complexity.
description (EN Store): Corryton Burge's South Australian Shiraz from Eden Valley delivers a full-bodied expression of the varietal's classic character. Dark plum and blackberry dominate the primary profile, supported by black pepper and licorice spice. Secondary smoke and leather notes add depth and structure, while the medium-high tannins provide grip and aging potential. A versatile red suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and smoky preparations.

name: VIK  Milla Cala
short_description (EN Store): Cachapoal Valley Cabernet-led blend with dark fruit, cedar, and peppery spice—structured for red meat and aged cheese.
description (EN Store): VIK Milla Cala is a Cachapoal Valley proprietary red blend anchored by 68% Cabernet Sauvignon, with supporting Carmenere, Syrah, Cabernet Franc, and Merlot. The 2021 vintage expresses dark plum and blackcurrant primary fruit, layered with cedar and black pepper from the Cabernet and Syrah components. Secondary cocoa and spice notes emerge from conventional winemaking, while tertiary tobacco and leather complexity suggest careful aging potential. Medium-full body and medium-high tannin structure make this wine food-friendly across grilled red meat, braised beef, and lamb preparations.

name: Tenuta San Guido  Sassicaia 2020
short_description (EN Store): Italy's only single-estate DOC: 85% Cab Sauv/15% Cab Franc from calcareous marl and gabbro soils at Bolgheri, 24 months in Tronçais barriques.
description (EN Store):
When Marchese Mario Incisa della Rocchetta planted Cabernet Sauvignon on the Bolgheri coast in 1944 — two decades before French varieties were fashionable in Tuscany — he produced the wine solely for family consumption until the commercial debut of the 1968 vintage. That founding decision to harvest just before full phenolic ripeness, preserving fragrance over power, remains the house's defining technical philosophy today.

The estate's plots — Castiglioncello, Aia Nuova, Cerreta, and others — span 100–300 m elevation across soils of calcareous marl, gabbro, alberese, and ancient alluvial pebbles (the 'sassi' that name the wine). The Tyrrhenian Sea moderates summer heat while an amphitheatre of hills channels cooling sea breezes, producing lower natural alcohol and bracing acidity that separates Sassicaia structurally from riper Bolgheri peers.

Fermentation proceeds in temperature-controlled stainless steel at ≤28–29°C with estate-selected indigenous yeasts; malolactic is spontaneous, also completed in steel. The wine then ages 24 months in French oak barriques (Tronçais, Allier, Jupilles; approximately 40% new, 40% first-fill, 20% second-fill), a regime Mario pioneered in Italy before it became standard practice.

Sassicaia holds the only single-estate DOC in Italy — a classification with no domestic parallel — making it categorically distinct from every other Super Tuscan. The 2020 vintage delivered a warmer growing season, yielding polished tannins and approachable fruit concentration relative to the more austere 2016 and 2019; drink 2025–2038.

name: Tenuta San Guido  Sassicaia 2021
short_description (EN Store): Bolgheri Sassicaia DOC's sole single-estate appellation: calcareous marl & gabbro soils, 24-month Tronçais barrique aging, 2021's polar winter yielding benchmark structure.
description (EN Store): Mario Incisa della Rocchetta planted Cabernet Sauvignon on the Castiglioncello hill in 1944 — decades before French varieties were accepted in Tuscany — and kept the wine entirely for family consumption until the commercial debut of the 1968 vintage, a deliberate withholding that allowed the style to mature on its own terms before facing the market. The estate's plots at 100–300 m a.s.l. sit on calcareous marl, gabbro, alberese, and the stone-studded alluvial soils that give Sassicaia its name (sasso = stone); the Tyrrhenian Sea moderates summer heat while the Bibbona-to-Castagneto hill amphitheatre blocks north-easterly winds, producing the bracing acidity and aromatic precision that separates this coastal site from warmer inland Tuscan appellations. Fermentation proceeds in temperature-controlled stainless steel at ≤28–29°C with estate-selected indigenous yeasts, malolactic completing spontaneously in steel before the wine moves to French oak barriques (Tronçais, Allier, Jupilles; extra-fine grain, light toast) for 24 months — approximately 40% new, 40% first-fill, 20% second-fill. The 2021 growing season opened with a polar 'Burian' cold snap driving February lows to −5/−7°C, causing natural bud thinning that reduced yields and concentrated the 85% Cabernet Sauvignon / 15% Cabernet Franc blend; the estate considers it a benchmark modern vintage alongside 2016. Sassicaia remains the only wine from a single estate in Italy to hold its own DOC appellation — a regulatory singularity with no domestic parallel. Drink 2026–2048.

name: Sena  2021
short_description (EN Store): Seña 2021: A structured Aconcagua blend of Cabernet, Malbec & Carmenere with dark fruit depth and elegant cedar spice.
description (EN Store): Seña 2021 is a Bordeaux-influenced proprietary blend from Chile's Aconcagua Valley, built on a foundation of Cabernet Sauvignon (50%) with supporting roles from Malbec (27%), Carmenere (17%), and Petit Verdot (6%). The wine displays a medium-full body with medium-high tannins, delivering ripe blackcurrant and dark plum on the nose, layered with black cherry fruit. Secondary oak aging introduces cedar and warm spice notes, while cocoa adds textural richness. With bottle age, tertiary tobacco and leather complexity emerge, suggesting this blend will evolve gracefully over the medium term. A food-friendly, structured red suited to grilled meats, braised beef, and aged cheeses.

name: Siro Pacenti  Brunello Di Montalcino Vecchie Vigne DOCG 2017
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Brunello di Montalcino from old vines; structured Sangiovese with dark fruit, cedar, and earthy complexity.
description (EN Store): Siro Pacenti's Brunello di Montalcino Vecchie Vigne (Old Vines) from the 2017 vintage represents the classical expression of Tuscany's most prestigious red wine appellation. Crafted from 100% Sangiovese grown on mature vineyard parcels, this wine displays the medium-full body and structured tannin profile characteristic of quality Brunello. Dark cherry and plum fruit form the aromatic foundation, layered with cedar, tobacco, and earthy minerality that speak to the wine's age-worthiness and terroir. The palate is balanced and food-friendly, with sufficient acidity to pair across a range of traditional Italian and Mediterranean dishes.

name: Siro Pacenti  Brunello Di Montalcino Vecchie Vigne DOCG 2018
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Brunello di Montalcino from old-vine Sangiovese; structured tannins, dark cherry, and earthy complexity.
description (EN Store): Siro Pacenti's Brunello di Montalcino Vecchie Vigne DOCG 2018 is a single-varietal expression of Sangiovese from mature vines in Tuscany's most prestigious red-wine zone. The wine displays a medium-full body with medium-high tannins and balanced acidity, offering primary notes of black cherry and plum alongside secondary cedar and oak spice. Tertiary layers of tobacco, leather, and earth emerge with air, reflecting the wine's structure and aging potential. This is a food-friendly Brunello suited to grilled red meats, braised beef dishes, and aged cheeses.

name: Piccini  Sasso al Poggio Rosso Toscana IGT
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Sangiovese from Tuscany with cherry, herbs, and earthy complexity. Medium-Full body, high acidity, food-friendly.
description (EN Store): Piccini's Sasso al Poggio Rosso Toscana IGT is a classically structured Sangiovese that captures the essence of Tuscan winemaking. The wine opens with bright cherry and herbaceous notes, supported by earthy minerality and subtle spice on the palate. Medium-Full body and high acidity provide excellent food compatibility, while developing leather and tobacco undertones add complexity and age-worthiness. A versatile red suited to both casual dining and more formal table settings.

name: Tenuta Moraia  Il Pacchia Maremma Toscana
short_description (EN Store): Maremma Toscana red blend with dark fruit, herbaceous notes, and food-friendly structure—ideal for grilled meats and braised dishes.
description (EN Store): Tenuta Moraia Il Pacchia is a Maremma Toscana red blend that exemplifies the region's tradition of balanced, approachable wines. Dark plum and black cherry dominate the primary profile, supported by herbaceous undertones that reflect the Tuscan terroir. Spice and earth emerge on the mid-palate, with subtle leather and tobacco complexity developing in the glass. Medium-full body and moderate tannin make this wine versatile at the table—equally at home with grilled red meat, braised beef, or tomato-based pasta dishes.

name: Masseria La Volpe Uno “1” Riserva Primitivo Di Manduria DOC
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Primitivo from Manduria with dark cherry, smoke, and peppery spice. Smooth, food-friendly, and richly drinkable.
description (EN Store): Masseria La Volpe's Uno Riserva Primitivo di Manduria DOC is a full-bodied red wine showcasing the depth and richness characteristic of Puglia's premier Primitivo terroir. The wine opens with a bold nose of dark cherry and plum, followed by a palate-filling mix of ripe red and dark fruits, smoke, and black pepper. Fully rounded tannins and smooth texture make this wine immediately approachable yet structured enough for food pairing. A touch of spice adds complexity without overwhelming the fruit-forward character, delivering an eminently drinkable expression of southern Italian winemaking.

name: Clarendelle  Medoc Red
short_description (EN Store): Médoc Bordeaux blend of ripe dark fruit, soft tannins, and mineral depth—food-friendly and approachable.
description (EN Store): Clarendelle Médoc is a classic Bordeaux red blend anchored by 85% Merlot with supporting Cabernet Sauvignon and Petit Verdot. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and blackcurrant fruit, complemented by subtle cedar and graphite minerality from careful oak aging. Medium-full body and balanced tannins make this wine versatile at the table, equally at home with grilled beef, braised lamb, or herb-roasted chicken. A food-friendly expression of Médoc terroir.

name: Clarendelle  Saint-Emilion Red
short_description (EN Store): Merlot-led Saint-Émilion with ripe dark fruit, soft tannins, and food-friendly balance—classic Bordeaux elegance.
description (EN Store): A classic Saint-Émilion expression anchored by 88% Merlot, with supporting Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon. The 2019 vintage delivers ripe black cherry and plum on the nose, underpinned by subtle herbaceous notes and a whisper of earth. On the palate, soft, well-integrated tannins and medium acidity create an approachable yet structured wine that rewards both immediate enjoyment and modest cellaring. The finish carries a gentle minerality and hints of oak integration, characteristic of quality Bordeaux craftsmanship.

name: VIK  Millahue
short_description (EN Store): Cabernet Franc–led Bordeaux blend from Chile's Millahue; dark fruit, cedar, and mineral complexity.
description (EN Store): VIK Millahue is a Bordeaux-style red blend from the Cachapoal Valley, anchored by 77% Cabernet Franc with 23% Cabernet Sauvignon. The 2021 vintage expresses the primary dark cherry and blackcurrant character typical of Cabernet Franc, layered with cedar and herbaceous notes from oak aging. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make this wine food-friendly across grilled meats, braised dishes, and Mediterranean cuisine. Mineral undertones and subtle tobacco complexity suggest modest aging potential.

name: Domaine Jaume Vinea Natura AOP
short_description (EN Store): Southern Rhône GSM blend: ripe red fruit, black pepper spice, and mineral earth. Food-friendly and approachable.
description (EN Store): Domaine Jaume's Vinea Natura AOP is a classic Southern Rhône blend of Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvèdre—the traditional GSM combination that defines the region's character. The wine opens with ripe red fruit and dark plum, supported by black pepper spice and subtle herbaceous notes from the Syrah and Mourvèdre components. Medium-full body and balanced tannin make this wine approachable yet structured, with a mineral-driven finish that speaks to the terroir of the Southern Rhône. Ideal for Mediterranean cuisine, grilled meats, and rustic French fare.

name: T' Spot  Bordeaux Red
short_description (EN Store): Classic Bordeaux blend of power and elegance—blackcurrant and cedar with age-worthy structure.
description (EN Store): T' Spot Bordeaux Red is a traditional Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot blend from France's most prestigious wine region. The wine opens with ripe blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, layered with cedary oak and subtle tobacco complexity. Full-bodied with firm, well-integrated tannins and medium-high acidity, it displays the structural balance characteristic of fine Bordeaux. Leather and mineral notes emerge on the finish, suggesting cellaring potential and evolution over time.

name: Talenti Brunello di Montalcino Riserva Pian di Conte (Magnum) (1.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): Riserva from Talenti's oldest vines at ~400 m, Pian di Conte — Allier & Slavonian oak, 25–30-day maceration, 2019's blue-fruit freshness in magnum.
description (EN Store):
Pierluigi Talenti spent decades shaping Brunello di Montalcino's modern identity — first as head winemaker at Il Poggione, then from 1980 as the architect of his own estate at Pian di Conte, Sant'Angelo in Colle, where he planted proprietary Sangiovese clones whose genetic lineage now runs three generations deep. The Riserva carries that founding act forward: it is drawn exclusively from the estate's oldest vines, clustered near the property's centre at approximately 400 m a.s.l. on sandy loam interlaced with Galestro marl and clay — the friable limestone-schist that drives the wine's structural tension and mineral lift, while the southern sector's warmth delivers flesh and depth.

Vinification proceeds parcel by parcel in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 28–29°C, with skin maceration of 25–30 days to extract the full architecture of these old-vine Sangiovese Grosso clones. Aging moves into a blend of Allier and Slavonian oak — a deliberate pairing of French finesse with Slavonian breadth — for an extended period before bottle rest; the Riserva 'Pian di Conte' is produced only in exceptional years, making 2019 a rare release. Where Montalcino's modernist camp leans on barrique and new-oak extraction, Talenti's medium-format vessels preserve site transparency over wood imprint. The 2019 vintage at Sant'Angelo in Colle delivered the southern sector's characteristic power alongside unusual freshness — juicy blue fruits, balsamic lift, and earthy licorice — with a drinking window opening from approximately 2027; the magnum format extends that trajectory further still.

name: Chateau Lafite-Rothschild  2020
short_description (EN Store): Pauillac's 'first among the firsts': plot-by-plot vinification across ~100 parcels, 100% new in-house cooperage oak, and a legal Saint-Estèphe dérogation held since 1868.
description (EN Store): When Baron James Mayer de Rothschild purchased this estate at auction in 1868 for 4.4 million francs, he inherited not only Pauillac's most celebrated terroir but also a unique regulatory anomaly: a 4.5-hectare Cabernet Sauvignon plot in the lieu-dit Blanquet, technically within AOC Saint-Estèphe, legally folded into the Grand Vin under a dérogation held continuously since that acquisition — a privilege no other First Growth possesses. The vineyard sits on deep fine gravel up to 10 metres in key parcels, layered over aeolian black sand and tertiary limestone, with the oldest plot, La Gravière, planted in 1886. The Gironde estuary moderates diurnal swings and suppresses frost risk, preserving the aromatic delicacy that separates Lafite from the muscularity of Latour or the opulence of Mouton. Winemaking proceeds parcel-by-parcel across roughly 100 separate vats — traditional oak, stainless steel, and concrete — with a cold pre-fermentation maceration of ~5 days, alcoholic fermentation at 28–30°C, and total maceration of 20–22 days. Malolactic fermentation occurs naturally in small stainless-steel foudres; the Grand Vin selection is finalised the following March. Aging runs 18–20 months in 100% new French oak barriques produced at La Tonnellerie des Domaines, Lafite's own on-site cooperage — giving the château direct control over the cedar and graphite character that defines the house style. The 2020 vintage is not individually scored in the brand library, but Lafite's average Wine Advocate rating across 16 vintages since 1995 stands at 96.5 points; the estate's consistent selection discipline and organic conversion (begun 2021) point to a drinking window of 2030–2055+.

name: Tenuta dell'Ornellaia  Ornellaia Bolgheri DOC "La Generosità" (Artist Label) 2021
short_description (EN Store): Bolgheri's 99-ha coastal estate — clay-to-alluvial soils, Tyrrhenian sea breezes, 18-month French barrique aging — 2021 'La Generosità' artist label.
description (EN Store):
Since 2006, Ornellaia's Vendemmia d'Artista project has assigned each vintage a single defining word — interpreted by a commissioned contemporary artist, with proceeds benefiting the Solomon R. Guggenheim Foundation. The 2021 edition carries 'La Generosità', a designation that anchors both the cultural programme and the winemaking intent for the year.

The estate's 99 ha span two distinct blocks: the original 41 ha surrounding the winery on denser clay-dominant soils, and the 58 ha Bellaria property to the north on stonier, alluvial ground at 50–120 m asl — the latter delivering fresher, more vibrant fruit that counterbalances the weight of the clay parcels. Tyrrhenian Sea breezes moderate summer heat and imprint a saline coastal tension that separates Ornellaia from any inland Bordeaux analogue.

Each variety and block is vinified separately: fermentation in stainless steel and concrete below 25°C, followed by 10–15 days maceration (total vat time ~3 weeks). Malolactic fermentation occurs in French oak barriques (70% new, 30% once-used), with aging continuing for ~18 months total — 12 months as individual base wines, then assembled and returned to barrel for a further 6 months — before a minimum 12 months of bottle age prior to release.

What no Bolgheri peer replicates: the estate simultaneously produces Ornus dell'Ornellaia, a late-harvest Petit Manseng from the micro-parcel 'Palmetta' — a variety with no precedent on the Tuscan coast, making Ornellaia the only Super Tuscan estate operating across both Bordeaux-blend and exotic late-harvest registers.

The 2021 vintage note is not yet fully documented in the brand library; the wine's structural architecture — coastal minerality, barrique-integrated tannin, and the estate's characteristic Mediterranean generosity — points to a drinking window opening from approximately 2026 through the mid-2030s and beyond.

name: Tenuta dell'Ornellaia  Ornellaia Bolgheri DOC "La Proportione" (Artist Label) 2020
short_description (EN Store): Warm 2020 Bolgheri from Ornellaia's 99 ha coastal estate — ripe black fruit, saline Tyrrhenian freshness, 18 months in 70% new French oak barriques.
description (EN Store):
Since 1981, when Marchese Lodovico Antinori planted Cabernet Sauvignon on the Tuscan coast in deliberate rivalry with his own family's Sassicaia and Solaia, Ornellaia has operated as both winery and cultural institution — the Vendemmia d'Artista project commissions a major international artist each vintage to interpret a single word chosen by the winemaker, with proceeds benefiting the Solomon R. Guggenheim Foundation. The 2020 word is La Proportione.

The estate's 99 ha span two adjacent blocks: the original 41 ha surrounding the winery on denser clay soils, and the 58 ha Bellaria property to the north on stonier, more alluvial ground. Elevations of 50–120 m asl and Tyrrhenian Sea breezes moderate summer heat, preserving the saline coastal freshness that distinguishes Ornellaia from inland Bordeaux analogues.

Each variety and block is vinified separately: fermentation in stainless steel and concrete below 25°C, followed by 10–15 days maceration (total vat time ~3 weeks). Malolactic fermentation occurs in barrel, and the wine ages 18 months in French oak barriques (70% new, 30% once-used) — 12 months as individual base wines, then assembled and returned to barrel for a further 6 months, followed by a minimum 12 months bottle age before release.

The 2020 vintage was warm, producing a darkly toned, generously ripe expression with pronounced black fruit character; approachable with aeration but structured for aging through approximately 2046.

name: Chateau Lafleur Pomerol 2020 (Magnum) (1.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): 4.58ha northern Pomerol plateau; ~50% pre-1956 massale Bouchet; unfined, unfiltered; 2020 described as among the estate's finest in history.
description (EN Store):
When André Robin selected cuttings from his Bouchet vines in the 1930s and then saved them from the 1956 frost by cutting damaged plants to ground level rather than uprooting them, he preserved a distinct Cabernet Franc biotype that winemaker Omri Ram asserts exists at only three addresses on earth: Lafleur, Cheval Blanc, and Ausone. That genetic singularity is the structural spine of every bottle made here.

The 4.58-hectare single-block sits at the northern crest of the Pomerol plateau, directly adjacent to Pétrus, on a mosaic of four micro-terroirs — gravel-over-brown-clay to the northwest, sandy-gravel over gravelly-clay to the south, and a ferric crasse de fer sublayer running beneath much of the property. Where Pétrus draws opulence from deep blue clay, Lafleur's iron-laced gravel drives tension and mineral precision.

Vinification: hand-harvest parcel by parcel, double-sorted; fermented in small traditional concrete vats with malolactic completed in barrel; ~3-week maceration; zero enzymes, inoculation, chaptalization, or acidification — gravity only throughout. Élevage: 18 months in French oak barriques, ~50% new, remainder sourced from sister estate Grand Village; racked every three months. Bottled unfined and unfiltered.

Lafleur is the highest-Cabernet-Franc Pomerol — a profile architecturally closer to Saint-Émilion than to its Merlot-dominant neighbours — and production is capped at roughly 1,000 cases per year, with no grand vin released in sub-standard vintages.

The 2020 is described as among the finest in the estate's history: dense, creamy, and very concentrated with remarkable length. This magnum format (1.5 L) extends the already exceptional ageing horizon of the standard bottle.

name: Chateau Latour 2013
short_description (EN Store): L'Enclos Grand Vin from Latour's first post-en-primeur era: deep gravel croupes, 100% new French oak, released only when deemed ready.
description (EN Store): In 2012, Château Latour became the first First Growth to abandon the en primeur system entirely — releasing wines only when the estate judges them ready to drink, a commercial rupture no peer has replicated. The 2013 Grand Vin is among the earliest expressions of that philosophy, arriving on the market years after the vintage rather than months. L'Enclos, the 47-hectare walled parcel just 300 metres from the Gironde Estuary, provides the sole source: deep gravel croupes over blue, red, and grey clay-slate subsoil, farmed biodynamically with horses since 2008 to preserve soil structure. The Gironde's moderating influence — reducing frost risk and slowing ripening — is precisely what allows Latour to maintain quality in a cool, challenging year like 2013, where other Pauillac estates struggled. Vinification proceeds parcel-by-parcel in 80 temperature-controlled stainless steel vats, with approximately three weeks of skin contact and malolactic fermentation completed in tank before the wine moves to 100% new French oak barriques for 18–24 months in one of the Médoc's only subterranean barrel cellars, dug below the water table. The 2013 skews toward the typical Grand Vin blend of roughly 75–80% Cabernet Sauvignon with Merlot and Cabernet Franc, the cooler season preserving firm tannic architecture and graphite-driven austerity over opulence. Latour's celebrated vintage consistency — its defining structural advantage over the other four First Growths — makes 2013 a genuine expression of L'Enclos terroir rather than a compromise year.

name: Chateau Ducru Beaucaillou 2017
short_description (EN Store): Saint-Julien 2nd Growth on 6–8m Günz gravel; 100% new French oak, only 27% of harvest selected for Grand Vin.
description (EN Store):
In 1878, the estate's manager Ernest David and owner Nathaniel Johnston developed Bordeaux mixture on this very property — the copper-sulfate treatment that saved global viticulture from mildew. That instinct to act against convention persists: under Bruno Borie, who took the helm in 2003, Grand Vin production was cut from over 20,000 to just 6,000–8,000 cases annually, with only 27% of vineyard output selected for the label — a selectivity rate rare even among classified growths.

The core 50-hectare block surrounding the château sits on a 6–8 metre bed of Günz gravel — quartz pebbles, lydian jasper, flint, and agatoids deposited by the ancient Garonne roughly two million years ago over Oligocene mud and Eocene limestone. The Gironde estuary's four daily tidal movements moderate summer heat and deflect hailstorms, preserving the linear, graphite-driven tension that defines Saint-Julien's southern end.

Harvest is fully manual with multiple sorting passes including optical sorting. Vinification proceeds parcel-by-parcel in variably sized stainless steel tanks, with maceration of approximately seven days and gentle extraction at moderate temperatures. Malolactic fermentation is completed in concrete and stainless vats before 18–20 months in 100% new French oak barriques from five selected cooperages — a counter-cultural choice at this level, bucking the Médoc trend toward reduced new oak.

The 2017 vintage, not documented in the château's own vintage notes, falls between the monumental 2016 and the torrid 2018, generally producing wines of freshness and mid-weight precision on the Left Bank. Drinking window: approachable from 2024, with genuine upside through 2040+.

name: Domaine Mongeard-Mugneret Grands-Echezeaux Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Second-largest Grands Échezeaux holder after DRC — 70-year-old vines, 'Pinot Mongeard' massal clone, up to 60% new Allier oak, unfiltered.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Mongeard identified a serrated-leaf, low-yielding Pinot Noir mutation in the 19th century and had it preserved at the Beaune Viticultural School in 1884, he anchored the domaine's Grand Cru identity to a genetic lineage no négociant can replicate. That clone — 'Pinot Mongeard' — is what grows across the domaine's 1.44 ha of Grands Échezeaux, making Mongeard-Mugneret the second-largest holder of this 9.14 ha Grand Cru after Domaine de la Romanée-Conti.

The Grands Échezeaux parcel sits on clay over Bajocian limestone, mid-slope, south-east facing — a geology that delivers mineral density alongside drainage, concentrating the fruit of vines averaging ~70 years old. Harvest is by hand; Grand Cru fruit retains 40–100% whole clusters, followed by a 4–5 day cold pre-fermentation maceration and native yeast fermentation in temperature-controlled stainless at ~30°C for 12–15 days. The wine is transferred to barrel by gravity and aged 18–22 months in French oak from Allier, Nièvre, and Vosges at up to ~60% new oak, air-dried. Bottled unfiltered.

What breaks category convention here is the house's deliberate pursuit of approachability in youth without sacrificing Grand Cru architecture — a counterpoint to the austere, reductive profiles common across the Côte de Nuits. The 2022 vintage, a warm year with excellent phenolic ripeness, delivers that signature generosity with the structural backbone for a long cellar horizon.

name: Domaine Mongeard-Mugneret Echezeaux Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Échezeaux Grand Cru from a family rooted in Vosne-Romanée since 1620 — whole-cluster fermentation, up to 60% new Allier oak, unfiltered.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Mongeard identified a serrated-leaf, low-yielding Pinot Noir mutation on the family's Vosne-Romanée parcels in the 19th century, he sent cuttings to the Beaune Viticultural School in 1884 — the Pinot Mongeard massal selection that still anchors the domaine's ~1.82 ha of Échezeaux today. The family's holdings here sit on clay-over-Bajocian-limestone soils mid-slope, a substrate that delivers mineral density and controlled drainage, concentrating fruit without sacrificing the site's characteristic iron-flecked tension.

Vinification retains 40–100% whole clusters through a 4–5 day cold pre-fermentation maceration, followed by native yeast fermentation in temperature-controlled stainless steel at ~30°C for 12–15 days. The wine is transferred to barrel by gravity and aged 18–22 months in French oak from Allier, Nièvre, and Vosges forests — new-oak proportion reaching up to 60% for Grand Cru, calibrated vintage by vintage at pressing by Vincent Mongeard's palate alone. Bottled unfiltered at ~13.5% ABV.

What separates Mongeard-Mugneret from Côte de Nuits peers is a deliberate house philosophy of approachability in youth without sacrificing structure — a fruit-forward generosity that the more austere, reductive school of the appellation rarely permits. The 2022 vintage, a warm and early harvest year in Burgundy, amplifies that ripeness while the whole-cluster component preserves spinal freshness; drink from 2028 through 2040+.

name: Domaine Mongeard-Mugneret Echezeaux Grand Cru "la Grande Complication"
short_description (EN Store): Échezeaux Grand Cru from a 19th-century massal clone, aged up to 60% new Allier oak, unfiltered — Mongeard's ~1.82 ha parcel on Bajocian limestone.
description (EN Store): Jean-Baptiste Mongeard identified a low-yielding, serrated-leaf Pinot Noir mutation in the 19th century — the 'Pinot Mongeard' — preserved at the Beaune Viticultural School since 1884 and still propagated across the domaine's Grand Cru parcels today, giving this wine a direct genetic lineage unavailable to any producer who sources from commercial nurseries. The ~1.82 ha Échezeaux parcel sits mid-slope on the Côte de Nuits, its clay-over-Bajocian-limestone soils delivering both mineral density and sufficient drainage to concentrate the Pinot Mongeard clone's already-restricted yields into deeply pigmented must. Whole-cluster retention of 40–100% is applied at Grand Cru level, followed by 4–5 days cold pre-fermentation maceration, native yeast fermentation in temperature-controlled stainless at ~30°C for 12–15 days, and gravity transfer to barrel. Élevage runs 18–22 months in French oak from Allier, Nièvre, and Vosges, with new-oak proportion reaching up to ~60% (some sources cite 75% for Échezeaux) — a level Vincent Mongeard calibrates personally by palate at pressing rather than by formula. The wine is bottled unfiltered as standard practice, preserving textural complexity that filtration would strip. Unlike many Côte de Nuits Grand Crus built for austerity in youth, Mongeard's house emphasis on ripe, supple tannins makes the 2022 unusually approachable early while retaining the structural architecture for a decade-plus of development.

name: Domaine Mongeard-Mugneret Echezeaux Grand Cru "la Grande Complication" (Magnum) (1.5 L)
short_description (EN Store): Échezeaux Grand Cru in magnum from Vosne-Romanée's second-largest Grands Échezeaux holder — 40–100% whole-cluster, unfiltered, ~60% new Allier oak.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Mongeard identified a serrated-leaf, low-yielding Pinot Noir mutation in the 19th century and had it preserved at the Beaune Viticultural School in 1884, he was encoding the domaine's entire philosophy into a single vine: less fruit, more concentration, longer life. That massal selection — the 'Pinot Mongeard' — still underpins every bottle from this estate, including this Échezeaux Grand Cru.

The ~1.82 ha Échezeaux parcel sits mid-slope on the Côte de Nuits, where clay over Bajocian limestone delivers both mineral density and drainage discipline, moderating the vine's natural generosity and driving vertical tension into the fruit. Average vine age across the estate runs 40–45 years, with root systems deep enough to express the limestone's chalky backbone directly.

Vinification retains 40–100% whole clusters, with cold pre-fermentation maceration over 4–5 days before native yeast fermentation at ~30°C for 12–15 days in temperature-controlled stainless steel. The wine transfers to barrel by gravity, then ages 18–22 months in French oak from Allier, Nièvre, and Vosges — up to ~60% new — with Vincent Mongeard personally calibrating the new-oak ratio at pressing by palate alone. Bottled unfiltered as standard practice.

The magnum format slows oxygen ingress, compressing the élevage curve and extending the drinking window well beyond what a 750 ml allows — an unusual choice for a Grand Cru already built for decades. The 2022 vintage, not yet documented in the brand library, follows a warm, early-ripening Burgundy season broadly noted for aromatic precision and supple structure; expect the Mongeard house style's characteristic fruit-forward approachability to arrive earlier than the 2019, with the limestone backbone ensuring a long arc.

name: Domaine Mongeard-Mugneret Richebourg Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): 0.31 ha of Richebourg, clay-over-Bajocian limestone, ~⅓ whole clusters, up to 60% new Allier oak, unfiltered — from a family rooted in Vosne since 1620.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Baptiste Mongeard identified a serrated-leaf, low-yielding Pinot Noir mutation in the 19th century and had it preserved at the Beaune Viticultural School in 1884, he was encoding the domaine's philosophy into the vine itself: concentration through restraint, not intervention. That massal selection — the 'Pinot Mongeard' — still anchors the 0.31 ha Richebourg parcel today, a fragment of mid-slope Vosne-Romanée where clay over Bajocian limestone delivers both mineral density and the drainage that keeps yields honest at this elevation.

Vinification retains approximately one-third whole clusters for tannic architecture, preceded by a 4–5 day cold pre-fermentation maceration and followed by 12–15 days of natural-yeast fermentation in temperature-controlled stainless at ~30°C. The wine moves to barrel by gravity, then ages 18–22 months in Allier, Nièvre, and Vosges French oak at up to ~60% new — a ratio Vincent Mongeard calibrates by palate at pressing, not by formula. Bottled unfiltered.

What separates Mongeard's Richebourg from austere Côte de Nuits orthodoxy is deliberate: the house pursues ripe, supple tannins and aromatic generosity even at Grand Cru level, making this one of the few Richebourgs that rewards relatively early access without sacrificing the structural depth for a 20–30 year arc. The 2022 vintage, a warm and even growing season in the Côte de Nuits, amplifies that fruit-forward signature while the limestone subsoil preserves the tension needed for long cellaring.

name: Domaine Gros Frère et Soeur Clos de Vougeot Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Clos de Vougeot 'En Musigni' — northwestern corner parcel, 100% new Bertranges oak, unfiltered, from the opulent Gros Frère et Soeur school.
description (EN Store):
When Gustave and Colette Gros divided Domaine Louis Gros in 1963, they inherited the ~1.5 ha 'En Musigni' parcel — the northwestern corner of Clos de Vougeot, wedged between the Château wall and the slope below Musigny itself. This is the highest, coolest sector of the clos, where Bajocian and Bathonian limestone sits closer to the surface beneath iron-rich clay, delivering a structural tension and floral lift that the lower, heavier soils of the clos cannot replicate.

Winemaking here is deliberately maximalist: hand-harvested fruit is fully destemmed and sorted on a vibrating table before fermentation in temperature-controlled concrete tanks, with remontage before alcoholic fermentation and twice-daily pigeage once underway. The wine then enters 100% new 228-litre pièces from Bertranges Forest — wood the domaine purchases green and dries for two full years before coopering — and rests on lees without racking through the first year.

The defining transgression: where most serious Côte de Nuits producers cap new oak at 50–70% for grand cru, Gros Frère et Soeur applies 100% new oak across all grand cru bottlings, a house conviction that produces a richer, darker-fruited Pinot Noir than virtually any peer in the appellation. Bottled unfiltered as standard. The 2022 vintage — not yet documented in the domaine's own notes — follows a warm, even growing season across Burgundy, pointing toward generous fruit density and early textural appeal, with the Musigni elevation providing the acidity needed for a 10–15 year arc.

name: Domaine Gros Frère et Soeur Richebourg Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): 0.7-ha Richebourg on Bajocian limestone-clay, aged in 100% new Bertranges-forest oak — Vosne's most unapologetically opulent grand cru.
description (EN Store): When Gustave and Colette Gros divided Domaine Louis Gros in 1963, they inherited a sliver of Richebourg — roughly 0.7 ha on the mid-slope of Vosne-Romanée — and built a house philosophy around extracting maximum expression from it rather than whispering it into restraint. That philosophy has never wavered across three subsequent generations. The parcel sits on the classic Bajocian and Bathonian limestone bedrock threaded with iron-rich clay that gives Richebourg its characteristic density and ferrous depth; mid-slope drainage concentrates the vine's stress and compresses the fruit into the kind of structural intensity that rewards long cellaring. Vinification begins with hand-harvest and vibrating-table sorting, followed by full destemming and fermentation in temperature-controlled concrete tanks with remontage before alcoholic fermentation and twice-daily pigeage once underway. Maturation proceeds in 100% new 228-litre Bertranges-forest pièces, the domaine's own wood stock dried for two years before coopering, with the wine resting on lees without racking through the first year. Bottled unfiltered as standard practice. The special feature here is the house's deliberate refusal of Burgundy's minimalist orthodoxy: 100% new oak on grand cru is a provocation in the Côte de Nuits, producing a Richebourg of uncommon density and aromatic richness that diverges sharply from the terroir-transparency school. The 2022 vintage — not documented in the brand library with domaine-specific notes — should be assessed against the estate's established profile of deep colour, full body, and cellar-worthy structure.

name: Domaine Georges Noellat Vosne-Romanee 1er Cru "Les Chaumes"
short_description (EN Store): Les Chaumes 1er Cru from a 20-year-old Maxime Cheurlin's debut-built domaine: infusion-not-extraction, cooled-cellar MLF delay, unfined 2022.
description (EN Store): When Maxime Cheurlin took his first harvest in 2010 at age twenty, the entire domaine had spent the previous two decades selling its fruit in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin — meaning every bottle of Noëllat in circulation today was built from scratch by a single young winemaker who is, by blood, a cousin of the Jayer lineage. Les Chaumes sits on the southern flank of Vosne-Romanée's Premier Cru belt, where classic argilo-calcaire (clay-limestone) soils deliver the Côte de Nuits signature: the limestone fraction provides drainage and aromatic precision while the clay fraction adds mid-palate density and concentration. Vines are worked entirely by hand under lutte raisonnée with no insecticides or chemical fertilisers, and yields are held to approximately 25 hl/ha — well below appellation norms. In the cellar, grapes are 100% destemmed (with occasional whole-cluster inclusion up to ~30%), cold-soaked with native yeasts only, and fermented with the rarest of gentle punch-downs — what Cheurlin calls an 'infusion' rather than extraction philosophy. Aging runs 14–20 months in French oak; the wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. The defining technical transgression: since approximately 2017, Cheurlin actively cools the cellar to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a move Allen Meadows credits with adding measurable precision and complexity. The 2022 vintage shows fine intensity and structural refinement; drink from 2026 through the mid-2030s.

name: Domaine Georges Noellat Vosne-Romanee 1er Cru "Les Petits Monts"
short_description (EN Store): Frost-reduced 2021 from a premier cru site above Vosne's village; Maxime Cheurlin's infusion-not-extraction philosophy on old-vine clay-limestone.
description (EN Store): When Maxime Cheurlin took his first vintage in 2010 at age twenty, the entire domaine's production had been sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin for two decades — meaning every bottle of Noëllat carrying a domaine label is, effectively, a post-2010 creation built from scratch by a single young winemaker with pre-war vines and a Jayer bloodline. Les Petits Monts sits on the upper slope of Vosne-Romanée's premier cru band, where the argilo-calcaire soils thin toward the limestone bedrock; the elevated limestone fraction sharpens drainage and amplifies aromatic lift, while residual clay sustains mid-palate concentration even in lean years. The 2021 frost compressed yields further below the domaine's already austere ~25 hl/ha average, concentrating the fruit into a focused, precise expression. Cheurlin's winemaking is built around restraint: 100% destemmed, native-yeast fermentation, rare and gentle punch-downs that extract color and tannin by infusion rather than force, and French oak aging (14–20 months) at a new-oak proportion calibrated to premier cru level. His most transgressive technical choice — active cellar cooling to deliberately delay malolactic fermentation, adopted from around 2017 — preserves a structural precision that Allen Meadows has credited with elevating the domaine's wines beyond their appellation weight. Bottled unfined and unfiltered. The 2021 vintage delivers focused, precise fruit with characteristic Noëllat delicacy; limited volumes make this one of the harder cuvées to source. Drink from 2025 through 2034.

name: Domaine Georges Noellat Vosne-Romanee 1er Cru "Les Beaux Monts"
short_description (EN Store): 80-year-old vines on Vosne's clay-limestone upper slope; Maxime Cheurlin built this reputation from zero at age 20 in 2010.
description (EN Store): When Maxime Cheurlin took his first harvest in 2010, the entire domaine's production had been sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin for the previous two decades — meaning every bottle of Noëllat on the market today is the work of a single young winemaker who rebuilt the estate's identity from scratch, starting with vines already over 70 years old. Les Beaux Monts sits on the upper slope of Vosne-Romanée's premier cru band, where the argilo-calcaire soils — clay retaining concentration, limestone providing drainage and aromatic lift — are worked by hand under lutte raisonnée, with no insecticides or chemical fertilisers. Yields average a severe ~25 hl/ha, compressing the output of vines now exceeding 80 years of age. In the cellar, grapes are 100% destemmed (with occasional whole-cluster inclusion up to ~30% in select vintages), cold-soaked for several days, then fermented with indigenous yeasts only under Cheurlin's signature 'infusion' philosophy — punch-downs rare and deliberately gentle to avoid extracting harsh tannin. Aging runs 14–20 months in French oak, with new oak calibrated to appellation level; the wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. The defining technical transgression: the cellar is actively cooled to delay and slow malolactic fermentation — a technique adopted from around 2017 that critics including Allen Meadows credit with adding measurable precision and complexity. The frost-affected 2021 vintage produced limited volumes but delivered focused, precise fruit that amplifies the site's characteristic lacy tension; drink from 2026 through the early 2030s.

name: Domaine Georges Noellat Nuits-Saint-Georges 1er Cru "Aux Boudots"
short_description (EN Store): Frost-reduced 2021 from Aux Boudots: infusion-philosophy Pinot Noir, native yeasts, delayed malo, unfined — built by Maxime Cheurlin from his very first 2010 vintage.
description (EN Store):
From 1990 to 2010, every grape grown at Domaine Georges Noëllat was sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin — meaning the entire reputation now attached to this address was constructed by Maxime Cheurlin, then 20 years old, starting from a single harvest. That biographical fact is inseparable from the technical ambition on display here.

Aux Boudots sits at the northern tip of Nuits-Saint-Georges, where the appellation boundary meets Vosne-Romanée, on classic Côte de Nuits argilo-calcaire soils — clay providing concentration and textural weight, limestone delivering drainage and aromatic precision. The proximity to Vosne lends the climat a translucency unusual for Nuits.

Cheurlin's winemaking is built around restraint: 100% destemmed fruit, cold pre-fermentation maceration, native yeasts only, and punch-downs that are rare and deliberately gentle — what he calls 'infusion' rather than extraction. Aging runs 14–20 months in French oak, with new-oak proportion calibrated by appellation. Crucially, the cellar is actively cooled to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a technique adopted from around 2017 that critics including Allen Meadows credit with adding precision and structural complexity. Bottled unfined and unfiltered.

The 2021 vintage — frost-ravaged across the Côte — produced severely limited volumes but wines of focused, precise fruit that amplify the domaine's characteristic lacy texture rather than compensating with weight. A Cheurlin-Noëllat lineage wine (he is a cousin of Emmanuel Rouget's sons) that drinks on the lighter side of Nuits but carries uncommon depth for its appellation level.

name: Domaine Georges Noellat Grands Echezeaux Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Flagey-Échézeaux Grand Cru from pre-WWII old vines; Maxime Cheurlin's cellar-cooled malolactic delay and infusion-not-extraction philosophy, unfined and unfiltered.
description (EN Store): From 2010 to today, Maxime Cheurlin — blood relative of Henri Jayer and cousin to Emmanuel Rouget's sons — built this domaine's entire reputation from scratch, having inherited parcels whose fruit was sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin for the preceding two decades. That context matters: every technical decision here is deliberate and recent, not inherited habit. Grands Échézeaux sits in Flagey-Échézeaux on classic Côte de Nuits argilo-calcaire (clay-limestone), where the limestone fraction drives drainage and aromatic precision while clay contributes structural depth — conditions that reward the low-intervention approach Cheurlin has staked his reputation on. In the cellar, 100% destemmed fruit undergoes cold pre-fermentation maceration before native-yeast fermentation with rare, gentle punch-downs — an infusion philosophy that preserves aromatic filigree over extracted mass. Aging runs 14–20 months in French oak at up to 100% new barrique for Grand Cru, while the cellar is actively chilled to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a technique adopted from 2017 onward that Allen Meadows/Burghound credits with adding measurable precision. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. What no peer does at this level: Cheurlin deliberately suppresses extraction where most Côte de Nuits producers amplify it, making Grands Échézeaux here a study in translucency rather than density. 2022 shows fine intensity and refinement; drink from 2027 onward.

name: Maxime Cheurlin Noellat Chambolle Musigny 1er Cru "les Feusselottes"
short_description (EN Store): Les Feusselottes 1er Cru from a 20-year-old Maxime Cheurlin's debut vintage estate — infusion-style, unfined, malolactic deliberately delayed for precision.
description (EN Store): From 1990 to 2010, every grape grown on this domaine was sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin — meaning Maxime Cheurlin, then just 20 years old and a blood relative of the Jayer family, built the entire estate reputation from a standing start with his very first vintage. Les Feusselottes sits on the eastern flank of Chambolle-Musigny, where the classic Côte de Nuits argilo-calcaire (clay-limestone) subsoil delivers the limestone's drainage and aromatic lift against clay's structural concentration — conditions that in Chambolle tend toward florality and translucency rather than the darker muscle of Vosne. Cheurlin's cellar approach is deliberately counter-extractive: 100% destemmed, native-yeast fermentation, rare and gentle punch-downs, and an 'infusion' philosophy that prioritises texture over grip. French oak aging runs 14–20 months with new oak calibrated well below Grand Cru levels for a Premier Cru, preserving site transparency. The domaine's signature technical transgression — active cellar cooling to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, adopted from around 2017 — adds a precision and tensile acidity that peers in the Côte de Nuits rarely pursue. The 2021 vintage, frost-reduced in volume, produced focused, precise fruit with the characteristic Noëllat delicacy amplified by the year's natural austerity; drink from 2026 onward.

name: Maxime Cheurlin Noellat Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Noirots
short_description (EN Store): Chambolle's Les Noirots through Maxime Cheurlin's 'infusion' lens: native yeasts, cooled-cellar MLF delay, unfined — built from scratch since 2010.
description (EN Store): From 1990 to 2010, every bottle this domaine could have made was sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin — meaning Maxime Cheurlin, grandson of Marie-Thérèse Noëllat and a blood relative of the Jayer family, built the entire estate reputation from his very first vintage at age twenty. Les Noirots sits on the northern flank of Chambolle-Musigny, where the classic Côte de Nuits argilo-calcaire (clay-limestone) subsoil delivers the appellation's signature aromatic lift from the limestone fraction while the clay underpins concentration — a combination that rewards Cheurlin's low-intervention approach far more than a heavier extraction style would. In the cellar, grapes are 100% destemmed, cold-soaked for several days, then fermented exclusively with indigenous yeasts using rare, gentle punch-downs — what Cheurlin calls infusion rather than extraction. Aging runs 14–20 months in French oak; the cellar is actively cooled to deliberately delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a technique adopted from around 2017 that Allen Meadows has credited with adding precision and complexity. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. The MLF-delay protocol is the technical transgression here: almost no Côte de Nuits producer applies it systematically across the range. The 2022 vintage shows fine intensity and refinement, continuing the domaine's run of strong recent releases.

name: Maxime Cheurlin Noellat Charmes Chambertin Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Charmes-Chambertin from a 20-year-old's debut vintage — built on infusion, not extraction, with cellar-cooled malolactic for surgical precision.
description (EN Store): When Maxime Cheurlin took the reins in 2010 at age twenty, the entire domaine's production had been sold in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin for two decades — meaning every bottle bearing the Noëllat name today was built from scratch by a single young winemaker whose bloodline runs directly to the Jayer family. Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru sits on the argilo-calcaire soils of Gevrey-Chambertin's southern flank, where the clay fraction delivers concentration and the fractured limestone beneath provides drainage and the aromatic lift that separates this climat from the more muscular northern Grands Crus. Cheurlin's approach is defined by indigenous-yeast fermentation, rare and gentle punch-downs — an 'infusion' philosophy that preserves lacy texture over extracted power — and French oak aging of 14–20 months at up to 100% new oak for Grand Cru. The cellar is actively cooled to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a technique adopted from around 2017 that critics including Allen Meadows credit with adding measurable precision and complexity. The 2019 vintage, a warm and seductive year yielding up to 35 hl/ha, delivers generous, ripe fruit with the decadent character the season produced across the Côte de Nuits; drinking window opens from 2024 onward.

name: Maxime Cheurlin Noellat Bonnes-Mares Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Bonnes-Mares 2019 from Maxime Cheurlin, who rebuilt this Vosne-Romanée domaine from zero at age 20 — infusion-style, unfined, malolactic deliberately delayed.
description (EN Store): When Maxime Cheurlin took his first vintage in 2010, the entire domaine had sold its production in bulk to Jadot and Drouhin for two decades — meaning every bottle of Noëllat on the market today is the work of a single young winemaker rebuilding a legacy from scratch. That biographical fact is inseparable from the technical ambition on display here. Bonnes-Mares straddles the Chambolle-Musigny/Morey-Saint-Denis boundary on classic Côte de Nuits argilo-calcaire soils — the limestone fraction delivering drainage and aromatic lift, the clay underlay providing the structural density that separates Bonnes-Mares from its more ethereal Chambolle neighbours. Cheurlin's winemaking is defined by restraint: 100% destemmed fruit, native yeast fermentation, punch-downs described as rare and deliberately gentle — an infusion philosophy rather than extraction. French oak aging runs 14–20 months, with Grand Cru cuvées receiving up to 100% new oak, yet the cellar is actively cooled to slow and delay malolactic fermentation — an unusual technical intervention Cheurlin adopted from around 2017 that critics including Allen Meadows credit with adding precision and complexity. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. In 2019 — a warm, seductive vintage with yields reaching ~35 hl/ha — Bonnes-Mares channels that ripeness into a decadent, generous expression; drink from 2024 onward, with a long window ahead.

name: Domaine Audiffred Vosne-Romanee 1er Cru "les Reignots"
short_description (EN Store): Aux Reignots 1er Cru: steep east-facing limestone above La Romanée-Conti, vinified in old oak only by a former DRC cellarmaster.
description (EN Store): Henri Audiffred spent 14 years at Domaine de la Romanée-Conti before returning in 2007 to reshape his family's 5-hectare estate — and the most consequential decision he brought back was the complete rejection of new oak, a direct counter to the new-wood-heavy orthodoxy of the Côte de Nuits. Aux Reignots sits on the upper slope of Vosne-Romanée at 280–320 m elevation, a steep 15% gradient with a due-east aspect and sandy topsoil over hard limestone bedrock — conditions that produce a slightly cooler mesoclimate than the Grand Cru belt directly below, tightening structure and extending aromatic development. The 1.62-ha climat sits immediately above La Romanée-Conti and La Romanée, sharing the same geological spine. Vinification begins with 100% destemming and a brief cold maceration, followed by wild-yeast fermentation in wooden vats over 15–18 days with pigeage and remontage at least three times daily. Élevage runs 16–18 months in exclusively old oak with two rackings; the wine is bottled manually, unfined and unfiltered. The special feature here is the zero-new-oak élevage on a Premier Cru that shares its geological address with two of Burgundy's most scrutinised Grand Crus — terroir transparency rather than oak-driven amplification is the explicit goal. The 2022 vintage delivered cool nights and a dry, even ripening season across the Côte de Nuits, yielding wines of precise structure and lifted aromatics; Reignots at this elevation will reward 5–12 years of cellaring.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Vosne Romanee
short_description (EN Store): 80-year-old vines adjacent to Premier Cru Les Suchots; whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered, biodynamic bottling — village Burgundy with Grand Cru adjacency.
description (EN Store): Jean-Pierre Guyon took full management of this family estate in 1991 and made a deliberate choice that separates him from virtually every peer in the Côte de Nuits: whole-cluster fermentation applied across the entire range, from Bourgogne Régionale to Grand Cru, made possible only because his massale-selected old vines consistently ripen stems to full phenolic maturity. The Vosne-Romanée village bottling draws from the 'Les Charmes de Mazières' parcel — a blend of Hautes Maizières and Les Chalandins plots planted on clay-limestone soils, with vines averaging 80 years of age, sitting directly adjacent to Premier Cru Les Suchots. That proximity and vine age compress the usual village-Premier Cru quality gap considerably. In the cellar, Guyon's proprietary 'en nuage' technique sees a 30-person sorting team hand-remove central bunch stems, leaving berry clusters free of rachis — a refinement of whole-cluster philosophy not replicated across a full range by any other domaine. Cold maceration runs 4–5 days, fermentation proceeds with indigenous yeasts at 27–29°C over 25–28 days, cap managed by hand with a watering can rather than pump-over. Élevage in one-year-old French oak barrels for 10–14 months, followed by 3–4 months in stainless steel before biodynamic-calendar bottling, unfiltered and unfined. The 2021 vintage — low-yielding and challenging across Burgundy — was cited by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for Guyon's low-intervention style, concentrating the parcel's character into a taut, precise expression. Drink 2025–2032.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Vosne Romanee "Les Charmes De Mazieres"
short_description (EN Store): 80-year-old vines adjacent to Premier Cru Les Suchots; whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered, biodynamic bottling — village Burgundy with Grand Cru-adjacent roots.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Pierre Guyon resolved not to uproot a single vine after taking full management in 1991, the decision was agronomic as much as philosophical — the 80-year-old massale-selected Pinot Noir in the Hautes Maizières and Les Chalandins plots that compose this cuvée sits directly adjacent to Premier Cru Les Suchots, and vine age alone concentrates what the appellation's clay-limestone soils deliver: tightly wound red-fruit precision and a structural depth that village-tier Burgundy rarely achieves. The 2021 vintage, low-yielding and demanding across the Côte de Nuits, compressed those qualities further, producing wines Berry Bros. & Rudd flagged as benchmarks for Guyon's low-intervention approach under pressure.

In the cellar, 100% whole-cluster fermentation proceeds via the proprietary 'en nuage' technique — a 30-person sorting team hand-removes central rachis stems, leaving intact berry clusters — followed by 4–5 days cold pre-fermentation maceration and a 25–28 day cuvaison at 27–29°C, managed by hand with a watering can rather than mechanical pump-over. Élevage runs 10–14 months in one-year-old French oak barrels, then 3–4 months in stainless steel before biodynamic-calendar bottling, unfiltered and unfined. What sets this apart: Guyon applies the full 'en nuage' protocol to his village wines — a commitment no other Côte de Nuits domaine extends across an entire range. The 2021 drinking window favours patience; the vine age and vintage tension suggest 2026–2033.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Vosne Romanee Premier Cru "En Orveaux"
short_description (EN Store): En Orveaux 1er Cru: 0.20ha clay-limestone, 45-yr vines, whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered — Échezeaux-adjacent Vosne at its most transparent.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Pierre Guyon acquired his Premier and Grand Cru parcels in 1987, he inherited a philosophy as much as land: not a single vine has been uprooted since he took over, and the En Orveaux parcel — a mere 0.20 hectares of clay-limestone soil in the Échezeaux climat — sits directly adjacent to Grands Échezeaux and Musigny, sharing their geological spine without their Grand Cru price.

Those ~45-year-old massale-selection vines on clay-limestone drive the wine's structural tension: clay retains moisture through Burgundy's increasingly erratic summers while limestone pulls the roots toward mineral depth, producing a concentration that needs no extraction to announce itself.

In the cellar, Guyon applies his proprietary 'en nuage' technique — a 30-person sorting team hand-removes central bunch stems, leaving berry clusters intact without the rachis — an evolution of whole-cluster philosophy that no other Côte de Nuits domaine applies across a full range. 4–5 days cold pre-fermentation maceration precedes a 25–28 day cuvaison at 27–29°C with indigenous yeasts; cap management is done by hand with a custom watering can rather than pump-over. Élevage runs 10–14 months in new French oak barriques, followed by 3–4 months in stainless steel before unfiltered, unfined bottling timed to the biodynamic lunar calendar.

What no peer replicates: whole-cluster fermentation from Bourgogne Régionale through Grand Cru, made viable only because Guyon's old-vine massale selections ripen stems fully every year. The 2021 vintage — low-yielding and demanding — was cited by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for the domaine's low-intervention style; expect taut red-fruit precision and fine-grained tannins that will resolve beautifully through 2030–2034.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Vosne Romanee Premier Cru "Les Brulees"
short_description (EN Store): 0.15 ha bordering Richebourg, 100% whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered — Guyon's most site-specific Premier Cru in a low-yield 2021.
description (EN Store): When Jean-Pierre Guyon began removing central bunch stems by hand on a 30-person sorting table — his proprietary 'en nuage' technique, evolving since 2016 — he was solving a specific problem: how to retain whole-cluster aromatic lift without the green rachis tannin that defeats it. That decision defines what Les Brûlées becomes in the glass. The 0.15-hectare parcel sits pressed against Richebourg Grand Cru to the south and Les Suchots Premier Cru to the east, on the clay-limestone mid-slope of Vosne-Romanée where iron-rich soils concentrate pigment and drive the site's characteristic dark-fruit tension against a chalky mineral spine. Fermentation runs 25–28 days in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 27–29°C — a long, gentle cuvaison Guyon describes as infusion rather than extraction — preceded by 4–5 days cold maceration with indigenous yeasts and zero added sulphur during alcoholic fermentation. Cap management is done by hand with a watering can, a method borrowed from Dirk Niepoort, avoiding mechanical shear on the skins. Élevage moves into new French oak barriques for 10–14 months, then 3–4 months in stainless steel before bottling unfined and unfiltered to a biodynamic lunar calendar. What no peer does across a full range: Guyon applies this whole-cluster philosophy from Bourgogne Régionale to Grand Cru, made credible only by massale-selected old vines that ripen stems fully. The 2021 vintage — low-yielding and challenging across the Côte de Nuits — is documented by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for the domaine's low-intervention approach; drink 2026–2034.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Echezeux Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Échezeaux 'En Orveaux' 0.20 ha, clay-limestone, 45-yr vines — whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered, biodynamic bottling. 2021.
description (EN Store):
When Jean-Pierre Guyon acquired his Échezeaux parcel in 1987, he committed to a principle that would define the domaine: not a single vine uprooted, not a stem removed by machine. The 0.20-hectare 'En Orveaux' parcel — clay-limestone soils, positioned adjacent to Grands Échezeaux and Musigny — sits among the most pressure-tested real estate in Burgundy, yet Guyon's approach is conspicuously unhurried. Clay retains moisture through the dry spells that define Côte de Nuits summers, while limestone drives the vertical mineral tension that separates En Orveaux from fleshier Échezeaux sites lower on the slope.

In the cellar, 100% whole-cluster fermentation proceeds via indigenous yeasts through a 4–5 day cold pre-maceration, followed by 25–28 days of gentle cuvaison at 27–29°C — cap management executed by hand with a watering can, a method borrowed from Dirk Niepoort to avoid mechanical extraction. Since 2016, Guyon has applied his proprietary 'en nuage' technique: a 30-person sorting team hand-removes central rachis stems while preserving intact berry clusters, capturing whole-cluster aromatic lift without green tannic intrusion. Élevage in new French oak barriques runs 10–14 months, followed by 3–4 months in stainless steel before unfiltered, unfined bottling timed to the biodynamic lunar calendar.

The 2021 vintage — low-yielding and challenging across Burgundy — was cited by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for Guyon's low-intervention philosophy: reduced volumes concentrated the old-vine intensity of 45-year massale-selected Pinot Noir into a wine of uncommon precision. Drink 2026–2038.

name: Domaine Bruno Clair Gevrey Chambertin 1er Cru "Les Cazetiers"
short_description (EN Store): Frost-reduced 2021 from Cazetiers' east-facing limestone slope — indigenous yeast, 18–20 months oak, unfined — bright cherry and firm mineral tension.
description (EN Store):
When Bruno Clair reconstituted his family's historic parcels after the Clair-Daü break-up in 1985, Les Cazetiers was among the premier cru sites he reclaimed — vines on an estate whose average age now sits at ~50 years, rooted in the shallow brown limestone soils characteristic of Gevrey-Chambertin's mid-slope. The east-facing aspect at roughly 300 m elevation channels morning sun while preserving cool afternoon temperatures, driving the mineral tension and aromatic lift that define the domaine's house signature.

Vinification follows a restrained protocol: hand-harvested, carefully sorted fruit fermented with ambient indigenous yeast in open wooden vats, preceded by a cold pre-fermentation maceration of 5–7 days and a total maceration of 10–15 days with punch-downs. Whole-cluster inclusion (20–50% range across the estate) adds structural backbone without obscuring site character. Aging runs 18–20 months in barriques at moderate new-oak levels, followed by a short tank blending period before bottling unfined and filtered only when the vintage demands it.

The 2021 vintage — frost-reduced, lower-yielding — produced wines Bruno Clair himself described as proof that 'for truly great wines, there are no bad years': bright cherry fruit, rose petal lift, crisp acidity, and firm tannins that reward 5–10 years of cellaring yet are already approachable.

name: Domaine Bruno Clair Gevrey Chambertin 1er Cru "Clos Saint Jacques"
short_description (EN Store): 1.0 ha of 1957/1972 vines on shallow brown limestone at 315 m — Clos Saint-Jacques, the Côte d'Or's most argued Premier Cru, in a frost-shaped 2021.
description (EN Store): When Bruno Clair reconstituted the parcels of the dissolved Domaine Clair-Daü after 1985, Clos Saint-Jacques was among the recovered jewels — a 1.0-hectare east/southeast-facing plot at 315 m whose vines, planted in 1957 and 1972, now average over 50 years of age under massal-selection material. The shallow brown limestone soils here drain freely while retaining just enough moisture to sustain old-vine root systems through dry summers, producing fruit of concentrated mineral tension rather than weight. Whole-cluster inclusion of 20–50% (adjusted by vintage), indigenous yeast fermentation in open wooden vats, and a cold pre-fermentation maceration of 5–7 days precede a total maceration of 10–15 days — a regime designed to extract structure from the site rather than the winemaker's hand. The wine then ages 18–20 months in barriques at approximately 35% new French oak, unfined and filtered only when the vintage demands it. The transgression here is classification: peers and négociants have long argued Clos Saint-Jacques deserves Grand Cru status, yet it remains Premier Cru — meaning buyers access Grand Cru-calibre terroir at a structural price discount. The frost-reduced 2021 delivers bright cherry, rose petal, and firm, precise tannins with crisp acidity; Bruno Clair himself noted no vintage is truly bad for great sites. Drink from 2027 through 2038+.

name: Domaine Bruno Clair Chambertin Grand Cru "Clos de Beze"
short_description (EN Store): Clos de Bèze 0.98 ha; ~⅔ planted 1912 on red clay; indigenous yeast, open-vat fermentation, 18–20 months oak — frost-reduced 2021 with piercing acidity and firm structure.
description (EN Store):
When Bruno Clair reconstituted his family's parcels after the Domaine Clair-Daü dissolution in 1985, he recovered vines in Chambertin-Clos de Bèze that had been in the ground since 1912 — plant material now over a century old, propagated through massal selection and never replanted. That continuity of root depth in red clay soils is the single greatest differentiator of this 0.98-hectare holding within the Grand Cru.

Red clay retains moisture through dry spells while delivering the iron-rich mineral tension that separates Clos de Bèze from its neighbour Chambertin proper. The 2021 frost dramatically reduced yields across the Côte de Nuits, concentrating what remained in these ancient vines to an uncommon degree.

Fruit is hand-harvested and sorted, then fermented with ambient indigenous yeast in open wooden vats with punch-downs; cold pre-fermentation maceration runs approximately 5–7 days before a total maceration of 10–15 days. Whole-cluster inclusion of 20–50% (adjusted to vintage) adds structural spine and aromatic lift. Aging runs 18–20 months in barriques at new-oak levels calibrated to the Grand Cru tier, bottled unfined and filtered only when the vintage demands it.

What no peer at this address routinely does: Bruno Clair's deliberate restraint — prioritising mineral precision over extraction — means this Clos de Bèze reads closer to a Chambolle in aromatic delicacy than the muscular profile many expect from Gevrey's greatest Grand Cru.

The 2021 vintage delivers bright cherry, rose petal, and crisp acidity with firm tannins; Bruno Clair himself noted 'for truly great wines, there are no bad years.' Approachable from 2027, with genuine mid-term cellaring capacity through 2038+.

name: Domaine Bruno Clair Bonnes Mares Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): 1.64 ha of Ostrea acuminata marl in Morey-Saint-Denis; vines from 1946; ~45% new oak; frost-reduced 2021 yields sharpen every edge.
description (EN Store):
When Bruno Clair rebuilt the domaine from the ruins of Clair-Daü after 1985, the 1.64-hectare Bonnes-Mares parcel in Morey-Saint-Denis was among the most prized parcels he recovered — planted across four generations of vines (1946, 1962, 1978, 1980) on southeast-facing clay-limestone soils laced with Ostrea acuminata marls at 280 metres elevation. That fossil-rich marl fraction is the geological signature that separates this corner of Bonnes-Mares from the more clay-dominant Chambolle side, driving a structural tension and saline mineral grip that outlasts the fruit.

Vinification follows the house protocol: hand-harvest with rigorous sorting, indigenous yeast fermentation in open wooden vats, cold pre-fermentation maceration of 5–7 days, and a total maceration of 10–15 days with punch-downs. The wine then ages 18–20 months in barriques at approximately 45% new French oak — the highest new-oak rate in the domaine's red range — before bottling unfined and filtered only if the vintage demands it.

2021's severe spring frosts compressed yields dramatically, concentrating the Morey fruit into a wine of bright cherry, rose petal, and firm, precise tannins — Bruno Clair himself noted 'for truly great wines, there are no bad years.' The reduced crop amplifies the Ostrea marl's mineral signature in a vintage where many peers produced dilute wines. Approachable from 2026, with genuine mid-term cellaring capacity through the mid-2030s.

name: Domaine Claude Dugat Gevrey Chambertin 1er Cru
short_description (EN Store): Lavaux Saint-Jacques or Craipillot, 2021: cool-season precision from ≤18 hl/ha old vines, unfined, unfiltered, up to 100% new François Frères oak.
description (EN Store):
When Claude Dugat began estate-bottling under his own name in 1991, the domaine's philosophy was already fixed: extreme yield restriction, zero fining, zero filtration — a non-interventionist stance that predated Burgundy's broader 'natural' movement by decades. Today, siblings Bertrand and Laetitia continue from the 2015 vintage, deliberately pulling harvest dates earlier to lock in acidity rather than chasing ripeness.

The Premier Cru parcels — Lavaux Saint-Jacques and Craipillot — sit on the limestone and clay-limestone soils of the Côte de Nuits, Lavaux occupying a south-western slope that borders the celebrated Clos Saint-Jacques. Semi-continental climate and slow ripening are the structural engine here; the clay fraction retains moisture through dry spells while the limestone drives the chalky mineral tension that defines the appellation's finest sites.

Vinification is 100% destemmed, fermented in concrete vats on indigenous yeasts, with cap punch-downs twice daily and virtually no pump-overs — a deliberate choice to build texture without extracting harsh tannins. Vatting runs approximately two weeks, followed by two days of settling before racking to François Frères 228 L barrels at up to 100% new oak for 15–18 months across two cellars. Yields are capped at ≤18 hl/ha, delivering Grand Cru-level concentration at Premier Cru tier.

The 2021 vintage — cool and challenging across the Côte de Nuits — produced wines of uncommon freshness and precision: lively acidity, taut red fruit, and crystalline terroir definition that rewards cellaring from 2026 through the mid-2030s.

name: Domaine Claude Dugat Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): 0.31 ha of iron-rich Charmes limestone, 100% destemmed, up to 100% new François Frères oak — unfined, unfiltered, ≤18 hl/ha. Final 2021 allocation before 2022 closes the chapter.
description (EN Store): Claude Dugat began estate-bottling under his own name in 1991, working from the Cellier des Dîmes — a cellar built in 1219 in the heart of Gevrey-Chambertin — and built one of Burgundy's most allocation-scarce cults without a single marketing effort. The Charmes-Chambertin parcel covers just 0.31 hectares of iron-rich reddish limestone over clay, a soil composition that drives pigment depth and structural grip distinct from the paler limestone of neighbouring Grands Crus. Harvest is manual and timed by taste rather than laboratory analysis, deliberately early to lock in natural acidity — a discipline that defines the 2021 vintage's signature precision. In the cellar: 100% destemming, fermentation in concrete vats on indigenous yeasts, cap punched down twice daily with virtually no pump-overs, two-week vatting, then up to 100% new François Frères 228 L barrique for 15–18 months across two cellars. Bottled unfined and unfiltered with minimal sulphur — a natural deposit is expected and intentional. What no peer does at this address: Grand Cru concentration achieved at ≤18 hl/ha across every tier, from Bourgogne Rouge upward. The 2021 cool growing season produced wines of uncommon freshness and acidity-driven purity; the Charmes rewards a decade of cellaring but its transparency is already legible. Drink 2027–2040.

name: Claude Dugat La Gibryotte Bourgogne - Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Dugat family's micro-négociant label: Gevrey-Chambertin village Pinot Noir, concrete-tank fermented, unfined/unfiltered, 2022 warm vintage with clean ripe fruit.
description (EN Store): When Claude Dugat's children — Bertrand, Laetitia, and Jeanne — established La Gibryotte as a micro-négociant label, they carried forward the same non-interventionist philosophy that made the domaine allocation-only: harvest by taste rather than laboratory analysis, zero pump-overs during fermentation, and bottling without fining or filtration.
