short_description (EN Store): Gevrey-Chambertin village from Guyon's whole-cluster, 'en nuage' low-intervention range — old-vine massale Pinot Noir, unfiltered, biodynamic bottling.
description (EN Store): Jean-Pierre Guyon built his domaine from 1987 by acquiring Grand Cru and Premier Cru parcels in the Côte de Nuits, earning ECOCERT Organic certification from the 2012 vintage — a deliberate commitment that underpins every decision in the cellar. Les Platieres is a Gevrey-Chambertin village-level wine grown on the clay-limestone soils characteristic of the Côte de Nuits, where the combination of cool mesoclimate and old-vine massale selections consistently ripens stems fully — the biological prerequisite for Guyon's whole-cluster philosophy. 100% whole-cluster fermentation with indigenous yeasts follows a 4–5 day cold pre-fermentation maceration, then a 25–28 day cuvaison at 27–29°C — long and gentle, described by Guyon as 'infusion' rather than extraction. Cap management is performed by hand using a custom watering can, a technique borrowed from Dirk Niepoort, avoiding the mechanical shear of pump-overs. Élevage runs 10–14 months in one-year-old French oak barrels, followed by 3–4 months in stainless steel before biodynamic lunar-calendar bottling, unfiltered and unfined. The 'en nuage' technique — central bunch stems hand-removed by a 30-person sorting team — is Guyon's proprietary evolution of whole-cluster that no other domaine applies across a full range. The 2021 vintage, low-yielding and challenging across Burgundy, was cited by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for Guyon's low-intervention style; drink from 2025 through 2032.

name: Domaine Jean-Pierre Guyon Nuits-Saint-Georges "aux Herbues"
short_description (EN Store): Nuits-Saint-Georges village from marl-limestone soils, whole-cluster 'en nuage' fermentation, unfiltered — Guyon's low-intervention signature in a demanding 2021.
description (EN Store): Jean-Pierre Guyon took full management of his family's Vosne-Romanée estate in 1991 and made a deliberate break from the Côte de Nuits convention of new oak and mechanical extraction — building structure instead through long, gentle cuvaison of 25–28 days described as infusion, with cap management by hand-held watering can rather than pump-over, a method borrowed from Dirk Niepoort. The Nuits-Saint-Georges aux Herbues is grown on the appellation's characteristic marl-limestone soils, which deliver firmer mineral architecture and darker-fruited density than the clay-limestone of Guyon's Vosne-Romanée parcels across the ridge. Fermentation proceeds with 100% whole-cluster, indigenous yeasts, preceded by a 4–5 day cold pre-maceration; since 2016, Guyon has evolved toward his proprietary 'en nuage' technique — a 30-person sorting team hand-removes central rachis stems, leaving intact berry clusters that ferment without the bitter tannin contribution of full stems. Élevage follows in 1-year-old French oak barrels (10–14 months), then 3–4 months in stainless steel before bottling unfined and unfiltered on the biodynamic lunar calendar. What sets this apart: Guyon applies whole-cluster philosophy across his entire range — from Bourgogne Régionale to Grand Cru — made possible only because old-vine massale selections ripen stems fully, a consistency no peer domaine replicates at scale. The 2021 vintage, low-yielding and challenging across Burgundy, is cited by Berry Bros. & Rudd as a benchmark for Guyon's low-intervention approach; expect taut, precise fruit with a longer structural horizon than warmer years.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay "la Montagne"
short_description (EN Store): Single-lieu-dit Marsannay Pinot Noir from organic, horse-ploughed vines — whole-cluster infusion, unfined/unfiltered, 15–30% new oak.
description (EN Store): Sylvain Pataille — a trained oenologist who consults for roughly 15–20 Burgundy domaines — founded this estate in 1999 farming entirely on leased land, a structural constraint that has never diluted the ambition: La Montagne is a single-lieu-dit Marsannay red built to articulate site over appellation convention. The parcel sits within the Marsannay AOC on the Côte de Nuits, where east-to-southeast-facing slopes at 270–330 m elevation combine limestone-based soils with iron-rich clay and gravel — conditions that push Pinot Noir toward mineral tension rather than plush generosity. Vineyards are certified organic (Ecocert) with biodynamic methods integrated since 2007, including horse-ploughing to preserve soil structure and massal selection to maintain genetic diversity. Vinification uses indigenous yeast throughout, with a meaningful proportion of whole-cluster fruit in an infusion-style maceration — long and gentle, designed to extract texture and aromatic lift rather than tannin mass. Aging runs 15–18 months in barrel at 15–30% new French oak, and the wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered with minimal SO₂ added only at bottling. What separates Pataille's approach from peers is the analytical rigour behind the low-intervention philosophy: no formula governs élevage duration — each cuvée and vintage is assessed independently. In the very warm 2022 harvest, the whole-cluster regime proved its value, preserving freshness and aromatic lift against the vintage's ripe, generous fruit profile. Drink 2025–2032.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay "le Chapitre" - Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Le Chapitre's iron-rich white clay and steepest slope in Marsannay — whole-cluster infusion, unfined, 2021's cool precision on full display.
description (EN Store): When Sylvain Pataille — a trained oenologist who simultaneously consults for roughly 15–20 other Burgundy domaines — founded his estate in 1999, he chose to lease rather than own, staking his reputation entirely on terroir expression over asset accumulation. Le Chapitre sits on the steepest slope in the Marsannay appellation, outside the AOC boundary in Chenôve, on white clay soils dense with iron that give the site its warmth and textural grip — a profile distinct from the limestone-dominant parcels of Clos du Roy or the gravelly En Clémengeots. That iron-rich clay retains heat on a cool slope, accelerating phenolic ripeness while the gradient ensures natural drainage and concentration. In the cellar, Pataille applies his signature whole-cluster infusion approach — long, gentle maceration in stainless steel or fibreglass vats using only indigenous yeast, extracting texture and aromatic complexity without force. The wine ages 15–18 months in barrel at 15–30% new oak, then goes to bottle unfined and unfiltered with sulphur added only at bottling. Le Chapitre carried a 16th–17th-century reputation on par with grand crus — a historical standing that no current Marsannay village wine can claim on paper. The 2021 vintage, shaped by frost and mildew pressure that slashed yields, delivered exactly the kind of cool-climate precision and mineral clarity that suits Pataille's low-intervention style; drink from 2025 through 2033+.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay "Clos du Roy"
short_description (EN Store): Clos du Roy's iron-laced Comblanchien limestone at 270 m, high whole-cluster infusion, unfined — Marsannay's most compelling lieu-dit case for Premier Cru.
description (EN Store): When Sylvain Pataille — a trained oenologist who simultaneously consults for roughly 15–20 other Burgundy domaines — founded this estate in 1999 on entirely leased land, his first ambition was to prove that Marsannay could speak in distinct, site-specific voices. Clos du Roy is his most historically charged argument: a mid-slope, east/southeast-facing parcel at 270 m elevation whose thin reddish topsoil sits over Comblanchien limestone interlaced with marl and iron-rich clay — soils that historically supplied the Dukes of Burgundy and are now under active INAO review for Premier Cru promotion. That iron-and-limestone matrix drives the wine's characteristic tension: a ferrous, almost blood-orange mineral thread running beneath the fruit. Vinification deploys a high proportion of whole-cluster fruit in stainless steel or fibreglass vats, with long, gentle maceration conceived as infusion rather than extraction, using only indigenous yeast and zero sulphur during fermentation. Aging runs 15–18 months in oak (15–30% new), and the wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered with minimal SO₂ added only at bottling. The transgression here is structural: in a village appellation where extraction and new oak once defined ambition, Pataille builds complexity through whole-cluster lift and soil transparency instead. The 2022 vintage — very warm with an early harvest — yields a generous, ripe fruit profile where the whole-cluster approach does critical work preserving aromatic freshness and lift; drink from 2026 through 2034+.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay "Les Longeroies"
short_description (EN Store): Les Longeroies, 280–330 m gravelly limestone, 2021 frost-reduced yields — Pataille's whole-cluster infusion at its most precise and mineral.
description (EN Store): Sylvain Pataille founded his domaine in 1999 without owning a single vine, leasing every parcel he farms — yet from this unconventional base he has become the defining voice of Marsannay AOC, a village whose lieux-dits are currently under active INAO review for Premier Cru promotion. Les Longeroies sits at 280–330 m elevation on the upper slope, where gravelly, mixed limestone soils drain rapidly and force the vine to root deep, producing fruit of taut structure and pronounced mineral tension rather than flesh. The 2021 growing season — marked by spring frost and mildew pressure — reduced yields sharply, concentrating what remained into wines of classic Marsannay freshness and site-driven precision. In the cellar, Pataille vinifies with indigenous yeast, a proportion of whole-cluster fruit, and a long, gentle infusion maceration in stainless steel or fibreglass vats — deliberately avoiding extraction. The wine then ages 15–18 months in oak barrel at 15–30% new oak, bottled unfined and unfiltered with minimal SO₂ added only at bottling. What sets this cuvée apart within Marsannay is its elevation and gravel-dominant soil profile, which delivers a cooler, more lifted aromatic register than the iron-clay mid-slope sites. The 2021 vintage rewards patience: the combination of low yields and Pataille's non-interventionist élevage suggests a drinking window of 2025–2033.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay En Clemengeots
short_description (EN Store): En Clémengeots: very shallow gravel over laves stone, whole-cluster infusion, unfined/unfiltered — 2021's frost-reduced yields distilled into pure Marsannay minerality.
description (EN Store): Sylvain Pataille founded his domaine in 1999 without owning a single vine — every parcel across Marsannay's three communes is leased, yet En Clémengeots has become one of his most forensically precise addresses. The lieu-dit sits on very shallow gravel over laves stone, a soil profile so thin and fast-draining that the vine is forced into deep root exploration, translating directly into the wine's stony, high-toned character rather than any fruit weight. 2021's combination of spring frost and mildew pressure slashed yields, concentrating what remained into wines of exceptional freshness and mineral definition — the vintage Pataille's low-intervention philosophy was built for. Vinification follows his signature whole-cluster infusion approach in stainless steel or fibreglass vats, prioritising gentle maceration over extraction, with indigenous yeast fermentation, no sulphur added during fermentation, and aging in oak barrel at 15–30% new wood for 15–18 months. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered with minimal SO₂ added only at bottling. What separates En Clémengeots from Pataille's other lieu-dit reds is the laves-stone substrate — a volcanic-origin compressed limestone that delivers a harder, more angular mineral signature than the iron-rich marl of Clos du Roy or the gravelly limestone of Longeroies. The 2021 drinking window is generous: the cool-year acidity and whole-cluster structure suggest peak expression from 2026 through 2034.

name: Domaine Sylvain Pataille Marsannay "L'Ancestrale"
short_description (EN Store): Old-vine Marsannay from three lieux-dits — Clos du Roy, En Clémengeots, Les Ouzeloy — vinified with high whole-cluster, 24-month élevage, unfined and unfiltered.
description (EN Store):
When Sylvain Pataille — a trained oenologist who consults for roughly fifteen to twenty Burgundy domaines — founded his estate in 1999 on entirely leased land, he reserved the name L'Ancestrale for a single purpose: a blend drawn in equal thirds from his oldest Pinot Noir vines, aged sixty to eighty years, across Clos du Roy, En Clémengeots, and Les Ouzeloy. The decision to blend rather than bottle each site separately is a deliberate inversion of his own single-lieu-dit philosophy — the argument being that vine age, not site specificity, is the defining variable here.

Clos du Roy sits at 270 m on thin reddish topsoil over Comblanchien limestone with iron-rich marl; En Clémengeots contributes very shallow gravel over laves stone, the highest-minerality parcel in the portfolio. Together they impose a structural tension that old-vine concentration alone would not produce.

Vinification uses a high proportion of whole clusters, fermented with indigenous yeast in fibreglass vats under a long, gentle infusion maceration — extraction is explicitly avoided. Élevage runs 24 months in oak at 15–30% new barrique, longer than the domaine's standard 15–18 months, with no sulphur added until bottling. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered.

In 2022 — a very warm, early-harvest vintage — the whole-cluster approach was critical: it preserved aromatic lift and freshness against the vintage's ripe, generous fruit profile, preventing the density from reading as heaviness. Drink from 2026 through 2034.

name: Domaine Bruno Clair Chambolle Musigny 1er Cru "Les Charmes"
short_description (EN Store): Frost-reduced 2021 from Chambolle's Les Charmes: indigenous yeast, 20–50% whole-cluster, unfined — rose petal and bright cherry over limestone tension.
description (EN Store):
Bruno Clair — heir to the pre-war Domaine Clair-Daü and its historic parcels — built this domaine parcel by parcel after the family dissolution in 1985, adding the Chambolle-Musigny Les Charmes vineyard as recently as 2019. That deliberate, unhurried accumulation of terroir is the philosophy made physical. Les Charmes sits within Chambolle-Musigny's limestone-rich soils, where the combination of shallow brown limestone and clay delivers the appellation's signature floral lift alongside a saline, stony tension that separates it from the more muscular Côte de Nuits neighbours to the north.

In the cellar, hand-harvested, sorted fruit undergoes cold pre-fermentation maceration of 5–7 days, followed by ambient/indigenous yeast fermentation in open wooden vats with punch-downs and a total maceration of 10–15 days. Whole-cluster inclusion — between 20–50% depending on vintage — adds structural grip and aromatic complexity. Aging runs 18–20 months in barriques at moderate new-oak levels, preserving site transparency over wood imprint. The wine is bottled unfined, filtered only if the vintage demands it.

The 2021 vintage — frost-reduced, lower-yielding — produced Pinot Noir of uncommon concentration per berry: bright cherry, rose petal, and crisp acidity frame firm but fine-grained tannins. Bruno Clair's own note — 'For truly great wines, there are no bad years' — is borne out here. Approachable now, with genuine mid-term cellaring capacity through the late 2020s.

name: Domaine Claude Dugat Gevrey Chambertin
short_description (EN Store): Gevrey-Chambertin Villages 2021: old-vine Pinot from limestone-clay soils, unfined/unfiltered, ~40–50% new François Frères oak, finesse-driven cool vintage.
description (EN Store):
When Claude Dugat began estate-bottling under his own name in 1991, the domaine's philosophy was already fixed: maximum yields of ≤18 hl/ha, zero fining, zero filtration — a non-interventionist stance that predated Burgundy's broader 'natural' movement by decades. Today, under second-generation siblings Bertrand and Laetitia, that conviction holds while the style has shifted perceptibly toward earlier harvesting and tempered oak.

The Villages-level fruit draws from Gevrey-Chambertin's characteristic limestone and clay-limestone soils, rich in silt and pebbles — a semi-continental microclimate that slows ripening and builds aromatic complexity without sacrificing structure. Vines averaging 40–50 years of age concentrate flavour at yields that most Grands Crus would envy.

In the cellar, 100% destemmed fruit ferments in concrete vats on indigenous yeasts; the cap is punched down twice daily with virtually no pump-overs, limiting oxygen and avoiding over-extraction. Aging runs 16–18 months in 228 L François Frères barrels at approximately 40–50% new oak, first in the semi-underground Cellier des Dîmes (built 1219), then transferred to a cooler secondary cellar. The wine is bottled without fining or filtration and may show a natural deposit.

What separates this Villages from its appellation peers is Grand Cru-level concentration achieved at a tier where most producers compromise — a direct consequence of extreme yield restriction and old-vine selection applied uniformly across the entire range.

The 2021 vintage — cool, precise, and vibrant — amplifies the domaine's finesse register: lively acidity, purity of fruit, and a taut structure that rewards cellaring through 2030 and beyond.

name: Domaine Trapet Gevrey-Chambertin "Ostrea"
short_description (EN Store): Fossilised oyster-shell soils in Gevrey yield a saline-mineral village Pinot Noir vinified with native yeasts, zero sulphur until bottling, and unfined/unfiltered.
description (EN Store):
The name is not metaphor: the parcel behind Trapet's village cuvée sits on fossilised oyster-shell deposits within Gevrey-Chambertin, a geological anomaly that drives the wine's defining saline-mineral signature — a quality so literal it earned the lieu-dit its own bottling identity. Jean-Louis Trapet, seventh generation and the architect of the domaine's biodynamic conversion (Demeter-certified), made a deliberate choice to name and isolate this terroir rather than blend it away into a generic village wine.

The oyster-shell subsoil, rich in calcium carbonate, restricts vine vigour and sharpens acidity, producing fruit of taut concentration rather than opulent weight — the structural opposite of the powerful, extracted Gevrey archetype. Vinification begins with a cool pre-fermentation maceration, followed by open-vat fermentation on native yeasts with approximately 30% whole-cluster inclusion. Extraction is deliberately light: punch-downs at the start of fermentation give way to gentle pump-overs only. No sulphur is added at harvest, during fermentation, or during ageing; a minimal dose is added at bottling only. The wine rests in French oak barriques for 12 months at roughly 20% new oak, then is bottled unfined and unfiltered.

What no peer in Gevrey does: Trapet combines high whole-cluster use with an open, non-reductive élevage — the wine is accessible from release without sacrificing structure. The 2021 vintage, a cool, precise year in the Côte de Nuits, amplifies the site's natural tension and salinity; drink 2024–2032.

name: Domaine Arlaud Morey Saint Denis 1er Cru "Les Millandes"
short_description (EN Store): Morey's first certified biodynamic estate; clay-heavy Les Millandes fermented with indigenous yeasts, ~20% new French oak, bottled unfiltered by lunar cycle.
description (EN Store):
When Cyprien Arlaud capped new oak at 30% even for his Grands Crus — and zero for village wines — he was deliberately inverting the prestige logic of the Côte de Nuits, where barrel weight had long signalled ambition. That philosophy reaches Les Millandes with ~20% new French oak over 14–16 months, leaving the site, not the cooperage, to do the talking.

Les Millandes sits in the upper band of Morey-Saint-Denis Premier Cru, its proximity to Clos de la Roche pushing the clay fraction higher than most of the village's limestone-dominant soils. That heavier clay retains moisture through dry summers and slows ripening, building density and structural grip rather than the lifted, translucent character of more limestone-rich neighbours.

Grapes are hand-harvested and sorted across multiple tables before gravity-flow transfer into temperature-controlled cement tanks. Spontaneous fermentation with indigenous yeasts runs approximately 20 days; extraction favours remontage over pigeage to preserve aromatic finesse over tannic mass. Whole-cluster inclusion is reserved selectively for upper-tier wines when stems are fully ripe — the 2022 warm vintage would have informed that call. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered, by gravity, according to the lunar cycle.

Domaine Arlaud was the first estate in Morey-Saint-Denis to hold simultaneous certified organic and biodynamic status (2014) — a distinction that, in a warm year like 2022, translates to dark-fruited density held in check by the site's clay-driven structure. The 2022 vintage across the domaine's Premier Cru range is noted for requiring extended cellaring; drinking window 2027–2035.

name: Domaine Arlaud Morey Saint Denis 1er Cru "Aux Cheseaux"
short_description (EN Store): Aux Cheseaux sits on Morey's limestone-rich Gevrey border — unfined, unfiltered, ~20% new French oak, indigenous-yeast ferment from certified biodynamic vines.
description (EN Store): Cyprien Arlaud made a deliberate choice that inverts Côte de Nuits convention: cap new oak at 20% for Premiers Crus, bottle unfined and unfiltered by gravity on the lunar cycle, and let the site speak without cosmetic intervention. Aux Cheseaux sits at the northern edge of Morey-Saint-Denis, hard against the Gevrey-Chambertin boundary, where the limestone proportion in the clay-limestone subsoil is measurably higher than in neighbouring Millandes — a geological shift that drives the wine's tighter, more angular structure and pronounced mineral tension. Hand-harvested fruit passes multiple sorting tables before gravity-flow transfer into temperature-controlled cement tanks, where cold pre-fermentation maceration precedes a ~20-day spontaneous fermentation with indigenous yeasts; extraction is kept deliberately gentle via remontage rather than pigeage. Aging runs 14–16 months in French oak at approximately 20% new barrique, assembled four to five weeks before bottling. The domaine's biodynamic certification — the first in Morey-Saint-Denis, achieved 2014 — governs every step from vine to bottle, including the bottling date itself. The 2022 vintage produced dense, brooding Pinot Noir with dark fruit character across the Côte de Nuits; Aux Cheseaux in this year carries the structural weight of the warm growing season but the site's limestone spine keeps acidity intact, and the wine is noted as requiring cellaring to 2030 and beyond.

name: Domaine Thibault Liger-Belair Gevrey-Chambertin "En Creot"
short_description (EN Store): Biodynamic Gevrey village Pinot from a family that once owned La Tâche — native yeast, unfined, whole-cluster infusion over extraction.
description (EN Store):
Thibault Liger-Belair is the first vigneron in nine generations of a family that operated exclusively as négociants — the same lineage that owned La Tâche before selling to DRC in 1929. When he founded the domaine in 2001, he made a deliberate break: farming the vine rather than trading the bottle, and building a philosophy of infusion over extraction that runs counter to Gevrey-Chambertin's historically muscular reputation.

The En Creot climat sits on Gevrey's limestone and chaille (limestone-flint mix) soils, the same geological backbone that gives Côte de Nuits village wines their structural tension and mineral grip. Biodynamic viticulture — soils plowed by horse, no synthetic inputs — preserves microbial life in the topsoil, translating directly into aromatic precision at the village level.

In the cellar, grapes undergo triple sorting (at vine, before and after destemmer), followed by native yeast fermentation in open-top vessels with minimal pumpovers and pigeage. Whole-cluster inclusion is calibrated per vintage; village wines receive a lower percentage than the Grand Crus. Aging runs 14–18 months in lightly charred oak (5–7 minutes), with village-level cuvées seeing roughly one-third new barrels, two-thirds older wood — all dried for three years for silkier tannin integration. Bottled unfined and unfiltered.

What distinguishes En Creot is the application of Grand Cru–level sorting and biodynamic rigour to a village appellation, a commitment most producers reserve for their top parcels alone.

The 2021 Burgundy vintage delivered naturally lower yields after spring frost, concentrating fruit while preserving the cool-climate acidity that suits Thibault's light-handed extraction style — drink from 2025 through 2032.

name: Domaine Thibault Liger-Belair Corton Grand Cru "Les Renardes"
short_description (EN Store): Corton Grand Cru from biodynamic vines on iron-rich limestone; native-yeast infusion, up to 50% new oak, bottled unfined — Liger-Belair's Côte de Beaune statement.
description (EN Store): Thibault Liger-Belair is the first vigneron in nine generations of a family that once owned La Tâche Grand Cru — sold to DRC in 1929 — and operated exclusively as a négociant since 1720. When he founded the domaine in 2001, he inherited not a cellar but a philosophy to invent, and the Corton Grand Cru Les Renardes sits within his Aloxe-Corton holdings as his most southerly red Grand Cru statement. Les Renardes occupies the lower-mid slope of the Corton hill, where iron-rich, reddish-brown clay over fractured limestone bedrock delivers a denser, more structured expression than the paler, stonier soils of Corton-Charlemagne above — the clay fraction retaining moisture through dry years like 2021 while the limestone drives mineral tension. In the cellar, Liger-Belair applies his documented 'infusion over extraction' philosophy: hand-harvested fruit passes triple sorting before native-yeast fermentation in open-top vessels with minimal pumpovers and pigeage. Whole-cluster inclusion — calibrated per vintage — adds aromatic lift and tannic grip without sacrificing transparency. Aging runs 14–18 months in lightly charred (5–7 minute) fine-grained oak, with up to 50% new barrels selected after fermentation and dried for three years to soften tannin integration. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered. What separates this from peers is the biodynamic discipline applied to a Grand Cru climat rarely farmed with this level of precision: horse-plowed soils, sulfites sourced from a natural Polish mine. The 2021 vintage, a cool, late-ripening year across Burgundy, rewards Liger-Belair's light-touch extraction — expect taut red-fruit concentration, firm but fine-grained tannins, and a decade of development ahead.

name: Domaine Henri Boillot Pommard 1er cru Clos Blanc - Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Pommard 1er Cru from Guillaume Boillot: limestone-schist terroir, 50–70% new oak, 18-month élevage — structured Pinot with serious cellaring intent.
description (EN Store):
When Henri Boillot formally reconstituted the domaine in 2005 — buying out his siblings and renaming it from Domaine Jean Boillot — he handed red wine responsibility to son Guillaume, a deliberate generational transfer that shifted the domaine's Pinot Noir philosophy toward greater textural depth and phenolic precision. The Clos Blanc sits within Pommard's Premier Cru hierarchy on soils of limestone bedrock interlaced with schistose layers, a combination that tempers Pommard's characteristic iron-fisted structure and introduces a cooler, more mineral-driven tension than the appellation's clay-heavy lower slopes typically allow.

Guillaume's vinification begins with hand-harvested fruit under lutte raisonnée protocols — no insecticides or herbicides — with partial whole-bunch inclusion before fermentation in stainless steel tanks. The wine then enters 228-litre barriques at 50–70% new oak for an 18-month élevage, a regime that builds considerable structural density while the schist-derived mineral core holds the oak in check. Late harvest timing is a deliberate house decision to maximise phenolic maturity rather than chase early freshness.

What separates this expression from the domaine's Volnay portfolio is the appellation itself: Pommard's firmer tannin architecture, amplified by the new-oak percentage, produces a wine built for a decade-plus of development. The 2022 vintage delivered ripe, well-integrated fruit with the electric mineral backbone documented across the domaine's range that year — a drinking window opening realistically from 2028 onward.

name: Domaine Henri Boillot Volnay 1er Cru "les Fremiets"
short_description (EN Store): Guillaume Boillot's Volnay 1er Cru from limestone-schist terroir: 50–70% new oak, partial whole-bunch, 18-month élevage — 2022 delivers power with mineral precision.
description (EN Store): When Henri Boillot formally reconstituted the domaine in 2005 — buying out his siblings and renaming it from Domaine Jean Boillot — he handed red wine production to his son Guillaume, a deliberate generational transfer that has since defined the house's Volnay identity. Les Fremiets sits on Volnay's limestone base threaded with schistose layers, a geological combination that tempers the appellation's characteristic softness with a stony, iron-edged grip absent from purely calcareous neighbours. Vines are planted at 10,000 vines per hectare under lutte raisonnée — no insecticides or herbicides — and harvested by hand with timing pushed toward full phenolic maturity. Guillaume ferments in stainless steel with partial whole-bunch inclusion, then ages the wine for 18 months in 228-litre barrique at 50–70% new oak, a regime notably more oak-intensive than most Volnay producers, who typically favour lighter wood to protect the appellation's delicacy. That deliberate richness is the house's rule-break: textural depth over transparency. In 2022, a warm, generous vintage across the Côte de Beaune, the wine carries concentration and structural density while the schist-driven mineral backbone keeps it from tipping into heaviness — a drinking window that rewards patience from 2027 onward.

name: Domaine Henri Boillot Volnay 1er Cru "les Caillerets"
short_description (EN Store): Volnay 1er Cru from limestone-schist terroir; Guillaume Boillot's 50–70% new oak regime delivers textural depth rare for the appellation.
description (EN Store): When Henri Boillot formally reconstituted the domaine in 2005 — buying out his siblings and renaming it from Domaine Jean Boillot — he handed the red wine programme to his son Guillaume, a deliberate generational transfer that shifted the Volnay style toward greater textural density without abandoning the site's inherent finesse. Les Caillerets sits on Volnay's limestone base threaded with schistose layers, a geology that drives the appellation's characteristic iron-edged minerality and fine-grained tannin structure. Hand-harvested at 10,000 vines per hectare under lutte raisonnée — no insecticides, no herbicides — fruit arrives with phenolic precision earned in the vineyard. Guillaume ferments in tank with partial whole-bunch inclusion, then ages the wine for 18 months in 228-litre barrels at 50–70% new oak, a proportion that sits at the assertive end of Volnay convention and builds a textural scaffold the appellation rarely attempts. What sets this cuvée apart is that oak weight: most Volnay producers keep new wood well below 40% to protect the grape's delicacy, yet the schist-driven mineral tension of Les Caillerets absorbs the regime without losing site character. The 2022 vintage delivered ripe, well-structured fruit across the Côte de Beaune; expect concentrated dark cherry, iron-flecked earth, and a firm but polished tannic frame that rewards cellaring through the late 2020s and beyond.

name: Domaine Pierre Girardin Volnay Premier Cru Les Taillepieds
short_description (EN Store): 13th-gen Girardin, launched at ~19, vinifies Volnay Taillepieds 100% whole-cluster, unfined/unfiltered, in low-new-oak 228L barrels for silken terroir transparency.
description (EN Store): Pierre-Vincent Girardin established his domaine in 2016 — at roughly 19 years old, 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family — and immediately broke from the extraction-forward conventions of his appellation. Les Taillepieds, one of Volnay's most prized premier cru lieux-dits, sits on the mid-slope at approximately 260 m elevation on east-to-south-facing Jurassic marls and marly limestones: thin, cool soils that suppress vigour and concentrate aromatic precision in Pinot Noir rather than weight. From the 2021 vintage onward, Girardin committed to 100% whole-cluster vinification across his reds — a deliberate structural choice that adds spinal tension and savouriness without sacrificing the silkiness he prizes over extraction. Fermentation is low-interventionist: little to no pigeage, cold cellar temperatures maintained throughout, and indigenous yeasts only. Aging runs 12 months in 228-litre barrels at low new-oak percentage, followed by 3 months in tank before bottling unfined and unfiltered on a lunar calendar. The special feature here is the combination of whole-cluster inclusion with a near-zero-intervention cellar philosophy — most Volnay producers choose one or the other, not both. The 2022 Burgundy vintage delivered generous fruit ripeness tempered by a cooler August, a profile that suits Girardin's transparency-first approach; drink from 2026 through 2034.

name: Domaine Pierre Girardin Morey-Saint-Denis 1er Cru "Les Monts Luisants"
short_description (EN Store): Côte de Nuits 1er Cru from a 13th-generation prodigy: 100% whole-cluster Pinot Noir, unfined/unfiltered, bottled by lunar calendar from Les Monts Luisants.
description (EN Store): Pierre-Vincent Girardin launched his domaine in 2016 at roughly 19 years old — the 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family — and immediately broke from the richer, more extracted house styles that dominated his appellation. Les Monts Luisants sits on the upper slope of Morey-Saint-Denis, where thin Jurassic marl over fragmented limestone scree at approximately 280–300 m elevation forces vines into deep root systems, producing small-berried fruit with pronounced mineral tension and cooler aromatic register than lower-slope premiers crus. The 2022 vintage, warm but retaining freshness in elevated parcels, amplifies the site's characteristic iron-laced earth and dark fruit concentration without sacrificing lift. In the cellar, Girardin vinifies with 100% whole-cluster inclusion (a practice adopted across his red range from 2021), employing long gentle pressing with little to no pigeage — a deliberate choice to build texture through stem tannin rather than extraction. Aging runs 12 months in 228-litre barrels at low new-oak percentage, followed by three months in tank before bottling unfined and unfiltered on the lunar calendar. The special feature here: Les Monts Luisants is one of Morey's most topographically extreme premiers crus, historically planted to Aligoté and Chardonnay on its uppermost sections — Girardin's red interpretation from this site is a deliberate transgression of that white-wine legacy, channelling the climat's stony austerity into Pinot Noir of uncommon structural tension.

name: Domaine Pierre Girardin Pommard 1er Cru "les Grands Epenots"
short_description (EN Store): 13th-gen Girardin, founded age ~19, vinifies Les Grands Épenots 100% whole-cluster, unfined/unfiltered, in 228L barrique at just 15% new oak.
description (EN Store): Pierre-Vincent Girardin launched his domaine in 2016 — at roughly 19 years old, 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family — and immediately broke from the extraction-heavy Pommard playbook by committing to 100% whole-cluster vinification (a practice formalised across his reds from 2021), minimal pigeage, and a cold cellar regime designed to preserve juice purity over power. Les Grands Épenots sits on the mid-slope of Pommard's most prestigious premier cru, where Jurassic marls and marly limestones at roughly 260 m elevation with east-to-south exposure produce a firmer, more structured Pinot Noir than the lighter soils of neighbouring Volnay — yet Girardin's low-intervention hand keeps that structure silky rather than muscular. The wine ages 12 months in standard 228-litre barrique at just 15% new oak, followed by 3 months in tank before bottling — a deliberately restrained wood regime that keeps the Épenots' iron-tinged marl character in the foreground rather than behind a veil of toast. Bottled unfined and unfiltered, timed to the lunar calendar, with minimal sulphur additions throughout — a transparency-first philosophy that few Pommard producers at this price point apply with such consistency. The 2022 vintage delivered generous, ripe fruit across the Côte de Beaune while retaining the freshness that Girardin's high-canopy vine training (1.60–1.70 m) is specifically engineered to protect; production per cuvée runs to approximately 1,400 bottles.

name: Domaine Pierre Girardin Pommard 1er Cru "les Rugiens Bas"
short_description (EN Store): 13th-gen Girardin, age ~19 at debut: 100% whole-cluster Pinot Noir from Pommard's iron-rich Rugiens Bas, unfined/unfiltered, 228L barrique, low new oak.
description (EN Store): Pierre-Vincent Girardin launched his domaine in 2016 — at roughly 19 years old, 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family — and by his debut 2017 vintage had already drawn serious critical attention, an almost unheard-of trajectory in a region where reputations are built over decades. Les Rugiens Bas sits at the southern foot of Pommard's most celebrated slope, where the iron-oxide-rich, reddish Jurassic marl and marly limestone soils that give the climat its name ('rougeots,' the red ones) produce Pinot Noir of notably deeper colour and firmer structure than the lighter soils further upslope. East-facing mid-slope exposure at approximately 260 m channels morning sun while preserving overnight cool, slowing ripening and locking in acidity. From the 2021 vintage onward, Girardin committed to 100% whole-cluster vinification across his reds — a deliberate structural choice that introduces stem tannin, lifts aromatics, and extends the wine's aging architecture without relying on new wood. Reds age in standard 228-litre Burgundy barriques at low new-oak percentage, then three months in tank before bottling unfined and unfiltered, timed to the lunar calendar. The special feature here: whole-cluster inclusion at this scale is rare in Pommard, where the appellation's reputation rests on extraction and density — Girardin inverts that logic entirely, chasing silkiness over power. The 2022 Burgundy vintage delivered generous, sun-ripened fruit with naturally moderate acidity; in Girardin's hands, the low-intervention approach preserves the iron-mineral tension of Rugiens Bas rather than letting the warmth of the year dominate.

name: Domaine Pierre Girardin Nuits Saint Georges 1er Cru "Aux Boudots"
short_description (EN Store): Nuits-Saint-Georges Aux Boudots 1er Cru: 100% whole-cluster Pinot Noir, unfined/unfiltered, from a 13th-generation Meursault prodigy who launched his domaine at ~19.
description (EN Store): Pierre-Vincent Girardin founded his domaine in 2016 — at roughly 19 years old, 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family — and immediately made a technical declaration: 100% whole-cluster vinification for all reds from 2021, the very vintage of this Boudots. That decision, combined with little to no pigeage and a cold cellar maintained for juice purity, defines the structural DNA here. Aux Boudots sits at the northern tip of Nuits-Saint-Georges, bordering Vosne-Romanée, on Jurassic marl and marly limestone soils that lend the climat its characteristic iron-flecked depth and finer-grained tannin relative to the harder, stonier southern premiers crus. Aging runs 12 months in standard 228-litre Burgundy barrels at low new-oak percentage, followed by 3 months in tank — a deliberate restraint that keeps wood subordinate to site. Bottling is unfined, unfiltered, and timed to the lunar calendar, with minimal sulphur throughout. The special feature: where most Nuits producers lean into extraction and new oak to amplify Boudots' natural power, Girardin inverts the logic — whole-cluster stems provide structure without pigeage-driven tannin, producing a silkier, more Vosne-adjacent texture. The 2021 vintage, ravaged by spring frost across Burgundy, yielded tiny quantities; the concentration is inherent, not manufactured. Drink 2025–2033.

name: Domaine Vincent Dancer Beaune 1er cru "Les Montrevenots"
short_description (EN Store): Beaune 1er Cru from an Alsatian engineer who broke Côte de Beaune convention: 100% Pinot Noir, zero new oak, unfined, unfiltered — 2022 radiant vintage.
description (EN Store):
Vincent Dancer arrived in Chassagne-Montrachet in 1996 with an engineering background and no winemaking lineage — and proceeded to become the village's first organically certified producer (2012), building a house style defined by deliberate restraint rather than extraction or oak imprint. Les Montrevenots sits within the Beaune Premier Cru belt on limestone-and-marl argilo-calcaire soils with a southeast aspect, conditions that push Pinot Noir toward transparency and perfume over weight — precisely what Dancer's approach is designed to amplify rather than correct.

Vinification is uncompromisingly minimal: destemmed grapes, short maceration, native yeasts throughout, occasional punch-down only — no pumping over. Aging proceeds in used barrels exclusively — no new oak purchased since 2019 — with no bâtonnage. The wine is bottled unfined and unfiltered, sealed under Diam 30 from the 2022 vintage onward.

The transgression here is categorical: in a Côte de Beaune where even village-level reds routinely see a percentage of new wood, Dancer's complete elimination of new oak since 2019 is a structural commitment, not a stylistic flourish — the barrel is purely a vessel, not a flavoring agent. The 2022 vintage is described as radiant across the domaine, delivering floral lift and stony complexity; this Beaune Premier Cru is drinking well now through 2030+.

name: Laura Hartwig Edición De Familia
short_description (EN Store): Chilean red blend with ripe red fruit, dark plum, and subtle spice—food-friendly and approachable.
description (EN Store): Laura Hartwig Edición De Familia is a medium-full-bodied red wine from Chile's Colchagua Valley. The wine opens with dominant red fruit and dark plum character, supported by black cherry notes and a gentle spice layer. Subtle oak integration provides structure without overwhelming the fruit-forward profile, making this a versatile table wine suited to everyday dining and casual entertaining.

name: Laura Hartwig Single Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Chilean Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—classic structure for red meat.
description (EN Store): Laura Hartwig's Single Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon from Cachapoal Valley delivers the hallmark intensity of a premium Chilean red. Ripe blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the palate, supported by pronounced cedar and oak from careful winemaking. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide excellent structure and aging potential, with tertiary notes of tobacco and leather emerging on the finish. A wine built for the table—pair with grilled steak, lamb, or slow-braised beef.

name: La Rue De L'Amour Premium Selected Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Bordeaux blend with blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco—age-worthy structure for premium red meat.
description (EN Store): La Rue De L'Amour Premium Selected Rouge is a classically structured Bordeaux blend from France's most prestigious region. Built on Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot, this full-bodied wine delivers ripe blackcurrant and dark plum on the nose, layered with cedar and graphite minerality. The palate shows firm, well-integrated tannins and medium-high acidity that frame tobacco and leather notes, suggesting age-worthiness and complexity. Ideal for grilled red meat, braised beef, and lamb dishes; also pairs beautifully with aged hard cheeses.

name: Miguel Domecq Entrechuelos
short_description (EN Store): Spanish red from Cádiz with medium-full body, dark fruit, and food-friendly spice—ideal for grilled meats and traditional tapas.
description (EN Store): Miguel Domecq Entrechuelos is a medium-full-bodied Spanish red wine from the Cádiz region, crafted in a food-friendly style. The wine opens with ripe red and dark plum fruit, supported by subtle spice and earthy undertones. A restrained oak presence adds structure without overwhelming the primary character, making this an approachable yet layered expression suited to grilled meats, braised beef, and Spanish charcuterie boards.

name: Roodeberg Black
short_description (EN Store): South African red blend with ripe dark fruit, spice, and approachable structure—versatile table wine for everyday meals.
description (EN Store): Roodeberg Black is a food-friendly South African red blend from the Western Cape, built on a foundation of ripe dark fruit and subtle spice. The medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make it an accessible choice for a range of casual dining occasions, from grilled meats to tomato-based pasta and roasted poultry. Earthy undertones add modest complexity without demanding extended cellaring.

name: Cantine Di Ora Valpolicella Ripasso Superiore D.O.C. Superiore
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Nebbiolo from Veneto with dark cherry, licorice, and cedar—a structured, age-worthy red.
description (EN Store): Cantine Di Ora's Valpolicella Ripasso Superiore showcases the depth and complexity of Nebbiolo in the Veneto tradition. Dark cherry and licorice dominate the nose, supported by herbaceous and cedar notes that speak to the grape's classic profile. On the palate, medium-full body and medium-high tannin provide structure, while the wine's acidity carries flavors of tar, rose, and dark plum through a lingering finish. This is a wine built for contemplation and food pairing—particularly suited to grilled red meat, braised beef, and game preparations.

name: Cantine Di Ora Amarone Della Valpolicella D.O.C.G.
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Amarone from Veneto: dark cherry, plum, and herbaceous notes with balanced oak and earthy complexity.
description (EN Store): Cantine Di Ora's Amarone della Valpolicella D.O.C.G. is a structured red wine from Italy's prestigious Veneto region, crafted from Corvina grapes. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and black cherry aromatics, supported by subtle herbaceous undertones characteristic of the Valpolicella style. On the palate, cocoa and oak provide winemaking complexity, while earthy tertiary notes emerge with air, suggesting modest age-worthiness. Medium-full body and medium-high tannins frame a wine suited to rich, savory cuisine—particularly braised meats, aged cheeses, and mushroom-forward dishes.

name: Le Morette Bardolino D.O.C. Classico
short_description (EN Store): Lazise glacial-moraine soils, 55% Corvina/35% Rondinella/10% Molinara — deliberately light-bodied counterpoint to Valpolicella's richer register.
description (EN Store):
Founded around 1960 by Gino Zenato, Le Morette built its reputation on Lugana's chalky clay — but the family's ~6 hectares in Lazise, Bardolino Classico DOC, represent a deliberate stylistic statement: a red wine stripped of weight by design, not circumstance. The Lazise parcels sit on glacial-moraine soils of compact, marly clay, the same mineral-dense substrate that defines the estate's whites, producing reds with taut structure and low phenolic mass rather than the extracted fruit profile of neighboring Valpolicella.

The blend is 55% Corvina, 35% Rondinella, 10% Molinara — Corvina providing the cherry-skin backbone, Molinara contributing its characteristic high-acid, low-tannin lift. Vinification follows a reductive, freshness-first logic: stainless steel throughout, no wood contact, temperature-controlled fermentation, preserving the site's cool-climate aromatic register. Vines are trained to cordon at 3,800 plants per hectare, a density that moderates vigor on these mineral-rich soils.

What sets this apart within Bardolino Classico is the estate's explicit positioning of the wine as a structural counterpoint to Valpolicella — lower alcohol, lower tannin extraction, higher acid tension — a producer philosophy, not a DOC accident. Drink now through 3–4 years for maximum aromatic vibrancy.

name: Le Morette Serai Rosso Veronese I.G.T.
short_description (EN Store): Bardolino Classico from glacial-moraine clay in Lazise — 55% Corvina, 35% Rondinella, 10% Molinara, stainless-fermented for pure terroir expression.
description (EN Store): Founded around 1960 by Gino Zenato, Le Morette built its reputation on scientific rigour — the estate runs a commercial vine-grafting nursery alongside the winery, and that same precision applied to clonal research shapes every red it produces. The Serai Rosso Veronese I.G.T. draws fruit from roughly six hectares in Lazise, on the compact, chalky, marly clay of glacial-moraine origin that defines the eastern Lake Garda basin — soils dense enough to slow drainage and concentrate mineral uptake, yet light enough in structure to keep the Corvina-dominant blend lifted rather than heavy. The blend runs 55% Corvina, 35% Rondinella, and 10% Molinara, a composition shared with the estate's Bardolino Classico DOC tier and deliberately positioned as a counterpoint to the richer, more extracted Valpolicella style made from the same varieties just kilometres north. Vinification is entirely in stainless steel, preserving the crunchy red-fruit character and the saline, mineral edge the glacial soils impart — no oak contact at any stage. The I.G.T. classification here is not a downgrade but a deliberate framing: it signals the estate's intent to bottle a terroir-driven, early-drinking expression of Veronese reds without the structural weight DOC rules can encourage. Drink now through two to three years for maximum freshness.

name: Terre Di Rai 931 Vino Rosso
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Veneto Cabernet-Merlot blend with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins.
description (EN Store): Terre Di Rai's 931 Vino Rosso is a classically structured Bordeaux-style blend from Veneto, built on a foundation of Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by pronounced cedar and oak character from careful winemaking. A full body and high tannin structure provide the backbone for extended cellaring, while secondary black pepper spice and tertiary tobacco and leather notes add complexity and sophistication. This is a wine for serious red-meat cuisine and aged cheese boards.

name: Sir Maiale Rosso Barbera Rubicone
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Barbera from Emilia-Romagna with red cherry, plum, and peppery spice—food-friendly and versatile.
description (EN Store): Sir Maiale Rosso Barbera Rubicone is a medium-full bodied red wine from Italy's Emilia-Romagna region, crafted from the classic Barbera grape. This wine delivers a harmonious balance of ripe red cherry and dark plum on the nose, complemented by black pepper spice and subtle earthy notes. The palate is structured yet approachable, with bright acidity and moderate tannin that make it an ideal companion to Italian cuisine, grilled meats, and rich pasta dishes. A versatile, food-friendly expression of Barbera's traditional character.

name: Reguta Carant Tre Venezie I.G.P.
short_description (EN Store): Friuli-Venezia Giulia Cabernet-Merlot-Refosco blend with blackcurrant depth, cedar structure, and medium-full body.
description (EN Store): A structured red blend from northeastern Italy's Friuli-Venezia Giulia region, combining Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and the indigenous Refosco varietal. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by cedary oak and black pepper spice on the mid-palate. Medium-high tannins and balanced acidity provide framework for food-friendly versatility, while subtle tobacco and earth notes emerge on the finish, suggesting modest aging potential.

name: Reguta Altropasso Tre Venezie I.G.P.
short_description (EN Store): Food-friendly Italian red from Friuli-Venezia Giulia with ripe cherry, plum, and herbaceous spice.
description (EN Store): Reguta's Altropasso Tre Venezie I.G.P. is a medium-full-bodied red wine from Italy's northeastern Friuli-Venezia Giulia region. The wine displays primary notes of cherry and plum with herbaceous undertones and subtle spice, supported by medium tannin structure and balanced acidity. A versatile, food-friendly expression suited to everyday dining across pasta, roasted meats, and Mediterranean fare.

name: Astrale Vino Ross0 D’Italia
short_description (EN Store): Tuscan Sangiovese with bright cherry, herbaceous notes, and structured tannins—a food-friendly classic.
description (EN Store): Astrale's Rosso d'Italia is a Tuscan Sangiovese expressing the region's signature character: vibrant cherry fruit balanced by herbaceous minerality and a firm, drying tannin structure. High acidity and medium-full body make this wine an ideal companion to tomato-based Italian dishes, grilled meats, and aged cheeses. The wine's earthy undertones and subtle leather complexity suggest modest aging potential, though it drinks with immediate pleasure.

name: Bulichella Maria Shizuko
short_description (EN Store): Tuscan red with red fruit, plum, and earthy spice—food-friendly medium-full structure.
description (EN Store): Maria Shizuko from Azienda Agricola Bulichella represents the Suvereto terroir of Tuscany with a medium-full bodied red wine built on red fruit and dark plum character. The palate shows balanced medium tannin and acidity, with supporting earth and spice notes that reflect the region's mineral-rich soils. A versatile food wine suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and tomato-based preparations.

name: Poliziano In Violas Cortona Merlot D.O.C.
short_description (EN Store): Tuscan Merlot with ripe plum, juicy berries, and soft tannins—elegant, food-friendly, and approachable.
description (EN Store): Poliziano's In Violas Cortona Merlot is a single-varietal expression from Tuscany's Cortona subregion, showcasing the approachable elegance of well-ripened Merlot. The wine opens with a medium-intensity nose of ripe plum and berry fruits, complemented by subtle vanilla notes from careful winemaking. On the palate, a juicy medley of berry and cranberry flavors unfolds with herbaceous undertones, while integrated tannins and moderate acidity create a balanced, medium-full structure. The 14% ABV sits comfortably within the wine's soft texture, delivering a solid finish without heaviness. This is a versatile, food-friendly Tuscan red suited to casual dining and everyday occasions.

name: Lohsa Mandrone Di Lohsa Maremma Toscana D.O.C.
short_description (EN Store): Tuscan Mandrone from Maremma: elegant red fruit, herbaceous minerality, food-friendly structure.
description (EN Store): Lohsa Mandrone di Lohsa is a single-varietal red wine from Maremma in Tuscany, crafted from the Mandrone grape. The 2018 vintage expresses the characteristic red and dark fruit profile of the region, with herbaceous undertones and mineral structure typical of Maremma's coastal terroir. Medium-full body and balanced tannins make this wine versatile at the table, equally at home with grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, or Mediterranean fare.

name: Valiano 6.38 Gran Selezione Chianti Classico D.O.C.G.
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Sangiovese from Chianti Classico with bright cherry, herbaceous notes, and structured tannins—a versatile food wine.
description (EN Store): Valiano's Gran Selezione Chianti Classico D.O.C.G. is a refined expression of Sangiovese from Tuscany's most prestigious region. The wine opens with vibrant cherry and herbaceous aromatics, supported by earthy undertones and subtle spice. On the palate, high acidity and medium-high tannin structure provide excellent grip and aging potential, while secondary leather and tobacco notes emerge with air. This is a classic Chianti built for the table—equally at home with grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, or aged cheeses.

name: Feudi Bizantini “8” Vino Rosso
short_description (EN Store): Sangiovese-based Abruzzo red with ripe cherry, earthy spice, and food-friendly tannin. Approachable yet structured.
description (EN Store): Feudi Bizantini's "8" is a well-balanced Sangiovese from Montepulciano d'Abruzzo, delivering the region's signature red-fruit character with supporting earth and spice. Medium-full body and moderate tannin make it versatile at the table—equally at home with grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, or mushroom dishes. The wine shows ripe cherry and plum on the nose, with herbaceous undertones and a clean, mineral-driven finish. A food-friendly expression that rewards both casual weeknight drinking and modest cellaring.

name: Nativ Blue Onice Irpinia Aglianico D.O.C.
short_description (EN Store): Structured Aglianico from Taurasi with dark fruit, mineral grip, and earthy complexity—a food-friendly Campanian classic.
description (EN Store): Nativ's Blue Onice Irpinia Aglianico D.O.C. is a single-varietal expression from Taurasi, Campania's premier Aglianico zone. The wine displays the varietal's signature dark plum and black cherry primary character, supported by peppery spice and a mineral backbone. Secondary leather and tobacco notes add savory depth, while tertiary earth and dried-fruit complexity emerge with bottle age. Medium-full body and firm tannin structure make this a versatile food wine, equally at home with grilled red meat, braised beef, or herb-forward lamb preparations.

name: Tagaro Passo Del Sud Appassimento Puglia I.G.P.
short_description (EN Store): Smooth, fruit-driven Primitivo from Puglia with dark cherry, pepper spice, and smoke—an easy-drinking red for pizza and pasta.
description (EN Store): Tagaro Passo Del Sud Appassimento is a medium-full-bodied Primitivo from Locorotondo in Puglia, crafted to deliver immediate appeal and food-friendly versatility. The wine opens with a bold nose of dark fruit—cherry and raspberry—layered with peppery spice and subtle smoke. On the palate, ripe red and dark fruit dominate, supported by fully rounded tannins that feel smooth and integrated rather than aggressive. A touch of earthiness adds subtle complexity without overwhelming the wine's primary fruit character. This is a wine built for enjoyment: eminently drinkable, unpretentious, and at home on the table with pizza, pasta, grilled meats, or casual entertaining.

name: Tagaro Mastro Zin Zinfandel Puglia I.G.P.
short_description (EN Store): Bold Zinfandel from Puglia with dark fruit, smoke, and peppery spice. Smooth, full-bodied, and food-friendly.
description (EN Store): Tagaro Mastro Zin is a characterful Zinfandel from Puglia's Locorotondo subregion, delivering a bold nose and a palate filled with dark fruit, cherry, and raspberry. The wine shows a confident spice and smoke signature, balanced by fully rounded tannins and a smooth, approachable finish. Medium-full bodied with medium acidity, it is eminently drinkable and versatile at the table—equally at home with pizza, pasta, grilled meats, or casual entertaining.

name: Tagaro Piè Del Monaco Primitivo Valle D’Itria Igp
short_description (EN Store): Smooth, fruit-driven Primitivo from Puglia with dark cherry, raspberry, and peppery spice—a bold, food-friendly red.
description (EN Store): Tagaro Piè Del Monaco is a characterful Primitivo from the Valle d'Itria subregion of Puglia, showcasing the warm-climate generosity of southern Italy's signature red varietal. The wine opens with a bold nose and delivers a palate filled with dark fruit—cherry and raspberry—complemented by subtle smoke and peppery spice. Medium-full bodied with fully rounded tannins, it drinks smoothly and accessibly, making it an ideal companion to pizza, pasta, and grilled meats. A delicious, unpretentious expression of Primitivo that prioritizes drinkability and flavor over complexity.

name: Chateau de G  Gran Reserva carmenere
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Carmenère from Colchagua Valley with dark plum, green pepper, and mocha—a classic Chilean expression.
description (EN Store): Château de G Gran Reserva Carmenère represents the signature style of Chile's Colchagua Valley, where this Bordeaux-origin varietal thrives in warm, well-drained soils. The wine displays a full body with ripe dark plum and black cherry fruit, complemented by distinctive green pepper notes characteristic of Carmenère. Mocha and oak-driven secondary flavors add complexity and structure, while subtle tobacco and earth undertones emerge on the finish. Medium tannins provide grip without harshness, making this a versatile food wine suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and smoked preparations.

name: Ricossa  Barbera Appassimento Piemonte DOC
short_description (EN Store): Piedmont Barbera with appassimento concentration—dark fruit, peppery spice, and food-friendly tannin.
description (EN Store): Ricossa's Barbera Appassimento from Piedmont showcases the traditional appassimento technique, where partial grape drying concentrates flavors before fermentation. Expect a medium-full-bodied red with dominant dark cherry and plum character, supported by black pepper spice and subtle earthy minerality. The wine's moderate tannin structure and bright acidity make it an excellent match for Italian cuisine, grilled meats, and aged cheeses. A versatile, food-friendly expression of Barbera's rustic charm.

name: Maturana Wines  Reserva Privada Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Chilean Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—a classic expression of Colchagua Valley.
description (EN Store): Maturana Wines Reserva Privada Cabernet Sauvignon from Chile's Colchagua Valley delivers the hallmark structure and depth expected of a premium single-varietal Cabernet. Dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit form the core, supported by pronounced cedar and oak spice from careful winemaking. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide the backbone for extended cellaring and food pairing versatility. Secondary tobacco and leather notes emerge on the palate, suggesting complexity and age-worthiness. A wine built for grilled red meat, lamb, and game—or patient collectors seeking evolution in the glass.

name: Chateau Lilian Ladouys  Saint-Estephe 2021
short_description (EN Store): Structured Saint-Estèphe Bordeaux blend with blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco complexity. Age-worthy, full-bodied, high tannin.
description (EN Store):
Château Lilian Ladouys Saint-Estèphe 2021 is a classically structured Bordeaux red blend anchored by Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. The wine displays a full body with high tannin grip and medium-high acidity, characteristic of the Left Bank's finest terroirs.

Tasting Profile: The primary layer opens with ripe blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by herbaceous black pepper notes. The secondary winemaking character introduces cedar and tobacco complexity, while tertiary mineral evolution—slate and graphite—suggests considerable age-worthiness. This is a wine built for the cellar, with the structure and depth to develop gracefully over the next 15–20 years.

Best served with grilled red meat, lamb, or aged cheeses. Decant 30 minutes before service.

name: Chateau Gloria  Saint Julien 2017
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Saint-Julien Bordeaux blend with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco—age-worthy structure for serious collectors.
description (EN Store): Château Gloria's 2017 Saint-Julien presents the classic Left Bank profile: a full-bodied Bordeaux blend anchored by Cabernet Sauvignon with supporting Merlot. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, layered with cedar and graphite minerality. Secondary oak and tobacco notes add complexity, while tertiary leather and dried-herb character suggest graceful aging potential. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide the structure for 15–20 years of evolution in a well-kept cellar.

name: VIK  La Piu Belle
short_description (EN Store): Cachapoal Valley red with dark fruit, plum, and balanced structure—food-friendly Chilean expression.
description (EN Store): VIK La Piu Belle is a medium-full-bodied red wine from Chile's Cachapoal Valley, crafted in the Millahue subregion. The wine opens with dominant dark fruit and plum character, supported by black cherry notes and subtle oak influence. A balanced tannin structure and medium acidity make this expression approachable across a range of food pairings, from grilled red meat and braised beef to roasted poultry and earthy mushroom dishes. The 2022 vintage reflects the region's capacity for ripe, food-friendly reds with classical proportions.

name: VIK  Stonevik
short_description (EN Store): Cabernet Franc–Sauvignon blend from Chile's Millahue Valley; dark fruit, cedar, and balanced structure for food-friendly red wine enjoyment.
description (EN Store): VIK Stonevik is a Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon blend from the Millahue Valley in Chile's Cachapoal region. The wine displays a medium-full body with ripe dark plum and blackcurrant fruit on the nose, complemented by cedar and black pepper spice. Well-balanced tannins and moderate acidity make this a versatile food wine suitable for grilled meats, lamb, and hearty pasta dishes. The blend's structure suggests approachability in youth while retaining enough complexity for short-term cellaring.

name: J. Lohr Carol Vineyards St. Helena Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Carol's Vineyard, St. Helena clay-loam soils — J. Lohr's 30-acre Napa estate bottled as a single-site Cabernet, a rare departure from the brand's Central Coast identity.
description (EN Store):
When Jerry Lohr built his reputation across 4,000+ Central Coast acres, he quietly planted a 30-acre foothold in St. Helena — Carol's Vineyard — a deliberate outlier in a portfolio defined by Paso Robles and Arroyo Seco. That decision now produces one of the brand's most site-specific expressions.

St. Helena's clay-loam soils sit at the warmer, northern end of Napa Valley's valley floor, where the Mayacamas and Vaca ranges compress the diurnal range less dramatically than Paso Robles, allowing Cabernet Sauvignon to ripen slowly toward full phenolic maturity while clay retention keeps vine stress moderate through dry summers.

The fruit is vinified in stainless steel before transfer to 60-gallon American oak barrels — predominantly medium and medium-plus toasted Missouri and Minnesota oak with toasted heads — at 20% new oak, the same regime applied to the Seven Oaks program. Petit Verdot is planted alongside Cabernet Sauvignon at Carol's Vineyard, providing structural depth and color density to the blend.

What distinguishes this bottling within the J. Lohr range is its St. Helena AVA single-vineyard provenance — the only Napa fruit in a portfolio exceeding 1.8 million cases, giving it a traceability that most wines at this scale cannot claim.

The 2021 vintage delivered classic structure with fruit-forward character on release; the clay-loam base and American oak framework suggest a drinking window extending comfortably through 2030.

name: J. Lohr Cuvee Pau
short_description (EN Store): Pomerol-inspired Merlot-dominant blend from Paso Robles' calcareous loams, shaped by 50°F diurnal swings that lock in freshness alongside ripe concentration.
description (EN Store):
Jerry Lohr planted in Paso Robles in 1986, before the region had any commercial reputation — a speculative bet on calcareous loams and extreme temperature swings that has since defined Central Coast red winemaking. Cuvée Pau is the direct expression of that wager: a Pomerol-inspired Bordeaux blend built not from Napa clay or Sonoma benchland, but from Paso's quaternary alluvial and calcareous soils with low water-holding capacity, forcing vines to concentrate energy into small, flavour-dense berries.

The Paso Robles AVA's ~50°F diurnal swing — scorching afternoons followed by cold Pacific-driven nights — preserves natural acidity in the Merlot-dominant core while allowing full phenolic ripeness. The blend draws on Merlot as the structural anchor, supported by Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, Malbec, and Petit Verdot, mirroring the Right Bank philosophy of texture-first architecture. Vinification follows conventional red winemaking with oak barrel maturation across J. Lohr's 46,000-barrel Greenfield cellar. The estate's full soil-to-bottle control — 4,000+ acres grown, vinified, and bottled in-house — is unusually rigorous at this production scale.

What sets Cuvée Pau apart within the J. Lohr portfolio is its deliberate Pomerol reference point applied to a warm-climate California AVA, a structural transgression that trades Napa's tannic authority for Paso's plush, early-accessible generosity. The 2021 vintage delivers fruit-forward character upon release with sufficient structure to develop through 2028+.

name: J. Lohr Los Osos Merlot
short_description (EN Store): Paso Robles Merlot from J. Lohr's 2,600-acre estate; calcareous loam soils and 50°F diurnal swings drive concentration with natural freshness.
description (EN Store):
Jerry Lohr planted his first Paso Robles vines in 1986, well before the appellation had any commercial cachet — a calculated bet on the region's extreme diurnal temperature swings, which routinely span 45–50°F between day and night. That thermal whiplash is the defining force behind Los Osos Merlot: daytime heat drives phenolic ripeness and deep color, while cool nights lock in acidity and aromatic precision that Merlot rarely achieves in warmer California sites.

The fruit is drawn from J. Lohr's 2,600+ acres of estate vineyards across Paso Robles, planted on quaternary alluvial soils and calcareous loams with low water-holding capacity — conditions that stress the vine into producing smaller, more concentrated berries rather than high-volume, dilute fruit. The Los Osos label sits within J. Lohr's Estates tier, vinified in-house under the family's full soil-to-bottle estate model, one of the few operations of this scale — over 1.8 million cases annually — that maintains genuine single-estate traceability across every SKU.

The 2021 vintage delivered classic Paso Robles character: fruit-forward on release with sufficient structural backbone to develop through the late 2020s. What distinguishes Los Osos within the California Merlot category is the calcareous soil signature — a chalky, mineral underpinning that cuts through the plush mid-palate weight typical of the variety and gives the wine a savory, almost Pomerol-adjacent tension.

name: J. Lohr Pure Paso Proprietary Red Wine
short_description (EN Store): Cabernet Sauvignon–Petite Sirah blend from Paso Robles' calcareous loams, shaped by 50°F diurnal swings that lock in structure alongside sun-driven concentration.
description (EN Store):
When Jerry Lohr planted in Paso Robles in 1986, the region had no established reputation — a deliberate bet on calcareous soils and extreme temperature variance that has since defined California's most compelling warm-climate red corridor. Pure Paso is the direct expression of that wager: a Cabernet Sauvignon and Petite Sirah blend sourced entirely from J. Lohr's 2,600+ estate acres across Paso Robles AVA.

The soils here — quaternary alluvials, calcareous loams, and sandy loams with low water-holding capacity — force deep root penetration and restrict vine vigor, concentrating flavor in small berries. The region's ~50°F diurnal temperature swing preserves natural acidity and tightens tannin structure even as afternoon heat drives phenolic ripeness. Petite Sirah in the blend adds inky pigment and a firm tannic spine that Cabernet Sauvignon alone rarely delivers at this price point.

The 2021 vintage in Paso Robles delivered classic growing-season character — steady heat accumulation without the stress spikes of drier years — producing fruit-forward concentration with adequate structural backbone for 7+ years of cellaring from vintage date. What distinguishes Pure Paso within the J. Lohr portfolio is the Petite Sirah co-ferment: a deliberate transgression against the Bordeaux-varietal orthodoxy that dominates Paso red blending, yielding a darker, denser profile than the house's Seven Oaks Cabernet.

name: J. Lohr  Seven Oaks Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Paso Robles Cab aged 12 months in Missouri & Minnesota American oak (20% new) — 4,000-acre estate fruit, ~50°F diurnal swing concentration.
description (EN Store): When Jerry Lohr planted in Paso Robles in 1986, the region had no established reputation — he was effectively writing its viticultural identity from scratch. That early-mover conviction is embedded in Seven Oaks, sourced entirely from J. Lohr's 2,600+ acres of Paso Robles estate vineyards. Calcareous loams and sandy alluvial soils with low water-holding capacity force vine roots to compete for moisture, compressing berry size and intensifying fruit character. The region's ~50°F diurnal temperature swing — scorching days followed by cold nights — preserves natural acidity while allowing full phenolic ripeness, the structural tension that separates Paso Robles from flatter warm-climate Cabernets. In the cellar, the wine undergoes 12 months in 60-gallon American oak barrels — predominantly medium and medium-plus toasted Missouri and Minnesota oak with toasted heads, 20% new — a deliberate choice that layers coconut-inflected spice without overwhelming the fruit. What sets Seven Oaks apart at its price tier is the uncommon traceability: over 1.8 million cases produced annually, yet every bottle carries a 'Produced and Bottled By' designation confirming fully estate-grown, estate-vinified fruit — a level of single-source accountability rare at this scale. The 2021 vintage delivers fruit-forward character on release with sufficient structure to develop through 2028+.

name: Georges Duboeuf Beaujolais Villages Nouveau 2025
short_description (EN Store): Vibrant Beaujolais Nouveau with bright cherry, raspberry, and earthy notes. Fresh, silky, and perfect for immediate enjoyment.
description (EN Store): Georges Duboeuf's 2025 Beaujolais Villages Nouveau is a quintessential expression of the region's light, fruit-forward style. Pinot Noir grapes deliver a bright palate dominated by red cherry and raspberry, underpinned by subtle earthy and mushroom notes that add complexity. The wine's high acidity and silky tannin structure create an elegant, refreshing mouthfeel ideal for casual dining and food pairing. This vintage exemplifies the Beaujolais tradition of early release, capturing the vintage's freshness and primary fruit character.

name: Georges Duboeuf Beaujolais Nouveau 2025
short_description (EN Store): Vibrant Beaujolais Nouveau with bright cherry, raspberry, and earthy notes—a fresh, food-friendly red for immediate enjoyment.
description (EN Store): Georges Duboeuf's 2025 Beaujolais Nouveau exemplifies the region's signature style: a light-bodied, high-acidity Pinot Noir brimming with red fruit character. On the nose, ripe cherry and raspberry dominate, supported by subtle earthy and mushroom undertones that hint at terroir complexity. The palate is silky and refreshing, with vibrant acidity that makes this wine remarkably versatile at the table. A classic expression of Beaujolais's approachable charm, ideal for casual dining and immediate consumption.

name: Chateau Margaux 2006
short_description (EN Store): 2006 Grand Vin from Margaux's 25-ha Quaternary gravel plateau — 18–24 months in 100% new estate-coopered French oak; one-third selection rate.
description (EN Store): When André Mentzelopoulos acquired Château Margaux in 1977 for roughly 72 million francs, the estate's own on-site cooperage was already part of the inheritance — a facility that remains unique among the five First Growths and gives the winemaking team direct control over the single most influential variable in barrel aging. That autonomy is fully expressed in the 2006 Grand Vin. The fruit originates from the estate's 25-hectare plateau parcel, where deep Quaternary Garonne gravel sits over a Tertiary limestone and clay-marl terrace — the 'La Mothe' gravel mounds rising above the flat Médoc plain, draining freely via the Lestonnat stream and forcing Cabernet Sauvignon roots deep, restraining vigour and concentrating aromatic intensity. Fermentation proceeded parcel-by-parcel across nearly 100 individual oak and stainless steel vats — some as small as 25 hL — with malolactic fermentation completed in vat for free-run wine and in barrel for press fractions. The Grand Vin then aged 18–24 months in 100% new French oak barrels, a significant proportion coopered on-site, before bottling unfined and unfiltered at the château. Rigorous selection — only roughly one-third of the harvest admitted to the Grand Vin — is the defining discipline here. The 2006 vintage in Margaux delivered a cooler, more structured profile than the opulent 2005, producing a wine of taut Cabernet architecture and slow-building aromatic complexity that rewards extended cellaring well into the 2030s.

name: Chateau Brane Cantenac Margaux 2017
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Margaux with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco—a structured, age-worthy Bordeaux blend.
description (EN Store): Château Brane Cantenac's 2017 Margaux exemplifies the elegance and structure characteristic of the appellation. A Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot blend, this wine displays a full body with firm, well-integrated tannins and vibrant acidity. The primary aromatics reveal ripe blackcurrant and dark plum, complemented by cedar and herbaceous notes. Secondary oak influence introduces subtle tobacco and graphite minerality, while emerging tertiary complexity hints at leather and dried herbs—hallmarks of a wine built for cellaring. The finish is long and refined, with a persistent mineral grip that speaks to the terroir of Margaux.

name: Chateau Les Clavelines Saint Emilion Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Structured Saint-Émilion Grand Cru with dark fruit, cedar, and tobacco complexity. Age-worthy Bordeaux blend.
description (EN Store):
Château Les Clavelines Saint-Émilion Grand Cru 2022 is a classically structured Bordeaux blend anchored by Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. The wine displays a full body with firm, high tannins and medium-high acidity, characteristic of serious Right Bank Bordeaux designed for cellaring.

On the nose and palate, blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the primary layer, supported by black cherry and subtle floral notes. Secondary winemaking influence introduces cedar and tobacco, with hints of graphite minerality. The wine's tertiary potential—leather, earth, and dried fruit complexity—will develop with age, rewarding patient collectors.
