This is a wine for contemplative drinking alongside grilled red meat, lamb, or game, or as a meditation piece with aged hard cheeses.

name: Famille Masse Givry1er Cru Vieilles Vignes
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Burgundy Pinot Noir with silky tannins, bright red fruit, and earthy minerality—a refined expression of Givry's terroir.
description (EN Store): Famille Masse's Givry Premier Cru Vieilles Vignes showcases the elegance and restraint characteristic of Burgundy's Pinot Noir tradition. The 2021 vintage reveals a medium-bodied profile with vibrant acidity and refined tannin structure, driven by primary notes of red cherry and raspberry complemented by earthy, mushroom-tinged secondary complexity. The wine's silky texture and mineral undertones reflect the limestone-rich soils of Givry, one of Burgundy's most underrated appellations. Aged vines contribute depth and subtle structural sophistication, making this a versatile food wine suited to both casual dining and more considered pairings.

name: Domaine Piguet Girardin Auxey Duresses 1er Cru Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Burgundy Pinot Noir with silky red fruit, earthy minerality, and vibrant acidity—a refined expression of Auxey Duresses terroir.
description (EN Store): Domaine Piguet Girardin's Auxey Duresses 1er Cru Rouge is a classically structured Burgundy Pinot Noir that showcases the region's hallmark elegance and precision. The wine opens with bright red cherry and raspberry aromatics, supported by subtle earthy and mushroom undertones that speak to the limestone-rich soils of this premier cru vineyard. A silky texture and medium tannin structure frame the palate, while high acidity provides freshness and food-friendliness. This is a wine built for the table—versatile with lighter proteins, earthy preparations, and tomato-based dishes—rather than for extended cellaring.

name: Domaine Piguet Girardin Chassagne Montrachet 1er Cru Rouge
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Burgundy Pinot Noir with silky tannins, bright red fruit, and earthy minerality—a refined expression of Chassagne-Montrachet terroir.
description (EN Store): Domaine Piguet Girardin's Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Rouge is a classically structured Burgundy Pinot Noir that showcases the region's hallmark balance of fruit purity and mineral complexity. The 2021 vintage delivers bright red cherry and raspberry on the nose, supported by subtle earthy and mushroom undertones that speak to the limestone-rich soils of the appellation. Medium body and silky tannins create an approachable yet age-worthy profile, while high acidity ensures freshness and food compatibility. This wine exemplifies the restrained elegance of Côte de Beaune Pinot Noir.

name: Bodega Volcanes de Chile Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Chilean Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—a classic expression of Maipo Valley terroir.
description (EN Store): Bodega Volcanes de Chile's Cabernet Sauvignon from Maipo Valley delivers a full-bodied, classically structured red wine. Blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the primary aromatics, supported by pronounced cedar and oak from careful winemaking. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide excellent aging potential and food-pairing versatility. Subtle tobacco and earth notes emerge on the finish, adding complexity and depth. This wine exemplifies the power and elegance characteristic of premium Chilean Cabernet Sauvignon.

name: Chateau Cheval Blanc 1er Grand Cru Classe A 2014
short_description (EN Store): Right Bank's Cabernet Franc anomaly: 56 plot-vinified parcels on Isle river alluvium, aged 16–18 months in 100% new Tronçais/Bercé oak.
description (EN Store): When Jean Laussac-Fourcaud replanted the estate in the 1860s with Cabernet Franc as the structural backbone — a choice considered audacious on a Right Bank dominated by Merlot — he set Cheval Blanc on a trajectory no peer has since replicated. That decision remains the defining transgression of the appellation. The 42-hectare vineyard sits on quaternary alluvium deposited by the Isle river at Saint-Émilion's north-western boundary with Pomerol — non-calcareous soils entirely unlike the limestone plateau typical of the appellation. Three distinct soil profiles coexist: ~40% gravel over deep blue clay (home to the oldest Cabernet Franc vines, some planted 1920), ~40% deep gravel, and ~20% sandy clay, producing a wine that simultaneously carries Pomerol's plush depth, Graves-like mineral tension, and Saint-Émilion's structural finesse. Each of the 56 plots is vinified separately in temperature-controlled concrete vats sized to individual plot tonnage, filled by gravity to two-thirds capacity; malolactic fermentation occurs in tank before 15–35 parcels are assembled by tasting alone — no prescriptive varietal ratio. The Grand Vin then ages 16–18 months in 100% new French oak (225 L barriques from 180–350-year-old Tronçais and Bercé trees), racked manually 5–7 times under nitrogen. The 2014 vintage — no blend percentages confirmed in primary sources — represents a season of precision and freshness on the Right Bank, drinking well now through the mid-2030s and beyond.

name: Chateau Cheval Blanc 1er Grand Cru Classe A 2015
short_description (EN Store): 2015 Cheval Blanc: 100p Wine Advocate. 90.5% of crop bottled as Grand Vin — no Petit Cheval made. Gravel-over-deep-blue-clay, 45% Cabernet Franc.
description (EN Store):
In 2015, Technical Director Pierre-Olivier Clouet made a decision that speaks louder than any score: no Petit Cheval was produced — 90.5% of the entire harvest was deemed worthy of the Grand Vin, an extraordinary selection rate that signals the vintage's near-flawless raw material. The Wine Advocate's Lisa Perrotti-Brown awarded 100 points.

The 42-hectare estate sits on quaternary alluvium from the Isle river at Saint-Émilion's north-western edge, directly bordering Pomerol. Soils divide into three identities: gravel over deep blue clay (home to the oldest Cabernet Franc vines, some planted 1920), deep gravel, and sandy clay — non-calcareous throughout, fundamentally unlike the limestone plateau that defines most of Saint-Émilion. That blue clay retains moisture and moderates the estate's slightly warmer, more precocious microclimate, preserving the tension that keeps this wine alive for decades.

The 2015 blend — 55% Merlot, 45% Cabernet Franc — was assembled from up to 56 individually vinified plots, each fermented in a purpose-sized, gravity-filled concrete vat. Malolactic fermentation in tank preceded 16–18 months in 100% new French oak (Tronçais and Bercé forest, 225 L barriques), racked manually 5–7 times under nitrogen. The assembled Grand Vin then returned to barrel for a further ~12 months.

Cheval Blanc remains the only major Right Bank estate where Cabernet Franc — planted here since Jean Laussac-Fourcaud's audacious 1860s replanting — structurally dominates the house identity, even in a Merlot-led vintage like 2015. Drinking window: 2027–2067.

name: Montes Alpha Cabernet Sauvignon Year Of The Snake
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Chilean Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—a classic expression of Maipo Valley power.
description (EN Store): Montes Alpha Cabernet Sauvignon from Chile's Maipo Valley delivers a full-bodied, classically structured red wine. Dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit on the nose give way to pronounced cedar and oak spice on the palate, supported by high, well-integrated tannins. Subtle tobacco and earth notes emerge on the finish, adding complexity and age-worthiness. This is a wine built for serious food pairing and cellaring potential.

name: Enjoy It Non Alcoholic Cabernet Sauvinon
short_description (EN Store): Approachable German Cabernet Sauvignon with ripe blackcurrant, cherry, and subtle cedar—soft tannins, food-friendly.
description (EN Store): A non-alcoholic interpretation of Cabernet Sauvignon from Germany's Rheinhessen region, this wine delivers the classic varietal character of blackcurrant and red cherry with a whisper of cedar oak. Medium body and soft tannins make it an accessible choice for those seeking the structure and flavor profile of traditional Cabernet without alcohol content. The wine's approachable nature suits casual dining, grilled meats, and tomato-forward dishes.

name: Concha Y Toro Frontera Merlot
short_description (EN Store): Approachable Chilean Merlot with ripe plum, berry, and soft tannins—ideal for weeknight red meat and pasta.
description (EN Store): Concha y Toro's Frontera Merlot from Chile's Maipo Valley delivers a fruit-forward profile anchored by ripe plum and mixed berry character. Medium-Full body and integrated tannins create a smooth, juicy palate suitable for casual dining and everyday enjoyment. Subtle vanilla undertones from winemaking add softness, while herbaceous notes provide balance. A reliable, value-conscious choice for grilled meats, braised dishes, and tomato-based preparations.

name: Edenvale Cabernet Sauvignon (Non-Alcoholic Wine)
short_description (EN Store): Australian Cabernet Sauvignon with blackcurrant fruit, cedar oak, and structured tannins—alcohol-free without compromise.
description (EN Store): Edenvale's 2023 Cabernet Sauvignon from South Australia delivers the classic varietal profile of blackcurrant and black cherry fruit, supported by cedar and oak complexity. Medium-full body and medium-high tannin structure provide the grip and aging potential expected of Cabernet, while the alcohol-free production preserves freshness and approachability. Suitable for casual enjoyment or as an inclusive option at the table without sacrificing wine character.

name: Edenvale Shiraz (Non-Alcoholic Wine)
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Australian Syrah with ripe blackberry, pepper spice, and smoky depth—a bold non-alcoholic red.
description (EN Store): Edenvale's 2023 Shiraz delivers the structure and flavor intensity of a traditional Australian red, without alcohol. Blackberry and dark plum dominate the primary profile, supported by black pepper and warming spice notes. The full body and medium-high tannin provide grip and complexity, while subtle smoke adds depth. Ideal for those seeking a serious, food-friendly red experience in non-alcoholic form.

name: Gérard Bertrand Autrement Merlot Pays D'Oc (375 ml)
short_description (EN Store): Demeter-certified biodynamic Merlot from Pays d'Oc — 2022 drought stress delivers concentrated fruit at entry-level scale, vinified to lunar calendar.
description (EN Store): Gérard Bertrand built his empire on a single conviction: that the world's largest biodynamic operation could be run without compromise. Every harvest decision across his Languedoc-Roussillon estates — including this Pays d'Oc Merlot — is timed to the Demeter-certified biodynamic lunar calendar, a discipline applied at a scale no other producer on earth matches. The Autrement range sits within the IGP Pays d'Oc framework, drawing from the warm, wind-swept Mediterranean lowlands where drought-stressed soils force vines to root deep, concentrating flavour at the cost of volume. The 2022 vintage across southern France was defined by heat and water deficit — yields fell sharply, and the resulting Merlot carries the density and dark-fruited weight that low-crop stress produces, with earlier accessibility than a structured AOP red. Vinification follows Bertrand's house protocol: destemming, maceration in concrete or stainless steel vats, with all winemaking timed to biodynamic root and fruit days. What separates this from conventional entry-level Languedoc Merlot is the Demeter certification at this price point — full biodynamic farming discipline applied to a 375 ml half-bottle format, making it one of the most accessible certified biodynamic reds in the South of France. Drink now through 2026 for primary fruit character.

name: Pierre Chavin  Le Petit Etoile Cabernet Sauvignon (Non-Alcoholic Wine)
short_description (EN Store): Approachable non-alcoholic Cabernet with soft red fruit, plum, and subtle oak—ideal for everyday enjoyment.
description (EN Store): Pierre Chavin's Le Petit Etoile Cabernet Sauvignon offers a refined non-alcoholic alternative rooted in Languedoc-Roussillon tradition. The wine presents a medium body with gentle tannins and balanced acidity, delivering primary notes of red fruit and plum with understated secondary oak complexity. Smooth and accessible, it suits casual dining, lighter meat dishes, and social occasions where alcohol-free wine is preferred without compromise on flavor depth.

name: San Marzano Crus Collezione Cinquanta 50 Vino Rosso D’Italia (Red Blend)
short_description (EN Store): Puglia red blend of Zinfandel & Negroamaro: dark fruit, smoke, and pepper with smooth tannins. Food-friendly and approachable.
description (EN Store): San Marzano's Crus Collezione Cinquanta is a Puglian red blend pairing Zinfandel and Negroamaro to deliver a medium-full-bodied wine with immediate appeal. The primary character showcases dark cherry and raspberry fruit, supported by secondary notes of smoke and black pepper that add complexity and spice. Fully rounded tannins and smooth texture make this wine eminently drinkable across a range of occasions—from casual pizza nights to braised meat dishes and pasta with tomato sauce.

name: Robert Foley Howell Mountain Ava Cabernet Sauvignon Napa Valley
short_description (EN Store): Estate Howell Mountain Cabernet from 2,000-ft volcanic tufa soils — Bob Foley's deliberate restraint on oak lets the mountain speak.
description (EN Store): Before founding his own label in 1998, Bob Foley spent fifteen years each shaping the house styles at Markham and Pride Mountain — making him one of the architects of the modern Napa mountain-Cabernet idiom before his name appeared on a bottle. That institutional knowledge is fully deployed here. The estate sits at approximately 2,000 feet on Howell Mountain, where decomposed volcanic ash ('tufa') and iron-rich red clay combine nutrient poverty with exceptional drainage, forcing vines into small-cluster, high-skin-to-juice-ratio fruit across just five acres. Elevation also pushes diurnal swings wide, preserving acidity that valley-floor sites routinely lose. Fermentation runs in stainless steel, targeting a peak of 85°F with twice-daily pump-overs, then cooled to the low-to-mid 70s°F to complete; cuvaison extends approximately 10 days, extracting structure without brutality. Pressing is held to no more than one bar via an open-style membrane press, protecting fruit integrity. Aging proceeds exclusively in French oak from the Tronçais forest, medium-plus toast — with new-oak deliberately kept well below saturation levels, treating wood as seasoning rather than flavor. That restraint is the house's defining transgression in a Napa landscape where 100% new oak remains common. The 2016 vintage, now approaching a decade of bottle age, sits in a drinking window where Howell Mountain's volcanic tannins are integrating without surrendering their mountain-born grip.

name: Robert Foley Claret Napa Valley (Bordeaux Blend)
short_description (EN Store): Howell Mountain volcanic tufa soils, ~80% Cabernet Sauvignon, French Tronçais oak — Bob Foley's hillside Claret built for structure over spectacle.
description (EN Store): Before founding his own label in 1998, Bob Foley spent fifteen years each at Markham and Pride Mountain Vineyards, effectively writing the playbook for Napa's mountain-Cabernet style — then launched Robert Foley Vineyards to execute it entirely on his own terms. The Claret draws from hillside sites across Napa's elevated AVAs, anchored by Howell Mountain fruit grown at approximately 2,000 feet in decomposed volcanic ash (tufa) and iron-rich red clay — nutrient-poor, free-draining soils that force small cluster development and compress flavors into dense, structured fruit rather than the plush, high-sugar profiles typical of valley-floor Cabernet. The documented blend runs approximately 80% Cabernet Sauvignon, 17% Merlot, 3% Petit Verdot. Fermentation proceeds in stainless steel targeting a peak of 85°F with twice-daily pump-overs, then cooled to the low-to-mid 70s°F to complete; skin contact runs approximately 10 days, and pressing is kept to no more than one bar via open-style membrane press. Aging takes place in French oak from the Tronçais forest, medium-plus toast, with new-oak percentage deliberately held well below 100% — Foley treats oak as seasoning, not architecture, a conscious departure from the extraction-heavy, heavily wooded Napa Cabernet mainstream. The 2015 vintage, drawn from a warm, drought-stressed California growing season that concentrated mountain fruit further, is drinking well now and should continue to develop through the late 2020s.

name: Robert Foley (Napa Valley) Mount Veeder Cabernet Sauvignon Ava Napa Valley
short_description (EN Store): Mount Veeder hillside Cabernet from Bob Foley — volcanic & ocean-floor soils, 10-day cuvaison, French Tronçais oak kept deliberately restrained.
description (EN Store): Bob Foley spent fifteen years each shaping the house styles at Markham and Pride Mountain before founding his own label in 1998 — a career that made him one of Napa's most consequential mountain-Cabernet architects. That institutional knowledge is encoded in every decision here. Mount Veeder's soils are a rare geological collision: decomposed volcanic material layered over ancient ocean-floor sediment, producing a nutrient-stressed, free-draining environment that forces deep root systems and yields small, intensely concentrated clusters. The elevation and Pacific-influenced diurnal swings preserve acidity that valley-floor sites simply cannot replicate. In the winery, hand-harvested fruit undergoes primary fermentation in stainless steel, peaking at approximately 85°F with twice-daily pump-overs before being cooled to the low-to-mid 70s°F to complete fermentation. Skin contact runs approximately 10 days, and pressing is executed at no more than one bar via an open-style membrane press — a gentle extraction philosophy that respects tannin quality over quantity. Aging proceeds in French oak from the Tronçais forest, medium-plus toast, with new-oak percentage deliberately held well below saturation — Foley treats oak as seasoning, not architecture, a direct counter to the over-extracted, heavily wooded Napa mainstream. The special feature here is the site itself: Mount Veeder's dual-geology soils produce a structural fingerprint — iron-laced savory depth alongside volcanic lift — that no valley-floor Napa Cabernet can approximate. The 2014 vintage has had a decade of bottle development; tannins will have integrated considerably, with the mountain fruit concentration now showing full complexity.

name: Robert Foley Cabernet Sauvignon Napa Valley
short_description (EN Store): Howell Mountain volcanic tufa & iron-red clay at 2,000 ft — Bob Foley's deliberate restraint on new French oak lets mountain terroir lead.
description (EN Store): Before launching his own label in 1998, Bob Foley spent fifteen years each shaping the house styles at Markham and Pride Mountain — two of Napa's most consequential mountain programs — before channeling that accumulated knowledge into fruit grown on his own volcanic hillside above Angwin. The estate sits at approximately 2,000 feet on Howell Mountain, where decomposed volcanic ash (tufa) and iron-rich red clay drain freely and stress the vines into producing small, intensely concentrated clusters; the same nutrient poverty that makes valley-floor farming impractical here is precisely what drives phenolic depth and structural density in the finished wine. Primary fermentation targets a peak of 85°F in stainless steel with twice-daily pump-overs, then cools to the low-to-mid 70s°F to complete; skin contact runs approximately ten days, and pressing is held to no more than one bar via an open-style membrane press to preserve aromatic integrity. Aging proceeds in French oak from the Tronçais forest, medium-plus toast, with new-oak percentage deliberately kept well below saturation — a conscious rejection of the valley-floor playbook that leans on heavy new wood to amplify richness. What separates Foley's mountain Cabernet from most Howell Mountain peers is that restraint: oak functions as seasoning, not architecture, leaving volcanic minerality and firm, fine-grained tannins structurally exposed. The 2016 vintage, drawn from this high-elevation volcanic site, carries the density and grip characteristic of the appellation alongside the aromatic lift that Foley's measured extraction philosophy consistently delivers; drink 2024–2034.

name: La Linda Private Selection Old Vines Malbec
short_description (EN Store): Old Vines Malbec from Mendoza with ripe blackberry, plum, and cocoa; smooth, structured, and food-friendly.
description (EN Store): La Linda's Private Selection Old Vines Malbec showcases the depth and elegance of mature Mendoza fruit. Aromas of blackberry and dark plum lead to a medium-full palate with well-integrated tannins and notes of cocoa, vanilla, and subtle earth. The wine's smooth structure and balanced acidity make it an excellent companion to grilled meats, braised beef, and aged cheeses.

name: Alto Los Romeros  Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Colchagua Cab from LFE's Puquillay Alto estate — granitic-volcanic soils up to 900 m give structural tension rare at this price tier.
description (EN Store): When Luis Felipe Edwards Sr. acquired the Puquillay Alto estate in 1976, he inherited vineyards already planted in the 1930s — mountain blocks sitting up to 900 m asl in a horseshoe-shaped micro-valley sheltered by the San Fernando ridge. That altitude, unusual for Colchagua, is the founding logic of the Alto Los Romeros label itself. The Puquillay Alto site sits on a complex of granitic and volcanic rock, soils that drain freely and stress the vine, concentrating phenolics while the elevation preserves the natural acidity that warmer valley floors surrender. The northwest-facing aspect extends the ripening window without accumulating excessive sugar. Winemaking is executed at the Puquillay estate facility using pneumatic presses and temperature-controlled fermentation under the direction of chief winemaker Nicolás Bizzarri; low-yield viticulture is a stated house philosophy to drive juice concentration rather than volume. Oak regime at the varietal tier is minimal, keeping the mountain-site character in the foreground rather than masking it with wood. What separates this Cabernet from typical warm-valley Chilean counterparts at ฿700 is precisely that elevation provenance — the same high-altitude blocks that inspire the label name deliver measurable structural tension and freshness that most peers at this price point cannot claim.

name: Alto Los Romeros Merlot
short_description (EN Store): Colchagua Merlot from LFE's multi-valley estate network — mountain-influenced structure at an entry price that punches well above its tier.
description (EN Store): Luis Felipe Edwards Sr. acquired the Puquillay Alto estate in 1976, planting Merlot alongside Cabernet Sauvignon and Carménère on a horseshoe-shaped micro-valley floor sheltered by the San Fernando ridge — a deliberate topographic choice that moderates Colchagua's summer heat and preserves natural acidity. The estate's soils are a complex mix of granitic and volcanic rock, draining freely and stressing the vine roots into producing concentrated, small-berried fruit rather than the plush, dilute Merlot common to warmer valley floors. Elevation at Puquillay Alto reaches up to 900 m asl — among the highest blocks in Colchagua — and the altitude differential between day and night temperatures slows ripening, locking in aromatic precision. Winemaking at the Puquillay facility employs pneumatic presses and temperature-controlled fermentation under Chief Winemaker Nicolás Bizzarri; low-yield viticulture is a stated house philosophy across all LFE estate sites. At this entry tier, oak influence is minimal to absent, keeping the wine's fruit-forward character unobscured. What separates Alto Los Romeros from typical warm-valley Chilean Merlot is the mountain-and-granite terroir context: the granitic volcanic substrate and high-altitude diurnal swing produce a firmer texture and brighter acidity than the category norm, giving the wine genuine structural tension rather than soft, extracted fruit.

name: Alto Los Romeros Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): High-altitude Colchagua Cabernet from Puquillay Alto's granitic-volcanic soils — more structural tension than the valley floor delivers at this price.
description (EN Store):
When Luis Felipe Edwards Sr. acquired the Puquillay estate in 1976, he inherited vineyards already planted in the 1930s — mountain blocks that would eventually define the Alto in Alto Los Romeros. The Puquillay Alto site sits in a horseshoe-shaped micro-valley facing northwest, sheltered from Andean wind by the San Fernando ridge, with soils built from granitic and volcanic rock at elevations reaching 900 m asl — among the highest in Colchagua Valley.

Those soils drain aggressively, stressing the vine and concentrating juice in line with LFE's stated low-yield viticulture philosophy. The granitic fraction contributes a mineral grip that distinguishes these reds from the softer, clay-dominant profiles of the valley floor. Winemaking is executed at the Puquillay facility using pneumatic presses and temperature-controlled fermentation; the Reserva tier receives oak treatment, though specific barrel regime and new-oak percentage are not publicly disclosed at this label level.

What sets this Cabernet apart within its price tier is the site itself: sourcing from genuinely high-altitude mountain blocks gives the wine structural acidity and firm tannin architecture that most Chilean Cabernet at ฿900 simply cannot replicate. Colchagua's warm, dry growing seasons concentrate dark fruit while the elevation preserves freshness — expect the wine to reward 2–4 years of cellaring from vintage.

name: Alto Los Romeros Reserva Carmenére
short_description (EN Store): Colchagua Carménère from high-altitude Puquillay Alto estate (up to 900 m), where granitic-volcanic soils and Andean shelter produce firmer structure than warm-valley peers.
description (EN Store): Luis Felipe Edwards Sr. acquired the Puquillay Alto estate in 1976, planting Carménère into a horseshoe-shaped micro-valley whose northwest-facing aspect and San Fernando ridge shelter create a distinct thermal buffer from the broader Colchagua floor. That topographic enclosure is the technical foundation of this wine's character. Granitic and volcanic rock soils at elevations reaching 900 m asl — among the highest blocks in Colchagua Valley — restrict water retention and force deep root development, yielding lower berry weights and more concentrated phenolic skins than the fertile clay benchlands below. The result is a structural tension — firmer tannin, higher natural acidity — that separates this wine from the soft, extracted profile that defines most Colchagua Carménère at this price tier. Low-yield viticulture is a stated house philosophy, and winemaking is executed at the Puquillay facility using temperature-controlled fermentation and pneumatic pressing; specific oak regime at Reserva tier is not disclosed by the producer. The special feature here is altitude: sourcing from genuinely mountain-influenced Colchagua blocks at sub-฿1,000 pricing is rare in Chilean Carménère. Drinking well now with modest cellaring potential of 3–5 years from vintage.

name: Apollonio Terragnolo Primitivo
short_description (EN Store): Fourth-generation Apollonio, Monteroni di Lecce: Primitivo from calcareous-clay Salento soils, oak-aged, built for depth over immediacy.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1870 and now stewarded by brothers Marcello and Massimiliano Apollonio — the fourth generation — this Impresa Storica d'Italia-designated house has aged approximately one million litres per year in oak as a deliberate house doctrine, at a time when most Puglian peers pivoted to stainless steel for fresher, earlier-drinking styles. Terragnolo is the estate's Primitivo expression, grown on the flat to gently karst-depressed terrain around Monteroni di Lecce at roughly 100 m elevation, where calcareous soils laced with clay concentrate sugars and phenolics while dual sea breezes from the Adriatic and Ionian coasts temper the intense Mediterranean sun and provide natural disease resistance. Harvest is manual, typically in the third decade of September, followed by destemming and temperature-controlled fermentation at 27–28 °C in large Slavonian oak vats with floating cap and pump-overs; skin maceration drives polyphenolic extraction before the wine moves to French oak barriques for structured maturation. What sets Terragnolo apart within the Apollonio range is its varietal focus: Primitivo — genetically identical to Zinfandel yet expressing a distinctly Salentine character of dried fig, carob, and iron-tinged earth that Negroamaro-dominant blends cannot replicate. The current vintage delivers a dense, sun-saturated profile that rewards 2–5 years of cellaring but is already accessible alongside robust food.

name: Apollonio Mani Del Sud Salice Salentino Rosso
short_description (EN Store): Salento's 150-year-old oak house: 80% Negroamaro + 20% Malvasia Nera from calcareous-clay soils near Lecce, aged in Slavonian oak.
description (EN Store):
Founded in 1870 and now designated an Impresa Storica d'Italia, Apollonio has aged approximately one million litres per year in oak across four generations — a deliberate house commitment that sets it apart from Puglian peers who pivoted to stainless steel for varietal freshness. The Mani del Sud Salice Salentino Rosso is the direct expression of that philosophy at its most accessible tier.

The fruit originates from the Valle della Cupa around Monteroni di Lecce, roughly 5 km from Lecce at approximately 100 m elevation. Calcareous soils laced with clay — classic Salento karst geology — stress the vines into concentrated berry production, while dual sea breezes from the Adriatic and Ionian coasts moderate the intense Mediterranean sun and impart a saline mineral thread through the wine.

The blend is 80% Negroamaro and 20% Malvasia Nera, harvested manually in the third decade of September. Fermentation proceeds at 27–28 °C in large Slavonian oak vats with floating-cap management and pump-overs; skin maceration extracts polyphenolic structure before the wine moves to large Slavonian oak barrels for 3–12 months. Malvasia Nera's aromatic lift — dried rose, anise — threads through Negroamaro's dark-fruit density without softening its characteristic bitter-cherry grip.

The Malvasia Nera component is the category transgression: most Salice Salentino producers blend Negroamaro with Montepulciano for structure, but Apollonio's aromatic co-ferment produces a distinctly perfumed, spice-forward profile unique within the DOC. Drink now through three to five years for primary fruit intensity, or cellar briefly for the oak integration to fully resolve.

name: Apollonio Elfo Primitivo Salento I.G.P. Rosso
short_description (EN Store): Fourth-generation Apollonio, Monteroni di Lecce: Primitivo from calcareous-clay Salento soils, aged in Slavonian oak — sun-driven, structured, Mediterranean-spiced.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1870 and now designated an Impresa Storica d'Italia, Apollonio has spent four generations refusing to abandon Salento's indigenous varieties in favour of international grapes — a deliberate stance that makes this Primitivo a direct expression of house philosophy rather than market convenience. The vineyards sit at roughly 100 metres above sea level in the Valle della Cupa near Monteroni di Lecce, where calcareous soils laced with clay retain just enough moisture to temper Salento's intense Mediterranean sun, while dual sea breezes from the Adriatic and Ionian coasts deliver natural acidity and disease resistance that keep the fruit focused rather than jammy. Fermentation proceeds at temperature-controlled 27–28 °C in large Slavonian oak vats with floating cap and pump-overs; entry-level reds receive 3–12 months of maturation in large Slavonian oak barrels, preserving Primitivo's dark-fruit density without heavy new-oak imprint. What sets this bottling apart within its IGP tier is Apollonio's insistence on oak aging across the entire range — a commitment the winery maintains at approximately one million litres per year — when most Puglian peers at this price point default to stainless steel. The current vintage delivers concentrated dried fig, sun-baked blackberry, and garrigue-inflected spice with the structural grip that calcareous-clay soils reliably produce; drink now through the near term with a 30-minute decant.

name: Apollonio Il 150 Susumaniello Rosso Salento IGP
short_description (EN Store): Rare Salento varietal: Susumaniello vinified solo at Apollonio's Monteroni di Lecce estate, calcareous-clay soils, Mediterranean dual-coast breezes.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1870 and now stewarded by fourth-generation brothers Marcello and Massimiliano Apollonio, this Lecce-based house holds the rare Impresa Storica d'Italia designation — and its Il 150 bottling signals exactly why longevity matters: Susumaniello, once nearly extinct across Salento, is here given a solo stage. The estate sits at roughly 100 m elevation near Monteroni di Lecce, on flat to gently karst-depressed terrain with calcareous soils over clay — geology that drives both mineral tension and the deep colour Susumaniello is prized for. Dual sea breezes from the Adriatic and Ionian coasts moderate the intense Puglian sun, preserving aromatic lift and natural acidity that the variety can otherwise lose in hotter inland sites. Vinification follows Apollonio's house method: manual harvest, destemming, temperature-controlled fermentation at 27–28 °C in large Slavonian oak vats with floating-cap pump-overs and extended skin maceration to extract the grape's characteristic deep pigment and polyphenolic structure. Aging proceeds in French oak, consistent with the winery's commitment to maturing approximately one million litres per year in wood — a deliberate counter-position to the stainless-steel freshness trend sweeping Puglia. The special feature here is categorical: a 100% Susumaniello bottling is vanishingly rare even within Salento, where the variety has historically played a blending role. Drink now through the near term for vivid fruit; decant 30 minutes to open the structure.

name: Fratelli Ponte Barbera D'Asti Superiore DOCG
short_description (EN Store): 100% Barbera from chalky San Damiano d'Asti soils, aged 12+ months in large French-oak botte — fruit-forward acidity preserved, label hand-drawn by grappa legend Romano Levi.
description (EN Store): The label on this bottle is itself a piece of Piedmontese cultural history: it was hand-drawn on a bread-bag by Romano Levi, the celebrated grappa artisan of Neive, in the year 2000 — a singular act of friendship that anchors the estate's identity as a keeper of regional tradition over commercial formula. Fratelli Ponte's Barbera d'Asti Superiore DOCG is grown on chalky soils at Frazione Gorzano, San Damiano d'Asti, positioned between Asti and Alba in the heart of Barbera country; the calcium-rich subsoil drives the variety's characteristically high natural acidity and keeps berry fruit taut rather than jammy. Fermentation proceeds in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 26–28°C with 10–15 days of skin maceration, preserving primary red-fruit intensity before the wine moves into large French-oak botte from Garbelotto cooperage for a minimum of 12 months — a deliberate rejection of the small-barrique extraction style favoured by many high-profile Barbera producers. The large-format wood integrates oxygen slowly without imposing vanilla or toast, leaving the grape's acidity structurally dominant. What sets this expression apart within the Superiore category is precisely that restraint: third-generation winemaker Renato Ponte, trained at Enologica Albese, uses oak as a stabiliser rather than a flavour agent. Drink now through 4–5 years from vintage; the chalky-soil acidity gives it genuine mid-term cellaring potential at this price point.

name: Fratelli Ponte Nebbiolo D'Alba DOC
short_description (EN Store): 100% Nebbiolo from San Damiano d'Asti's chalky soils, fermented in temperature-controlled steel — Piedmont's noble grape at an accessible entry point.
description (EN Store): Giuseppe Ponte established the family's Frazione Gorzano cellar in 1968, and three generations later the estate remains one of the few independent Piedmontese houses covering the full arc of the region's indigenous varieties — from Barolo to Brachetto — under a single family roof. The Nebbiolo d'Alba DOC draws on chalky soils in the corridor between Asti and Alba, a terrain that tempers Nebbiolo's structural aggression and pushes the variety toward red-fruit clarity rather than brooding tannin mass. Fermentation runs 10–15 days in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 26–28°C with skin maceration, a house-wide protocol that prioritises varietal transparency over extraction. The estate's deliberate rejection of small barriques — a stance codified across their red range — means this Nebbiolo avoids the vanilla overlay that can obscure the grape's characteristic tar-and-rose signature at this price tier. What makes this expression unusual is its DOC status: Nebbiolo d'Alba permits fruit from the Roero and the Langhe hills without the strict single-commune rules of Barolo or Barbaresco, giving the winemaking team flexibility to select for freshness over power. Drink now through 3–4 years for maximum fruit definition; the chalky subsoil lends enough grip to reward short cellaring.

name: Fratelli Ponte Barolo DOCG
short_description (EN Store): 100% Nebbiolo from Fratelli Ponte's Gorzano estate — traditional Piedmontese house, 14.5% ABV, fermented in stainless steel with 10–15 day maceration.
description (EN Store):
Giuseppe Ponte established the family cellar at Frazione Gorzano, San Damiano d'Asti in 1968, positioning the estate deliberately between Asti and Alba — a geographic middle ground that gives the third-generation Ponte brothers (Massimo, Ivan, and Renato) access to Barolo DOCG fruit without abandoning their Asti-province roots. Renato trained at the Enologica Albese, and the house philosophy — fruit purity over oak extraction — shapes every wine in the range, including this Nebbiolo.

The 100% Nebbiolo undergoes temperature-controlled fermentation at 26–28°C in stainless steel with 10–15 days of skin maceration, extracting Barolo's characteristic tar-and-rose aromatic profile and firm tannin structure without the risk of over-extraction. At 14.5% ABV, the wine carries the density expected of the Langhe's late-ripening Nebbiolo. Oak aging parameters follow the house's broader philosophy of using wood as a frame rather than a flavour source.

What distinguishes this expression within the Fratelli Ponte range is its appellation reach: sourced under Barolo DOCG classification, it is the estate's sole foray into the Cuneo-province heartland of Nebbiolo, produced by a family whose primary holdings sit on chalky soils in San Damiano d'Asti — making this an outlier in their portfolio and a value-anchored entry into one of Piedmont's most tightly regulated appellations. Drink from release through medium-term cellaring.

name: Fratelli Fiorellino Red
short_description (EN Store): Fratelli Ponte's entry-level Barbera from chalky San Damiano d'Asti soils — stainless-fermented, fruit-forward, high-acid, zero barrique influence.
description (EN Store):
The Ponte family has worked the chalky hillsides of Frazione Gorzano, San Damiano d'Asti — a corridor between Asti and Alba where Barbera's naturally elevated acidity is amplified by calcium-rich soils — since Giuseppe Ponte established the current cellar in 1968. The third generation, led by Massimo, Ivan, and Renato Ponte (Renato a graduate of the Enologica Albese wine school), made a deliberate house decision to resist the heavy-barrique style that dominated Barbera's prestige tier in the 1990s and 2000s, betting instead on varietal transparency at accessible price points.

The Fiorellino Red is 100% Barbera, fermented in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 26–28°C with 10–15 days of skin maceration, then aged 6 months in stainless steel followed by 6 months in bottle before release — no oak contact whatsoever. This regime locks in Barbera's signature deep ruby colour, sharp red-cherry acidity, and low-tannin texture without the vanilla overlay that barriques would impose. What sets this expression apart within its category is precisely that absence: at a price point where many producers reach for oak chips or short-stave contact, Fratelli Ponte uses none, letting the chalky-soil minerality and grape tannin speak unmediated. Drink young, within 2–3 years of vintage, for maximum fruit clarity.

name: Talenti Brunello di Montalcino Riserva Pian di Conte
short_description (EN Store): Oldest vines at Pian di Conte (~400 m, Galestro marl), 25–30-day maceration, Allier & Slavonian oak — Riserva released only in exceptional years.
description (EN Store):
Pierluigi Talenti spent decades shaping Brunello di Montalcino's modern identity — first as head winemaker at Il Poggione, then from 1980 as the architect of his own estate at Pian di Conte in the southern Sant'Angelo in Colle sector, where he personally selected Sangiovese clones through the 1970s and 1980s that remain genetically distinct from most Montalcino peers. The Riserva Pian di Conte is sourced exclusively from the estate's oldest vines near the property's centre at roughly 400 m a.s.l., where sandy loam over Galestro marl and clay with rock fragments drives the tension between structural grip and mineral transparency that defines this southern-sector address.

Fermentation proceeds in temperature-controlled stainless steel at 28–29°C, followed by an extended 25–30-day skin maceration that builds the tannin architecture without resorting to extraction shortcuts. Aging unfolds in a combination of Allier and Slavonian oak — a deliberate pairing that layers spice and oxidative complexity without the vanilla dominance of heavy new French barriques. The Riserva is bottled only in exceptional years, making 2019 a rare release.

What separates this from the appellation's two dominant camps — ultra-traditional large-botti and modernist barrique-heavy — is Talenti's middle path: medium-format oak, proprietary clonal material, and parcel-by-parcel vinification that keeps site character legible. The 2019 growing season delivered the power typical of Sant'Angelo in Colle alongside mouthwatering freshness — juicy blue fruit, floral lift, balsamic and earthy licorice — with a drinking window opening from approximately 2027.

name: Talenti Brunello di Montalcino “Piero” DOCG
short_description (EN Store): 1.95-ha single plot at 410 m, Pierluigi's own clonal selection from the 1970s — 100% tonneaux-aged Brunello from Sant'Angelo in Colle's clay-Galestro soils.
description (EN Store):
Pierluigi Talenti spent decades shaping Brunello di Montalcino's modern identity — first as head winemaker at Il Poggione, then from 1980 on his own estate at Pian di Conte, Sant'Angelo in Colle. The Vigna di Piero is his most personal act: 1.95 ha of Sangiovese Grosso planted on his own clonal selections in the 1970s–1980s, now tended by grandson Riccardo, making these vines genetically distinct from virtually every other Montalcino estate.

The site sits at 410 m a.s.l. on a south-east-facing slope enclosed by forest, in clay-rich soil laced with Galestro marl — the friable limestone-schist that drives mineral tension and structural precision. The forest boundary moderates diurnal temperature swings, preserving acidity through a warm southern-sector growing season.

Fruit is hand-harvested late and vinified parcel-by-parcel: temperature-controlled fermentation at 28–29°C with 25–30 days of skin maceration in stainless steel, followed by 100% 500-litre French oak tonneaux for a minimum of 24 months — no Slavonian oak, no large botti, no barrique. This mid-format vessel is the house's deliberate middle path: more oxygen exchange than a foudre, less extraction than a barrique.

The exclusive tonneaux regime for 'Piero' is unique within the Talenti range, where the standard Brunello blends tonneaux with Slavonian casks — making this the estate's most oak-integrated, structurally precise expression. The 2020 vintage has no published Talenti-specific notes; drinking window is best assessed from ~2027 onward given the appellation's DOCG release schedule and the site's structural density.

name: Talenti Toscana Rosso IGT
short_description (EN Store): Talenti's IGT tier draws on Pian di Conte's Galestro-marl soils to express Colorino, Canaiolo, Syrah, Merlot, and Cabernet alongside Sangiovese — a rare multi-varietal window into southern Montalcino.
description (EN Store): Pierluigi Talenti spent decades selecting Sangiovese clones across Montalcino before formally establishing the estate in 1980 — but it is the remaining five hectares at Pian di Conte planted to Colorino, Canaiolo, Syrah, Merlot, and Cabernet Sauvignon that give rise to this Toscana IGT, a deliberate departure from the appellation's Sangiovese-only orthodoxy. The Pian di Conte estate sits in the southern sector of Montalcino (Sant'Angelo in Colle) at 200–400 m a.s.l. on sandy loam with clay and Galestro marl — the friable limestone-schist that drains freely, concentrates root stress, and imparts the chalky mineral tension that runs through every Talenti bottling. South-easterly and south-westerly aspects extend the ripening window in this warmer, drier corner of the hill. Fruit is hand-harvested and vinified parcel by parcel in temperature-controlled stainless steel, consistent with the estate's broader philosophy of preserving individual site character rather than blending toward a house style. What distinguishes this IGT within Talenti's range is precisely its compositional freedom: where the Brunello and Rosso are locked to 100% Sangiovese Grosso, this bottling assembles the estate's non-Sangiovese blocks into a wine that carries Pian di Conte's Galestro signature through an entirely different varietal lens. Drink on release through the near term for primary fruit character.

name: Talenti Brunello di Montalcino DOCG
short_description (EN Store): Pian di Conte's galestro-marl soils, 60% tonneaux aging, and Pierluigi's proprietary Sangiovese clones define this 2019 with blue fruit, balsamic lift, and southern Montalcino power.
description (EN Store): Pierluigi Talenti spent decades as head winemaker at Il Poggione before formally establishing his own estate at Pian di Conte in 1980 — and the clonal selections he began planting in the 1970s remain genetically distinct from most Montalcino peers, giving grandson Riccardo's wines a fingerprint no neighbour can replicate. The southern sector of Montalcino at Sant'Angelo in Colle sits between 200–400 m a.s.l. on sandy loam threaded with Galestro marl and clay; the friable limestone-schist drains freely while retaining just enough mineral charge to keep Sangiovese's acidity taut even in warm years. Eleven plots are vinified parcel-by-parcel, each harvested by hand at late ripeness, then fermented in stainless steel at 28–29°C before the blend is aged ~60% in 500-litre French oak tonneaux and ~40% in 15–25 hl Slavonian oak barrels for a minimum 24 months, followed by bottle rest — a deliberate middle path that avoids both the oxidative weight of large botti and the vanilla extraction of heavy barrique regimes. What sets this estate apart is that proprietary clonal research spanning fifty years means the Sangiovese itself is the differentiator, not the cellar. The 2019 growing season delivered exceptional freshness alongside the structural power characteristic of Sant'Angelo in Colle: juicy blue fruits, floral lift, balsamic and earthy licorice emerge now, but the architecture points firmly toward a drinking window opening from ~2027.

name: Talenti Rosso di Montalcino
short_description (EN Store): Pian di Conte's sandy Galestro-marl soils, 200–400 m elevation, 100% Pierluigi's proprietary Sangiovese clones — southern Montalcino's terroir transparency in a 2022 Rosso.
description (EN Store):
Pierluigi Talenti spent decades in Montalcino before founding the estate in 1980, and his most consequential act was personal: beginning in the 1970s, he selected and propagated his own Sangiovese clones across the Pian di Conte property at Sant'Angelo in Colle — clones now carried forward by grandson Riccardo that remain genetically distinct from most peers in the appellation. That clonal specificity is the invisible architecture beneath every bottle.

The southern Montalcino site sits at 200–400 m a.s.l. on south-easterly and south-westerly slopes, where sandy loam over Galestro marl (friable limestone-schist with rock fragments) drains freely and stresses the vine into concentration without sacrificing lift. The warmer, drier southern sector imparts structural generosity that the Galestro tempers with mineral tension.

Vinification follows temperature-controlled maceration and fermentation in stainless steel at 28–29°C, preserving primary fruit clarity. As a Rosso di Montalcino, the wine draws from the same eleven parcel-specific plots as the Brunello but is released earlier, offering a direct window into the vintage's raw character before extended oak and bottle aging reshape it.

Talenti's deliberate refusal of both heavy new-oak extraction and ultra-traditional large-botti means this Rosso occupies a genuinely uncommon middle register — site-driven and structured, yet approachable without being simplified. The 2022 vintage, from a warm Tuscan growing season, delivers forward red-fruit energy with the Galestro's characteristic chalky grip already present at release; drink 2024–2028.

name: Montecillo Crianza Rioja
short_description (EN Store): Classic Rioja Crianza: medium-full Tempranillo with ripe plum, leather, and vanilla oak. Balanced acidity, food-friendly structure.
description (EN Store): Montecillo Crianza Rioja is a benchmark expression of traditional Spanish winemaking. This single-varietal Tempranillo displays a deep red color with aromas of dark plum and red cherry, supported by vanilla oak and dried herbs. The palate is dry and bold with medium-high acidity and balanced tannins, delivering a long, satisfying finish. A versatile food wine that bridges casual dining and more formal occasions.

name: Bodegas Volver Tarima Hill
short_description (EN Store): Old-vine Monastrell from ungrafted 1935–1970 plots at 600–750 m in Alicante's Sierra ranges, dry-farmed on shallow limestone with yields as low as 1.5 kg per plant.
description (EN Store):
When Rafael Cañizares and Jorge Ordóñez launched Bodegas Volver in 2004, their deliberate target in Alicante was not new plantings but near-abandoned ungrafted Monastrell vines — some dating to 1935 — that co-operative growers had written off as uneconomical precisely because their yields were so punishingly low. Tarima Hill draws from those plots scattered between the Sierra de Salinas, Sierra de Umbría, and Sierra de La Sima at 600–750 m elevation, where shallow, rocky limestone soils with minimal organic matter force roots deep and restrict canopy, concentrating what little the vine produces into each berry.

The altitude delivers the diurnal swings that keep acidity alive in a region better known for heat than freshness. Grapes are hand-harvested and sorted on a selection table before primary fermentation in open-top stainless steel tanks at ~28°C with indigenous yeast — a choice that preserves the site's wild aromatic character rather than imposing a house yeast profile. The Monastrell-dominant blend incorporates Garnacha Tintorera (Alicante Bouschet), adding inky depth to the variety's natural dark-fruit density.

What separates Tarima Hill from the broader Tarima range is the extreme vine age and the 1.5 kg-per-plant yield ceiling — a concentration benchmark that most Alicante producers, working far higher-yielding plots, simply cannot replicate at this price point.

name: Bread & Butter Howell Mountain Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Howell Mountain Cab from Bread & Butter's grower network — MLF-driven texture meets high-elevation Napa tannin structure, 2019.
description (EN Store): When Gregory Ahn launched Bread & Butter in 2013, he encoded the winemaking philosophy directly into the brand name: oak = bread, malolactic fermentation = butter — a deliberate manifesto against the lean, unoaked California style that had dominated the preceding decade. This Howell Mountain expression applies that same doctrine to one of Napa's most demanding sub-appellations. Howell Mountain sits above the fog line on the eastern Vaca Range, where volcanic, iron-rich soils with minimal water retention force deep root development and produce naturally small-berried Cabernet with concentrated pigment and firm, fine-grained tannin — a structural profile that distinguishes it sharply from valley-floor fruit. Fermentation in stainless steel preserves primary fruit integrity before the wine moves into a combination of French and American oak barrels for aging; 100% malolactic fermentation converts the mountain's inherent tartaric sharpness into a rounder lactic texture without softening the tannin architecture. What sets this expression apart within the Bread & Butter portfolio is the site itself: Howell Mountain's elevation-driven phenolic density gives the wine a structural backbone that the brand's house style — built for approachability — rarely encounters at this tier. The 2019 vintage in Napa delivered a long, even growing season with no major heat events at harvest, producing fruit with both ripeness and natural acidity intact; drink now through 2027.

name: Larkmead Solari Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description (EN Store): Calistoga Cabernet of power and structure: blackcurrant and cedar with firm tannins and aging potential.
description (EN Store): Larkmead Estate's 2018 Solari Cabernet Sauvignon from Calistoga showcases the full-bodied character and structural complexity expected of premium Napa Valley fruit. Dominant blackcurrant and dark plum aromatics are complemented by pronounced cedar and vanilla oak, with supporting black pepper spice. The wine's high tannin profile and medium-high acidity provide excellent aging framework, while tertiary notes of tobacco and leather suggest evolution potential. A classically styled Cabernet built for the cellar and the table.

name: Il Bastardo Sangiovese di Toscana
short_description (EN Store): Tuscan Sangiovese with bright cherry, herbaceous character, and structured tannins—a versatile, food-friendly red.
description (EN Store):
Il Bastardo Sangiovese di Toscana is a classic expression of Tuscany's signature varietal, built on a foundation of ripe cherry and red fruit with herbaceous undertones. High acidity and medium-high tannin structure give the wine grip and freshness, while subtle earth and spice notes add complexity. Leather and tobacco emerge on the finish, reflecting the wine's age-worthiness and traditional winemaking approach.

This is a versatile table wine suited to Italian cuisine, grilled meats, and tomato-based dishes. The bright acidity and moderate alcohol make it approachable young, yet the tannin framework suggests modest cellaring potential.

name: Renzo Masi Contrappasso Rosso di Toscana
short_description (EN Store): Sangiovese-Syrah blend from Chianti with bright cherry, herbaceous notes, and supple tannins—food-friendly Tuscan red.
description (EN Store): Renzo Masi's Contrappasso Rosso di Toscana blends Sangiovese and Syrah to deliver a medium-full-bodied red with vibrant acidity and refined tannin structure. On the nose, ripe cherry and red fruit dominate, supported by herbaceous undertones characteristic of Chianti terroir. The palate reveals layers of leather and tobacco complexity, with mineral-driven earth notes emerging on the finish. This is a versatile food wine suited to grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, and aged cheeses.

name: Finca Fella El Maso Garnacha Tintorera
short_description (EN Store): Spanish Grenache from Almansa with ripe dark fruit, spice, and earthy complexity—food-friendly and versatile.
description (EN Store): Finca Fella's El Maso Garnacha Tintorera is a single-varietal red from Spain's Almansa region, showcasing the depth and versatility of Grenache. The wine displays a medium-full body with balanced tannins and a medium acidity that makes it approachable yet structured. On the nose and palate, dark cherry and blackberry lead, supported by subtle spice and earthy undertones that suggest careful winemaking and terroir expression. A food-friendly expression ideal for grilled meats, Mediterranean cuisine, and classic Spanish fare.

name: Finca Fella Altado Monastrell Tinto
short_description (EN Store): Almansa Mourvèdre with dark fruit, spice, and earthy complexity—a food-friendly Spanish red.
description (EN Store): Finca Fella's Altado Monastrell Tinto is a single-varietal expression from Spain's Almansa region, showcasing the depth and rusticity of Mourvèdre (locally known as Monastrell). Dark plum and black cherry dominate the primary profile, supported by peppery spice and herbaceous notes characteristic of the variety. Secondary layers reveal earthy undertones and subtle leather, while tertiary development brings dried-fruit complexity and garrigue minerality. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make this wine a versatile table companion for grilled meats, braised dishes, and Mediterranean fare.

name: Chateau Ducru Beaucaillou 2019
short_description (EN Store): Saint-Julien Second Growth on 6–8m Günz gravel; 100% new French oak, ~80% Cabernet Sauvignon; 2019 regarded as a defining expression of the Bruno Borie era.
description (EN Store):
When Bruno Borie assumed control in 2003, he made a decision that runs counter to the prevailing Médoc consensus: he cut Grand Vin production from over 20,000 cases to just 6,000–8,000, selecting only 27% of total vineyard output for the label — a severity of selection rare even among First Growths. The 2019 is the clearest argument for that discipline.

The estate's core 50-hectare block, including the Les Sadons parcel planted in 1918, sits on a 6–8 metre bed of Günz gravel — quartz pebbles, lydian jasper, flint, and agatoids deposited by the ancient Garonne roughly two million years ago over Oligocene mud and Eocene limestone. This exceptional gravel depth forces vine roots deep, concentrating mineral tension while the adjacent Gironde estuary moderates temperature extremes through four daily tidal movements.

The 2019 is approximately 80% Cabernet Sauvignon, 20% Merlot, harvested manually with multiple sorting passes including optical sorting, vinified parcel-by-parcel in variable-volume stainless steel tanks with a minimum 7-day maceration. Malolactic fermentation was completed in concrete and stainless vats before 18–20 months in 100% new French oak barriques sourced from five cooperages — a counter-cultural commitment to maximum new oak that Borie has maintained against the broader Bordeaux drift toward restraint.

Unusually, this maximum-oak regime coexists with full organic farming certified from the 2018 vintage (ISO 14001, HVE3). The 2019 is described as regal and concentrated with flawless balance and graphite-laced cassis; drink from 2027 through the 2040s and beyond.

name: Chateau Montrose 2021
short_description (EN Store): 1855 Second Growth on a gravelly Saint-Estèphe croupe; 18 months in 60% new French oak. The 'Latour of Saint-Estèphe' demands cellaring.
description (EN Store): Founded in 1815 by Étienne Théodore Dumoulin on land that had never before been planted to vine, Château Montrose achieved Second Growth status within just 40 years — a trajectory unmatched across all 1855 Classified Growths. That ambition is geological as much as historical: the estate's 95-hectare single-block vineyard — unusually undivided for modern Bordeaux — sits on a gravelly croupe in the south-eastern corner of Saint-Estèphe, where Pyrenean and Massif Central coarse gravel extends more than two metres deep over a clay-marl subsoil. The gravel drains aggressively, stressing roots downward and concentrating phenolics; the clay beneath retains just enough moisture to sustain old Cabernet Sauvignon vines planted as far back as 1932. Eight hundred metres of Gironde estuary buffer the site against spring frost and summer heat spikes, preserving acidity through ripening. Parcel-by-parcel vinification in a gravity-flow winery (installed during the Bouygues family's €20 million, 2007–2013 renovation) feeds 18 months of aging in French oak barriques at 60% new wood — a regime that builds structural density without masking the estate's signature graphite-mineral spine. The Grand Vin represents only 35–40% of total production, a strict selection rate that keeps the blend tightly focused. What no peer in Saint-Estèphe replicates is Montrose's deliberate refusal to chase early accessibility: the tannic architecture here is built for decades, not years. The 2021 vintage, a cool, precise growing season in the Médoc, produced wines of firm structure and lifted acidity — expect this to reward patience well into the 2040s.

name: Chateau Palmer  Margaux 2021
short_description (EN Store): Médoc rule-breaker: 47% Merlot planted on Cantenac-Margaux gravel croupes, biodynamic since 2017, unfined and unfiltered.
description (EN Store): When Major General Charles Palmer renamed and expanded the Domaine de Gascq from 1814, he could not have anticipated that two centuries later the estate would deliberately invert Médoc orthodoxy by planting Merlot on its finest gravel outcrops — the very parcels where Cabernet Sauvignon conventionally dominates. That transgression defines every bottle. The Cantenac-Margaux plateau sits on Gunzian Garonne alluvial gravel over deeper gravel subsoil, with 18 distinct soil mosaic types across 122 plots; the Gironde estuary moderates diurnal swings and extends the growing season, allowing Merlot to ripen with aromatic complexity rather than jammy weight. Vinification proceeds plot-by-plot across 42 temperature-controlled stainless steel vats, with indigenous yeasts, a cold soak near 8°C, and zero sulfur at harvest — a protocol locked in from 2015. Élevage runs 20–22 months: the first year in 225-litre French oak barriques at under 50% new wood, the second in 30-hectolitre oak vats to integrate without over-oaking; the Grand Vin is bottled unfined and unfiltered. The special feature here is structural: no other classified Médoc growth commits 47% of its vineyard to Merlot on premier gravel, and full biodynamic certification (2017–2018) adds a terroir transparency that separates Palmer from peers still in conventional viticulture. The 2021 vintage is not represented in the brand library's scored notes, but the growing season's cool, precise character points to a wine of Margaux finesse and restrained concentration; drink from 2027 through 2045.

name: Château Lafon-Rochet 2019
short_description (EN Store): Powerful Saint-Estèphe Bordeaux blend with dark cassis, cedar, and structured tannins. Age-worthy classic.
description (EN Store): Château Lafon-Rochet 2019 is a classically structured Bordeaux blend from Saint-Estèphe, built on a foundation of ripe blackcurrant and dark plum fruit. Cedar and tobacco notes emerge on the mid-palate, supported by firm, well-integrated tannins and bracing acidity. The wine displays the mineral precision and age-worthiness characteristic of the appellation, with leather and slate complexity developing in the glass. Designed for cellaring, it rewards patient collectors with evolving secondary and tertiary layers over the next 15–20 years.

name: Chateau Pontet-Canet  AOC Pauillac 2017
short_description (EN Store): Biodynamic 5th Growth from Günz-era gravel over limestone; indigenous-yeast fermentation, 35% concrete dolia aging — Pauillac redrawn without oak dominance.
description (EN Store): When Alfred Tesseron certified all 81 hectares of Pontet-Canet under both Ecocert and Biodyvin in 2010 — the first Médoc classified growth to do so — the decision was not cosmetic: it forced a complete rethinking of extraction, vessel choice, and cellar design, culminating in a 2017 facility built with hemp insulation and 12-volt LED lighting to eliminate electromagnetic interference during élevage. The estate's core plateau sits on Günz-era Garonne gravel over limestone bedrock, poor and sharply drained, concentrating Cabernet Sauvignon into tight, mineral-driven fruit; a secondary zone of clay-limestone closer to the Gironde carries the ≈32% Merlot component, adding mid-palate density without diluting structure. Hand harvest with double manual sorting, hand destemming above the vat (introduced 2017), and gravity-fed transfer into custom 40 hl conical concrete tanks precede indigenous-yeast fermentation with soft extraction. Élevage runs 16–18 months across three vessels: ≈50% new French oak barriques, 35% custom limestone-and-cement dolia (introduced 2012, a vessel type unprecedented at this classification level in Bordeaux), and 15% second-fill French oak. The 2017 vintage delivered a cooler, more tension-driven Pauillac than the opulent 2016, with the dolia fraction preserving fruit clarity and saline minerality. Drink 2025–2042.

name: Château Dauzac 2019
short_description (EN Store): Margaux classic: blackcurrant, cedar, tobacco. Age-worthy Bordeaux blend with structured tannins.
description (EN Store): Château Dauzac 2019 is a classically structured Margaux that exemplifies the region's elegant power. Blackcurrant and dark plum dominate the primary aromatics, supported by cedar and graphite minerality. Secondary oak and tobacco notes add complexity, while the wine's firm, high tannin structure and medium-high acidity promise graceful evolution over the next 15–20 years. The tertiary leather and dried-herb character will emerge with bottle age, rewarding patient cellaring.

name: Comte Desclos Bordeaux
short_description (EN Store): Full-bodied Bordeaux blend with blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco—age-worthy structure for serious red meat.
description (EN Store): Comte Desclos Bordeaux is a classically structured Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot blend from France's most prestigious red wine region. This full-bodied wine displays dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, complemented by cedar and tobacco notes that speak to careful oak aging. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide the backbone for extended cellaring, while tertiary leather and graphite complexity emerge with time. A serious, food-friendly expression of traditional Bordeaux winemaking.

name: Torbreck Descendant 2008
short_description (EN Store): Barossa Valley Syrah-Viognier blend with blackberry, pepper, and smoke—a full-bodied, age-worthy classic.
description (EN Store): Torbreck's Descendant 2008 is a Rhône-inspired Syrah-Viognier blend from Barossa Valley's Roennfeldt Road, showcasing the region's signature dark-fruit intensity and peppery spice. The 2008 vintage displays ripe blackberry and plum on the nose, layered with black pepper, vanilla oak, and subtle smoke. Full-bodied with medium-high tannins and acidity, this wine carries the structure and complexity expected of a premium Barossa Syrah, with tertiary notes of earth and leather emerging from over a decade of bottle age. A sophisticated choice for fine dining and serious red-meat pairings.

name: Chateau Mitrotes Saint Emilion Grand Cru
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Saint-Émilion Grand Cru blending Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot with blackcurrant depth, cedar complexity, and age-worthy structure.
description (EN Store): Château Mitrotes Saint-Émilion Grand Cru represents the classic Right Bank Bordeaux style, marrying the structure of Cabernet Sauvignon with the elegance of Merlot. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by bright black cherry notes. Cedar and tobacco emerge on the mid-palate, adding sophisticated secondary complexity, while spice rounds the profile. With bottle age, leather and earthy undertones develop, revealing the wine's tertiary potential. Full-bodied with high tannins and medium-high acidity, this is a serious, age-worthy expression suited to formal dining and cellar investment.

name: Chateau Pichon Longueville Baron 2009
short_description (EN Store): Elegant 2009 Pauillac with blackcurrant, cedar, and tobacco complexity. Age-worthy Bordeaux blend showing classic Left Bank structure.
description (EN Store): Château Pichon Longueville Baron's 2009 vintage exemplifies the power and refinement of Pauillac. A Cabernet Sauvignon–led blend with Merlot support, this wine displays the region's signature blackcurrant and cedar character, layered with tobacco and graphite notes that speak to its terroir and aging potential. Full-bodied with firm, well-integrated tannins and medium-high acidity, it demands decanting and pairs beautifully with grilled red meat, lamb, and game. The wine's secondary oak influence and emerging tertiary complexity suggest it will continue evolving gracefully over the next decade.

name: Penfolds Bin 389 Cabernet Shiraz 2023 with Gift Boxes
short_description (EN Store): Cabernet/Shiraz aged in ex-Grange barrels — Penfolds' deliberate hand-me-down oak strategy gives Bin 389 its structural backbone without new-wood dominance.
description (EN Store): Nicknamed the 'Baby Grange' not for diminutive ambition but for a literal material connection: Bin 389 is matured in the same barrels that previously held Grange (Bin 95), a deliberate resource-chain decision that transfers residual tannin structure and seasoned oak character without the aggression of virgin wood. The logic is economic and stylistic simultaneously — the barrels are already shaped by one of Australia's most age-worthy reds, and Bin 389 inherits that architecture. Fruit is drawn from Penfolds' multi-regional South Australian network, with the Barossa Valley's ancient, deep alluvial and sandy loam soils — phylloxera-free, old-vine material from Kalimna's 290-hectare property — contributing the Shiraz's characteristic dark-fruited density and plush mid-palate weight. Cabernet Sauvignon adds structural spine and cassis-driven length. True to Penfolds' founding philosophy, no single terroir anchors the blend; winemaker judgment over fixed geography is the house doctrine, unchanged since Max Schubert's era. What separates Bin 389 within its category is precisely this ex-Grange barrel regime: no peer at this price point can claim the same oak provenance. The 2023 vintage reflects a growing season assessed by James Suckling's team for polish and precision across the Penfolds collection; drink 2026–2034.

name: Penfolds FWT 585 Cabernet Merot Petit Verdot 2022 with Gift Box
short_description (EN Store): Barossa Cabernet-Merlot-Petit Verdot from Penfolds' Kalimna estate — ancient alluvial soils, house blending philosophy, gift-boxed 2022.
description (EN Store): The French Winemaking Trials (FWT) series represents one of Penfolds' most deliberate transgressions: a house built on American oak and Shiraz supremacy turning its multi-regional blending intellect toward Bordeaux varieties and French oak discipline. FWT 585 is a Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Petit Verdot blend drawing on Barossa Valley fruit — anchored by Kalimna's ancient, deep alluvial and sandy loam soils, phylloxera-free and among the oldest continuously-farmed red-wine country in Australia. These free-draining soils concentrate berry flavour while moderating vine vigour, producing small-berried fruit with natural structural density. Winemaking follows Penfolds' signature approach of acclimatised pure yeast cultures and temperature-controlled fermentation — a legacy of Ray Beckwith's pH-meter innovations — with maturation in French oak, departing from the American hogshead regime that defines Grange and Bin 389. The inclusion of Petit Verdot is the structural signature here: a variety rarely bottled at this price point in Australia, it deepens colour, lifts mid-palate density, and extends the tannin frame beyond what Cabernet alone delivers. The 2022 vintage in Barossa delivered moderate temperatures and well-distributed rainfall, producing fruit with natural freshness alongside the valley's characteristic generosity — a drinking window that opens earlier than the flagship Bins while retaining Penfolds' house architecture.

name: Domaine Du Pegau  Chateauneuf du Pape da Capo 2020
short_description (EN Store): Grenache-led Châteauneuf-du-Pape with dark cherry, garrigue, and structured tannins—a classic Rhône South blend.
description (EN Store): Domaine du Pegau's Châteauneuf-du-Pape da Capo 2020 is a traditional Rhône South blend anchored by Grenache, with supporting Syrah and Mourvèdre. The wine opens with dominant dark cherry and blackberry fruit, layered with garrigue herbs and black pepper spice characteristic of the appellation's terroir. Medium-full body and medium-high tannins provide structure without heaviness, while tertiary notes of leather and earth suggest complexity and age-worthiness. A food-friendly expression suited to grilled lamb, beef stew, and Mediterranean cuisine.

name: Chateau Clerc Milon  2022
short_description (EN Store): Pauillac classic: blackcurrant, cedar, tobacco. Age-worthy Bordeaux blend with structured tannins.
description (EN Store): Château Clerc Milon's 2022 vintage exemplifies the power and elegance of Pauillac. A Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot blend, this wine opens with ripe blackcurrant and dark plum, layered with cedar and graphite minerality. The palate is structured and full-bodied, with high tannins that promise decades of evolution. Secondary notes of tobacco and leather emerge with air, while the finish lingers with black pepper spice and subtle oak influence. This is a wine built for the cellar—classic Left Bank Bordeaux architecture designed to develop complexity over 15–25 years.

name: Chateau Clerc Milon  2023
short_description (EN Store): Pauillac powerhouse: ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins. Age-worthy Bordeaux classic.
description (EN Store): Château Clerc Milon's 2023 vintage exemplifies the power and elegance of Pauillac. A Cabernet Sauvignon–led Bordeaux blend, it opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, layered with cedar and tobacco complexity. The palate is full-bodied and structured, with high tannins that promise graceful evolution over the next 15–20 years. Secondary oak and black pepper notes frame the wine's architecture, while tertiary slate and earth minerality hint at the gravelly terroir. This is a wine built for the cellar and the table—equally at home aging in a collection or paired with grilled red meat, lamb, and aged cheeses today.

name: Viticcio Chianti Classico DOCG
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Sangiovese from Chianti Classico with bright cherry, dried herbs, and mineral-driven complexity.
description (EN Store): A classically structured Sangiovese from Tuscany's Chianti region, this wine expresses the varietal's signature combination of bright red fruit, herbaceous minerality, and earthy undertones. The medium-full body and high acidity provide excellent food compatibility, while medium-high tannins offer structure and aging potential. Dried fruit and subtle spice notes emerge on the mid-palate, with leather and tobacco complexity developing in the finish—hallmarks of well-made Chianti Classico.

name: Viticcio Chianti Classico Riserva DOCG
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Sangiovese Riserva from Chianti Classico with cherry, dried herbs, and mineral complexity.
description (EN Store): A classically structured Sangiovese Riserva from Chianti's heartland, this wine displays the region's hallmark balance of bright acidity and refined tannin. Cherry and red fruit dominate the primary impression, supported by dried herb and mineral notes that speak to Tuscan terroir. Secondary leather and tobacco complexity emerges on the palate, suggesting careful aging and winemaking discipline. Medium-Full body and high acidity make this a versatile food wine, equally at home with tomato-based pasta, grilled red meat, or aged cheeses.

name: Viticcio Prunaio Gran Selezione Chianti Classico DOCG
short_description (EN Store): Elegant Chianti Classico with bright cherry, herbaceous herbs, and earthy leather—a structured, food-friendly Tuscan red.
description (EN Store): Viticcio's Gran Selezione Prunaio represents the refined expression of Sangiovese from Chianti's heartland. This medium-full-bodied red opens with vibrant cherry and herbaceous aromatics, grounded by earthy minerality and subtle spice. The palate reveals layered complexity: bright acidity drives the wine forward, while medium-high tannins provide structure and grip. Secondary leather and tobacco notes emerge with air, suggesting careful oak aging and potential for further development. A classic Chianti built for the table—equally at home with rustic Tuscan fare or contemporary cuisine.

name: Viticcio Bolgheri DOC
short_description (EN Store): Sangiovese-based Bolgheri red with cherry, herbaceous notes, and structured tannins. Food-friendly Italian classic.
description (EN Store): Viticcio's Bolgheri Sangiovese expresses the characteristic profile of Tuscany's coastal terroir: bright cherry fruit, herbaceous undertones, and a mineral spine. Medium-Full body and high acidity provide structure and food compatibility, while medium-high tannins suggest moderate aging potential. The wine balances primary fruit expression with earthy secondary complexity and emerging tertiary leather and tobacco notes, positioning it as a versatile table wine for grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, and aged cheeses.

name: Cono Sur  Bicicleta Pinot Noir
short_description (EN Store): Chile's Pinot Noir pioneer, entry-tier Bicicleta from Colchagua — screwcap-sealed since 2002, cool-climate fruit, temperature-controlled stainless.
description (EN Store): When Cono Sur launched as an independent subsidiary of Concha y Toro in 1993, its founding ambition was singular: prove that Chile could produce serious Pinot Noir. The Bicicleta line sits at the base of that pyramid, but it draws on the same house philosophy that placed Burgundian consultant Martin Prieur of Domaine Jacques Prieur in a New World cellar — a first for Chile. Sourced from Colchagua Valley, where the Santa Elisa estate in Chimbarongo holds the oldest documented Pinot Noir vines in Chile (planted 1968), the valley's alluvial soils and warm days tempered by Andean night-cooling deliver ripe, fruit-forward character without sacrificing freshness. Vinification is entirely in temperature-controlled stainless steel, preserving primary aromatics and keeping the wine approachable from release at 13% ABV. No oak aging is applied at this tier — the fruit speaks without wood interference.
